### [1] [Stereo Vision-Based Fall Prediction and Detection using Human Pose Estimation on the AMD Kria K26 SOM](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12473)
*Shreyas Narasimhiah Ramesh,P. D. Rathika,Mahasweta Sarkar,Kristen Wells,Michel Audette,Christopher Paolini*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A portable, low-power, privacy-preserving, vision-based fall detection system on an AMD Kria K26 SOM using RGB for person detection, depth for 3D keypoints, and a CNN classifier; on-device processing with improved throughput and no cloud dependency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Protect elderly individuals by enabling real-time, non-intrusive fall prediction/detection while preserving privacy and reducing dependence on cloud processing.

Method: Three-stage edge pipeline: (1) YOLOX on RGB frames to detect humans; (2) A2J on depth frames to estimate 15 joint keypoints per person; (3) a CNN that classifies falls from selected joint coordinates. The RGB data are discarded after detection to preserve privacy. The system runs on an AMD Kria K26 SOM with a single-core DPU for a serial pipeline and a dual-core DPU for a multi-threaded setup, using synchronized RGB and depth streams (640x480 at 60 FPS). Training data sources include CrowdHuman (YOLOX), ITOP/MP-3DHP/UR Fall Detection/SDSU PSG (A2J), and UR Fall Detection/SDSU PSG (CNN).

Result: Quantized accuracy: YOLOX IoU ≥50% = 74%; A2J mAP with 10-cm rule = 84.13%; CNN classification accuracy = 75.85%. Throughput improved from 2.5 FPS (single-thread) to 4.5 FPS (multi-thread).

Conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility of privacy-preserving, on-device fall prediction/detection on an edge device (Kria K26) with no cloud dependency; suitable for elderly monitoring. Future work to boost accuracy and speed.

Abstract: Background and Objective: Falls among elderly people can cause serious injury and reduce quality of life. Timely prediction and detection are essential to prevent harm and support well-being. We propose a portable, low-power, battery-operated, vision-based fall prediction and detection system using HPE on an AMD Kria K26 System-on-Module (SOM). The objective is a non-intrusive, privacy-preserving system for real-time fall detection.
  Methods: The system uses an Intel RealSense D455 range-sensing camera connected to the K26 SOM by USB. It captures synchronized RGB and depth frames, 640 x 480 x 3 and 640 x 480 pixels, at 60 FPS. The SOM runs a three-stage pipeline with quantized YOLOX, Anchor-to-Joint (A2J), and fall-detection models. YOLOX identifies human bounding boxes from RGB frames, then discards the RGB frames to preserve privacy. A2J uses depth frames to estimate 15 joint keypoints per person. A CNN uses selected joint coordinates (x, y, z) to classify fall activity. YOLOX was trained on CrowdHuman; A2J on ITOP, MP-3DHP, UR Fall Detection, and a custom SDSU PSG dataset; and the CNN on UR Fall Detection and SDSU PSG. The design used a single-core DPU with a serial pipeline and a dual-core DPU running YOLOX and A2J with multiple threads.
  Results: Quantized accuracy was evaluated using IoU >= 50% for YOLOX, mAP with a 10-cm rule for A2J, and classification accuracy, (TP + TN)/(TP + TN + FP + FN), for the CNN. Accuracies were 74%, 84.13%, and 75.85%. Throughput improved from 2.5 FPS for the single-threaded pipeline to 4.5 FPS for the multi-threaded version.
  Conclusion: Results demonstrate the feasibility of privacy-preserving fall detection on an AMD Kria K26 edge device. On-device HPE and fall classification runs without cloud dependency, supporting elderly monitoring and assistive healthcare. Future work will improve model accuracy and speed.

</details>


### [2] [HairPort: In-context 3D-aware Hair Import and Transfer for Images](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12562)
*Alireza Heidari,Amirhossein Alimohammadi,Wallace Michel Pinto Lira,Adi Bar-Lev,Ali Mahdavi-Amiri*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: 3D-aware hairstyle transfer (HairPort) handles large pose/scale gaps by separating hair removal from transfer, introducing a Bald Converter (LoRA-adapted FLUX.1 Kontext), a Baldy dataset, a 3D-aware transfer pipeline for geometry-aligned reference guidance, and a conditional flow-matching generator, yielding pose-consistent, identity-preserving results better than prior methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address limitations of prior hairstyle transfer methods under large viewpoint and scale differences, where missing hair content must be synthesized while preserving identity and realism.

Method: 1) Decouple hair removal from transfer; enforce geometric consistency before synthesis. 2) Bald Converter: create realistic bald faces via LoRA-based in-context adaptation of FLUX.1 Kontext. 3) Baldy dataset: 6,000 paired bald/original images across identities/conditions. 4) 3D-Aware Transfer Pipeline: reconstructs and re-renders the reference hairstyle from the target viewpoint, then composites onto the source. 5) Conditional flow-matching generator: synthesizes the transferred result using bald source and geometry-aligned guidance.

Result: The approach enables accurate, pose-consistent, identity-preserving hairstyle transfer under large pose/scale differences and outperforms existing methods both qualitatively and quantitatively, supported by the Baldy dataset and 3D-aware pipeline.

Conclusion: HairPort combines hair removal, geometry alignment, and learned synthesis to enable robust 3D-aware hairstyle transfer. It introduces the Bald Converter and Baldy dataset and demonstrates improved performance; future work could explore efficiency, more diverse identities, and real-time applications.

Abstract: Transferring hairstyles between images is an important but challenging task in computer graphics, computer vision, and visual effects. It enables users to explore new looks without physically altering their hair, with applications in virtual try-on systems, augmented reality, and entertainment. Most prior works operate best under small pose gaps, and they fall short under large viewpoint and scale differences, where missing hair content must be synthesized rather than transferred. We propose HairPort, a 3D-aware hairstyle transfer framework that attempts to solve these issues by explicitly separating hair removal from transfer and enforcing geometric consistency before synthesis. We introduce a Bald Converter, which produces realistic bald versions of faces through LoRA-based in-context adaptation of FLUX.1 Kontext. To train our Bald Converter, we introduce a new dataset, Baldy, containing 6,000 paired bald and original images across diverse identities and conditions. We also use a 3D-Aware Transfer Pipeline that reconstructs and re-renders the reference hairstyle from the target viewpoint before compositing it onto the source image. Being 3D aware, our method supports large pose and scale discrepancies between the source and target. Finally, a conditional flow-matching generator synthesizes the transferred result from the bald source and geometry-aligned reference guidance. Together, our method enables accurate, pose-consistent, and identity-preserving hairstyle transfer, outperforming existing methods both qualitatively and quantitatively.

</details>


### [3] [High-Fidelity Two-Step Image Generation via Teacher-Aligned End-to-End Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12575)
*Dongyang Liu,Ruoyi Du,David Liu,Dengyang Jiang,Liangchen Li,Qilong Wu,Zhen Li,Steven C. H. Hoi,Hongsheng Li,Peng Gao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A 2-step diffusion model, Z-Image Turbo++, distilled from an 8-step teacher, uses distribution-aligned adversarial learning, step-decoupled parameterization, and end-to-end iterative regularization to narrow the quality gap with the 8-step model.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Push from 4-8-step maturity to reliable 2-step generation; address bottlenecks of task difficulty and limited capacity in aggressive distillation.

Method: Three design choices: Distribution-Aligned Adversarial Learning (teacher-generated images as GAN real targets); Step-Decoupled Parameterization (separate parameters for the two denoising steps); End-to-End Training with Iterative Regularization (gradient from final image quality to first step with step-1 loss).

Result: Significant narrowing of the quality gap between 2-step and 8-step generation in both qualitative and quantitative evaluations; demonstrates potential of tailored distillation for improved quality-efficiency trade-off.

Conclusion: Tailored distillation strategies can bridge the 2-step vs 8-step gap; might generalize to other few-step regimes and encourage efficient high-quality generation.

Abstract: Few-step diffusion distillation has become increasingly mature for 4-8-step generation, yet pushing further to 2 steps remains challenging. In this work, we introduce Z-Image Turbo++, a high-quality 2-step image generation model distilled from the 8-step Z-Image Turbo teacher. Our method addresses the central bottlenecks of increased task difficulty and limited model capacity in 2-step generation through three simple but effective design choices tailored to this regime. First, we propose Distribution-Aligned Adversarial Learning, which uses teacher-generated images rather than external real images as real samples for GAN training, providing a more attainable and informative adversarial target. Second, we adopt Step-Decoupled Parameterization, assigning independent model parameters to the two denoising steps to better match their distinct capacity demands. Third, we perform End-to-End Training with Iterative Regularization, allowing the first step to receive gradients from final image quality while preserving a meaningful intermediate generation through an explicit step-1 loss. Together, these designs substantially narrow the quality gap between 2-step and 8-step generation in both qualitative and quantitative evaluations, highlighting the potential of carefully tailored distillation strategies for improving the quality-efficiency trade-off in few-step generation.

</details>


### [4] [Analyzing and Improving Fine-grained Preference Optimization in Medical LVLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12590)
*Shayan Mohammadizadehsamakosh,Pritam Sarkar,Leonid Sigal,Ali Etemad,Elham Dolatabadi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: On-policy, token-wise alignment for medical LVLMs using a bidirectional KL regularizer and a visual-contrastive grounding objective, constructing preference pairs via minimal edits to improve factual accuracy and visual grounding; validated on medical imaging and clinical text benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address three core limitations of post-training alignment in medical LVLMs: (1) sequence-level rewards treat critical clinical tokens as filler, (2) off-policy bias from static supervised references, and (3) lack of explicit visual-grounding constraints.

Method: Introduce a bidirectional token-wise KL regularizer combined with a visual-contrastive grounding objective that pairs clean and lesion-corrupted images. Build preference pairs by minimally editing model outputs to fix clinically erroneous spans, preserving original linguistic style; training is on-policy and grounding-aware.

Result: Extensive experiments across medical imaging tasks and clinical text generation benchmarks show improved factual correctness, visual grounding, and alignment with clinically meaningful feedback.

Conclusion: The proposed framework provides a fine-grained, on-policy alignment approach for medical LVLMs, mitigating three key limitations of prior post-training methods and demonstrating robust empirical validation.

Abstract: Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) have achieved strong performance across medical imaging tasks, yet they remain prone to factual inconsistencies, poor visual grounding, and misalignment with clinically meaningful feedback. Existing post-training alignment approaches, including Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) and its variants, face three critical limitations in the medical domain: (1) sequence-level reward signals treat clinically critical tokens identically to generic filler text; (2) reliance on static supervised fine-tuning references as preferred responses introduces an off-policy distribution shift, steering optimization toward stylistic artifacts over clinical correctness; and (3) alignment objectives lack explicit visual grounding constraints, leaving models insensitive to subtle yet diagnostically decisive pathological features. Our method leverages a bidirectional token-wise KL regularizer alongside a visual-contrastive grounding objective that pairs clean and lesion-corrupted images to penalize responses generated without adequate visual evidence. Together, these components form a fine-grained, on-policy alignment framework that constructs preference pairs by minimally editing model-generated outputs, correcting only clinically erroneous spans while preserving the original linguistic style. Extensive experiments across medical imaging tasks and clinical text generation benchmarks validate the effectiveness of our approach.

</details>


### [5] [Dual-State Slot Attention: Decoupling Appearance and Identity for Video Object-Centric Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12601)
*Sieu Tran,Duc Nguyen,Hao Vo,Khoa Vo,Ngan Le*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Dual-State Slot Attention (DSSA) separates per-frame appearance from temporally stable identity within each slot to address object-centric video learning challenges.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome slot swapping and token renormalization instability in unsupervised slot-based video object-centric models, particularly under rapid motion and occlusion, by decoupling appearance and identity representations.

Method: Each slot comprises a local appearance state (per-frame) and an identity state (temporally stable). The identity state is updated by a learned recurrent transition that acts as a temporal filter. Competition-modulated aggregation (CMA) down-weights updates from weakly matching slots to prevent tokens from one object being absorbed by another.

Result: DSSA yields improved segmentation quality and temporal consistency across MOVi-C, MOVi-D, and YouTube-VIS; also enhances downstream object recognition and video dynamics prediction compared to prior methods.

Conclusion: Separating appearance and identity with a recurrent identity state and CMA stabilizes object-centric video learning under challenging conditions, offering robust performance and stronger downstream capabilities; code/models to be released.

Abstract: Unsupervised video object-centric learning aims to decompose dynamic scenes into persistent, object-level representations without supervision. However, existing slot-based methods struggle to maintain stable object identity in challenging settings such as rapid motion and partial occlusion. First, they typically encode both the per-frame appearance of an object and its identity across frames in a single slot vector, creating an objective conflict that leads to slot swapping: reconstruction requires sensitivity to transient visual changes, whereas temporal consistency requires invariance to them. Second, the token renormalization used in Slot Attention can amplify weakly attending slots, allowing them to absorb tokens from other objects and destabilize slot-to-object correspondence.
  We propose Dual-State Slot Attention (DSSA), a fully self-supervised framework that addresses these limitations by separating appearance from identity and by reducing spurious updates from weakly matching slots. DSSA decomposes each slot into a local state for per-frame appearance and an identity state for temporally stable object information, thereby aligning reconstruction and temporal consistency with separate representations. The identity state is updated through a learned recurrent transition that acts as a temporal filter on the local state, while competition-modulated aggregation (CMA) down-weights updates from weakly matching slots and prevents them from absorbing tokens from other objects. Experiments on MOVi-C, MOVi-D, and YouTube-VIS demonstrate that DSSA consistently improves segmentation quality and temporal consistency over prior methods, while also yielding stronger downstream object recognition and video dynamics prediction. Code and models will be made publicly available upon acceptance.

</details>


### [6] [Context-Aware Feature-Fusion for Co-occurring Object Detection in Autonomous Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12628)
*Binay Kumar Singh,Niels Da Vitoria Lobo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CCFF is a context-focused detector that fuses local RoI interactions with a global co-occurrence context to boost relational consistency, small-object detection, and rare-class recovery with real-time efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Autonomous driving requires precise localization and understanding of relations among co-occurring objects. Complex, heterogeneous scenes with rare and small objects challenge standard detectors, leading to poor relational consistency and missed rare classes.

Method: Two attention-based modules: Local Context Fusion Module (LCFM) uses RoI-to-RoI self-attention to model local spatial interactions, aiding small/occluded objects; Global Context Attention Module (GCAM) pools the top-K RoI features into a global context token to capture co-occurrence priors without pixel-level pooling; fused embeddings enhance classification and co-occurring-object detection.

Result: Evaluated on Cityscapes and BDD100K; CCS scores 0.973 and 0.969, indicating strong relational consistency; notable gains for small objects (AP_S +14.1%) and recovery of rare classes like 'Train'; real-time processing with only 0.2 FPS overhead; code released at https://github.com/BinayKSingh/CCFF.

Conclusion: Context-Centric Feature Fusion (CCFF) demonstrates that combining local RoI-level interactions with a global context token yields contextualized embeddings that improve relational reasoning, small-object detection, and rare-class recovery while maintaining efficiency across two benchmarks.

Abstract: Object detection in autonomous driving requires precise localization and an inherent understanding of the relational context between co-occurring objects. In extremely complex heterogeneous environments rare classes, small-scale objects, and frequently appearing objects are difficult for standard object detection frameworks to handle. In this paper, we propose a novel framework called Context-Centric Feature Fusion (CCFF), which utilizes two attention-based modules, Local Context Fusion Module (LCFM) uses the RoI-to-RoI self-attention mechanism to resolve spatial interactions, mainly considering small and partially obscured objects, while Global Context Attention Module (GCAM) converts the co-occurrence of objects priors by pooling top-K RoI features into a global context attention token, avoiding the computational overhead of pixel-level global pooling. This fusion of local and object-centric global features yields contextualized embeddings that enhance classification results and co-occurring objects detection. Our method is evaluated on two datasets, Cityscapes and BDD100K which demonstrate significant improvement on relational consistency, achieving a Category-level Consistency Strategy (CCS) of 0.973 and 0.969, respectively. Furthermore, our approach produces substantial gains in small object detection (AP_S: 14.1%) and successfully recovers rare classes such as "Train" that are typically lost in large distributions. Our efficiency report shows that the framework processes images in real time with a 0.2 FPS overhead. The code is available at https://github.com/BinayKSingh/CCFF.

</details>


### [7] [ECA: Efficient Continual Alignment for Open-Ended Image-to-Text Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12633)
*Jiangtao Kong,Peijun Zhao,Chun-Fu Chen,Youngwook Do,Shaohan Hu,Tianyi Zhou,Huajie Shao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ECA: An exemplar-free continual learning method for OpenITG that online-adapts the cross-modal alignment of pre-trained vision-language models to shifting data distributions using a Mixture of Query, Fisher Dynamic Expansion, and embedding dictionary with Dictionary Replay to mitigate forgetting.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Open-ended image-to-text generation faces non-stationary visual data distributions where the dominant categories evolve over time. There is a need to preserve previously learned cross-modal representations without accessing raw data from previous tasks.

Method: Introduce Efficient Continual Alignment (ECA) with three core mechanisms: (1) Mixture of Query (MoQ) for task-specific query tokens, (2) Fisher Dynamic Expansion (FeDEx) that expands model structure guided by a Fisher Information Matrix (FIM) metric, and (3) embedding dictionary with Dictionary Replay (DR) to retain past knowledge—all exemplar-free and focused on adapting the alignment module of pre-trained vision-language models (VLMs).

Result: Empirical evaluation on four newly constructed IL OpenITG benchmarks shows that ECA substantially reduces catastrophic forgetting and improves continual learning performance compared with baselines.

Conclusion: ECA demonstrates effective exemplar-free continual alignment for OpenITG under evolving data distributions, offering a practical approach for maintaining high-quality cross-modal representations; code and benchmarks are publicly available for reproduction.

Abstract: Incremental Learning (IL) for Open-ended Image-to-Text Generation (OpenITG) enables models to continuously generate accurate, contextually relevant text for new images while preserving previously acquired knowledge. Unlike prior studies, this paper addresses a more practical scenario in which the predominant category of visual data shifts over time as environments evolve. In this context, we introduce a new notion of continual alignment, which incrementally adapts the alignment module within pre-trained VLMs to preserve high-quality cross-modal representations. Based on this idea, we propose Efficient Continual Alignment (ECA), a novel exemplar-free IL approach for OpenITG. The key challenge is enabling the model to acquire new, task-specific features while minimizing interference with the established alignment without accessing raw data from previous tasks. To address this, ECA employs three core mechanisms: a Mixture of Query (MoQ) module that adapts task-specific query tokens, a Fisher Dynamic Expansion (FeDEx) that dynamically expands model structure based on a Fisher Information Matrix (FIM)-based metric, and an embedding dictionary with Dictionary Replay (DR) to retain past knowledge. To evaluate ECA's performance, we construct four new IL OpenITG benchmarks that better reflect real-world scenarios. Experimental results demonstrate that ECA significantly mitigates catastrophic forgetting and improves IL performance compared to baseline methods. Code and benchmarks are available at https://github.com/Snowball0823/ECA.

</details>


### [8] [CD-RCM: Generalizable Continuous-Depth Novel View Synthesis for Reflectance Confocal Microscopy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12635)
*Tooba Imtiaz,Milind Rajadhyaksha,Kivanc Kose,Jennifer Dy*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CD-RCM: a novel-view synthesis approach that converts sparse, depth-resolved RCM stacks into continuous-depth, isotropic 3D representations for arbitrary-view visualization, enabling histology-like analysis without per-patient tuning; achieves high-fidelity synthesis with sub-second inference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RCM produces anisotropic 3D stacks with poor axial resolution, hindering interpretation and cross-sectional analysis. Existing neural rendering focuses on surface views and cannot account for depth-resolved, occlusive imaging in RCM. A dedicated, RCM-specific NVS method is needed for continuous-depth, isotropic visualization without per-patient optimization.

Method: A feedforward network architecture tailored to RCM physics that predicts intermediate depth slices from sparsely sampled en-face stacks. The model accounts for depth-resolved occlusion and the unique axial imaging geometry of RCM, enabling synthesis of unseen depths. Training leverages depth diversity and the occlusive imaging process; designed for fast, sub-second inference.

Result: CD-RCM achieves high-fidelity novel-view synthesis with sub-second inference time. Experimental results demonstrate effective synthesis of intermediate depths and arbitrary-view visualization, indicating isotropic reconstruction and potential for histology-like cross-sections.

Conclusion: CD-RCM provides a tissue- and modality-specific NVS solution that yields continuous-depth, isotropic visualization of RCM data without per-patient optimization, enabling arbitrary-direction sectioning and efficient visualization.

Abstract: Reflectance confocal microscopy (RCM) provides noninvasive, cellular-resolution "optical biopsies" of human skin \emph{in vivo} by acquiring en-face images at successive depths, forming a sparse z-stack. Due to optical limitations, these stacks are anisotropic 3D volumes with lateral resolution (0.5 $μ$m) $\sim$6 times higher compared to axial resolution, which is defined by the optical sectioning (3 $μ$m), limiting the interpretation of tissue. Our goal is to provide continuous-depth visualization by interpolating intermediate sections and making the 3D volume isotropic. Such a representation permits arbitrary-direction sectioning, including histopathology-like cross-sectional examination, without requiring per-patient optimization. To that end, we introduce the first RCM-specific novel-view synthesis (NVS) approach, CD-RCM, a feedforward model that predicts realistic, unseen depths from sparsely sampled RCM stacks. Classical neural rendering methods focus on reconstruction from surface-level multi-view observations. In contrast to surface-level camera views, RCM can acquire optically sectioned en-face images of tissue beyond the surface up to 200 $μ$m. However, during visualization of the RCM stacks, observations of the shallower sections (towards the surface) obscure the deeper ones. This unique axial imaging geometry and layer-dependent anatomical organization motivated our development of a tailored architectural and training framework that explicitly accounts for RCM's depth-resolved, occlusive imaging physics. Experiments demonstrate that CD-RCM achieves high-fidelity novel-view synthesis with sub-second inference time.

</details>


### [9] [SalArt-VQA: Diagnosing Whether VLMs Understand Salient Artifacts in Generated Images](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12671)
*Xiaoxiao Sun,Ruotian Zhang,Junzhe Huang,James Burgess,Serena Yeung-Levy*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SalArt-VQA is a diagnostic benchmark for fine-grained, grounded artifact understanding in AI-generated images, revealing that strong image-level detection can hide poor local grounding in vision-language models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the gap where whole-image artifact detection does not guarantee correct cues, localization, or evidence-based claims, and to quantify grounded understanding in VLMs.

Method: Introduce SalArt-VQA with 950 images and 3,681 human-authored multiple-choice questions across four aligned types (presence, semantic localization, spatial grounding, evidence-grounded defect identification) plus reference splits for calibration and abstention. Evaluate 20 VLMs on artifact images, matched real references, and paired generated references.

Result: The best model achieves 99.37% detection recall on artifact images but only 53.26% of images have all four artifact-side questions correct; sensitivity-calibration tradeoff observed where more sensitive models issue unsupported artifact claims, while conservative models miss true artifacts to avoid false alarms.

Conclusion: High artifact-detection accuracy does not ensure grounded artifact understanding. SalArt-VQA exposes hidden failure modes and enables fine-grained assessment of whether VLM artifact claims are supported by local visual evidence.

Abstract: Vision-language models (VLMs) are increasingly used to detect whether AI-generated images contain visible artifacts, yet their ability to analyze such artifacts remains poorly understood. A correct image-level decision can still hide important failures: a model may correctly flag an artifact while relying on the wrong visual cue, selecting the wrong region, or describing a defect that the image does not support. To evaluate these behaviors directly, we introduce SalArt-VQA, a diagnostic benchmark for fine-grained SALient ARTifact understanding in AI-generated images. SalArt-VQA contains 950 images and 3,681 human-authored multiple-choice questions spanning artifact images, matched real reference images, and paired generated reference images. Four aligned question types evaluate presence detection, semantic localization, spatial grounding, and evidence-grounded defect identification, while the reference splits test calibration and abstention when the annotated defect is absent. Across 20 VLMs, SalArt-VQA reveals failures that image-level detection accuracy hides: the strongest model reaches 99.37% detection recall on artifact images but answers all four artifact-side questions correctly on only 53.26% of images. Comparing artifact images with artifact-free references reveals a sensitivity-calibration tradeoff: sensitive models often make unsupported artifact claims, while conservative models avoid false alarms largely by missing real artifacts. These results show that high artifact detection accuracy alone does not imply grounded artifact understanding. SalArt-VQA exposes these hidden failure modes and provides a fine-grained evaluation of whether VLM artifact claims are supported by local visual evidence.

</details>


### [10] [VLADriveBench: Evaluating CoT-Action Relationship in VLA for Autonomous Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12706)
*Thach Nguyen,Danhua Guo,Tom Lampo,Fei Wu,Burhan Yaman*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VLADriveBench couples observational CoT metrics with a CoT intervention protocol to evaluate the relation between CoT reasoning and driving actions in VLA models, revealing that observational alignment and causal influence of CoT can diverge across models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current benchmarks assess only trajectory quality, neglecting whether generated chain-of-thought (CoT) is relevant, consistent, or causally linked to actions. A framework that jointly measures observational CoT properties and causal impact is needed to properly evaluate VLA models.

Method: Propose VLADriveBench with observational metrics (mentioning, hallucination, contradiction, action alignment) and a CoT intervention protocol. Apply to three models across two architectures to compare CoT-functionality.

Result: Observational alignment and causal influence can diverge: ORION shows high observational alignment but epiphenomenal CoT, Alpamayo v1.5 shows lower observational metrics but strong causal CoT; visual salience gates the CoT influence.

Conclusion: Evaluating CoT relevance requires both observational metrics and causal intervention analyses; frameworks like VLADriveBench can uncover mismatches between what the model says and what it actually uses to drive actions, guiding more reliable evaluation and model design.

Abstract: Vision-language-action (VLA) models generate chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning alongside driving trajectories, but existing benchmarks evaluate only trajectory quality and do not assess whether the CoT is relevant, consistent, or causally connected to the driving action. We introduce VLADriveBench, a framework that combines observational metrics (mentioning, hallucination, contradiction, action alignment) with a CoT intervention protocol to provide complementary views of the CoT-action relationship. Applying VLADriveBench to three models across two architectures, we find that the two analyses can diverge sharply: ORION scores highest on observational alignment yet its CoT is epiphenomenal, while Alpamayo v1.5 scores lower yet its CoT is strongly causal, with visual salience gating the extent of CoT influence.

</details>


### [11] [GRIP: Feedback-Guided Prompt Retrieval for Large Multimodal Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12744)
*Garvita Allabadi,Matteo Sodano,Roberto Estevão,Yuxiong Wang,Vikram Adve,Emre Kiciman,Ranveer Chandra*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GRIP: a learnable, feedback-driven vision-only retrieval for M-ICL that outperforms similarity-based retrieval across classification, captioning, and VQA; demonstrates cross-model transfer (including GPT-4o and Gemini) and strongest gains in classification on Idefics2-8B; code to be released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In-context learning with multimodal models often relies on similar-looking examples, but semantic similarity does not always yield the best prompts. A retrieval strategy that uses model feedback to select beneficial prompts can improve predictive performance.

Method: GRIP trains a vision-only retriever using contrastive learning driven by LMM feedback to distinguish beneficial vs detrimental in-context examples, going beyond pure feature-space similarity. The retriever is transferable across LMMs (open and closed) without retraining.

Result: Across three multimodal tasks (classification, captioning, VQA) on Qwen2.5-VL-7B and Idefics2-8B, GRIP consistently surpasses similarity-based retrieval, with notable gains in classification on Idefics2-8B. Demonstrates cross-model transferability of retrievers to GPT-4o and Gemini without retraining.

Conclusion: Feedback-guided, vision-only retrieval enables more effective M-ICL than similarity-based approaches and can be deployed across models, including closed-source LMMs, with retrievers transferable without retraining.

Abstract: In-Context Learning (ICL) has become a powerful mechanism for adapting Large Language Models (LLMs) to new tasks without fine-tuning. Extending this concept to Large Multimodal Models (LMMs), Multimodal In-Context Learning (M-ICL) relies on retrieving relevant examples, such as images, captions, or question-answer pairs, to guide predictions across tasks like classification, captioning, and visual question answering (VQA). Most existing approaches select in-context examples based on feature-space similarity, assuming that semantically similar samples provide the most useful context. However, our systematic analysis reveals that this assumption does not always hold: visually similar examples are not necessarily those that most effectively enhance in-context learning performance.
  To address this, we propose the Guided Retrieval of In-context Prompts (GRIP), a learnable vision-only retrieval framework that leverages feedback from LMMs to identify examples that truly improve model predictions. GRIP learns to distinguish beneficial from detrimental in-context examples through contrastive training, refining retrieval beyond pure similarity. Across three multimodal tasks, namely classification, captioning, and VQA, GRIP improves consistently over similarity-based retrieval on Qwen2.5-VL-7B, with its strongest gains in classification on Idefics2-8B. Moreover, we demonstrate that retrievers trained with feedback from one open LMM can be transferred to other models without retraining, including closed-source GPT-4o and Gemini, enabling scalable and cost-efficient deployment of M-ICL. Code will be published upon acceptance.

</details>


### [12] [DIMOS: Disentangling Instance-level Moving Object Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12826)
*Hongxiang Huang,Hongwei Ren,Xiaopeng Lin,Yulong Huang,Zeke Xie,Bojun Cheng*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Dual-disentangling feature extraction for multimodal moving instance segmentation (MIS) using event and image data, coupled with multi-granularity cross-modal alignment to improve small-instance performance under fast motion and low-light conditions; achieves state-of-the-art results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MIS struggles with sparse event features under limited resolution and entangled appearance/motion in event data; disentangling and cross-modal alignment aim to enrich feature density and enable effective fusion.

Method: Introduce a dual-disentangling framework that separates appearance and motion in both image and event modalities, followed by multi-granularity cross-modal alignment to align distributions and semantics across modalities for robust fusion of spatial and temporal cues.

Result: State-of-the-art performance in multimodal MIS, with notable improvements for small instances under challenging conditions (fast motion, low-light).

Conclusion: Disentangled feature extraction and multi-granularity cross-modal alignment enhance MIS robustness and small-object segmentation by effectively leveraging complementary information from images and events.

Abstract: Moving instance segmentation (MIS) attracts increasing attention due to its broad applications in traffic surveillance, autonomous driving, and animal tracking. Event cameras record asynchronous brightness changes, providing high temporal resolution and dynamic range, which makes them highly sensitive to motion information. By fusing event and image features, motion cues from events can complement spatial details from images, enhancing the performance of MIS. However, current multimodal MIS methods still struggle to segment small moving instances, as event cameras often yield sparse features under limited resolution. Moreover, event features entangle appearance attributes with motion cues, which further restricts effective cross-modal fusion. To address these challenges, we first propose a dual-disentangling feature extraction framework that separates and extracts appearance and motion information within both image and event modalities, thereby improving feature density. Subsequently, a multi-granularity cross-modal alignment is introduced to align distributionally and semantically consistent features across modalities, enabling more effective fusion with rich spatial and temporal details. The experiment results demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance in multimodal MIS, especially for small instances under challenging conditions such as fast motion and low-light settings.

</details>


### [13] [Perceive, Interact, Reason: Building Tool-Augmented Visual Agents for Spatial Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12830)
*Changye Li,Meng Lu,Yi Wu,Ligeng Zhu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PERIA is a tool-augmented visual agent that uses perception and interaction tools to improve spatial reasoning across multiple benchmarks, achieving substantial gains versus strong baselines and approaching larger models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Vision-language models rely on implicit visual representations that are insufficient for fine-grained spatial evidence and active evidence gathering; a system enabling explicit visual evidence exposure and interactive manipulation can enhance spatial reasoning.

Method: Introduce PERIA with two tool families (vision perception tools and vision interaction tools). Train with unified recipe combining supervised tool-use trajectory synthesis, composite rewards, and Observation-Relaxed Group-in-Group Policy Optimization (OR-GIGPO) for effective multi-tool behavior.

Result: On 13 benchmarks across 8 datasets, PERIA-8B improves over the Qwen3-8B backbone by 10.0% in-distribution and 4.4% out-of-distribution, surpassing prior SOTA baselines of similar size by 7.0%-14.8%; comparable to much larger models such as Qwen3-VL-235B-A22B-Thinking and GPT-5.

Conclusion: Tool-augmented PERIA demonstrates enhanced spatial reasoning, showing strong performance gains and competitiveness with larger VLMs.

Abstract: While recent vision-language models (VLMs) demonstrate strong multimodal understanding, they remain limited in spatial reasoning tasks that require active evidence acquisition and multi-step visual interaction. This limitation suggests that relying solely on implicit visual representations from vision encoders is insufficient for recovering fine-grained spatial evidence. We introduce PERception-Interaction-reason Agent (PERIA), a tool-augmented visual agent for spatial reasoning tasks across map reasoning, visual probing, and vision reconstruction. PERIA uses two lightweight tool families: vision perception tools for exposing textual, symbolic, and spatial evidence, and vision interaction tools for manipulating visual context, tracing paths, and verifying spatial relations. To train PERIA, we develop a unified recipe that combines supervised tool-use trajectory synthesis, composite rewards, and Observation-Relaxed Group-in-Group Policy Optimization (OR-GIGPO) for effective multi-tool behavior. Experiments on 13 benchmarks from 8 datasets show that PERIA-8B improves over the Qwen3-8B backbone by 10.0% on in-distribution benchmarks and 4.4% on out-of-distribution benchmarks, while outperforming previous state-of-the-art baselines of similar size by 7.0%-14.8%. It also achieves performance comparable to much larger models such as Qwen3-VL-235B-A22B-Thinking and GPT-5, demonstrating the effectiveness of PERIA in enhancing spatial reasoning capabilities.

</details>


### [14] [Language-Guided Abstraction for Visual Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12847)
*Xu-Jing Ye,Yuan-Gen Wang,Ruping Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces L-VARC, a lightweight, language-guided ARC framework that uses a training-time privileged information (LUPI) branch with a Semantic Compression Module and a Cross-Attention Projector to align visual features with semantic embeddings, achieving state-of-the-art visual reasoning on ARC with ~18M parameters and no LUPI during inference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: ARC methods suffer from either heavy language models (billions of parameters) or vision-only models that struggle with high-level semantics and can overfit pixel-level patterns. A hybrid approach leveraging linguistic priors while maintaining lightweight inference is needed.

Method: Incorporates a Semantic Compression Module that feeds a unified, task-agnostic prompt into DeepSeek-V3 to refine LARC into a context-friendly semantic representation. Adds a Cross-Attention Projector to align visual features with semantic embeddings. Employs a training-time LUPI branch that is discarded at inference, yielding a lightweight model (~18M params). Evaluates on ARC with ablations to validate contributions.

Result: Empirically outperforms state-of-the-art on ARC visual reasoning tasks. Ablation studies confirm the contributions of the Semantic Compression Module and the Cross-Attention Projector. Code is provided at the project URL.

Conclusion: L-VARC demonstrates that linguistic priors can substantially improve visual reasoning in ARC when integrated through a training-time privileged information pathway, while maintaining a compact, inference-efficient model. The two proposed components are jointly beneficial and well-validated through ablations.

Abstract: The Abstraction and Reasoning Corpus (ARC) is viewed as a critical avenue to Artificial General Intelligence (AGI), as it enables models to learn abstract transformation rules from few-shot examples and then generalize to new tasks. However, prevalent ARC methodology is either pure language or vision-only (i.e., VARC). The former depends heavily on LLMs, consuming billions of parameters. The latter often struggles to capture high-level semantics, leading to overfitting on pixel-level patterns. To bridge this gap, we propose L-VARC, a novel framework that enhances visual reasoning via a language-guided Learning Using Privileged Information (LUPI) branch. Specifically, we design a Semantic Compression Module by feeding a unified, task-agnostic prompt into DeepSeek-V3. In this way, the raw LARC (a crowd-sourced language description dataset) can be substantially refined and structured, fitting with the context length constraint of standard text encoders (e.g., CLIP). Moreover, we design a Cross-Attention Projector to align visual features with semantic embeddings, aiming to guide the training of the ARC model. Notably, the LUPI branch is taken in the training process and will be discarded during inference, thereby yielding a lightweight model with a mere 18 million parameters. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our L-VARC effectively leverages linguistic priors to boost visual reasoning and outperforms state-of-the-art. Ablation studies further confirm the contribution of the two new designs towards the L-VARC framework. The code is available at https://github.com/GZHU-DVL/L-VARC.

</details>


### [15] [Learning Task-Aware Sampling with Shared Saliency through Density-Equalizing Mappings](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12869)
*Tsz Lok Ip,Han Zhang,Lok Ming Lui*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DECNN introduces density-equalizing convolution, using a learned density function to warp the input domain so that informative regions receive denser sampling, enabling adaptive feature extraction with fewer parameters and interpretable saliency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Informative structures are often localized rather than uniformly distributed; uniform convolution wastes computation on uninformative regions, especially in medical imaging where pathologies are spatially confined.

Method: A density-equalizing mapping guides convolution through a learned density function, enlarging informative areas and compressing less relevant ones, resulting in non-uniform receptive field distribution. This density-guided warping is coupled with standard convolution to enable task-adaptive feature extraction and produces an interpretable saliency map.

Result: Empirical validation on image classification and craniofacial surface analysis shows competitive or superior performance with fewer parameters, robust under geometric variation, and accurate identification of task-relevant regions via saliency maps.

Conclusion: Density-equalizing convolution enables lightweight yet expressive architectures with adaptive focus on informative regions, improving efficiency and interpretability without sacrificing performance.

Abstract: In image and surface-based learning tasks, convolutional features are typically extracted using receptive fields that are sampled uniformly across the entire domain. However, informative structures are rarely distributed uniformly in practice and are often concentrated in localized regions. Such phenomena are particularly common in medical imaging, where pathological changes are spatially confined. Consequently, uniform convolution allocates equal computational effort to both informative and uninformative regions, resulting in inefficient feature extraction and suboptimal utilization of model capacity. To address this issue, we propose a framework for task-adaptive sampling that dynamically redistributes computational attention according to the spatial importance of the data. Specifically, we introduce the Density-Equalizing Convolutional Neural Network (DECNN), which employs density-equalizing mappings to guide convolution through a learned density function. The density function encodes the relative importance of different regions and induces a transformation that enlarges informative areas while compressing less relevant ones. As a result, convolutional receptive fields are redistributed non-uniformly over the domain, enabling denser sampling in task-relevant regions. By coupling this importance-driven transformation with convolution, DECNN performs adaptive feature extraction that focuses computational resources on informative structures. This leads to more efficient use of model capacity, yielding a lightweight yet expressive architecture while simultaneously producing an interpretable saliency map. Experiments on image classification and craniofacial surface analysis demonstrate that DECNN achieves competitive or superior performance with fewer parameters, accurately identifies task-relevant regions, and remains robust under complex geometric variations.

</details>


### [16] [Bridging Modal Isolation in Interleaved Thinking: Supervising Modality Transitions via Stepwise Reinforcement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12886)
*Tingyu Li,Le Zhou,Siyuan Li,Yujun Wu,Xinglong Xu,Jingxuan Wei,Conghui He,Cheng Tan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A two-stage training framework (MoTiF) addresses Modal Isolation in interleaved multimodal reasoning by optimizing modality-transition fidelity at boundaries, using Reflective SFT and Flow-GRPO, yielding improved cross-modal coherence and task accuracy on four visual-puzzle benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Interleaved textual reasoning and visual generation can diverge when chained reasoning is long, due to information loss at modality boundaries, causing cross-modal hallucinations and under-utilization of visual information. Boundary-level supervision is needed beyond end-task metrics.

Method: Two-stage training: (1) Reflective SFT to detect and recover from erroneous visual outputs, and (2) Flow-GRPO to enhance image generation fidelity via reinforcement learning. All training signals are transition-level fidelity metrics (cross-modal hallucination and visual-utilization deficit) at each boundary rather than end-task accuracy.

Result: On four visual puzzle benchmarks, transition-level supervision substantially improves cross-modal coherence and final task accuracy, demonstrating that boundary-aware supervision enhances interleaved reasoning.

Conclusion: Effective interleaved multimodal reasoning requires explicit, boundary-level structural supervision of modality transitions, not merely scaling or optimizing end-task performance.

Abstract: Interleaved thinking, where a unified multimodal model alternates between textual reasoning and visual generation, has shown promise on spatial and physical tasks. However, in complex long-chain scenarios, we identify a fundamental failure mode: generated images diverge from the textual context while subsequent text ignores the visual evidence, causing the two modalities to alternate without genuinely informing each other. We term this Modal Isolation and attribute it to compounding information loss at modality boundaries. We decompose each reasoning cycle into atomic operations and define modality transition loss, quantifying cross-modal hallucination (text-to-image) and visual utilization deficit (image-to-text) at each boundary. We propose MoTiF (Modality Tiransition Fidelity), a two-stage training framework that directly optimizes these transitions: Reflective SFT trains the model to detect and recover from erroneous visual outputs; Flow-GRPO improves image generation fidelity via reinforcement learning. All training signals in MoTiF derive from transition-level fidelity rather than end-task accuracy. Across four visual puzzle benchmarks, this transition-level supervision substantially improves both cross-modal coherence and final task accuracy. The results demonstrate that effective interleaved reasoning requires explicit structural supervision at modality boundaries, not merely scaling or end-task optimization.

</details>


### [17] [Magnifying What Matters: Attention-Guided Adaptive Rendering for Visual Text Comprehension](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12898)
*Shenglai Zeng,Qirui Wang,Kai Guo,Xinnan Dai,Xianxuan Long,Hui Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: AGAR: a training-free, model-agnostic Attention-Guided Adaptive Rendering method for Vision Text Comprehension (VTC) QA. It uses a VLM's middle-to-late layer attention to identify important visual patches, enlarges their word spans in rendering, and re-infers. It consistently improves VLMs across nine benchmarks and four backbones, is plug-and-play, composes with post-training, and is robust to input degradations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VTC pipelines treat rendering/layout as fixed, content-agnostic preprocessing and lack insight into how VLMs process visualized text. An empirical finding shows a localization-without-utilization regime where attention/localization emerges in mid-to-late layers but is not tied to correctness, suggesting a way to adapt rendering to emphasize relevant text.

Method: Compute VLM middle-to-late layer attention to identify top-K visual patches; map these patches to word spans; render the page with enlarged spans corresponding to those words; re-infer the answer without training (training-free) and in a model-agnostic fashion. This acts as a plug-and-play enhancement and can be combined with further post-training.

Result: AGAR consistently improves off-the-shelf VLMs across nine VTC benchmarks (short-form, long-context, multi-page memory QA) and four backbones; it yields additional gains when combined with VLM post-training; it remains robust under visual and textual input degradation.

Conclusion: Attention-guided adaptive rendering is an effective, training-free strategy to mitigate localization-related limitations in VTC, offering a practical, robust enhancement that leverages the VLM’s own attention to identify and amplify salient text regions.

Abstract: Visual Text Comprehension (VTC) renders text into images for a vision-language model (VLM) to read, sidestepping LLM context-window limits and powering applications from long-page OCR to multi-page memory QA. Yet existing VTC pipelines treat rendering and layout as a fixed, content-agnostic preprocessing step and offer little mechanistic understanding of how VLMs internally process visualized text. Through a focused empirical study on VTC QA tasks, we reveal that VLMs exhibit a localization-without-utilization regime: evidence-localizing attention emerges sharply in the middle-to-late layers and is largely decoupled from answer correctness, yet simply enlarging the localized spans on the rendered page recovers a large fraction of the failures. Building on these observations, we propose AGAR (Attention-Guided Adaptive Rendering), a training-free, model-agnostic method that leverages a VLM's own middle-to-late layer attention to identify the top-K important visual patches, maps them back to word spans, and re-renders the page with those spans enlarged before re-inferring the answer. Extensive experiments across nine VTC benchmarks (short-form, long-context, and multi-page memory QA) and four VLM backbones show that AGAR (i)consistently improves off-the-shelf VLMs as a plug-and-play enhancement, (ii)composes with VLM post-training to yield further gains, and (iii)remains robust under both visual- and text-side input degradation.

</details>


### [18] [Multi-Label Test-Time Adaptation with Bayesian Conditional Priors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12925)
*Qiru Li,Ao Zhou,Zhiwei Jiang,Zifeng Cheng,Cong Wang,Yafeng Yin,Qing Gu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces Bayesian Conditional Priors (BCP) Estimation for multi-label recognition with frozen Vision-Language Models, enabling gradient-free test-time adaptation that reweights label co-occurrences; uses anchor-conditioned Bayesian refinement with online priors from unlabeled test streams; yields PMI-like interpretation and improves mAP across backbones without retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Zero-shot VLMs treat labels independently and fail under distribution shift, producing incoherent label sets. There is a need to exploit label dependencies and perform adaptation without labeled target data or backbone fine-tuning.

Method: For each test image, select a high-confidence anchor label and compute an anchor-conditioned Bayesian refinement that updates logits in closed form in logit space; interpret as PMI; estimate anchor-conditioned priors online using second-order co-occurrence statistics from unlabeled test data; gradient-free, adds minimal overhead beyond a forward pass.

Result: On standard multi-label benchmarks with multiple CLIP backbones, BCP outperforms strong test-time augmentation baselines, e.g., RN50 mAP 57.31→69.22; ViT-B/16 mAP 62.61→71.79.

Conclusion: BCP provides coherent, label-consistent predictions under distribution shift, achieving strong performance without target annotations and with lightweight online prior estimation.

Abstract: Multi-label recognition with frozen Vision-Language Models (VLMs) is brittle under distribution shift: standard zero-shot inference scores labels independently, ignoring co-occurrence structure and producing incoherent label sets where dominant concepts suppress weaker but compatible labels. We introduce Bayesian Conditional Priors (BCP) Estimation, a gradient-free test-time adaptation method that injects label dependency without tuning the backbone. BCP views zero-shot logits as a proxy for marginal posteriors under a fixed image-text likelihood and attributes shift-induced errors mainly to a mismatched label prior. For each test image, it selects a high-confidence anchor label and applies an anchor-conditioned Bayesian refinement. This update is closed-form in logit space and admits a pointwise mutual information (PMI) interpretation, explicitly promoting compatible labels and suppressing incompatible ones. BCP operates without target annotations by estimating anchor-conditioned priors online from the unlabeled test stream via lightweight second-order co-occurrence statistics, adding negligible overhead beyond a single forward pass. Across standard multi-label benchmarks and multiple CLIP backbones, BCP consistently outperforms strong TTA baselines, e.g., improving RN50 average mAP from 57.31 to 69.22 and ViT-B/16 from 62.61 to 71.79.

</details>


### [19] [MAMVI: 3D Test-Time Adaptation via Masked Multi-View Point Clouds](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12939)
*Inseok Kong,Geunyoung Jung,Jiyoung Jung*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MAMVI introduces a unified single-step multi-view test-time adaptation for 3D point clouds, using a hybrid masking strategy and multi-view consensus to outperform existing TTA methods with 4.9–8.9× faster inference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: 3D point cloud models degrade under distribution shifts caused by sensor noise, occlusions, and environmental changes. Test-time adaptation with multi-view augmentation helps mitigate this, but existing methods rely on sequential optimization per view, causing high latency incompatible with real-time use.

Method: Replace sequential optimization with a single backward pass for adaptation. Use a hybrid masking strategy that combines fixed ratios for stability with Beta-distributed sampling for diversity. Aggregate losses across multiple views and perform adaptation through a single backward pass based on multi-view consensus. Employ a confidence-based adaptive learning rate to adjust adaptation intensity per sample. Code available at the provided repository.

Result: MAMVI achieves state-of-the-art accuracy on ShapeNet-C and ScanObjectNN-C and remains competitive on ModelNet-40C, while delivering 4.9–8.9× faster inference, making it highly suitable for real-time applications.

Conclusion: MAMVI enables real-time, accurate test-time adaptation for 3D point clouds by replacing per-view sequential optimization with a unified single-step adaptation that leverages multi-view consensus and adaptive learning rates. This yields superior performance with substantially reduced latency; code is publicly available.

Abstract: 3D point cloud models suffer significant performance degradation under distribution shifts caused by sensor noise, occlusions, and environmental changes. Test-time adaptation (TTA) has emerged as a practical paradigm for mitigating this issue during inference. Recently, leveraging multi-view augmentation has shown promise in improving 3D TTA performance. However, existing multi-view approaches are often constrained by sequential optimization that treats each view independently. This sequential optimization leads to substantial inference latency due to repetitive optimization steps, making real-time adaptation impractical. To address this, we propose Masked Multi-View Test-Time Adaptation (MAMVI), which replaces sequential optimization with a unified single-step adaptation. Specifically, MAMVI utilizes a hybrid masking strategy that combines fixed ratios for stability with Beta-distributed sampling for diversity. By aggregating losses across multiple views, MAMVI performs adaptation through a single backward pass based on multi-view consensus. Additionally, a confidence-based adaptive learning rate is used to dynamically adjust the adaptation intensity for each sample. Extensive experiments on ModelNet-40C, ShapeNet-C, and ScanObjectNN-C demonstrate that MAMVI achieves state-of-the-art accuracy on ShapeNet-C and ScanObjectNN-C. Moreover, it remains competitive on ModelNet-40C while delivering 4.9-8.9 times faster inference, making it highly suitable for real-time applications. Our code is available at https://github.com/Inseok-kong/MAMVI

</details>


### [20] [YOLO-AMC: An Improved YOLO Architecture with Attention Mechanisms for Building Crack Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12958)
*Ching-Yu Tsai,Chia-Min Lin,Chih-Hsiang Yang,Yung-Che Wang,Jen-Shiun Chiang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: An improved YOLOv11-based detector for crack detection (YOLO-AMC) integrates multiple attention mechanisms (GAM, Res-CBAM, SA) in the Neck, removing C2PSA, to enhance cross-scale feature fusion and accuracy, achieving state-of-the-art mAP with acceptable compute and deployment performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cracks are thin, low-contrast structures highly affected by background noise, making detection challenging for standard detectors. There is a need for robust cross-scale feature fusion and attention-enabled models to improve crack detection while considering deployment efficiency.

Method: Modify YOLOv11 by removing the C2PSA module and inserting multiple attention mechanisms (GAM, Res-CBAM, SA) into the neck's multi-scale feature fusion. Evaluate on a crack-detection dataset against YOLOv11n and YOLOv8n, report mAP@0.5 and mAP@0.5:0.95, and measure GFLOPs and FPS on RTX 4090 and Raspberry Pi 5. Compare attention modules to identify best performer.

Result: YOLO-AMC outperforms baselines across metrics. GAM achieves best results with mAP@0.5 = 0.9917 and mAP@0.5:0.95 = 0.9506 on the test set, surpassing YOLOv11 (0.9833 / 0.9112) and YOLOv8 (0.9707 / 0.8921). The model maintains 7.6 GFLOPs, runs at 110.95 FPS on RTX 4090 and ~5 FPS on Raspberry Pi 5. Also, the paper provides a GitHub link for implementation.

Conclusion: Attention-enhanced cross-scale feature fusion (especially GAM) significantly improves crack detection performance while maintaining a practical inference speed, offering a favorable accuracy-efficiency trade-off and reproducibility through provided code.

Abstract: Crack detection plays an important role in infrastructure inspection and Structural Health Monitoring (SHM). However, cracks typically appear as thin, low-contrast structures and are easily affected by background noise, posing challenges for existing object detection models. This study proposes an improved YOLO-based architecture with integrated attention mechanisms, termed YOLO-AMC (YOLO with Attention Mechanisms for Crack Detection), to enhance automated crack detection performance. Based on YOLOv11, the original C2PSA module is removed, and multiple attention mechanisms, including Global Attention Mechanism (GAM), Residual Convolutional Block Attention Module (Res-CBAM), and Shuffle Attention (SA), are introduced into the multi-scale feature fusion layers of the Neck to strengthen cross-scale feature integration. Experimental results demonstrate that YOLO-AMC consistently outperforms baseline models YOLOv11n and YOLOv8n across multiple evaluation metrics. Among the evaluated attention modules, GAM achieves the best detection performance, obtaining mAP@0.5 = 0.9917 and mAP@0.5:0.95 = 0.9506 on the test dataset, which are higher than those of YOLOv11 (0.9833 / 0.9112) and YOLOv8 (0.9707 / 0.8921). Furthermore, while maintaining a computational complexity of 7.6 GFLOPs, the proposed model achieves 110.95 FPS on an NVIDIA RTX 4090 platform and approximately 5 FPS on a Raspberry Pi 5 edge device, demonstrating a favorable trade-off between accuracy and deployment efficiency. The implementation code for this study is available on GitHub at https://github.com/CY-Tsai24/YOLO-AMC.

</details>


### [21] [Efficient, Robust, and Anti-Collusion Fingerprinting of Image Diffusion Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12977)
*Jianwei Fei,Yunshu Dai,Zhihua Xia,Xiaochun Cao,Jiantao Zhou,Alessandro Piva,Benedetta Tondi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes a robust anti-collusion fingerprinting framework for text-to-image (T2I) models by embedding fingerprints into a personalized normalization module (PNM) and applying lossless function-invariant parameter transformations to deter collusion; supports reparameterization to create multiple fingerprinted copies without retraining; demonstrates high fingerprint recovery (>99.5%) and improved robustness (high FID in colluded models).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing generative model fingerprinting lacks robustness against collusion attacks where multiple attackers combine models to remove or obscure fingerprints, risking IP protection of T2I models.

Method: Embed bitstrings into PNM coefficients; apply anti-collusion via lossless function-invariant parameter transformations; enable creation of multiple fingerprinted copies through PNM reparameterization without retraining; incorporate worst-case optimization to withstand model-level attacks.

Result: Fingerprint extraction accuracy >99.5% across multiple T2I generation/editing tasks; demonstrated proactive robustness to collusion by increasing the FID of colluded models; efficient production of multiple copies without retraining.

Conclusion: First step toward robust fingerprinting with anti-collusion for T2I models. The approach provides reliable fingerprint recovery and proactive disruption of colluded models, enabling scalable IP protection through PNM reparameterization and worst-case optimization.

Abstract: Model fingerprinting, embedding user-specific identifiers (fingerprints) into generated outputs, has recently emerged as a popular solution to protect the intellectual property rights (IPR) of generative text-to-image (T2I) models and prevent unauthorized redistribution. In this work, we reveal a previously unexplored systematic vulnerability in existing generative model fingerprinting methods: they lack robustness against collusion attacks, where multiple attackers combine their models to remove or obscure the fingerprints. To address this issue, we take the first step towards a robust fingerprinting method for T2I models with anti-collusion capabilities. The proposed method encodes strings of bits, namely fingerprints, into the coefficients of a personalized normalization module (PNM) incorporated into T2I models, so that fingerprints can be reliably recovered from any generated image. To defend against collusion attacks and prevent unauthorized model redistribution, we introduce an anti-collusion mechanism based on lossless function-invariant parameter transformations. This mechanism significantly degrades the image generation quality of colluded models, making them effectively unusable. Moreover, our method allows developers to efficiently create multiple copies of fingerprinted T2I models by reparameterizing the PNM without the need for retraining. We also introduce a worst-case optimization strategy to improve robustness against model-level attacks. Our experiments demonstrate that the proposed method achieves high fidelity and robustness across multiple T2I image generation and editing tasks, with fingerprint extraction accuracy exceeding 99.5%. Compared with existing methods, our method demonstrates, for the first time, a notable proactive robustness to collusion attacks by significantly increasing the FID of colluded models.

</details>


### [22] [Camera and LiDAR BEV Fusion for Cooperative 3D Object Detection on TUMTraf V2X](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12981)
*Muhammad Shahbaz,Shaurya Agarwal*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Camera-LiDAR fusion detector for V2X DriveX 2026; fuses three roadside cameras with a fused infrastructure-plus-vehicle point cloud in BEV; CenterPoint-style head with IoU-based losses; 3D mAP 0.85 on Codabench public split; data-leakage studies raise to 0.89 (oversampling) and 0.99 (ground-truth replacement); three configurations reported.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evaluate a multimodal detector for cooperative 3D object detection and quantify the effect of train/test overlap leakage on reported performance.

Method: BEV fusion of three roadside cameras and fused infrastructure-plus-vehicle point cloud; CenterPoint-like detection head with generalized IoU regression loss and an IoU quality re-ranking head; trained on provided train/val splits; additional studies include oversampling the 44 overlapping frames (finetuning) and replacing predictions with released ground truth for those frames (post-processing).

Result: 3D mAP = 0.85 on the public Codabench test split; oversampling yields mAP = 0.89; ground-truth replacement yields mAP = 0.99 (not published on leaderboard). Per-class results reported for all configurations.

Conclusion: The study reveals that data leakage from overlapping train/test frames can substantially inflate reported performance; while the fusion detector achieves competitive results, the post-hoc GT replacement demonstrates an upper bound and underscores the need for leakage-free evaluation for fair comparison.

Abstract: We describe a Camera and LiDAR fusion detector developed for the TUMTraf V2X cooperative 3D object detection track of the DriveX 2026 challenge. The detector fuses three roadside cameras with a fused infrastructure-plus-vehicle point cloud in a shared bird's-eye-view space and predicts boxes through a CenterPoint-style head with a generalized IoU regression loss and an IoU quality re-ranking head. Trained on the provided train and validation splits, the model reaches a 3D mAP of 0.85 on the public Codabench test split. While iterating on the system, we observed that 44 of the 50 test frames are also present in the released train (40) and validation (4) splits with their labels. We therefore conducted two additional studies to quantify how this overlap affects the final score: (1) a finetuning run that oversamples the 44 overlapping frames, reaching 0.89 mAP, and (2) a post-processing run that replaces predictions on those frames with the released ground truth, reaching 0.99 mAP (uploaded to our Codabench account for testing but not published on the leaderboard). All three configurations and their per-class results are reported.

</details>


### [23] [Objects Before Words: Object-First Inductive Biases for Grounding Language in Child-View Video](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12985)
*Sathira Silva,Abrham Kahsay Gebreselasie,Muhammad Umer Sheikh,Kartik Kuckreja,Daniel Harari,Muhammad Haris Khan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: BabyMind introduces an object-first bias for child-view contrastive learning under sparse supervision, improving grounded word meaning learning in SAYCam-like data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Resolve when and where named referents appear in cluttered egocentric video with sparse, weakly synchronized caregiver speech; single-frame contrastive pairing yields noisy positives where the intended object may be absent or entangled with distractors.

Method: Offline mask-based region interface to extract candidate object embeddings; cross-frame linking into lightweight object files via tracking within a short utterance-centered window; align utterances to bags of object files with a prototype-space multiple-instance contrastive objective; track-coherence and global-object agreement regularizers stabilize learning and transfer object-file structure into the global frame embedding used at evaluation.

Result: On SAYCam-S, BabyMind improves Labeled-S 15 forced-choice accuracy by +2.6 points over CVCL; yields consistent gains on in-vocabulary out-of-distribution benchmarks.

Conclusion: Demonstrates the viability of an object-first bias for grounded word learning under sparse supervision, stabilizing object-file representations and improving transfer to global frame embeddings; code available.

Abstract: Learning grounded word meaning from natural experience requires resolving two ambiguities in infant-view recordings: when the named referent appears and where it is in a cluttered frame. In SAYCam-style data, caregiver speech is sparse and weakly synchronized with egocentric video, so single-frame contrastive pairing yields noisy positives in which the intended object is absent or entangled with distractors. We propose BabyMind, an object-first bias for child-view contrastive learning under sparse, noisy supervision. BabyMind extracts candidate object embeddings using an offline mask-based region interface, links candidates across a short utterance-centered window into lightweight object files via tracking, and aligns utterances to bags of object files with a prototype-space multiple-instance contrastive objective. Track-coherence and global-object agreement regularizers stabilize learning and transfer object-file structure into the global frame embedding used at evaluation. On SAYCam-S, BabyMind improves Labeled-S 15 forced-choice accuracy by +2.6 points over CVCL and yields consistent gains on in-vocabulary out-of-distribution benchmarks. Code is available at https://github.com/sathiiii/BabyMind.

</details>


### [24] [Diffusion Transformer World-Action Model for AV Scene Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12987)
*Ruslan Sharifullin,Benjamin Jiang,Kai Xi Chew*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A diffusion-based latent world model for action-conditioned video prediction outperforms regression baselines on autonomous driving scenes, addressing distortion metrics bias and demonstrating demonstrable action controllability and efficient motion recovery with a compact latent predictor.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable planning and simulation for autonomous agents without real-world rollouts, while avoiding the blurring and misalignment introduced by standard pixel-space regression and metrics; to evaluate where in the data/prediction stack best to predict and to establish perceptual metrics that reflect true distributional alignment.

Method: 1) Build a compact latent world model: predict future scene latents from present front-camera latent and ego-actions; decode to 256×256 frames for up to 8s on 150 held-out nuScenes scenes. 2) Benchmark encoders: compare six frozen encoders across four representation families, finding V-JEPA2 with temporal context best reduces steering RMSE. 3) Develop a latent Diffusion Transformer (DiT) and diagnose four ingredients: spatial tokens, x0 objective, residual anchoring, and sampling matched to uncertainty. 4) Use a Stable-Diffusion-VAE encode-predict-decode pipeline to reveal distortion- perceptual trade-offs; evaluate with FID/KID. 5) Analyze action controllability (steering vs displacement) and motion magnitude with a compact 1.7M-parameter “jump” model for full-motion recovery.

Result: - V-JEPA2 with temporal context reduces steering RMSE by ~40% over best single-frame encoder.  - Latent DiT with the four ingredients achieves significantly better perceptual metrics: KID 0.078 (diffusion) vs 0.375 (regression, 4.8× better).  - Distortion metrics like cosine similarity and SSIM favor blurry regression, while FID/KID reveal diffusion aligns more closely with the real frame distribution.  - Perceptual metrics reflect real distribution fit; action controllability is strong (Spearman ρ = 0.81 for steering-induced displacement vs -0.18 for regression).  - A 1.7M-parameter jump model captures full ground-truth motion magnitude (1.02× GT), outperforming single-pass models which capture less than half.

Conclusion: Diffusion-based latent world modeling better reconciles realism with predictive usefulness for action-conditioned forecasts, mitigating the distortions from standard metrics and enabling effective control and compact inference. The identified ingredients provide a practical blueprint for building scalable, controllable predictive models in driving contexts; a compact jump module facilitates accurate motion magnitude recovery with minimal parameters.

Abstract: Action-conditioned world models let an autonomous vehicle predict future camera scenes from its own planned controls, enabling planning and simulation without real-world rollouts, but at compact, trainable scale the futures are ambiguous and the field's standard distortion metrics actively mislead: they reward a blurry regression mean over a realistic prediction. We confront this with a compact latent world model that, given the present front-camera latent and a sequence of ego-actions, predicts future scene latents a frozen decoder renders to $256 \times 256$ frames up to 8 seconds ahead, evaluated on 150 held-out nuScenes scenes. We first benchmark where to predict: across six frozen encoders spanning four representation families, V-JEPA2 with temporal context reduces steering RMSE by 40% over the best single-frame encoder. We then train a latent Diffusion Transformer (DiT) and, through a controlled diagnosis, identify the four ingredients it needs: spatial tokens, the $x_0$ objective, residual anchoring, and sampling matched to target uncertainty. In a Stable-Diffusion-VAE encode-predict-decode pipeline we expose the central tension: distortion metrics (cosine similarity, SSIM) favor the blurry mean, masking that the diffusion model is far closer to the real frame distribution. Inception-based FID and KID reveal a clean perception-distortion frontier: diffusion attains KID 0.078 versus 0.375 for regression ($4.8\times$ better), and a deployable train-derived calibration makes this practical without test-time ground truth. The model is genuinely action-controllable (steering drives scene displacement, Spearman $ρ= 0.81$, vs $-0.18$ for regression). We trace limited single-pass motion to a shared-present anchor and engineer a compact 1.7M-parameter "jump" model that recovers full ground-truth motion magnitude ($1.02\times$ GT), where single-pass models capture less than half.

</details>


### [25] [A Machine Learning Framework for Real-Time Personalized Ergonomic Pose Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12988)
*Manex Atxa,Bruno Simoes,Julen Balzategui*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A real-time 3D volumetric-video method for ergonomic pose prediction, using 3D point clouds from multiple angles with auto-inference and user-labeled training for personalization; demonstrated via RGB-D load-lifting case study; scalable real-time ergonomic evaluation leveraging 3D data and 2D pose methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable accurate, robust, real-time ergonomic assessment despite occlusions and fixed camera viewpoints, addressing workplace safety monitoring needs.
