Method: Real-time pose prediction from volumetric (3D) video data; analysis of 3D point clouds from multiple viewpoints; continuous inference on streaming data; personalized DL classifier trained only on poses labeled by the user; case study with RGB-D cameras for skeletal labeling; combines 3D data technologies with traditional 2D pose estimation.

Result: Enables real-time skeletal labeling and inference on new streaming data after training; demonstrates a scalable, pragmatic approach to real-time ergonomic evaluation; leverages both state-of-the-art 3D data techniques and 2D pose estimation.

Conclusion: Provides a adaptable framework for real-time posture analysis in ergonomics; could be extended to other applications requiring real-time posture analysis; contributes to safety and health monitoring in workplaces by reducing occlusion issues and enabling multi-view data fusion.

Abstract: This paper introduces a new methodology for real-time prediction of ergonomic and non-ergonomic human poses using volumetric video data in three dimensions. Although the methodology was designed for ergonomic assessments, it can be adapted to other applications requiring real-time analysis of human posture. One aspect that makes this system stand out is its ability to analyze 3D point clouds during the assessment, enabling computation from multiple angles. This overcomes a critical limitation of cameras which provide often a fixed viewpoint, thereby restricting the data available for a thorough postural evaluation, especially when occlusions occur. The system continuously and automatically performs pose inference using the chosen perspective on the real-time streaming data; however, only the poses manually selected and labeled by the user are used to train the personalized deep learning classifier. The methodology has been refined through a case study in which RGB-D cameras captured subjects performing load-lifting tasks, enabling real-time skeletal labeling. The model was trained on this data and, following the training phase, performs inference on new streaming data in real time. This research offers a scalable and pragmatic approach for real-time ergonomic evaluation by combining state-of-the-art 3D data technologies and traditional 2D pose estimation algorithms. It addresses the increasing need for safety and health monitoring in workplace environments, marking a notable contribution to the domain.

</details>


### [26] [Quality-Preserving Imperceptible Adversarial Attack on Skeleton-based Human Action Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13022)
*Ziyi Chang,Kanglei Zhou,Xiaohui Liang,Hubert P. H. Shum*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces a distribution-based, quality-preserving adversarial attack for skeletal action recognition that avoids noise-like perturbations, addressing the risk-gap issue; demonstrates improved attack success and preserved motion naturalness using a perceptual metric across two datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing attacks degrade motion quality due to perturbations; the discrepancy between empirical and true risks during optimization causes this degradation; need attacks that preserve motion quality and evaluation metrics aligned with human perception.

Method: Develops a distribution-based attack that does not inject noise-like perturbations; seeks to minimize the risk gap and preserve motion quality; introduces a perceptual naturalness metric for evaluating motion quality.

Result: Empirical results on state-of-the-art S-HAR methods and two datasets show higher attack success rates and better motion quality after attack, validated by qualitative and quantitative analyses.

Conclusion: Quality-preserving, distribution-based adversarial attacks reveal robustness weaknesses in S-HAR and motivate defenses and improvements in future work.

Abstract: Adversarial attacks on skeletal human action recognition have received significant attention. However, existing methods typically introduce noise-like perturbations that degrade motion quality post-attack, and thereby are inherently perceptible with recent advancements in S-HAR systems. We discover that this degradation stems from the gap between empirical and true risks during the optimization process of previous adversarial attacks. To address this issue, we propose an attack where adversarial motions are obtained without compromising their motion quality. To minimize the risk gap and preserve motion quality, we propose a distribution-based adversarial attack method without introducing noise-like perturbations. To faithfully evaluate the motion quality, we propose a new metric that aligns with human perception on real-world naturalness. Experiments have been conducted on the state-of-the-art S-HAR methods across two datasets, demonstrating the superiority of our method in both the attack success rate and the post-attack motion quality through qualitative and quantitative analyses. The success of our quality-preserving attack application and distribution-based method raises serious concerns about the robustness of action recognizers, highlighting the need for further enhancements in this domain.

</details>


### [27] [A Multi-Modal Framework with Cross-Subject Pseudo-Labeling and Semantic Alignment for Micro-Gesture Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13030)
*Haoran Zhang,Haokun Zhang,Pengyu Liu,Yujia Zhang,Weibao Xue,Yanbin Hao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Multi-modal micro-gesture recognition in untrimmed videos using saliency-guided extraction, tail-aware losses, and cross-modal pseudo-labeling for unsupervised domain adaptation; achieves 68.13% F1 and 4th place.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MGs are tiny, noisy signals with long-tailed class distribution and cross-subject domain shift, requiring robust, multi-modal representations and domain adaptation.

Method: Saliency-guided pipeline combining 68-keypoint skeletons, 3D heatmaps, and high-res RGB features; gentle square-root weight for tail classes; Orthogonal Semantic Embedding Loss; Cross-Modal Pseudo-Labeling (CMPL) for unsupervised domain adaptation; temperature-scaled soft-voting for late fusion.

Result: Competitive F1-score of 68.13% with 4th place in MiGA-IJCAI Challenge Track 1, demonstrating effectiveness of the integrated framework.

Conclusion: Integrating multi-modal cues with tail-aware learning and cross-subject pseudo-labeling mitigates long-tail and domain-shift issues, improving robustness of MG recognition in untrimmed videos.

Abstract: Micro-gestures (MGs) are spontaneous and subtle body movements that frequently convey hidden human emotions. Recognizing MGs in untrimmed videos remains highly challenging due to their extremely low signal-to-noise ratio, severe long-tailed class distribution, and the inherent domain shift encountered in cross-subject evaluation scenarios. In this paper, we propose a comprehensive multi-modal framework for Track 1 of the 4th MiGA-IJCAI Challenge. To capture fine-grained representations, we design a saliency-guided multi-modal extraction pipeline integrating 68-keypoint skeleton joint coordinates, 3D heatmap volumes, and high-resolution RGB visual features. We introduce a gentle square-root smoothed weighting mechanism paired with an Orthogonal Semantic Embedding Loss to protect tail classes without compromising overall recognition capabilities. More importantly, to bridge the cross-subject generalization gap, we propose a Cross-Modal Pseudo-Labeling (CMPL) strategy for unsupervised domain adaptation, which significantly boosts single-modal robustness. A temperature-scaled soft-voting mechanism is finally utilized to alleviate overconfidence during late fusion. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our framework achieves a competitive F1-score of 68.13\%, securing the 4th place.

</details>


### [28] [GeoCFNet: Geometry-Aware Confidence Field Network for Robot-Assisted Endoscopic Submucosal Dissection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13032)
*Rui Tang,Guankun Wang,Long Bai,Haochen Yin,Huxin Gao,Jiewen Lai,Jiazheng Wang,Hongliang Ren*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GeoCFNet introduces geometry-aware confidence field estimation for dissection guidance in robot-assisted ESD, leveraging a DINOv3 backbone with Token-Differentiated Fusion, SegFormer decoding, and Geometry-Aware Spatial Regularization to produce geometrically coherent confidence maps in challenging endoscopic scenes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-stakes, dynamic endoscopic environments (smoke, highlights, deformation) demand stable and precise visual guidance for en-bloc ESD; conventional confidence fields struggle under perturbations, risking tissue margins.

Method: GeoCFNet uses a pretrained DINOv3 backbone. It employs a Token-Differentiated Fusion module to merge class-token context with dense patch features, a SegFormer-based decoder to regress confidence fields, and Geometry-Aware Spatial Regularization (GASR) to enforce spatial coherence and respect local geometry during inference.

Result: Quantitative evaluation reports RMSE 0.0480, PSNR 27.1995, SSIM 0.3397, and CC 0.2466, indicating accurate and geometrically stable confidence field estimation for robot-assisted ESD guidance.

Conclusion: The work demonstrates that incorporating geometric understanding into confidence-field estimation yields more reliable dissection guidance under challenging endoscopic conditions and could improve safety and efficacy of robot-assisted ESD.

Abstract: Advanced surgical robotics has made robot-assisted endoscopic submucosal dissection (ESD) a promising approach for the en-bloc resection of large lesions, with the potential to reduce recurrence and improve long-term outcomes. However, the technical complexity and risk of complications in ESD demand stable and precise visual guidance to maintain an accurate dissection corridor and a safe tissue margin. Dense confidence fields provide an effective representation for this purpose by describing both the preferred dissection region and its spatial transition to surrounding tissue. However, reliable confidence field estimation remains challenging in dynamic endoscopic scenes due to smoke, specular highlights, tissue deformation, weak texture, and the thin geometric structure of the target region. To address these challenges, we formulate dissection guidance as a geometry-aware confidence field estimation problem and propose GeoCFNet, a geometry-aware confidence field network built on a pretrained DINOv3 backbone. GeoCFNet integrates a Token-Differentiated Fusion module to aggregate class-token context with dense patch representations, a SegFormer decoder for confidence regression, and Geometry-Aware Spatial Regularization (GASR) to preserve spatial coherence and local geometric transitions. Experimental results show that GeoCFNet achieves RMSE 0.0480, PSNR 27.1995, SSIM 0.3397, and CC 0.2466, indicating accurate and geometrically stable confidence field estimation for robot-assisted ESD guidance.

</details>


### [29] [SAM-Deep-EIoU: Selective Mask Propagation for Multi-Object Tracking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13033)
*Alexander Holmberg*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Selective mask propagation dispatches from a base MOT tracker to a VOS model only on windows flagged by an assignment-uncertainty signal, reducing costly VOS use while preserving identities on hard frames. It is training-free and black-box-friendly, and improves base trackers on DanceTrack; achieves state-of-the-art HOTA (86.8) on SportsMOT with SAM3-Deep-EIoU.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multi-object tracking exhibits a heavy-tailed difficulty distribution: most frames are easy for lightweight trackers, while a minority are intrinsically hard. VOS models help preserve identity on hard frames but are computationally expensive. A selective scheme aims to exploit the strengths of VOS only when needed, maintaining efficiency.

Method: Maintain a base tracker; compute an assignment-uncertainty signal per frame. When uncertainty plus potential contradiction with the base identity arises, invoke a VOS model to propagate masks; use VOS output to override base identities only if confident and contradicting; otherwise keep the base output. The approach is training-free, treats both components as black boxes, and can swap in a more capable VOS model. Evaluated on DanceTrack (improves three base trackers) and SportsMOT (SOTA with SAM3-Deep-EIoU and global association).

Result: Demonstrates improved identity preservation on DanceTrack across multiple base trackers; achieves state-of-the-art HOTA score on SportsMOT (86.8) with a strong VOS integration, using a globally associated tracker.

Conclusion: Selective, uncertainty-driven propagation of VOS in MOT yields robustness gains with lower compute by avoiding unnecessary VOS runs, is model-agnostic (black-box), and can be extended by upgrading either the base tracker or the VOS component.

Abstract: Multi-object tracking has a heavy-tailed difficulty distribution: most frames are easy for a lightweight base tracker, while a small fraction are intrinsically hard. Video object segmentation (VOS) models can often preserve identity through the hard frames where the base tracker fails, but they are much more expensive in compute and memory. We propose selective mask propagation, a tracking algorithm that dispatches from a base tracker to a VOS model only on windows where an assignment-uncertainty signal fires. The base tracker's output is modified only when the VOS model makes a confident prediction that contradicts the base tracker's identity assignment; weak or inconclusive predictions preserve the base output. The method is training-free, treats both the base tracker and the VOS model as black boxes, and can benefit from replacing the VOS component with a more capable model. On DanceTrack, selective mask propagation improves three different base trackers. On SportsMOT, where identity preservation is central to sports analytics, SAM3-Deep-EIoU with global track association achieves state-of-the-art performance on the benchmark with 86.8 HOTA.

</details>


### [30] [TetherCache: Stabilizing Autoregressive Long-Form Video Generation with Gated Recall and Trusted Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13035)
*Yu Meng,Xiangyang Luo,Letian Li,Wenyuan Jiang,Chen Gao,Xinlei Chen,Yong Li,Xiao-Ping Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introducing TetherCache, a training-free cache management approach for drift-resistant long-horizon autoregressive video diffusion. It partitions the cache into sink, memory, and recent regions and uses GRAB for diverse long-range recall and TAME for memory editing, enabling stable, high-quality long videos.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Autoregressive video diffusion models face drift and quality degradation for long sequences because of limited KV-cache and accumulating drift from self-generated frames, leading to artifacts and temporal inconsistency.

Method: Cache is organized into sink, memory, and recent regions. GRAB (Gated Recall with Attention-Diversity Balancing) selects long-range memory with a gated score combining attention relevance and temporal diversity. TAME (Trusted Alignment via Memory Editing) lightly edits recalled memory tokens by aligning their statistics to a trusted context distribution. Built on Self-Forcing and training-free; plug-and-play.

Result: On VBench-Long across 30s, 60s, and 240s, TetherCache improves long-video generation quality; at 240s, overall and semantic scores improve and quality drift decreases from 7.84 to 1.33, demonstrating drift-resistant long-horizon generation.

Conclusion: TetherCache provides effective, training-free stabilization for long-horizon autoregressive video diffusion through memory-aware cache management and memory editing, enabling drift-resistant long videos.

Abstract: Autoregressive video diffusion models provide a natural formulation for streaming and variable-length video generation by conditioning newly generated frames on previously generated content. However, extending these models to minute-level generation remains challenging: the limited KV-cache budget prevents the model from retaining the full history, while repeatedly conditioning on self-generated frames induces a context distribution shift that accumulates over time, leading to visual artifacts, quality degradation, and temporal drift. In this paper, we propose TetherCache, a training-free and plug-and-play cache management strategy for drift-resistant long video generation. TetherCache organizes the cache into sink, memory, and recent regions, and introduces two complementary mechanisms. First, GRAB (Gated Recall with Attention-Diversity Balancing) selects long-range memory frames using a gated score that combines attention-based relevance with temporal diversity, preserving informative yet diverse historical context under a fixed cache budget. Second, TAME (Trusted Alignment via Memory Editing) lightly edits newly recalled memory tokens by aligning their statistics to a trusted context distribution, reducing the pollution caused by drifted historical features. Built on Self-Forcing, TetherCache consistently improves long-video generation quality on VBench-Long across 30s, 60s, and 240s settings. In particular, for 240s generation, it substantially improves overall and semantic scores while reducing quality drift from 7.84 to 1.33, demonstrating its effectiveness for stable long-horizon autoregressive video diffusion.

</details>


### [31] [SeamEdit: A Black-Box VLM-Agnostic Pipeline for Large-Image Semantic Editing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13041)
*Xiangyu Lyu,Dan Lei*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A training-free, model-agnostic pipeline (SeamEdit) enables high-quality, seam-free semantic edits on large images by treating any inpainting-capable VLM as a black-box and applying a five-stage post-hoc process.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable high-quality large-image semantic editing while leveraging closed-source VLMs without suffering from seam artifacts, misalignment, or semantic deformation inherent in naive tiling approaches.

Method: Five-stage post-hoc pipeline: (1) overlay-based tile decomposition, (2) black-box VLM inpainting, (3) geometric/color-consistency corrections, (4) seam-risk-based multi-candidate ranking, (5) dynamic-programming curved seam fusion; treats VLM as a black-box oracle.

Result: The pipeline reduces seam visibility and enables semantic modification of arbitrary tile regions in large images, while remaining training-free and model-agnostic.

Conclusion: SeamEdit offers a practical, training-free solution for seamless semantic region editing on large images by leveraging closed-source VLMs with a robust post-hoc fusion workflow.

Abstract: Semantic region editing for large images must satisfy two requirements at the same time: high generative quality and natural integration with surrounding content. Some related methods rely on white-box models and leave the strong generation capability of closed-source models underexplored. Directly applying closed-source models to tiled editing, however, introduces several failure modes: semantic deformation, canvas-level alignment drift, and visible seam artifacts. This paper presents SeamEdit, a training-free and model-agnostic pipeline that treats any VLM with inpainting capability as a black-box oracle. SeamEdit mitigates these issues through a five-stage post-hoc pipeline: overlay-based tile decomposition, black-box VLM inpainting, geometric and color-consistency correction, seam-risk-based multi-candidate ranking, and dynamic-programming curved seam fusion. The pipeline reduces seam visibility and supports semantic modification of arbitrary tile regions.

</details>


### [32] [LaME: Learning to Think in Latent Space for Multimodal Embedding via Information Bottleneck](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13061)
*Peixi Wu,Biao Yang,Feipeng Ma,Bosong Chai,Bo Lin,Wei Yuan,Fan Yang,Tingting Gao,Hebei Li,Xiaoyan Sun*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: LaME introduces latent reasoning tokens as a fixed-capacity bottleneck to perform multimodal embedding reasoning in a single forward pass, avoiding autoregressive CoT costs and CoT-annotation dependence, achieving fast throughput with competitive accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tackle high computational cost and annotation dependency of autoregressive chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning in reasoning-driven multimodal embeddings, and question whether textual CoT is optimal or latent-space reasoning suffices.

Method: Use K learnable reason tokens as a fixed-capacity bottleneck to complete all reasoning in one pass; employ two weak supervision signals to decouple contrastive and autoregressive objectives; implement a two-stage training pipeline for stable convergence.

Result: LaME achieves competitive performance on MMEB-v2 and MRMR, surpasses some explicit CoT-based models, with 60x faster inference than explicit CoT methods and 2x faster than latent baselines, with throughput comparable to discriminative embedding models.

Conclusion: Latent reasoning embedding via a fixed-capacity bottleneck with weak supervision is an effective alternative to textual CoT for multimodal embeddings, enabling faster inference without CoT annotations and stable training.

Abstract: Reasoning-driven universal multimodal embedding has advanced rapidly by introducing Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning into the embedding pipeline. Despite the strong performance across both general and complex tasks, this paradigm suffers from two core limitations: (i) autoregressive CoT reasoning incurs high computational cost, making it impractical for low-latency retrieval; and (ii) embedding performance is heavily coupled with CoT annotation quality, making large-scale training unreliable. These raise fundamental questions: Is textual CoT the optimal form of reasoning for embedding, and can effective embedding reasoning be accomplished in latent space? To this end, we propose LaME (Latent Reasoning Multimodal Embedding), which formulates embedding-oriented latent reasoning as a weakly supervised information bottleneck. LaME employs K learnable reason tokens as a fixed-capacity bottleneck, completing all reasoning within a single forward pass. The two weak supervision signals structurally decouple contrastive from autoregressive objectives and eliminate dependence on CoT annotations, while a two-stage training pipeline ensures stable convergence. Experiments on MMEB-v2 and MRMR show that LaME achieves competitive performance, surpassing some explicit CoT-based models, while delivering 60x faster inference than explicit CoT methods and 2x faster than latent baselines with throughput comparable to discriminative embedding models. Code will be released.

</details>


### [33] [Unified MRI Brain Image Translation via Hierarchical Tumor Structure Comparison](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13096)
*Yupeng Cai,Jia Wei,Jianlong Zhou*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: HTSCGAN is a unified multi-modal brain MRI translation GAN that preserves tumor-region structure with a Patch Contrast Module, Patch Classifier, and Structure-Aware Encoder, yielding improved translation fidelity and downstream segmentation on BraTS2020/2021.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing brain image translation methods neglect region-specific structural information of tumors, which is critical for clinical usefulness; incorporating structural cues can improve translation fidelity and downstream tasks.

Method: Generator uses three Patch Contrast Modules (PCM) with different patch sizes to capture hierarchical tumor structure. A pretrained Patch Classifier (PC) and a pretrained Structure-Aware Encoder (SAE) provide patch-level and perceptual guidance via patch classification loss and tumor perceptual loss, respectively. Training on BraTS2020/2021; evaluation on translation tasks and downstream segmentation; code released.

Result: Demonstrates strong performance in both translation tasks and downstream segmentation; improved quality and clinical relevance of translated images.

Conclusion: HTSCGAN effectively integrates tumor-region structure into multi-modal brain image translation, enhancing fidelity and clinical applicability; potential for improved diagnosis and treatment planning; public code.

Abstract: Multi-modal MRI brain image translation via available modalities holds significant practical importance in modern medicine, providing robust support for early diagnosis, treatment planning, and outcome assessment of diseases. For this purpose, it is important to ensure the fidelity of the tumor regions after translation. However, existing brain image translation methods ignore the structure information of different tumor regions, which could assist translation models in enhancing the quality and clinical applicability of the translated images.
  In this work, we propose a novel translation model called HTSCGAN, which is a unified multi-modal brain image translation generative adversarial model integrating the structural information within tumor regions with the aim of improving the quality of brain image translation. Specifically, the generator employs three Patch Contrast Module (PCM) with different patch sizes to capture the hierarchical structural information of the tumor regions. In addition, a pretrained Patch Classifier (PC) and a pretrained Structure-Aware Encoder (SAE) are employed to derive the generated image containing the same tumor region structure as the ground truth image via patch classification loss and tumor perceptual loss, respectively. The experiments on BraTS2020 and BraTS2021 demonstrate strong performance of our model in both translation tasks and down stream segmentation tasks, highlighting its effectiveness in enhancing the quality and clinical relevance of the translated brain images. Our code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/HTSCGAN.

</details>


### [34] [PP-OCRv6: From 1.5M to 34.5M Parameters, Surpassing Billion-Scale VLMs on OCR Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13108)
*Yubo Zhang,Xueqing Wang,Manhui Lin,Yue Zhang,Penglongyi Deng,Ting Sun,Tingquan Gao,Zelun Zhang,Jiaxuan Liu,Changda Zhou,Hongen Liu,Suyin Liang,Cheng Cui,Yi Liu,Dianhai Yu,Yanjun Ma*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PP-OCRv6 is a lightweight OCR system using a MetaFormer-style backbone with structural reparameterization, unifying detection and recognition through a shared block and task-specific strides; it achieves high accuracy with far fewer parameters and faster inference across server to edge.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: OCR requires precise localization, reduced hallucinations, and low latency. VLM-based OCR can be computationally heavy; PP-OCRv6 targets data-centric optimization and architectural simplifications to improve efficiency and accuracy for dedicated OCR tasks.

Method: Redesign of the backbone, detection neck, and recognition neck around a unified MetaFormer-style block with structural reparameterization that decouples spatial token mixing from channel mixing. Supports task-specific stride configurations and shares the same primitive blocks across three model tiers (medium, small, tiny).

Result: On in-house benchmarks, PP-OCRv6_medium achieves 83.2% recognition accuracy and 86.2% detection Hmean, outperforming PP-OCRv5_server by +5.1% and +4.6% respectively, and surpassing Qwen3-VL-235B, GPT-5.5, and Gemini-3.1-Pro with orders of magnitude fewer parameters. The tiny tier attains 3.9× faster inference than PP-OCRv5_mobile on Intel Xeon CPUs while maintaining comparable accuracy.

Conclusion: The architecture demonstrates that a unified, reparameterizable MetaFormer-based OCR design can deliver high accuracy, robust localization, and edge-friendly latency, enabling server-to-edge deployment with a significantly reduced parameter count.

Abstract: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have achieved impressive results on general vision-language tasks, yet they suffer from hallucination, imprecise localization, and prohibitive computational cost when applied to dedicated OCR scenarios. This paper presents PP-OCRv6, a lightweight OCR system that combines architectural innovation with data-centric optimization. PP-OCRv6 redesigns the backbone, detection neck, and recognition neck around a unified MetaFormer-style building block with structural reparameterization, decoupling spatial token mixing from channel mixing and supporting both tasks through task-specific stride configurations. Three model tiers (medium, small, tiny) share the same block primitives, covering deployment scenarios from server to edge. On our in-house benchmarks, PP-OCRv6_medium achieves 83.2% recognition accuracy and 86.2% detection Hmean, outperforming PP-OCRv5_server by +5.1% and +4.6% respectively while surpassing Qwen3-VL-235B, GPT-5.5, and Gemini-3.1-Pro with orders of magnitude fewer parameters. The tiny tier achieves 3.9$\times$ faster inference than PP-OCRv5_mobile on Intel Xeon CPU while maintaining comparable accuracy.

</details>


### [35] [Fully Distributed Multi-View 3D Tracking in Real-Time](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13127)
*Byron Hernandez,Fangyu Li,Aotian Wu,Paul J. Shin,Kaustubh Purandare,Henry Medeiros*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MV3DT is a distributed, peer-to-peer framework for real-time multi-view 3D tracking that eliminates central fusion while delivering competitive accuracy and high scalability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Centralized fusion in multi-camera tracking causes computational bottlenecks and hampers deployment at scale; there is a need for scalable, real-time, zero-shot capability.

Method: Each camera runs monocular 3D perception, distributed multi-view association, and collaborative fusion via lightweight messaging; peer-to-peer coordination, no central aggregator.

Result: On WILDTRACK: 94.3% IDF1 and 93.3% MOTA; scales to 100 cameras at 30 FPS; inter-camera latency <10 ms; communication overhead ~2.2%; zero-shot with camera calibrations.

Conclusion: MV3DT demonstrates practical, scalable, real-time multi-view tracking for large networks with competitive accuracy and zero-shot deployability.

Abstract: Multi-camera tracking with overlapping fields of view typically relies on centralized fusion, which creates computational bottlenecks that prevent deployment at scale. We present MV3DT, a fully distributed framework for real-time multi-view 3D tracking that achieves accurate identity propagation and occlusion recovery through peer-to-peer coordination, eliminating the need for central aggregation. Each camera node executes a lightweight modular pipeline comprising monocular 3D perception, distributed multi-view association, and collaborative fusion via lightweight messaging. MV3DT achieves 94.3% IDF1 and 93.3% MOTA on WILDTRACK, competitive with state-of-the-art centralized methods, while demonstrating superior scalability by sustaining 30 FPS on 100 cameras with less than 10 ms inter-camera latency and only 2.2% communication overhead. MV3DT operates in a zero-shot regime given camera calibrations, requiring no scene-specific learning and making it directly deployable in new environments. These results establish MV3DT as a practical solution for real-time multi-view tracking in large-scale overlapping camera networks.

</details>


### [36] [Cascade Classification of Dermoscopic Images of Skin Neoplasms with Controllable Sensitivity and External Clinical Validation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13135)
*Elena S. Kozachok,Sergey S. Seregin,Aleksandr V. Kozachok,Ilya P. Latyshev,Oleg I. Samovarov*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DL architectures and classification schemes for dermoscopic skin neoplasms were compared; a two-stage cascade improves macro F1 and allows tunable sensitivity, but a persistent generalization gap requires external validation and recalibration before deployment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evaluate and compare modern DL architectures and classification schemes for dermoscopic lesion triage and differentiation, and assess how well models trained on open datasets generalize to independent, clinical datasets representative of Russian practice.

Method: Four architectures (ViT-B/16, Swin-S, ConvNeXt-S, EfficientNetV2-S) were evaluated under three schemes: binary malignant/benign; single-stage four-class (benign, MEL, SCC, BCC); and a two-stage cascade (binary triage followed by three-class differentiation MEL/SCC/BCC). All models used ImageNet-pretrained weights with a single augmentation protocol trained on aggregated ISIC Archive data, and were tested on an internal held-out sample and two clinical datasets (Melanoscope AI mobile system; Sechenov University). Performance metrics included ROC-AUC, sensitivity, calibration error (ECE), and macro F1; paired tests assessed architecture-specific differences.

Result: Internally, binary classification achieved ROC-AUC 0.952-0.966. On the Sechenov dataset, performance dropped to ROC-AUC 0.797-0.893 with sensitivity 0.53-0.67 and ECE rising from 0.02 to 0.27-0.39, indicating a generalization gap and underconfident malignancy predictions. A single inter-architecture difference showed ViT-B/16 underperforming at the binary stage (p<0.05); at differentiation stage no architecture had clear advantage. The two-stage cascade increased macro F1 for most architectures, significantly so only for ViT-B/16, by reclaiming malignant lesions misclassified as benign. On ISIC MILK10k, an 11-class direct classification yielded mean-class sensitivity 0.525.

Conclusion: A tunable triage threshold offers sensitivity control not possible with standard argmax single-stage classification and better reflects clinical differential-diagnosis logic. However, the persistent generalization gap across external clinical datasets necessitates external validation and recalibration prior to deployment.

Abstract: Purpose. To compare deep learning architectures and classification schemes for dermoscopic images of skin neoplasms and assess their generalization on transfer from open international datasets to independent clinical datasets of Russian practice.
  Methods. Four architectures (ViT-B/16, Swin-S, ConvNeXt-S, EfficientNetV2-S) were compared in three schemes: binary (malignant/benign), single-stage four-class (benign, MEL, SCC, BCC), and a two-stage cascade (binary triage, then three-class differentiation MEL/SCC/BCC). All models used ImageNet-pretrained weights and a single augmentation protocol on aggregated open ISIC Archive data, and were evaluated on an internal held-out sample and two clinical datasets (Melanoscope AI mobile system; Sechenov University).
  Results. Internally the binary stage attains ROC-AUC 0.952-0.966; on Sechenov University it drops to 0.797-0.893, sensitivity to 0.53-0.67, and ECE rises from 0.02 to 0.27-0.39 with underestimation of malignancy, quantifying a generalization gap in ranking and calibration. Paired tests confirm one inter-architecture result on clinical data: the deficit of ViT-B/16 at the binary stage (p<0.05); at the differentiation stage no architecture has a proven advantage. The cascade raises macro F1 over single-stage four-class classification for most architectures, but significantly only for ViT-B/16, by recovering malignant lesions assigned to the dominant benign class. On ISIC MILK10k, direct 11-class classification yields mean-class sensitivity 0.525.
  Conclusion. A tunable triage threshold gives sensitivity control not attainable in standard single-stage (argmax) classification and better reproduces clinical differential-diagnosis logic. The persistent generalization gap mandates external clinical validation and recalibration before deployment.

</details>


### [37] [An Extensible and Lightweight Unified Architecture for Demosaicing Pixel-bin Image Sensors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13136)
*Saurabh Kumar,Nutan Sairam Yenneti*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A modular unified demosaicing framework for pixel-bin sensors with a learning-free CFA identification module to enable plug-and-play across CFAs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Pixel-bin sensors offer improved resolution and light gathering but have larger inter-color separation, making demosaicing harder. CNN-based demosaicing methods are typically CFA-specific, requiring multiple resource-intensive models and high maintenance. A lightweight, extensible solution and automatic CFA detection are needed.

Method: Propose a modular unified architecture that can demosaic various pixel-bin sensors. Introduce a learning-free CFA-identification module to accurately detect the CFA type of raw data, enabling plug-and-play operation without retraining.

Result: Claimed enhancement in image quality with a framework that is extensible and lightweight. CFA-identification module enables automatic CFA detection without learning, supporting seamless deployment across CFAs.

Conclusion: The work provides a scalable, maintenance-friendly demosaicing approach for pixel-bin sensors, reducing model proliferation and enabling plug-and-play deployment in smartphone pipelines.

Abstract: Pixel-bin image sensors are becoming the default choice for smartphone cameras due to their resolution vs light-gathering trade-off. However, their larger inter-color separation compared to the Bayer color filter array (CFA) makes them challenging to demosaic. Furthermore, existing deep learning-based demosaicing methods are CFA-specific, requiring multiple individual models that take up precious onboard resources and demand larger development and maintenance efforts. In this work, we propose a modular unified architecture for demosaicing various pixel-bin sensors that provides higher image quality while being extensible and lightweight. Additionally, to enable plug-and-play operation, we introduce a learning-free CFA-identification module to detect the CFA type of raw data accurately.

</details>


### [38] [Iterative Visual Thinking: Teaching Vision-Language Models Spatial Self-Correction through Visual Feedback](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13156)
*Animesh Tripathy,Aswanth Krishnan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: IVT enables vision-language models to self-correct spatial grounding through an iterative visual-feedback loop, augmented by a two-phase training regimen (distilled corrective traces and GRPO optimization) that stabilizes multi-step refinement with modest data and single-GPU training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge the gap between a VLM's raw grounding ability and its capacity to observe and correct its own predictions by leveraging visual feedback and learned corrective reasoning.

Method: Iterative Visual Thinking (IVT) forms a closed loop: (1) predict a bounding box, (2) render and observe the prediction, (3) refine iteratively. Training uses (a) supervised corrections distilled from a teacher VLM issuing corrective reasoning traces based on the base model's errors, and (b) Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) with a simple IoU reward to stabilize multi-step refinements. Evaluation combines SFT warm-up with IVT on 505 test samples from RefCOCOg, Ref-Adv, and Ref-L4 across Acc@0.5, Acc@0.7, and Acc@0.9.

Result: Naive prompting collapses Acc@0.5 from 79.6% to 48.7%. With IVT and SFT warm-up, Acc@0.5 rises to 82.0% (+2.4pp), Acc@0.7 to 74.1% (+3.2pp), and Acc@0.9 to 48.3% (+2.8pp). GRPO further reduces per-step IoU degradation by ~5x, stabilizing refinements. Training uses only 2,400 samples on a single GPU.

Conclusion: Spatial self-correction is learnable at modest scale; IVT closes the self-correction gap and yields more robust, stable multi-step grounding refinements.

Abstract: Vision-language models (VLMs) achieve strong singleshot spatial grounding, yet lack any mechanism to observe and correct their own predictions. We find that naively prompting a VLM to iterate over rendered visualizations of its predictions causes catastrophic failure: Acc@0.5 on referring expression comprehension collapses from 79.6% to 48.7% (a 31 percentage point drop), revealing a fundamental gap between grounding capability and self-correction ability. We propose Iterative Visual Thinking (IVT), a closed-loop framework in which the model predicts a bounding box, observes the prediction rendered on the image, and iteratively refines through visual feedback. A two-phase training recipe closes the self-correction gap: first, we exploit the base model's own predictions as realistic errors and prompt a teacher VLM to generate corrective reasoning traces, yielding supervised data without human annotation; second, we apply Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) with a simple IoU reward to stabilize multi-step refinement. On a mixed benchmark spanning RefCOCOg, Ref-Adv, and Ref-L4 (505 test samples), SFT warm-up with IVT surpasses the single-shot base model on every metric: Acc@0.5 rises to 82.0% (+2.4pp), Acc@0.7 to 74.1% (+3.2pp), and Acc@0.9 to 48.3% (+2.8pp). GRPO further reduces per-step IoU degradation by 5x, stabilizing the refinement trajectory. All training uses only 2,400 samples on a single GPU, demonstrating that spatial self-correction is a learnable capability that can be instilled at modest scale.

</details>


### [39] [Transformer-Guided Graph Attention for Direct Cardiac Mesh Reconstruction: A Structural Digital Twin Framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13188)
*Abhishek H S,Akash Ganamukhi,Abhimanyu Suresh,Aditya G Hiremath,Prasad B Honnavalli,Adithya Balasubramanyam*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: An end-to-end 3D Swin Transformer encoder–decoder plus a Graph Attention Network head deforms a template cardiac mesh directly from CT/MRI to a simulation-ready surface, avoiding traditional segmentation–meshing steps. It yields competitive Dice scores and superior mesh quality (Chamfer ~1.8 mm; 95th percentile <5 mm) with a single forward pass, aiming to accelerate clinical adoption of patient-specific cardiac models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current workflows for patient-specific cardiac modeling rely on sequential segmentation, meshing (Marching Cubes), and manual cleanup. This is slow, inconsistent, and requires specialist expertise. An end-to-end approach could drastically reduce time, remove bottlenecks, and improve consistency for clinical use.

Method: An end-to-end network: a 3D Swin Transformer encoder–decoder extracts volumetric features from CT/MRI, paired with a Graph Attention Network (GAT) head that iteratively deforms a template mesh to fit the patient boundary. Trained and evaluated on MM-WHS 2017 (CT and MRI). Outputs a smooth, simulation-ready mesh in a single forward pass without marching cubes or post-processing.

Result: Dice scores: ~0.84 on CT, ~0.83 on MRI. Mesh quality metrics: mean Chamfer distance 1.8 mm; 95th-percentile surface distance < 5 mm. All meshes produced in a single forward pass, with no post-processing.

Conclusion: End-to-end mesh generation can prioritize geometric fidelity and topological correctness over pixel-wise segmentation accuracy, potentially accelerating clinical deployment of patient-specific cardiac simulations by removing post-processing bottlenecks.

Abstract: Building patient-specific cardiac models sits at the heart of precision cardiology, yet getting those models into clinical use keeps running into the same wall: mesh generation is slow, messy, and frustrating. The standard workflow -- segmenting the image, running Marching Cubes, and then manually cleaning up the result -- is time-consuming, inconsistent across operators, and demands specialist knowledge most clinical teams do not have. We take a fundamentally different approach. Instead of treating segmentation and mesh generation as two separate problems, we train a single end-to-end network that goes directly from a raw 3D medical image to a smooth, simulation-ready cardiac surface mesh. The core is a 3D Swin Transformer encoder-decoder that extracts volumetric features from CT or MRI volumes, paired with a Graph Attention Network (GAT) head that iteratively deforms a template mesh to fit the patient's cardiac boundary. We tested on the MM-WHS 2017 benchmark using both CT and MRI. Segmentation scores were competitive (Dice of 0.84 on CT, 0.83 on MRI), but the primary focus is mesh quality: mean Chamfer distance of 1.8 mm, with 95th-percentile surface distance below 5 mm. Every mesh is produced in a single forward pass -- no Marching Cubes, no smoothing filters, no manual cleanup. We argue that for cardiac digital twin pipelines, geometric fidelity and topological correctness matter more than pixel-level Dice scores. By removing the post-processing bottleneck, this approach makes patient-specific cardiac simulation substantially more accessible for clinical use.

</details>


### [40] [Visual Place Recognition in Forests with Depth-Aware Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13206)
*Walter Nedov,Saimunur Rahman,Kavindie Katuwandeniya,David Hall,Kaushik Roy,Peyman Moghadam*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A lightweight depth-aware distillation framework augments a DINOv2-based place recognizer with depth cues, preserving the pre-trained descriptor space, and achieves improved robustness on WildCross.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Visual place recognition in natural forests is hindered by repetitive vegetation, weak structural cues, and significant appearance variation; there is a need to leverage geometric depth to improve robustness.

Method: A depth-aware distillation framework injects geometric cues into a DINOv2-based place recognition model while preserving the existing descriptor space; depth information is incorporated through a distillation mechanism without large re-training of the descriptor subspace.

Result: Empirical gains over an appearance-only baseline on the WildCross benchmark, demonstrating robustness to appearance variations in forest environments.

Conclusion: Depth is a strong complementary modality for forest place recognition, and depth-aware distillation is a promising direction for more robust forest perception.

Abstract: Visual place recognition in natural forest environments remains challenging due to repetitive vegetation, weak structural cues, and significant appearance variation across traversals. To address this limitation, this paper proposes a lightweight depth-aware distillation framework that injects geometric cues into a DINOv2-based place recognition model, while maintaining its pre-trained descriptor space. Evaluated on the recent WildCross benchmark, the proposed approach yields gains over an appearance-only counterpart, providing robustness to appearance variations. These results demonstrate the importance of depth as a strong complementary modality for place recognition in natural environments and identify depth-aware distillation as a promising direction for more robust forest perception.

</details>


### [41] [TimeLens: On-Device Artifact Recognition with Retrieval-Augmented Question Answering for the Grand Egyptian Museum](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13267)
*Rawan Hesham,Ali Ashraf,Amr Ahmed,Malak Alaa,Omar Ahmed,Omar Wagih*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: On-device fine-grained artifact detector plus bilingual RAG guide; production-ready TimeLens app with ~10 s latency; data-quality-driven annotation; 108-record knowledge base; LM selection (Gemma 4 E2B) with 10 optimizations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address in-gallery deployment constraints: distinguish between near-identical artifacts, adapt curated data to handheld camera conditions, and prevent unsupported facts; deliver fast, reliable bilingual guidance.

Method: Data-quality-driven detector development: auto-annotation with YOLO-World, spatial label-cleaning rules, hand-annotation; train YOLOv8n; quantize to 5.97 MB TensorFlow Lite; mAP@0.5=0.995, mAP@0.5:0.95=0.924. RAG guide: 108-record knowledge base in ChromaDB; evaluate seven LMs; select Gemma 4 E2B; apply ~10 latency-reduction optimizations; integrate in Flutter app with bilingual UI and TTS.

Result: Detector: mAP@0.5=0.995; mAP@0.5:0.95=0.924; 5.97 MB TFLite; end-to-end latency ~10 s (from >30 s). System is production-ready in a bilingual Flutter app with location gating and TTS; RAG with bilingual support.

Conclusion: Shows practical viability of a data-quality–driven, on-device detector combined with a retrieval-augmented, latency-optimized bilingual guide for in-gallery AI assistants; emphasizes the importance of data curation and LM selection for real-time museum applications.

Abstract: TimeLens is an AI-powered bilingual mobile guide for the Grand Egyptian Museum (GEM). Pointing a phone at an exhibit, a visitor sees the artifact recognized in real time and can ask follow-up questions answered in English or Arabic. The work addresses three problems specific to in-gallery deployment: fine-grained visual similarity among 51 catalogued artifacts (many near-identical Ramesside statues), the gap between curated training data and handheld camera conditions, and the risk of an AI guide stating unsupported historical facts. Two engineering contributions are reported. First, an on-device artifact detector was developed through a data-quality-driven iteration study -- from foundation-model auto-annotation (YOLO-World), through spatial label-cleaning rules, to a fully hand-annotated dataset -- isolating label quality as the decisive factor: the final YOLOv8n model resolves every previously failing class while remaining a 5.97 MB TensorFlow Lite asset that runs in real time on a mid-range phone (mAP@0.5 = 0.995, mAP@0.5:0.95 = 0.924). Second, a bilingual Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) guide, grounded in a 108-record ChromaDB knowledge base, was benchmarked across seven candidate language models, with Gemma 4 E2B (Q4 K M) selected; ten targeted optimizations reduce end-to-end latency from over 30 s to approximately 10 s. Both subsystems are integrated in a production Flutter application with bilingual interface, museum location gating, and text-to-speech support.

</details>


### [42] [Zero-Shot Captioning for Cultural Heritage: Automated Image Analysis of Traditional Indonesian Clothing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13275)
*Anugrah Aidin Yotolembah,Novanto Yudistira,Gembong Edhi Setyawan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Custom ZeroCLIP proposes a retrieval-augmented zero-shot captioning system for Indonesian heritage garments, achieving strong multimodal metrics on unseen provinces using a training-caption retrieval bank; validated on a 3.8k-image, 38-province dataset with 19.3% METEOR gain from retrieval.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable culturally grounded image captioning in low-resource heritage contexts and extend zero-shot generalization to unseen regions through retrieval-augmented domain adaptation.

Method: A province-level inductive zero-shot protocol trained on 24 seen provinces, validated on 6 seen provinces, and tested on 8 unseen provinces. The framework uses a frozen CLIP ViT-B/32 image encoder, a CLIP text encoder, a BERT text encoder, and an LSTM caption decoder. During inference, unseen-province labels/captions are unavailable; retrieval uses only training-province captions. No unseen-province images, labels, or captions are used in training/validation/retrieval-bank construction.

Result: Quantitative: CLIPScore 0.8536, BLEU-4 0.3342, METEOR 0.4859. Ablation shows retrieval yields a 19.3% METEOR gain in cultural vocabulary recovery. Human evaluation indicates stronger cultural accuracy and fluency. The dataset is publicly available at the provided GitHub URL.

Conclusion: Retrieval-augmented domain adaptation is effective for culturally grounded caption generation in low-resource heritage settings, enabling zero-shot generalization to unseen provinces through leveraging training-caption retrieval.

Abstract: This paper presents Custom ZeroCLIP, a retrieval-augmented vision-language framework for zero-shot captioning of Indonesian traditional garments. The dataset contains 3,800 expert-annotated images from all 38 Indonesian provinces. Using a province-level inductive zero-shot protocol, the model is trained on 24 seen provinces, validated on 6 seen provinces, and evaluated on 8 unseen provinces. The framework combines a frozen CLIP ViT-B/32 image encoder, a CLIP text encoder, a BERT text encoder, and an LSTM caption decoder. During inference, unseen-province labels and captions are unavailable, and retrieval uses only captions from training provinces. No unseen-province image, label, or caption is used during training, validation, or retrieval-bank construction. Custom ZeroCLIP achieves a CLIPScore of 0.8536, BLEU-4 of 0.3342, and METEOR of 0.4859, outperforming existing baselines. Ablation results show that retrieval improves cultural vocabulary recovery with a 19.3\% METEOR gain, while human evaluation confirms stronger cultural accuracy and fluency. The results demonstrate the effectiveness of retrieval-augmented domain adaptation for culturally grounded caption generation in low-resource heritage settings. The dataset is publicly available at https://github.com/AnugrahAidinYotolembah/Traditional-Indonesian-Clothing-Captioning-Dataset.

</details>


### [43] [Cross-Modal Masked Compositional Concept Modeling for Enhancing Visio-Linguistic Compositionality](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13288)
*Wei Li,Zhen Huang,Xinmei Tian*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MACCO introduces masked compositional concept modeling to improve cross-modal compositionality in vision-language models via masking and reconstructing concepts across modalities, aided by two auxiliary objectives that align and regularize features intra- and inter-modally, yielding gains in compositionality, syntax understanding, and downstream multimodal tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current contrastively trained VLMs (e.g., CLIP) struggle with compositional understanding, showing bag-of-words behavior and weak object-relation/attribute-object binding and word-order sensitivity due to global single-vector optimization and underutilization of the rich compositional information in image-text pairs.

Method: MACCO masks compositional concepts in one modality and reconstructs them using full context from the other modality. It introduces two auxiliary objectives to jointly align and regularize masked features across modalities (inter-modally and intra-modally).

Result: Extensive experiments on five compositional benchmarks show significant improvements in compositionality and better capture of syntactic structure and linguistic information. The approach also benefits text-to-image generation and multimodal large language models. Code is released at the provided GitHub repository.

Conclusion: Masking-based cross-modal reconstruction with dual alignment/regularization objectives enables VLMs to better capture cross-modal compositional structures, enhancing both representational compositionality and related downstream tasks.

Abstract: Contrastively trained vision-language models like CLIP, have made remarkable progress in learning joint image-text representations, but still face challenges in compositional understanding. They often exhibit a "bag-of-words" behavior--struggling to capture the object relations, attribute-object bindings, and word order dependencies. This limitation arises not only from the reliance on global, single-vector representations for optimization, but also from the insufficient exploitation and modeling of the rich compositional information inherently present in paired image text data. In this work, we propose MACCO (MAsked Compositional Concept MOdeling), a framework that masks compositional concepts in one modality and reconstructs them conditioned on the full contextual information from the other, enabling the model to capture and align cross-modal compositional structures more effectively. To facilitate this process, we introduce two auxiliary objectives that jointly align and regularize masked features both inter-modally and intra-modally. Extensive experiments on five compositional benchmarks, along with in-depth analyses, demonstrate that our approach not only significantly enhances compositionality in VLMs but also improves their ability to capture syntactic structure and linguistic information. Additionally, the improved compositionality also benefits text-to-image generation and multimodal large language model. Code is available at https://github.com/hiker-lw/MACCO.

</details>


### [44] [HYDRA-X: Native Unified Multimodal Models with Holistic Visual Tokenizers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13289)
*Guozhen Zhang,Xuerui Qiu,Yutao Cui,Tianhui Song,Changlin Li,Junzhe Li,Tao Huang,Xiao Zhang,Yang Li,Jianbing Wu,Miles Yang,Zhao Zhong,Liefeng Bo,Limin Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: HYDRA-X unifies image and video tokenization within a single ViT, achieving strong cross-modal performance by using frame-level causal temporal attention, hierarchical temporal compression, and a lightweight temporally upsampling decompressor with joint video–image supervision, plus latent-space editing at the tokenizer level.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the lack of a single-tokenizer, unified multimodal model by integrating image and video tokenization inside a ViT to enable spatiotemporal reconstruction and latent-space semantic alignment.

Method: Key design choices: (1) frame-level causal temporal attention suffices for visual reconstruction, while full spatiotemporal attention degrades it; (2) hierarchical temporal compression beats single-step approaches; (3) a lightweight decompressor upscales temporally compressed features under joint image–video teacher supervision to enforce complementary semantic structures in the latent space; (4) latent-level source–target editing inside the tokenizer improves consistency and convergence; (5) evaluation instantiated on a 7B dense model across image and video understanding/generation tasks.

Result: HYDRA-X reports strong performance across image and video tasks and demonstrates improved editing consistency and faster convergence, supporting the viability of unified-tokenizer unified multimodal models.

Conclusion: HYDRA-X provides a blueprint for unified-tokenizer UMMs, showing that a ViT can jointly tokenize and reconstruct images/videos in a shared latent space and that latent-space editing at the tokenizer level yields benefits for consistency and efficiency.

Abstract: Holistic visual tokenizers are fundamental to unified multimodal models (UMMs) as they map diverse visual inputs into a unified representation space. In this paper, we present HYDRA-X, the first UMM that unifies image and video tokenization within a single Vision Transformer (ViT). Our design is driven by two core challenges: efficiently injecting spatiotemporal reconstruction capability into a native ViT, and embedding image- and video-level semantic awareness into the latent space. To address the first, comprehensive ablations reveal two key findings: (1) frame-level causal temporal attention suffices for visual reconstruction, whereas full spatiotemporal attention degrades it; and (2) hierarchical temporal compression substantially outperforms single-step alternatives. To tackle the second, we propose a lightweight decompressor that upsamples temporally compressed features under joint image-video teacher supervision, thereby enforcing complementary semantic structures within the compact latent space. Building on this holistic tokenizer, we further propose a principled improvement of the editing pipeline: source-target interaction should occur at the latent level inside the tokenizer rather than at the semantic level inside the LLM, substantially improving editing consistency and accelerating convergence. Instantiated at the 7B dense model, HYDRA-X achieves strong performance across image and video understanding and generation tasks, paving the way for future unified-tokenizer UMMs.

</details>


### [45] [DuET: Dual Expert Trajectories for Diffusion Image Editing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13303)
*Lidia Troeshestova,Alexander Ustyuzhanin,Sergey Kastryulin*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DuET: a training-free inference method that temporarily relaxes source conditioning via a text-to-image phase to improve instruction-based edits in diffusion models, with a trade-off between source preservation and edit fidelity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Persistent source-image conditioning can throttle the extent of edits and naturalness when the target scene diverges from the input; a training-free method to enhance edit fidelity without changing model weights is needed.

Method: During inference, introduce a text-to-image phase to relax source conditioning, then revert to edit mode; this transient diffusion trajectory moves toward the target distribution while retaining structural guidance from the image, without additional sampling cost or weight updates; demonstrated across multiple models and benchmarks.

Result: Consistent improvements in instruction relevance, semantic fidelity, and perceptual quality across diverse models and benchmarks; sometimes a modest reduction in source-image preservation, revealing a predictable trade-off between preservation and edit fidelity.

Conclusion: DuET provides a practical, training-free approach to balance source structure preservation and edit fidelity by temporarily shifting the denoising trajectory toward a text-to-image distribution, improving edit quality with a known preservation-edit trade-off.

Abstract: Recent diffusion editors perform diverse instruction-based edits while conditioning on the source image at every denoising step. Yet persistent source-image conditioning can limit how fully an edit is executed and how natural the result appears, especially when the target scene diverges substantially from the input. We introduce DuET (Dual Expert Trajectories), a training-free inference method that temporarily relaxes source-image conditioning by transitioning through a text-to-image phase before returning to edit mode, allowing the denoising trajectory to move toward the target distribution while retaining the structural benefits of image-conditioned editing. Without modifying model weights or increasing sampling cost, DuET consistently improves instruction relevance, semantic fidelity, and perceptual quality across diverse models and benchmarks. In some cases, these gains come with a modest reduction in source-image preservation, revealing a predictable trade-off between source preservation and edit fidelity.

</details>


### [46] [ReFree: Towards Realistic Co-Speech Video Generation via Reward-Free RL and Multilevel Speech Guidance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13304)
*Salaheldin Mohamed,M. Hamza Mughal,Rishabh Dabral,Christian Theobalt*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ReFree-S2V is a flow-matching speech-to-portrait animation framework that uses multi-level speech representations injected into transformer blocks via learnable level selectors, plus reward-free reinforcement learning for natural head motion, achieving state-of-the-art lip-sync and expressivity without handcrafted metrics or heavy annotations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the trade-off between precise lip articulation and expressive, natural facial motion in speech-driven portrait animation.

Method: Flow-matching framework built on a pretrained video generation model; introduces multi-level phonetic/prosodic representations at local/global granularity; selective injection into transformer blocks via learnable level selectors; reward-free reinforcement learning integrated into flow-matching training to discourage perceptually implausible motion without handcrafted metrics or annotations.

Result: Experiments show state-of-the-art performance, with significant improvements in lip-sync accuracy and in qualitative assessments of naturalness and expressivity compared to existing methods.

Conclusion: ReFree-S2V provides accurate lip synchronization and natural expressive behavior in speech-driven portrait animation through multi-level representation and reward-free learning, mitigating the need for manual synchronization metrics or preference data.

Abstract: Speech-driven talking character animation seeks to generate life-like portrait videos that convey natural conversation behavior, aligning facial motion with spoken audio. Although recent advances in video generation have substantially improved realism in video-based animation, achieving both accurate lip articulation and expressive behavior remains challenging. Existing approaches typically trade off precise phoneme-to-lip synchronization against dynamic facial expressions and head motion, yielding animations that are either accurate yet rigid, or expressive but poorly synchronized. We address this challenge by proposing ReFree-S2V, a flow-matching speech-to-portrait animation framework that builds upon a pretrained video generation model to achieve fine-grained speech articulation and high-level expressive cues in speech-driven portrait animation. This model introduces a multi-level speech representation capturing phonetic and prosodic information at both local and global granularities. These representations are selectively injected into transformer blocks via learnable level selectors, enabling both accurate lip synchronization and natural expressive motion. To achieve natural head movements, we further introduce a novel reward-free reinforcement learning scheme into flow-matching training to discourage perceptually implausible motion without relying on handcrafted synchronization metrics or reward models, or the high cost of human preference annotation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that ReFree-S2V achieves state-of-the-art performance, significantly outperforming existing methods in both quantitative lip-sync accuracy and qualitative human evaluations of naturalness and expressivity.

</details>


### [47] [MagPlus: Bridging Micro-to-Regular Facial Expressions through Learnable Magnification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13312)
*Sliman Jammal,Andrei Sharf*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MagPlus/DeMagPlus provide a transferable pipeline that magnifies micro-expressions to macro ranges to leverage existing macro-expression models for transfer/synthesis, then restores micro-intensity, enabling pretrained models to generate more realistic micro-expressions without retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the scarcity and weak signal of micro-expression data by enabling micro-expression generation using established macro-expression models, improving quality, robustness, and generalization without retraining backbones.

Method: A magnification module (MagPlus) scales subtle facial motions into the range of regular expressions, enabling compatibility with standard macro-expression models (e.g., FOMM, FSRT, MetaPortrait, EmoPortraits). A DeMagPlus module then reduces the generated motion back to realistic micro-expression intensities while preserving dynamics. The framework is evaluated with four macro models that were never trained on micro-expressions, demonstrating transfer.

Result: Experiments show that MagPlus-DeMagPlus allows pretrained macro-expression models to generate more realistic micro-expression motion without retraining the backbones.

Conclusion: The approach provides a transferable bridge between micro- and macro-expression processing, enabling improved micro-expression generation by reusing existing macro-models.

Abstract: Facial micro-expressions are subtle and short-lived facial movements that provide important cues about genuine human emotions. However, modeling and generating them remains difficult because annotated micro-expression data is limited and the underlying facial motions are extremely weak. Existing micro-expression generation methods therefore often suffer from limited quality, weak robustness, and poor generalization. We propose MagPlus, a transferable micro-expression processing pipeline that connects micro-expression analysis with standard facial animation models. Instead of training a dedicated generator from scratch, MagPlus learns to magnify subtle facial motions into the range of regular facial expressions, transforming micro-expressions into signals that are compatible with existing facial expression processing models. The magnified sequence is then used by a standard facial expression model for tasks such as transfer and synthesis. A complementary DeMagPlus module then restores the generated motion back to realistic micro-expression intensity levels while preserving the synthesized dynamics. We evaluate the framework using four facial animation models: FOMM, FSRT, MetaPortrait, and EmoPortraits. None of these models are trained on micro-expression data. Experiments show that MagPlus-DeMagPlus enables pretrained macro-expression models to generate more realistic micro-expression motion without retraining the backbones.

</details>


### [48] [Masked and Predictive Self-Supervised Foundation Models for 3D Brain MRI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13315)
*Esra Ergün,Hersh Chandarana,Dan Sodickson,Gözde Ünal*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MAE with spectral-domain supervision substantially improves MRI-based disease detection; spectral regularization benefits high-frequency anatomical signals, while covariance regularization benefits multi-dimensional decorrelated features; the downstream gains depend on task structure, highlighting that self-supervised objectives encode biases.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Explore how self-supervised pretraining paradigms and auxiliary objectives shape MRI-based disease detection performance, addressing the gap beyond segmentation-focused studies.

Method: Compare reconstruction-based MAE and predictive JEPA frameworks; introduce spectral-domain reconstruction loss for MAE and variance–covariance regularization for JEPA; pretrain on heterogeneous single-contrast MRI volumes in a contrast-agnostic setting (no modality concatenation); evaluate on five downstream disease-detection tasks.

Result: Spectral-domain supervision within MAE yields the strongest downstream gains; spectral regularization enhances sensitivity to high-frequency anatomical structures, while VCR fosters decorrelated latent representations; the benefit of each objective is task-structure dependent.

Conclusion: Self-supervised objectives embed biases that influence downstream performance; selecting objectives aligned with the task structure is crucial for effective MRI disease detection with foundation models.

Abstract: Self-supervised foundation models have shown strong promise in medical imaging. However, existing MRI foundation-model studies have primarily emphasized segmentation and dense prediction tasks, while systematic investigation of self-supervised foundation models for MRI-based disease detection remains limited. In this work, we investigate two major self-supervised pretraining paradigms for MRI-based disease detection: reconstruction-based learning via Masked Autoencoders (MAE) and predictive representation learning via Joint Embedding Predictive Architectures (JEPA). We study the role of auxiliary objectives by introducing a novel spectral-domain reconstruction loss for MAE to enhance sensitivity to fine-grained anatomical structure, and by integrating variance--covariance regularization (VCR) within our JEPA framework to encourage decorrelated latent representations. Our models are pretrained on heterogeneous single-contrast MRI volumes in a contrast-agnostic setting, without modality concatenation. Across five downstream disease detection tasks, our results highlight the importance of self-supervised objective design for medical foundation model pretraining, demonstrating that the downstream benefit of each objective is determined by its relevance to the task's structure. Specifically, spectral regularization yields the largest improvements when the downstream discriminative signal is characterized by strong high-frequency anatomical structures, while covariance regularization is most beneficial when discriminative information spans multiple decorrelated feature dimensions. MAE with spectral-domain supervision consistently achieves superior downstream performance for MRI-based disease detection. These findings suggest that self-supervised objectives in medical imaging encode specific biases, and their downstream benefit is fundamentally conditioned on the task's structure.

</details>


### [49] [OR-Action: Multi-Role Video Understanding with Fine-Grained Actions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13332)
*Felix Tristram,Ege Özsoy,Christian Benz,Marcel Walch,Ghazal Ghazaei,Nassir Navab*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: First action-centric temporal benchmark for operating-room understanding, built from ego-exo data, with a fine-grained multi-role action taxonomy distilled from scene-graph state changes; shows a vision-only temporal model outperforms graph-based methods and introduces multi-to-single-view feature alignment; benchmark and code to be released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable principled temporal evaluation of OR understanding methods. Existing scene-graph predictions struggle to capture temporal structure under clutter, occlusions, and limited sensing; a robust temporal model using vision and cross-view alignment could reduce reliance on exhaustive egocentric data.

Method: Define a fine-grained, multi-role action taxonomy for OR activities; generate dense action segments by distilling from ground-truth scene-graph state changes; develop a vision-only temporal model; introduce a multi-to-single-view feature alignment strategy to improve single-view action recognition as a form of data-efficient cross-view transfer.

Result: Experiments show current scene-graph prediction methods struggle to model temporal structure, even with explicit Graph Neural Networks; the proposed vision-only temporal model outperforms graph-based methods when using all available egocentric video; the alignment strategy enhances single-view performance on multi-role action recognition.

Conclusion: The benchmark and the proposed vision-centric temporal modeling approach advance temporal understanding in OR settings, with cross-view feature alignment helping mitigate data collection needs. Benchmark and code will be released upon acceptance.

Abstract: Fine-grained understanding of operating room (OR) activity could enable workflow-aware assistance, yet remains difficult due to clutter, occlusions, and limited sensing. The prevailing approach to model this environment is scene graphs as an interpretable representation of OR interactions. Converting their frame-wise relational predictions into temporally extended, fine-grained actions however, is challenging without explicit temporal modeling. To enable a principled temporal evaluation of current OR understanding methods, we introduce the first action-centric benchmark built on a publicly available ego-exocentric OR dataset by defining a fine-grained, multi-role action taxonomy and generating dense action segments via distillation from ground-truth scene graph state changes. Experiments on this benchmark show that current scene graph prediction methods struggle to model temporal structure, even when adding explicit modeling through Graph Neural Networks. We therefore introduce a vision-only temporal model that outperforms graph-based methods significantly when using all available egocentric video as input. Building on this model we also introduce a novel multi- to single-view feature alignment strategy that improves single-view performance on multi-role action recognition, mitigating the need for extensive egocentric video capture. Benchmark and code will be released upon acceptance.

</details>


### [50] [Dual-Domain Equivariant Generative Adversarial Network for Multimodal CT-PET Synthesis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13341)
*Gabriel Steele,Alzahra Altalib,Alessandro Perelli*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A dual-domain, rotation-equivariant GAN (DDE-GAN) for multimodal CT-PET synthesis that jointly learns spatial and Fourier-domain representations and enforces geometric consistency through hierarchical, multi-stage losses, achieving superior performance on HECKTOR 2022 dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the limitation of traditional spatial-domain GANs that ignore geometric consistency and spectral information, leading to suboptimal anatomical fidelity in CT-PET synthesis; incorporate dual-domain learning and rotational equivariance to improve accuracy and robustness.

Method: Proposes DDE-GAN that (1) learns in both spatial and Fourier domains to capture complementary anatomical and spectral cues; (2) embeds rotational equivariance from CT/PET physics into generator and discriminator losses; (3) uses hierarchical dual-domain training with multi-stage losses to enforce intra- and inter-domain consistency.

Result: On the HECKTOR 2022 CT-PET dataset, DDE-GAN achieves superior synthesis quality compared with baseline models for CT-PET image synthesis, demonstrating improved accuracy and robustness.

Conclusion: Combining dual-domain learning with geometric equivariance substantially enhances multimodal CT-PET image synthesis, enabling practical applications in PET completion and data augmentation.

Abstract: We present a Dual-Domain Equivariant Generative Adversarial Network (DDE-GAN) for multimodal CT-PET image synthesis. Traditional GAN-based approaches often operate solely in the spatial domain and ignore geometric consistency, resulting in limited structural fidelity. DDE-GAN addresses these challenges by jointly learning from both spatial and frequency (Fourier) domains, capturing complementary anatomical and spectral information. Furthermore, rotational equivariance embedded in the physics of the CT and PET measurements are integrated into the loss of both the generator and discriminator to ensure consistent responses under rotations, improving anatomical accuracy. A hierarchical dual-domain training strategy enforces intra- and inter-domain consistency through multi-stage loss functions. Evaluated on the HECKTOR 2022 CT-PET dataset, DDE-GAN achieves superior synthesis quality over baseline models for CT-PET image synthesis. The results demonstrate that combining dual-domain learning with geometric equivariance substantially enhances multimodal image synthesis accuracy and robustness, enabling practical applications in PET completion and data augmentation.

</details>


### [51] [JointEdit3D: Feed-Forward 3D Scene Editing in a Unified Latent Space](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13345)
*Xinnan Zhu,Ruijie Xu,Jiayu Ying,Daoguo Dong,Jiachen Xu,Yuan Xie,Xin Tan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: JointEdit3D introduces a feed-forward 3D scene editing framework that unifies RGB-geometry reconstruction-generation latent spaces, enabling asymmetric latent inpainting from a single edited reference. It adds a SceneAnchor Branch and edit/background-aware losses, and provides SceneEdit3D-15K/SceneEdit3D-Bench for evaluation. It boosts edited-region fidelity and 3D structural completeness while preserving background.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
