Motivation: Existing 3D scene editing methods rely on per-scene optimization or cascaded edit-and-reconstruct pipelines, incurring high test-time cost, limited 3D awareness, and structural inconsistencies. A unified latent space that couples appearance and geometry is needed for efficient, coherent edits.

Method: Propose JointEdit3D: a feed-forward framework that performs asymmetric latent inpainting by observing a single edited RGB latent and generating remaining RGB views and edited geometry latent anchored to the source scene. Introduces a SceneAnchor Branch to inject source-structure without direct copying, and employ edit-background-aware losses to balance edits with content preservation. Also introduces SceneEdit3D-15K (15k paired edits with 3D annotations) and SceneEdit3D-Bench (100-sample benchmark).

Result: Experiments show that JointEdit3D improves edited-region quality and 3D structural completeness over prior baselines, while maintaining competitive background preservation.

Conclusion: JointEdit3D demonstrates that coupling appearance and geometry in a unified latent space with a SceneAnchor and targeted losses enables accurate, efficient 3D scene editing. The accompanying SceneEdit3D-15K/Bench provide a resource for standardized evaluation.

Abstract: Existing 3D scene editing methods typically rely on per-scene optimization over explicit 3D representations or cascaded edit-and-reconstruct pipelines, resulting in high test-time cost, limited 3D awareness, and structural inconsistencies. To couple appearance synthesis and geometry prediction during editing, we build on a unified RGB-geometry reconstruction-generation latent space and adapt it to feed-forward 3D scene editing. The resulting framework, \textbf{JointEdit3D}, performs asymmetric latent inpainting by observing only a single edited RGB reference latent and generating the remaining RGB views and edited geometry latent under source-scene anchoring. JointEdit3D introduces a dedicated SceneAnchor Branch to inject source-scene structure without forcing direct copying, and adopts edit/background-aware losses to balance edited-region fidelity with unedited-content preservation. To address the lack of paired resources for standardized 3D scene editing evaluation, we introduce SceneEdit3D-15K, a dataset with 15K paired editing samples and renderer-provided 3D annotations, together with SceneEdit3D-Bench, a curated 100-sample benchmark. Experiments show that JointEdit3D improves edited-region quality and 3D structural completeness over prior baselines while maintaining competitive background preservation.

</details>


### [52] [Dual-Constrained Diffusion Image Compression for Operational Rate-Distortion-Perception Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13366)
*Sanxin Jiang,Jiro Katto,Heming Sun*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DCIC enables dual-constrained diffusion-based image compression to realize the full RDP surface with adjustable fidelity-realism at fixed rate, achieving state-of-the-art BD-PSNR and BD-FID on standard datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RDP extends rate-distortion theory by including a distributional/perceptual constraint; practical realization is hard due to the challenge of introducing common randomness at the decoder; need a framework that jointly controls distortion and perceptual realism without extra rate.

Method: Combine a learned codec with a diffusion-based decoder guided by two joint constraints: distortion (Fidelity relative to base codec output) and idempotence (re-encoding the restored image recovers the base reconstruction). Use iterative optimization with consistent noise injection to steer the reverse diffusion and realize common randomness without rate overhead. Introduce two attenuation factors (K_D, K_P) to navigate the RDP frontier at fixed rate, yielding boundary curves DCIC_RD (K_P=0), DCIC_RP (K_D=0), and interior DCIC_RDP (K_D=K_P=1).

Result: Empirical evaluation on CelebA-HQ, CLIC2020, and ImageNet-1K shows DCIC_RDP achieves superior BD-PSNR compared with all perceptual codecs; DCIC_RP matches dedicated perception-oriented methods in BD-FID across CNN, Transformer, and hybrid backbones.

Conclusion: Full RDP surface navigation is practically realizable with a dual-constraint, diffusion-based decoder framework, enabling controllable fidelity-realism trade-offs at fixed rate without extra rate cost and validated across multiple datasets and architectures.

Abstract: The rate-distortion-perception (RDP) trade-off extends classical rate--distortion theory by imposing a distributional constraint on reconstructions, providing a unified framework for neural image compression that jointly governs fidelity and perceptual realism. While prior work achieves near-optimal rate--perception trade-offs, practical frameworks explicitly realizing the full RDP surface remain scarce, primarily due to the difficulty of introducing common randomness at the decoder. We propose DCIC (Dual-Constrained Diffusion Image Compression), which integrates a learned codec with a diffusion-based decoder governed by joint distortion and idempotence constraints. The distortion constraint bounds reconstruction fidelity relative to the base codec output; the idempotence constraint -- requiring that re-encoding the restored image recovers the base codec reconstruction -- serves as a tractable surrogate for the distributional perception requirement. Together, they steer the reverse denoising process via iterative optimization with consistent noise injection, realizing common randomness without additional rate overhead. At fixed rate, dual attenuation factors $(K_D, K_P)$ jointly navigate the Pareto frontier of the distortion-perception plane, enabling continuously adjustable fidelity-realism trade-offs from a single bitstream. DCIC$_{RD}$ ($K_P{=}0$) and DCIC$_{RP}$ ($K_D{=}0$) arise as boundary curves, with DCIC$_{RDP}$ ($K_D = K_P=1$) realizing the optimal interior operating point. Experiments on CelebA-HQ, CLIC2020, and ImageNet-1K across CNN, Transformer, and hybrid architectures confirm that DCIC$_{RDP}$ achieves superior BD-PSNR over all perceptual codecs, while DCIC$_{RP}$ matches dedicated perception-oriented methods in BD-FID, validating the practical value of full RDP surface navigation.

</details>


### [53] [MoVerse: Real-Time Video World Modeling with Panoramic Gaussian Scaffold](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13376)
*Yang Zhou,Ziheng Wang,Yuqin Lu,Haofeng Liu,Jun Liang,Shengfeng He,Jing Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MoVerse is a real-time single-image world model that generates interactively navigable 3D scenes and video by decoupling world construction from rendering, using panorama diffusion, a 3D Gaussian scaffold, and a Gaussian-conditioned video renderer with teacher-student diffusion distillation, achieving 8 FPS on an RTX 4090.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: From a narrow-field-of-view image, the task is to create a persistent, coherent 3D environment for interactive roaming with controllable camera and temporally coherent, high-fidelity observations. This requires reconciling incomplete input with long-range consistency, 3D structure, and real-time rendering.

Method: 1) Expand the input into a gravity-aligned 360° panorama using topology-aware diffusion to fill missing FOV. 2) Lift the panorama into a persistent 3D Gaussian scaffold via panoramic geometry-aware residual prediction for a dense, renderable spatial memory. 3) Use a Gaussian-conditioned video renderer to render scaffold renderings along user-specified camera paths into photorealistic video. 4) Train a bidirectional diffusion teacher for high-quality conditional rendering and distill into a causal autoregressive student for bounded-latency streaming, enabling interactive performance.

Result: Achieves real-time scene roaming at 8 FPS on a single NVIDIA RTX 4090. Produces a persistent 3D representation with controllable camera trajectories and high-quality video output, combining explicit 3D structure with diffusion-based rendering for improved perceptual realism.

Conclusion: MoVerse demonstrates that integrating explicit 3D representations with diffusion-based video models can enable interactive, real-time world creation from a single image, bridging structured world memory with high-fidelity rendering for navigable virtual environments.

Abstract: We present MoVerse, a real-time video world model that creates an interactively navigable scene from a single narrow-field-of-view image. This setting is challenging because the input observes only a small fraction of the environment, while interactive roaming requires a complete surrounding world, persistent geometry, controllable camera motion, and temporally coherent high-fidelity observations. MoVerse addresses this problem by separating world construction from observation rendering. It first expands the input into a gravity-aligned 360$^\circ$ panorama with topology-aware diffusion, closing the missing field of view before 3D reasoning. It then lifts the panorama into a persistent 3D Gaussian scaffold using panoramic geometry-aware residual prediction, yielding a dense and directly renderable spatial memory. Finally, a Gaussian-conditioned video renderer translates scaffold renderings along user-specified camera trajectories into photorealistic video. To make this renderer practical for interaction, we train a bidirectional diffusion teacher for high-quality conditional rendering and distill it into a causal autoregressive student for bounded-latency streaming. This design combines the controllability and long-range consistency of explicit 3D representations with the perceptual quality of generative video models. MoVerse supports real-time scene roaming at 8~FPS on a single NVIDIA RTX~4090 GPU, demonstrating a practical path toward single-image world creation with interactive video output.

</details>


### [54] [SmartFont: Dynamic Condition Allocation for Few-Shot Font Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13382)
*Zian Yang,Zixin Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Diffusion-based few-shot font generation (SmartFont) combines global content-style synthesis with weakly supervised local corrective experts, using an adaptive denoising-state condition allocation to balance global and local cues across timesteps, achieving better global-local fidelity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the trade-off between global structural completeness and local style fidelity in few-shot font generation, and to overcome disentanglement issues and heavy reliance on local priors in existing methods.

Method: Two-branch architecture: (1) global content-style branch for robust, holistic generation; (2) local corrective expert branch learning semantic-spatial local concepts and spatial maps under weak supervision; denoising-state condition allocation module adaptively weights global content, global style, and local features across timesteps and injection blocks.

Result: Empirical evaluation shows improved global-local balance, higher glyph quality, and better preservation of local details compared to baselines.

Conclusion: SmartFont effectively integrates global and local cues through multi-level allocation and adaptive conditioning, advancing few-shot font generation.

Abstract: Few-shot font generation simultaneously requires global structural completeness and fine-grained local style fidelity. Existing methods usually either rely on global content-style modeling, which is robust but imperfectly disentangled, or emphasize component/local modeling, which captures fine details but relies heavily on local priors and reference coverage. We argue that the key challenge is not merely to learn purer conditions, but to organize complementary yet biased global and local conditions through multi-level allocation during generation. To this end, we propose SmartFont, a diffusion-based few-shot font generation framework that combines global content-style generation with weakly supervised local corrective experts. The local branch performs semantic-spatial allocation by learning expert-wise local concepts and semantically meaningful spatial maps under weak component supervision, enabling fine-grained correction without requiring explicit component-conditioned inference. On top of this, a denoising-state condition allocation module adaptively weights global content, global style, and local corrective feature across timesteps and injection blocks. Extensive experiments show that SmartFont achieves better global-local balance, improves glyph quality and local detail fidelity.

</details>


### [55] [Heterogeneous LiDAR Early Fusion and Learned Re-Ranking Strategy for Robust Long-Term Place Recognition in Unstructured Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13503)
*Judith Vilella-Cantos,Juan José Cabrera,Mónica Ballesta,David Valiente,Luis Payá*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MinkUNeXt-VINE++ proposes early fusion of two heterogeneous LiDAR sensors and a learned re-ranking step to improve place recognition in unstructured, repetitive vineyard environments, achieving notable gains over single-sensor methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robust place recognition in unstructured agricultural environments is challenging due to appearance variability and repetitive structures; leveraging multiple LiDAR modalities and a re-ranking mechanism could improve true positive identification under varying phenological stages.

Method: The approach performs early fusion of data from two LiDAR sensors (Livox Mid-360 and Velodyne VLP-16) to create a richer representation, followed by a learned re-ranking stage deployed during inference to refine candidate matches, with evaluation on the TEMPO-VINE dataset.

Result: The method significantly improves recall at 1 (Recall@1) over single-sensor baselines (≈20% gain) and further gains (~+30%) when re-ranking is included; code is publicly available.

Conclusion: Fusing heterogeneous LiDAR data with an inference-time re-ranking strategy yields substantial gains in LiDAR-based place recognition in vineyard environments, suggesting benefits for unstructured agricultural robotics and dataset-specific improvements; broader generalization remains to be validated.

Abstract: Robust localization in unstructured environments, such as agricultural fields, is a critical challenge for autonomous systems. LiDAR sensors provide detailed 3D information about the environment and are invariant to lighting conditions. For this reason, LiDAR-based place recognition methods have gained significant attention. In this paper, we propose MinkUNeXt-VINE++, a novel approach that combines early fusion of heterogeneous LiDAR data from two sensors (Livox Mid-360 and Velodyne VLP-16) and a learned re-ranking strategy in inference time. This fusion leverages the strengths of each sensor to provide a more comprehensive representation of the environment. Additionally, the re-ranking approach is particularly important in repetitive environments, such as vineyards, as finding true positives is a major challenge. We evaluated our approach using the TEMPO-VINE dataset, which provides heterogeneous LiDAR data in vineyard environments across different phenological stages. Our results demonstrate that MinkUNeXt-VINE++ significantly improves place recognition performance compared to single-sensor approaches and state-of-the-art methods. MinkUNeXt-VINE++ achieves a 20% improvement in the Recall@1 metric compared to single-sensor approaches, and +30% including re-ranking. The code of our method is publicly available for reproduction.

</details>


### [56] [Person Identification from Contextual Motion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13410)
*Igor Kviatkovsky,Ehud Rivlin,Ilan Shimshoni*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Interactive motion-based person identification using a HIP-inspired generative model with mutual-information cue selection; sequential cues elicit responses; Bayesian updating; first interactive setting; strong results on multiple datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address identification from motion patterns in surveillance/authentication; leverage information-theoretic cue selection and interactive responses to improve accuracy.

Method: Propose a probabilistic generative model of action instance creation; implement an interactive session where at each stage the system presents a cue to the subject and records the motion response; choose cues to maximize mutual information between response and identity; update posterior over identities after each response; terminate when a confidence threshold is reached.

Result: High recognition rates reported on five public datasets plus a novel dataset with 4,476 recordings from 22 subjects across 15 cues.

Conclusion: Introduces and evaluates the first interactive setting for motion-based identity recognition; demonstrates the feasibility of mutual-information guided, HIP-inspired generative modeling for efficient identity inference; encourages further datasets and broader validation.

Abstract: We consider the problem of identifying people based on their motion styles. We present a generative model describing the action instance creation process and derive a probabilistic identity inference scheme for two common person identification scenarios motivated by the surveillance and authentication applications. We introduce a novel, \emph{interactive}, scenario for person identification from motion patterns. To this end, we formalize the identification process in the context of a sequential message exchange session between the subject and the system. The subject's behavior is modeled using a probabilistic generative model inspired by the Human Information Processing (HIP) paradigm. At each stage, the system presents a visual stimulus (a cue) to the subject and records their motion response. The cue is selected so as to maximize the mutual information of the expected response and the subject's identity. Once recorded, the response is used to update the a posteriori probability over possible subjects' identities. The process terminates once a sufficient classification confidence level is reached. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first time person identification is addressed in such interactive setting. We report high recognition rates on five publicly available datasets and our own novel dataset consisting of 4,476 recordings of 22 test subjects responding to 15 cues.

</details>


### [57] [MaskWAM: Unifying Mask Prompting and Prediction for World-Action Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13515)
*Hanyang Yu,Haitao Lin,Jingbo Zhang,Wenyao Zhang,Chenghao Gu,Heng Li,Ping Tan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Summary generation failed


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Motivation analysis unavailable

Method: Method extraction failed

Result: Result analysis unavailable

Conclusion: Conclusion extraction failed

Abstract: World Action Models (WAMs) present a promising paradigm for robotic control via video prediction. However, current WAMs suffer from fundamental spatial bottlenecks: standard text inputs introduce referential ambiguity in cluttered scenes, while unstructured RGB predictions lack semantic grounding and remain biased by task-irrelevant backgrounds. To overcome these limitations, we introduce MaskWAM, an object-centric world-action model. By jointly integrating masks as both explicit inputs and predictions via a unified Mixture of Transformers (MoT), MaskWAM unlocks robust policy generalization. This design provides two key benefits: (1) predicting future masks yields object-centric semantic supervision that suppresses visual noise, significantly enhancing even standard text-conditioned WAMs; and (2) coupling this predictive supervision with first-frame visual prompts, such as target object masks, establishes a precise spatial anchor that substantially reduces language ambiguity. Crucially, as WAMs are inherently vision-driven architectures, direct mask conditioning yields substantially stronger guidance than text alone, establishing a precise and robust paradigm for manipulating unseen objects. Evaluations on LIBERO, RoboTwin, and real-world tasks demonstrate that MaskWAM significantly outperforms baselines in both language-clear and language-ambiguous tasks.

</details>


### [58] [VietFashion: Benchmarking Sketch-Text Composed Image Retrieval for Cultural Outfits](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13427)
*Hoang-Nguyen Cao,Le-Hoang Bui,Dinh-Khoi Vo,Minh-Triet Tran,Trung-Nghia Le*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VietFashion introduces a sketch-text composed image retrieval benchmark for Ao Dai, combining 650 sketches with 21k photorealistic images and aligned captions, using multi-target retrieval to reflect design ambiguity, and benchmarks state-of-the-art methods; reveals gaps in modeling fine-grained cultural semantics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the challenge of retrieving culturally significant garments whose identity relies on subtle structural and symbolic details not captured by standard multimodal models; support designers/researchers in retrieving culturally meaningful outfits through sketches and textual descriptions.

Method: Dataset construction starts from 650 hand-drawn sketches, expanded to over 21,000 photorealistic images with aligned captions generated via generative models. Text prompts describe detailed outfit attributes sourced from fashion magazines to ensure authenticity. A multi-target retrieval setting allows a single query to map to multiple valid results. Standardized evaluation protocols are established, and state-of-the-art composed image retrieval methods are benchmarked.

Result: Experiments show significant performance gaps in modeling fine-grained cultural semantics and multi-modal composition, establishing VietFashion as a challenging benchmark for fine-grained fashion retrieval.

Conclusion: VietFashion provides a challenging testbed for sketch-text image retrieval with cultural semantics, highlighting the need for models that better capture fine-grained attributes and multi-modal composition; the dataset is publicly available.

Abstract: Cultural garments pose a unique challenge for visual retrieval systems, as their identity often depends on subtle structural and symbolic details that are poorly captured by standard AI models. We introduce VietFashion, a new benchmark for sketch-text composed image retrieval centered on the Ao Dai, a traditional Vietnamese garment. VietFashion enables designers and researchers to retrieve culturally meaningful outfits using a combination of hand-drawn sketches, which convey garment structure, and textual descriptions, which encode cultural semantics. The dataset is initialized with 650 sketches and expanded using generative models to produce over 21,000 photorealistic images with aligned captions. Textual prompts that describe detailed outfit attributes, which are extracted from fashion magazines to ensure authenticity and diversity. To better reflect the inherent ambiguity of design intent, VietFashion adopts a multi-target retrieval setting, where a single query may correspond to multiple valid results. We establish standardized evaluation protocols and benchmark state-of-the-art composed image retrieval methods. Experimental results reveal significant performance gaps in modeling fine-grained cultural semantics and multi-modal composition, positioning VietFashion as a challenging benchmark for fine-grained fashion retrieval. The dataset is publicly available at: https://hng0303.github.io/VietFashion.

</details>


### [59] [OmniDirector: General Multi-Shot Camera Cloning without Cross-Paired Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13432)
*Jiwen Liu,Shujuan Li,Zhixue Fang,Xiaohan Li,Yan Zhou,Zijie Meng,Zhimin Zhang,Yawen Luo,Guoxin Zhang,Yu-Shen Liu,Pengfei Wan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A camera-grid video representation and OmniDirector enable scalable, director-level control for multi-shot video generation by cloning camera motion, aided by a hierarchical prompt expansion mechanism and large-scale training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing camera-motion cloning methods rely on parametric representations that do not handle multi-shot trajectories well, and cross-paired data leads to scarcity and poor performance for complex camera motions. A unified, scalable representation and training framework is needed to coordinate camera motion with characters and actions.

Method: Introduce a camera-as-grid-motion-video representation (camera grid). Build OmniDirector trained on 1M+ camera-grid video pairs to coordinate characters, actions, and cameras in multimodal diffusion transformers. Develop a hierarchical prompt expansion agent to harmonize control signals by describing camera motion and visual content and modeling signal relationships.

Result: Experiments demonstrate superior performance and strong controllability in multi-shot camera-motion cloning tasks, validating the effectiveness of the camera-grid representation and the unified Omnidirector framework.

Conclusion: A scalable, director-level control framework for multimodal diffusion-based video generation is achievable through a camera-grid representation and large-scale, multi-signal training; this approach alleviates data scarcity and improves multi-shot cloning quality.

Abstract: Cloning camera motion from reference videos is an important task in video generation, as videos provide intuitive and precise control. Existing methods either directly use parametric representations that fail to handle multi-shot generation or synthesize cross-paired data, which suffer from data scarcity, resulting in poor performance in complicated camera motion cloning. To address these issues, we introduce a general camera motion representation that encodes cameras as grid motion videos. This camera grid represents the camera parameters visually and supports the integration of diverse trajectories for multi-shot video generation. Building upon this, we propose OmniDirector, a unified framework trained on a million-scale camera grid-video pairs that coordinates characters, actions, and cameras to provide director-level control for multimodal diffusion transformers. Furthermore, we design a novel hierarchical prompt expansion agent that harmoniously integrates different control signals by systematically describing camera motion and visual content through understanding signal relationships. Extensive experiments demonstrate the superior performance and outstanding controllability of our framework. Project page: https://ymlinfeng.github.io/OmniDirector.github.io/

</details>


### [60] [VISA: VLM-Guided Instance Semantic Auditing for 3D Occupancy World Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13460)
*Ruiqi Xian,Yuehan Xian,Jing Liang,Xuewei Qi,Dinesh Manocha*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VISA is a training-time semantic auditing framework that uses an offline Vision-Language Model (VLM) to audit each 3D object crop, producing a structured, reliability-weighted semantic audit that is distilled into occupancy logits. This improves closed-set occupancy and rare-class mIoU without changing inference, addressing the mismatch between VLM embeddings and occupancy accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Closed-set occupancy accuracy degrades due to object-level and rare-class errors; while VLMs improve text-space similarity, they do not reliably boost occupancy mIoU. A reliability-aware audit can leverage VLMs to guide semantic logits during training while keeping inference intact.

Method: For each 3D object instance, VISA queries an offline VLM on a representative crop to obtain class hypotheses, plausible confusions, reliability metrics, attributes, and evidence. This audit is propagated along the object track and grounded to matched 3D voxels. The audit is distilled into semantic logits through a reliability-weighted taxonomy, an attribute-factor, and a scene-level audit graph losses. Inference uses the original occupancy model without requiring VLM at test time.

Result: On nuScenes, averaged over three runs: OccWorld mIoU improves from 19.06 to 20.05; GaussianWorld from 21.36 to 21.91. On GaussianWorld, object mIoU improves from 18.18 to 19.16 and rare-class mIoU from 15.60 to 16.79.

Conclusion: VLMs are more effective when used as reliability-aware semantic auditors for closed-set occupancy rather than as generic caption-embedding targets; VISA yields consistent gains in overall and rare-class occupancy metrics without changing inference.

Abstract: Semantic 3D occupancy provides a voxelized world state for autonomous driving and robot decision making, but object and rare-class errors can affect free-space interpretation, collision checking, and temporal state propagation. We show that a common VLM strategy, aligning 3D voxel or object features with crop-caption embeddings, improves text-space similarity without reliably improving closed-set occupancy mIoU. Motivated by this mismatch, we propose VISA, a training-time semantic auditing approach for existing occupancy world models. VISA queries an offline VLM on a representative crop of each physical object instance, obtains a structured audit with class hypotheses, plausible confusions, reliability, attributes, and evidence, and propagates it along the object track. The audit is grounded to matched 3D object voxels and distilled into semantic logits through reliability-weighted taxonomy, attribute-factor, and scene-level audit graph losses, while inference remains unchanged and requires no VLM. On nuScenes, averaged across three runs, VISA improves OccWorld from 19.06 to 20.05 mIoU and GaussianWorld from 21.36 to 21.91 mIoU; on GaussianWorld, object mIoU improves from 18.18 to 19.16 and rare-class mIoU from 15.60 to 16.79. These results suggest that VLMs are better suited to closed-set occupancy as reliability-aware semantic auditors than as generic caption-embedding targets.

</details>


### [61] [Point-Wise Geometry-Aware Transformer for Partial-to-Full Point Cloud Registration in Computer-Assisted Surgery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13488)
*Siyu Zhou,Zhongliang Jiang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GAPR-Net: a coarse-to-fine registration framework that fuses local and global features via cross-attention, introducing a transformation-invariant point-wise geometric representation for robust partial-to-full point cloud alignment, validated on four bone datasets with high recall and strong R^2 scores.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Partial-to-full registration is hindered by varying overlaps, fluctuating densities, and noise. Prior transformers emphasized global context; fine-grained local geometry essential for accurate correspondences is underutilized. A method that jointly exploits local geometry and global context through cross-attention can improve registration accuracy, particularly in medical geometry where partial observations are common.

Method: A coarse-to-fine architecture combining convolutional and transformer modules. Local and global information from partial and full point clouds are fused using a cross-attention mechanism. Introduces a transformation-invariant point-wise geometric feature representation that captures relative geometry to neighboring points. The framework is evaluated on four geometrically distinct bones (tibia, femur, pelvis, thoracic cartilage).

Result: Registration recall: 94.2%. RMSE: 1.992 mm. R^2 for rotation: 0.908; R^2 for translation: 0.974. The approach effectively addresses partial-to-full registration and supports surgical navigation and robotic interventions in computer-assisted surgery. Code will be released after double-blind review.

Conclusion: GAPR-Net demonstrates that integrating coarse-to-fine fusion with cross-attention and transformation-invariant local features yields highly accurate partial-to-full point cloud registration, with strong performance on diverse skeletal geometries and potential clinical impact; further details await beyond double-blind review.

Abstract: Partial-to-full registration remains challenging due to varying overlap ratios, fluctuating point densities, and the presence of noise. While transformers have shown strong potential for point cloud processing, prior methods typically confine them to global context aggregation, overlooking fine-grained local geometry crucial for accurate correspondence. We propose \emph{GAPR-Net}, a learning-based point cloud registration framework with a coarse-to-fine architecture that combines convolution and transformer modules, in which local and global information is fused between the partial and full point clouds using a cross-attention mechanism. To achieve this, a transformation-invariant point-wise geometric feature representation is proposed, which can robustly capture relative geometric features for individual points with respect to their neighboring points. To evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed approach, experiments are conducted on four geometrically distinct bones, including the tibia, femur, pelvis, and thoracic cartilage. The overall registration recall reaches 94.2\%, the method results in a low RMSE of 1.992 mm and $R^2$ values of 0.908 and 0.974 for rotation and translation, respectively. The results demonstrate that the proposed method effectively addresses the partial-to-full point cloud registration problem. The proposed method enables highly accurate 3D point cloud registration using partial observation, providing a critical foundation for precise surgical navigation and robotic interventions in computer-assisted surgery. The code will be accessed after the double-blind review process.

</details>


### [62] [Budget-Constrained Step-Level Diffusion Caching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13496)
*Mingkun Lei,Tong Zhao,Liangyu Yuan,Chi Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: BudCache flips the caching objective for diffusion models by fixing a compute budget and offline-searching for a cache policy that preserves final output quality, using a hybrid of Simulated Annealing and deterministic Hill Climbing. It introduces cache-aware schedule alignment for tight budgets, avoids online search, and reports quality gains over heuristic baselines on FLUX.1-dev and Wan2.1, with code provided.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing per-step caching uses threshold-based heuristics that optimize intermediate step error, leading to variable latency and suboptimal end-quality under fixed budgets. BudCache aims to directly optimize final output quality under a predefined compute budget, ensuring predictable performance at deployment.

Method: Offline optimization: search for high-quality per-step cache policies under a fixed compute budget using a hybrid of Simulated Annealing (global exploration) and deterministic Hill Climbing (local refinement). This yields a policy with minimal online overhead. For very small budgets, introduce cache-aware schedule alignment to adapt time discretization to the policy and reduce trajectory mismatch. Evaluation on diffusion benchmarks (FLUX.1-dev and Wan2.1) with a provided codebase.

Result: BudCache achieves better generation quality than heuristic caching baselines under the same inference budgets and incurs no online search or thresholding overhead during inference. The offline search identifies high-quality policies within minutes. Schedule alignment improves robustness when budgets are very tight.

Conclusion: BudCache offers a principled, budget-driven caching strategy for diffusion models, combining offline global search with local refinement and a schedule-aware adjustment to minimize trajectory mismatch. It improves quality under fixed compute budgets and provides reproducible, deployment-friendly performance with publicly available code.

Abstract: Step-level caching accelerates diffusion models by exploiting temporal redundancy across denoising steps. Existing methods make per-step cache decisions using threshold-based heuristics, without directly optimizing for final output quality. As a result, their inference latency varies across inputs and is difficult to control at deployment. In this work, we propose BudCache, which inverts this formulation: rather than letting per-step error thresholds dictate the runtime cost, we fix the compute budget in advance and search for the cache policy that best preserves the final output. To tackle the combinatorial complexity of step selection, we combine Simulated Annealing with deterministic Hill Climbing. This offline search identifies high-quality cache policies within minutes and introduces no online search or thresholding overhead during inference. When the compute budget is very tight, we further introduce cache-aware schedule alignment, which adapts the time discretization to the selected cache policy to reduce cache-induced trajectory mismatch. Experiments on FLUX.1-dev and Wan2.1 show that BudCache achieves better generation quality than heuristic caching baselines under the same inference budgets. Code is available at https://github.com/Westlake-AGI-Lab/BudCache

</details>


### [63] [Measurement-Calibrated Multi-Camera Fusion for Vision-Based Indoor Localization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13509)
*Mateo Toro Diz,Jonathan Hoss,Noah Klarmann*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Measurement-calibrated multi-camera fusion for indoor localization, quantifying per-component errors (homography, detection, tracking) and performing component-wise evaluation to guide fusion; improves stability and smoothness more than absolute accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Brings transparency to multi-camera fusion by mapping error sources to calibration decisions, addressing the black-box evaluation of fusion components.

Method: Quantify errors for homography calibration, human detection, and motion tracking; perform component-wise evaluation; integrate measurement-calibrated calibration into the fusion process.

Result: Fusion improves localization compared to single-camera baselines; measurement-calibrated fusion offers limited absolute gains over standard fusion but substantially reduces trajectory variance and improves motion smoothness.

Conclusion: Explicit error characterization enhances the design of data fusion strategies for vision-based indoor positioning, yielding more stable and reliable estimates even when end-to-end accuracy gains are modest.

Abstract: Indoor vision-based localization systems are affected by detection noise, occlusions, and limited camera coverage, leading to uncertainty at multiple stages of the pipeline. While multi-camera data fusion is widely used to mitigate these issues, it is typically treated as a black-box component and evaluated solely end-to-end, obscuring its mechanistic contributions. To address this gap, this work investigates whether explicitly characterizing single-camera localization errors can be leveraged to calibrate and optimize multi-camera data fusion.
  We introduce a measurement-calibrated fusion approach that integrates component-wise error quantification, specifically isolating homography calibration, human detection, and motion tracking. A component-wise evaluation is conducted to quantify error contributions from homography calibration, human detection, and motion tracking.
  Experimental results show that data fusion improves localization accuracy compared to single-camera baselines. While measurement-calibrated fusion provides only limited improvement in absolute accuracy over standard fusion, it substantially reduces trajectory variance and improves motion smoothness, which are critical for applications requiring stable and continuous motion estimates. These results highlight the value of explicit error characterization when designing data fusion strategies for vision-based indoor positioning systems.

</details>


### [64] [What's Old is New Again: Classical Dimensionality Reduction for Efficient Saliency-Guided Biometric Attack Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13528)
*Samuel Webster,Walter Scheirer*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces PCA/LDA-inspired saliency maps for saliency-guided training in biometric PAD, enabling annotation-free, scalable gains across iris, face, and fingerprint modalities, with success in additional domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Saliency-guided training improves robustness but current saliency acquisition is costly and domain-specific; a scalable, annotation-free solution is needed.

Method: Derive saliency maps directly from raw data using classical dimensionality reduction (PCA, LDA) without human annotations; apply to multiple PAD tasks; assess scalability in additional domains.

Result: Models trained with DR-derived saliency maps outperform baselines and sometimes SOTA saliency methods, without extra resources or domain-specific tooling; demonstrates scalability across new domains.

Conclusion: DR-inspired saliency maps address key barriers to adoption of saliency-guided training in biometric attack detection and can generalize beyond to other domains.

Abstract: Saliency-guided training is a paradigm in visual recognition that encourages models to focus on the most relevant image regions during learning. While its application in biometric presentation attack detection (PAD) has shown strong benefits in robustness and generalization, adoption is often limited by the high cost, domain specificity, and limited scalability of existing saliency acquisition methods, such as human annotations over a limited dataset. We present a novel, cost-efficient, and highly-scalable approach to saliency acquisition using maps inspired by classical dimensionality reduction techniques: PCA and LDA. Our proposed methods generate saliency maps directly from raw training data, requiring no human annotation nor domain knowledge. We contextualize the effectiveness of these saliency sources in three saliency-explored domains (iris PAD, synthetic face detection, fingerprint PAD) and demonstrate its scalability in two saliency-novel domains (fingerprint vein PAD and ID card PAD). Across all domains tested, models trained using dimensionality reduction-sourced saliency maps exceed baseline and sometimes SOTA saliency methods without any resource investment or domain-specific tooling. Our findings overcome an important yet unaddressed barrier to saliency-guided training for biometric attack detection and beyond.

</details>


### [65] [Edit the Bits, Diff the Codes: Bitwise Residual Editing for Visual Autoregressive Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13558)
*Shengqiang Zhang,Ruotong Liao,Volker Tresp,Barbara Plank,Hinrich Schütze*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A training-free editor for bitwise-residual VAR generators (BitResEdit) that combines BitEdit (per-bit log-odds guidance with Bernoulli-KL trust region) and ResEdit (per-scale residual injection with a localization mask) to improve text alignment and preserve background in text-guided image editing. Demonstrates state-of-the-art alignment among same-backbone editors on PIE-Bench/Infinity-2B (+1.07 CLIP) with complementary ablations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing VAR editors focus on token streams, features, or flat logits, leaving native bitwise-residual structures underutilized. There is a need for effective, training-free control that leverages per-bit Bernoulli heads and additive multi-scale residuals to improve alignment to text while preserving background.

Method: BitEdit performs source-negative guidance by tilting per-bit log-odds along a source-target contrast computed on a shared edited prefix and projects updates into a closed-form Bernoulli-KL trust region around the CFG sampler. ResEdit converts sampled bits into per-scale continuous-code residuals, gates them with a localization mask, and re-injects them through the generator's sum-of-scales. The two modules are used in tandem to couple decision-time bit guidance with composition-time code edits, preserving masked latent features while applying localized, scale-aware edits.

Result: On PIE-Bench with Infinity-2B, BitResEdit achieves the strongest text alignment among editors sharing the same backbone VAR, with CLIP improvement of +1.07 over the strongest prior editor and competitive background preservation. Ablations indicate BitEdit and ResEdit provide complementary benefits for alignment and preservation.

Conclusion: BitResEdit demonstrates that explicitly leveraging native bitwise-residual representations and scale-based code composition yields superior text-guided editing performance without training, with complementary components enabling robust alignment and background preservation.

Abstract: Text-guided image editing with visual autoregressive (VAR) generators requires controlling both what the model samples and where the sampled change is written back into the image code. Existing VAR editors mainly operate on token streams, features, or flat next-token logits, leaving two native structures of bitwise-residual VAR models underused: the per-bit Bernoulli prediction head and the additive multi-scale residual code field from which the image is assembled. We propose BitResEdit, a training-free editor for bitwise-residual VAR generators such as Infinity. BitEdit performs source-negative guidance by tilting the post-CFG per-bit log-odds along a source--target contrast computed on a shared edited prefix, then projects each update into a closed-form Bernoulli-KL trust region around the clean CFG sampler. ResEdit converts the sampled bits into per-scale continuous-code residuals, gates them with a localization mask, and re-injects them through the generator's native sum-of-scales. Together they couple decision-time bit guidance with combination-time code composition, so masked-out latent features are preserved exactly by code arithmetic while localized, scale-aware edits are applied inside the target region. On PIE-Bench with Infinity-2B, BitResEdit attains the strongest text alignment among same-backbone VAR editors, improving CLIP on the edited region by +1.07 over the strongest prior editor while keeping background preservation competitive with it. Ablations show BitEdit and ResEdit play complementary roles in target alignment and background preservation.

</details>


### [66] [Contrast-Informed Augmentation and Domain-Adversarial Training for Adult-to-Neonatal MR Reconstruction Generalization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13562)
*Stephen Moore,Lara Leijser,Richard Frayne,Roberto Souza*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Contrast-informed augmentation and domain-adversarial training improve adult-to-neonatal generalization for E2E-VarNet MR reconstruction; Mixed training with DAT yields best neonatal performance, especially at R=4; at R=8, DAT improves SSIM and overall mixed approaches show competitive PSNR.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Domain shift between adult and neonatal brain MRI limits generalization of deep learning-based MR reconstruction; improving cross-domain robustness is needed for undersampled neonatal imaging.

Method: Retrospective undersampled multi-coil adult T2-weighted brain MR data used. Three training regimes: (1) Unaug-Only (adult-only, unaugmented); (2) Mixed (paired unaugmented adult + neonatal-informed augmented adult); (3) Mixed-DAT (same as mixed with domain-adversarial objective). Evaluations at acceleration factors R=4 and R=8 on neonatal and adult test data using SSIM and PSNR; qualitative latent analysis via t-SNE.

Result: Mixed and Mixed-DAT outperform Unaug-Only for neonatal data. At R=4, Mixed-DAT achieves SSIM 0.924±0.027, PSNR 33.98±1.15 dB. At R=8, Mixed-DAT yields best SSIM 0.848±0.031 (vs 0.766±0.037 Unaug-Only, 0.814±0.035 Mixed); Mixed yields best PSNR 29.56±0.83 dB (vs 26.26±0.78 Unaug-Only, 29.43±0.83 Mixed-DAT). t-SNE latent analysis shows Mixed-DAT increases overlap among latent representations of unaugmented adult, augmented adult, and neonatal data.

Conclusion: Contrast-informed augmentation plus domain-adversarial training enhances adult-to-neonatal generalization in deep learning-based MR reconstruction and may improve robustness to domain shifts in undersampled neonatal MR imaging.

Abstract: Purpose: To investigate whether contrast-informed data augmentation and domain-adversarial training improve the adult-to-neonatal generalization of the E2E-VarNet. Methods: Three training regimes were investigated: (1) adult-only training with unaugmented adult data, (2) mixed training with paired unaugmented and neonatal-informed augmented adult data, and (3) mixed training with a domain-adversarial objective. Models were trained on retrospectively undersampled multi-coil adult T2-weighted brain MR data and evaluated on neonatal and adult test data at acceleration factors $R=4$ and $R=8$ using quantitative metrics and qualitative evaluation. Feature analyses assessed whether domain-adversarial training altered the latent representations of unaugmented adult, augmented adult, and neonatal test samples. Results: Mixed training (Mixed) and mixed domain-adversarial training (Mixed-DAT) outperformed unaugmented adult-only training (Unaug-Only) when evaluated on neonatal data. At R=4, Mixed-DAT achieved the best performance (SSIM = 0.924 +/- 0.027, PSNR = 33.98 +/- 1.15 dB). At R=8, Mixed-DAT performed best when measured using SSIM (0.848 +/- 0.031 vs. 0.766 +/- 0.037 for Unaug-Only and 0.814 +/- 0.035 for Mixed) and Mixed performed best when measured using PSNR (29.56 +/- 0.83 dB vs. 26.26 +/- 0.78 dB for Unaug-Only and 29.43 +/- 0.83 dB for Mixed-DAT). Qualitative assessment of t-SNE plots suggested that Mixed-DAT increased the overlap among the latent representations of the unaugmented adult, augmented adult, and neonatal test data. Conclusion: Contrast-informed augmentation and domain-adversarial training improved adult-to-neonatal generalization of deep learning-based MR reconstruction. These findings suggest that contrast-informed data augmentation combined with adversarial training may improve robustness to domain shift in undersampled neonatal MR reconstruction.

</details>


### [67] [EvTexture++: Event-Driven Texture Enhancement for Video Super-Resolution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13580)
*Dachun Kai,Jiayao Lu,Yueyi Zhang,Xiaoyan Sun*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: An event-driven texture enhancement framework (EvTexture++) for video super-resolution that uses high-frequency event information to enhance texture recovery and temporal consistency, and can be plugged into existing VSR models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Event cameras offer ultra-high temporal resolution and wide dynamic range. Prior work on event-based VSR focused on motion refinement; there is a gap in texture enhancement and texture-consistent reconstruction. Leveraging continuous-time event cues can improve texture restoration and inter-frame alignment, especially in texture-rich regions.

Method: Propose EvTexture++ with (1) a dedicated texture enhancement branch, (2) an iterative texture enhancement module that progressively exploits high-temporal-resolution event data for texture restoration, and (3) a temporal texture alignment module that uses event-guided texture-aware flow for precise inter-frame texture alignment. It is designed as a plug-and-play addition to existing VSR models.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance across five datasets. When integrated into recent VSR models, it yields notable PSNR gains (up to 1.55 dB on the texture-rich Vid4 dataset) and improves texture quality and temporal consistency.

Conclusion: EvTexture++ demonstrates that event information can be effectively exploited for texture enhancement and temporal alignment in VSR, offering a flexible, plug-and-play solution that complements existing VSR architectures and delivers substantial perceptual and numerical gains.

Abstract: Event-based vision has drawn increasing attention owing to its distinctive properties, including ultra-high temporal resolution and extreme dynamic range. Recent works have introduced it to video super-resolution (VSR) to enhance flow estimation and temporal alignment. In contrast, this paper shifts the focus of event signals from motion refinement to texture enhancement in VSR. We propose EvTexture++, the first event-driven framework dedicated to texture enhancement in VSR. It leverages high-frequency spatiotemporal details from events to improve texture recovery. EvTexture++ incorporates a customized texture enhancement branch, along with an iterative texture enhancement module that progressively exploits high-temporal-resolution event information for texture restoration. This enables gradual refinement of texture regions across iterations, yielding more accurate and detailed high-resolution outputs. Besides intra-frame texture recovery, large motions could degrade inter-frame temporal consistency, particularly in texture regions, leading to texture flickering. To mitigate this, we further exploit the continuous-time motion cues of events to enhance temporal consistency, introducing a temporal texture alignment module that estimates event-guided texture-aware flow for precise inter-frame texture alignment. Moreover, EvTexture++ is designed as a plug-and-play tool to flexibly boost the performance of existing VSR models. Experiments on five datasets demonstrate that EvTexture++ achieves state-of-the-art performance. When integrated into recent VSR models, it yields significant improvements, with gains of up to 1.55 dB in PSNR on the texture-rich Vid4 dataset. Code: https://github.com/DachunKai/EvTexture.

</details>


### [68] [Towards Effective Waste Segmentation for Automated Waste Recycling in Cluttered Background](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13587)
*Mamoona Javaid,Mubashir Noman,Abdul Hannan,Shah Nawaz,Mustansar Fiaz,Sajid Ghuffar*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A cascaded spatial–spectral waste segmentation network with an auxiliary feature enhancement module improves automatic waste segmentation by jointly exploiting local structures and global context while boosting boundaries, achieving robust performance on multiple Waste datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current DL-based automated waste recycling (AWR) methods rely on large backbone networks and degrade in cluttered scenes; there is a need for efficient, context-aware segmentation that performs well in realistic clutter.

Method: Introduce an optimal waste segmentation network that leverages spatial-domain local structure and spectral-domain global context in a cascaded design, complemented by an auxiliary feature enhancement module (AFEM) to sharpen object boundaries and amplify blobs in cluttered scenes; experiments on ZeroWaste-aug, ZeroWaste-f, and SpectralWaste datasets.

Result: Extensive experiments demonstrate improved segmentation accuracy and boundary delineation, with robust performance in cluttered scenes and superior results over baselines on the three datasets.

Conclusion: The cascaded spatial–spectral network with AFEM effectively integrates local and global representations for accurate, efficient waste segmentation in AWR and shows strong generalization across diverse waste datasets.

Abstract: Rapid expansion of urban areas and population growth is causing an immense increase in waste production, which demands the need for efficient and automated waste management. In this scenario, automated waste recycling (AWR) using deep learning methods can assist humans in optimal waste management. Recent deep learning approaches for AWR provide promising waste segmentation performance, however, these methods rely on large backbone networks that are inefficient for AWR systems and suffer from performance deterioration in cluttered scenes. To this end, an optimal waste segmentation network is introduced which effectively utilizes the spatial domain to capture localized structural dependencies and the spectral domain to efficiently extract global contextual relationships. This cascaded design allows the network to progressively leverage both local and global representations across complementary domains to highlight the semantic information necessary for effective segmentation of various waste objects. Furthermore, auxiliary feature enhancement module (AFEM) is introduced to enhance the target objects' boundaries and blob amplification for better segmentation in cluttered scenarios. Extensive experimentation on ZeroWaste-aug, ZeroWaste-f and SpectralWaste datasets reveals the merits of the proposed method.

</details>


### [69] [Revisiting Vehicle Color Recognition in Long-Tailed Surveillance Scenarios](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13625)
*Vinícius Orrú,Bruno H. Foggiatto,Gabriel E. Lima,David Menotti,Rayson Laroca*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Imbalance-aware vehicle color recognition on UFPR-VeSV using synthetic minority augmentation and ensemble techniques yields high micro and macro accuracies; macro gain of 8.2 percentage points over prior work; data/code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Color-based vehicle identification is challenged by imbalanced color distributions and real-world conditions (low resolution, occlusion, blur, poor illumination). The study seeks to improve macro (per-class) performance on rare colors, not just overall accuracy.

Method: Explore synthetic minority-class augmentation via two off-the-shelf generative strategies: text-conditioned image generation (RunDiffusion/JuggernautXL) and image-conditioned color editing (Gemini 2.0 Flash). Combine synthetic data with modern visual representations, loss reweighting, learning-rate scheduling, color-safe augmentation, foreground-aware preprocessing, and ensemble fusion. Evaluate on UFPR-VeSV; report micro and macro accuracies.

Result: Best-performing approach achieves 94.6% micro accuracy and 79.7% macro accuracy; macro accuracy increases by 8.2 percentage points relative to recent literature.

Conclusion: Despite improvements, many remaining errors are visually ambiguous even to human annotators, indicating practical limits of color-based vehicle identification in unconstrained surveillance. The work provides public data and code for reproducibility.

Abstract: Vehicle color recognition is an important cue for vehicle identification in surveillance systems, especially when license plates are illegible due to low resolution, occlusion, motion blur, or poor illumination. However, real-world vehicle color distributions are highly imbalanced, making overall accuracy insufficient to assess performance on rare but operationally relevant colors. This paper presents a comprehensive study of vehicle color recognition under severe class imbalance using UFPR-VeSV, a challenging real-world surveillance dataset. We investigate synthetic minority-class augmentation through two off-the-shelf generative strategies: text-conditioned image generation with RunDiffusion/JuggernautXL and image-conditioned color editing with Gemini 2.0 Flash. The curated synthetic data are combined with modern visual representations, loss reweighting, learning-rate scheduling, color-safe augmentation, foreground-aware preprocessing, and ensemble fusion. The bestperforming approach achieves 94.6% micro accuracy and 79.7% macro accuracy, improving macro accuracy by 8.2 percentage points over recent literature. A manual error analysis further shows that many remaining failures are visually ambiguous even for human annotators, highlighting the practical limits of color-based vehicle identification in unconstrained surveillance imagery. The generated images and source code are publicly available at https://github.com/viniciusorru/vcr-synthetic

</details>


### [70] [Surflo: Consistent 3D Surface Flow Model with Global State](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13644)
*Antoine Guédon,Shu Nakamura,Nicolas Dufour,Jiahui Lei,Ko Nishino,Angjoo Kanazawa*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Surflo introduces a global latent token-based decoder that converts unposed RGB views into arbitrary-resolution oriented 3D surface points via flow matching, with inference-time photometric guidance to enforce local consistency; it outperforms feed-forward baselines, is faster than optimization-based methods, and supports scalable outputs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing reconstruction methods either use per-view decoders with overlapping, unaligned pointmaps that grow with input count or global latent models with fixed, low-resolution outputs. There is a need for a single-pass, feed-forward method that can produce high-resolution, view-invariant surface representations from varying numbers of unposed views.

Method: Compress variable numbers of unposed RGB views into a single global latent token (K tokens total, i.e., one global state). Decode oriented 3D surface points by transporting them from noise to the surface using flow matching. Output is not tied to a fixed grid or token budget, enabling arbitrary-resolution decoding (from thousands to millions of points). At inference time, apply a photometric gradient as guidance during ODE integration to correlate nearby points and reduce per-point artifacts.

Result: Surflo achieves surface-quality metrics on par with or better than existing feed-forward baselines. It is about an order of magnitude faster than optimization-based methods that require many views, and uniquely allows arbitrary-resolution decoding with a global latent.

Conclusion: Surflo provides a scalable, fast, single-pass feed-forward framework that unifies a global latent with high-resolution surface decoding, addressing the limitations of both per-view and fixed-output methods and enabling efficient 3D reconstruction from unposed views.

Abstract: Geometry is invariant to viewpoint, which makes any collection of images a redundant encoding of a single 3D state. Existing feed-forward reconstruction models fail to exploit this: per-view methods emit overlapping, unaligned pointmaps that grow linearly with input count, while global-latent methods commit to a fixed, low-resolution output. We introduce Surflo, which compresses a variable number of unposed RGB views into K latent tokens-one global state-and decodes oriented 3D surface points by independently transporting them from noise onto the surface via flow matching. This frees the output from any fixed grid or token budget: the same latent yields from a few thousand to a million points in a single forward pass. To suppress the local inconsistencies inherent to independent per-point decoding, an inference-time guidance term correlates nearby points by injecting a photometric gradient during ODE integration. Surflo matches or surpasses feed-forward baselines on surface metrics, runs an order of magnitude faster than optimization-based methods that require hundreds of views, and is the only feed-forward approach to combine a global latent with arbitrary-resolution decoding.

</details>


### [71] [World Tracing: Generative Pixel-Aligned Geometry Beyond the Visible](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13652)
*Hao Zhang,Mohamed El Banani,Jen-Hao Cheng,Paul Zhang,Yi Hua,Ben Mildenhall,Christoph Lassner,Narendra Ahuja,Gengshan Yang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: World Tracing introduces a pixel-aligned, multi-layer 3D representation that per-pixel predicts an ordered stack of camera-space 3D points (visible surface first, then occluded surfaces), enabled by WT-DiT diffusion transformer to fuse geometry layers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the trade-off in image-to-3D methods between faithfully reconstructing the visible surface (depth anchored to input pixels) and generating complete geometry that may be misaligned; the goal is to maintain pixel alignment while recovering occluded structure.

Method: Represent per-pixel geometry as a stack of 3D points in camera space, with the first layer matching the visible surface and subsequent layers capturing front-to-back occluded intersections. Implement WT-DiT, a diffusion-transformer where multiple geometry layers are treated as denoising tokens connected by factorized and global attention. Train using pixel-space flow matching and a mixed-noise schedule to balance visible-surface reconstruction and occluded-geometry generation.

Result: Achieves strong visible-surface reconstruction and complete geometry across object, scene, and dynamic benchmarks, outperforming both depth predictors and image-to-3D generators.

Conclusion: Preserves 2D-to-3D correspondence enabling text-driven 3D scene editing, geometry-conditioned novel-view video synthesis, and zero-shot integration with textured-mesh generators.

Abstract: Image-to-3D methods often trade off faithfulness and completeness: depth estimators are anchored to input pixels but stop at the visible surface, while image-to-3D models generate complete shapes that are often misaligned with the input. We introduce World Tracing, a generative pixel-aligned geometry representation that predicts 3D points aligned with observed pixels while completing geometry beyond the visible surface. For each input pixel, World Tracing predicts an ordered stack of camera-space 3D points, where the first layer represents the visible surface and subsequent layers represent front-to-back intersections with occluded surfaces. We instantiate this representation with a world-tracing diffusion transformer, WT-DiT, which treats multiple geometry layers as separate denoising tokens coupled through factorized and global attention. WT-DiT is trained with pixel-space flow matching and a mixed noise schedule that balances visible-surface reconstruction with occluded-geometry generation. World Tracing achieves strong performance on visible-surface reconstruction and complete geometry generation across object, scene, and dynamic benchmarks, outperforming both depth predictors and image-to-3D generators. It also preserves 2D-to-3D correspondence, enabling text-driven 3D scene editing, geometry-conditioned novel-view video synthesis, and training-free integration with textured-mesh generators.

</details>


### [72] [Flex4DHuman: Flexible Multi-view Video Diffusion for 4D Human Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13655)
*Jen-Hao Cheng,Yipeng Wang,Hao Zhang,Gengshan Yang,Jenq-Neng Hwang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Diffusion-based 4D human video synthesis (Flex4DHuman) that uses relative camera-pose encoding to transform monocular or sparse multi-view videos into synchronized dense multi-view videos without explicit geometry priors, enabling downstream 4D Gaussian splatting; state-of-the-art on DNA-Rendering and ActorsHQ with cross-domain generalization to animals via mixed human-animal training; three-stage curriculum and test-time captions for control.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable scalable 4D content creation from casual monocular or sparse multi-view videos without reliance on geometry priors (skeletons, depth, normals, or rendered target-view geometry). Improve temporal coherence, view-consistency, and downstream integration with 4D Gaussian splatting, while offering test-time control and cross-domain generalization.

Method: Builds on Wan 2.1 1.3B text-to-video model, preserving backbone and encoding camera/view information through a five-axis positional encoding that extends RoPE with view indices and continuous SE(3) relative geometry. Introduces a three-stage curriculum: pose following, reference-to-target view generation, and temporal rollout. Trains with clean historical target-view tokens for rollout; adds multi-view captions for test-time text control. Outputs feed into an off-the-shelf 4D Gaussian Splatting stage to produce dynamic 4D content.

Result: Surpasses prior state-of-the-art on DNA-Rendering and ActorsHQ. Generalizes to animal categories with mixed human-animal training. Demonstrates practical potential for scalable 4D content creation from casual monocular videos in applications like simulation, gaming, AR/VR, and video re-shooting.

Conclusion: Flex4DHuman offers a geometry-prior-free, view-consistent diffusion framework for dynamic 4D video synthesis, achieving SOTA performance on relevant benchmarks and extending to cross-domain animal categories, thereby enabling scalable 4D content pipelines from casual videos.

Abstract: We present Flex4DHuman, a multi-view video diffusion model that transforms a monocular or sparse multi-view video of a dynamic subject into synchronized dense multi-view videos using only relative camera-pose conditioning. Unlike prior human-centric methods that rely on skeletons, depth maps, normals, or rendered target-view geometry, Flex4DHuman requires no explicit geometry priors and instead conditions generation through relative camera-pose positional encoding. The generated videos can be directly ingested by downstream reconstruction pipelines to create dynamic 4D Gaussian splats. Built on the Wan 2.1 1.3B text-to-video model, Flex4DHuman preserves the backbone architecture and encodes camera and view information through a five-axis positional encoding that extends spatio-temporal RoPE with view indices and continuous SE(3) relative camera geometry. A three-stage curriculum progressively trains the model for pose following, flexible reference-to-target view generation, and temporal rollout. To support temporal rollout, we train with clean historical target-view tokens. We also add multi-view captions to enable test-time text control. Combined with an off-the-shelf 4D Gaussian Splatting stage, our framework lifts monocular static-camera videos into dynamic 4D Gaussian splats. Experiments on DNA-Rendering and ActorsHQ show that Flex4DHuman surpasses prior state-of-the-art methods, while the same formulation generalizes to animal categories after mixed human-animal training. These capabilities make Flex4DHuman a practical step toward scalable 4D content creation from casual monocular videos for simulation, gaming, AR/VR, and video re-shooting.

</details>


### [73] [SpatialClaw: Rethinking Action Interface for Agentic Spatial Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13673)
*Seokju Cho,Ryo Hachiuma,Abhishek Badki,Hang Su,Byung-Kwan Lee,Chan Hee Song,Sifei Liu,Subhashree Radhakrishnan,Seungryong Kim,Yu-Chiang Frank Wang,Min-Hung Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SpatialClaw uses a Python-based, stateful code interface as the action channel for VLM-backed agents, enabling flexible, stepwise spatial reasoning; it outperforms existing single-pass and structured-tool approaches on 20 static/dynamic 3D/4D benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current spatial reasoning agents are hampered by either single-pass code execution (precommits to analysis strategy) or rigid, task-agnostic tool-call interfaces, limiting open-ended 3D/4D reasoning.

Method: A training-free framework where a VLM-backed agent operates inside a pre-loaded, mutable Python kernel containing perception and geometry primitives; the agent emits one executable cell per step, conditioned on prior outputs and observations, enabling flexible composition and iterative refinement.

Result: Across 20 spatial benchmarks and six VLM backbones, SpatialClaw achieves 59.9% average accuracy, a +11.2 point improvement over a recent spatial agent, with no benchmark- or model-specific adaptation.

Conclusion: Code-as-action is a powerful, task-adaptive interface for open-ended spatial reasoning in vision-language agents, delivering robust gains across diverse models.

Abstract: Spatial reasoning, the ability to determine where objects are, how they relate, and how they move in 3D, remains a fundamental challenge for vision-language models (VLMs). Tool-augmented agents attempt to address this by augmenting VLMs with specialist perception modules, yet their effectiveness is bounded by the action interface through which those tools are invoked. In this work, we study how the design of this interface shapes the agent's capacity for open-ended spatial reasoning. Existing spatial agents either employ single-pass code execution, which commits to a full analysis strategy before any intermediate result is observed, or rely on a structured tool-call interface that often offers less flexibility for freely composing operations or tailoring the analysis to each task. Both designs offer limited flexibility for open-ended, complex 3D/4D spatial reasoning. We therefore propose SpatialClaw, a training-free framework for spatial reasoning that adopts code as the action interface. SpatialClaw maintains a stateful Python kernel pre-loaded with input frames and a suite of perception and geometry primitives, letting a VLM-backed agent write one executable cell per step conditioned on all prior outputs, enabling the agent to flexibly compose and manipulate perception results and adapt its analysis to both intermediate text and visual observations and the demands of each problem. Evaluated across 20 spatial reasoning benchmarks spanning a broad range of static and dynamic 3D/4D spatial reasoning tasks, SpatialClaw achieves 59.9% average accuracy, outperforming the recent spatial agent by +11.2 points, with consistent gains across six VLM backbones from two model families without any benchmark- or model-specific adaptation.

</details>


### [74] [RepWAM: World Action Modeling with Representation Visual-Action Tokenizers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13674)
*Junke Wang,Qihang Zhang,Shuai Yang,Yiming Luo,Yujun Shen,Zuxuan Wu,Yu-Gang Jiang,Yinghao Xu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: RepWAM introduces a representation-centric world action model that uses a visual-action tokenizer to learn semantic latent actions, enabling better instruction-following control and closed-loop manipulation compared to reconstruction-based tokenizers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reconstruction-oriented video tokenizers preserve pixel fidelity but provide weak supervision for learning control-oriented dynamics; there is a need for semantic representations that align visuals with latent actions to connect future prediction with robot manipulation.

Method: Train a representation visual-action tokenizer mapping visuals to aligned visual and latent-action tokens; pretrain a WAM to jointly predict future visuals and latent actions under language instructions; adapt to real robot trajectories for closed-loop manipulation.

Result: On real-world manipulation tasks and simulation benchmarks, RepWAM achieves strong performance; ablations show semantic visual-action tokenization outperforms reconstruction-oriented alternatives; supports tokenization as a foundation for world action models; code release planned.

Conclusion: Semantic visual-action tokenization is a promising foundation for world action models and a step toward generalist robot policies; future work may extend to broader tasks and domains.

Abstract: This work presents RepWAM, a representation-centric world action model (WAM) built on representation visual-action tokenizers. Existing WAMs typically inherit reconstruction-oriented video tokenizers from pretrained video generation models. Although these tokenizers preserve visual fidelity, pixel reconstruction alone provides limited guidance for learning instruction-following dynamics that connect future prediction with robot control. To address this, we explore a semantic visual-action latent space for representation-centric world action modeling. Specifically, we train a representation visual-action tokenizer that maps visual inputs into aligned visual and latent action tokens. We then pretrain our WAM to jointly model future visual states and the latent actions that connect them under language instructions, followed by adaptation to real robot trajectories for closed-loop manipulation. Experiments on real-world manipulation tasks and simulation benchmarks show that RepWAM delivers strong performance across diverse manipulation settings, while ablations highlight the value of semantic visual-action tokenization over reconstruction-oriented alternatives. These results establish representation visual-action tokenization as a promising foundation for world action models and a step toward generalist robot policies. Code and weights will be available at https://github.com/wdrink/RepWAM.

</details>


### [75] [Modality Forcing for Scalable Spatial Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13676)
*Bardienus Pieter Duisterhof,Deva Ramanan,Jeffrey Ichnowski,Justin Johnson,Keunhong Park*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A scalable post-training recipe (Modality Forcing) enables joint image-depth generation using a single diffusion model with per-modality noise and decoders, trained on sparse depth data, achieving competitive monocular depth and improved joint depth generation as model size grows.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable accurate depth perception and spatial understanding within text-to-image generation without requiring dense depth data or complex pipelines, by leveraging the scalable pre-training of vision-language diffusion models.

Method: Post-training framework that injects per-modality noise levels and separate decoders to a single DiT, enabling conditional and joint image-depth generation in any modality permutation. Trains on sparse, real-world depth data and can be scaled by training larger T2I models from scratch (370M to 3.3B params).

Result: Stronger depth prediction from joint image-depth generation; AbsRel improvement of 57% relative to existing joint models; strongest model competitive with state-of-the-art monocular depth estimators; depth benefits from larger models and more image data; demonstrates scalability of image generation as a pre-training objective for spatial perception.

Conclusion: Image generation with diffusion models can serve as a scalable pre-training objective for spatial perception tasks like depth estimation, enabling effective joint generation and conditional control with simple post-training adaptations.

Abstract: Text-to-image (T2I) models contain rich spatial priors. Synthesizing photorealistic, cluttered scenes requires an understanding of geometry, including perspective and relative scale. Prior works adapt T2I models to leverage this prior for depth prediction, but they require dense depth data and involve complex recipes. We propose Modality Forcing, a simple, scalable post-training recipe for joint image-depth generation using a single DiT trained on sparse depth data. Modality Forcing enables conditional and joint generation of image and depth in any permutation by assigning separate noise levels per modality. Per-modality decoders let us train on sparse, real-world depth and achieve strong, generalizable depth prediction. We further show that Modality Forcing inherits the scalability of T2I pre-training: by training a set of T2I models from scratch (370M to 3.3B parameters), we find that larger models trained on more image data produce more accurate depth. Our strongest model is competitive with state-of-the-art monocular depth estimators and reduces AbsRel by 57% relative to existing joint image-depth generative models. These results provide strong evidence that image generation is a scalable pre-training objective for spatial perception. https://modality-forcing.github.io/

</details>


### [76] [InterleaveThinker: Reinforcing Agentic Interleaved Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13679)
*Dian Zheng,Harry Lee,Manyuan Zhang,Kaituo Feng,Zoey Guo,Ray Zhang,Hongsheng Li*

Main category: cs.CV
