TL;DR: InterleaveThinker introduces a two-agent pipeline (planner and critic) to add interleaved text-image generation to existing image generators, enabling sequential visual narratives and guided manipulation. It uses SFT-based warm-start datasets and RL fine-tuning with single-step rewards to optimize multi-step generation, achieving competitive results with domain-specific baselines and improving reasoning benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current image generators and Unified Multimodal Models struggle with interleaved generation over image-text sequences, limiting capabilities for visual narratives and embodied manipulation. A modular, model-agnostic approach is needed to enable this capability in existing generators.

Method: A multi-agent pipeline: a planner agent organizes the text-image input sequence and instructs the generator at each step; a critic agent evaluates outputs against the plan and refines instructions. Training uses Interleave-Planner-SFT-80k and Interleave-Critic-SFT-112k for cold-start formatting, and Interleave-Critic-RL-13k with GRPO for step-wise instruction correction. Since trajectories can involve >25 generator calls, the design employs accuracy reward and step-wise reward for single-step RL to influence the full trajectory.

Result: Across multiple image generators and interleaved benchmarks, InterleaveThinker improves performance, with results approaching Nano Banana and GPT-5 on interleaved tasks. It also enhances base reasoning benchmarks (e.g., 4-step FLUX.2-klein, improving WISE and RISE).

Conclusion: The InterleaveThinker framework provides a modular, scalable solution to endow existing image generators with interleaved generation capabilities, delivering competitive interleaved performance and auxiliary gains in reasoning tasks.

Abstract: Recent image generators have demonstrated impressive photorealism and instruction-following capabilities in single-image generation and editing. However, constrained by their architectures, they cannot achieve interleaved generation (text-image sequence), which has crucial applications in visual narratives, guidance, and embodied manipulation. Even the latest open-source Unified Multimodal Models (UMMs) exhibit limited performance in this regard. In this paper, we introduce InterleaveThinker, the first multi-agent pipeline designed to endow any existing image generator with interleaved generation capabilities. Specifically, we employ a planner agent to organize the image-text input sequence, instructing the image generator on the required execution at each step. Subsequently, we introduce a critic agent to evaluate the generator's outputs, identify samples that deviate from the planned instructions, and refine the instructions for regeneration. To implement this pipeline, we construct the Interleave-Planner-SFT-80k and Interleave-Critic-SFT-112k to perform a format cold-start. Then we develop Interleave-Critic-RL-13k to reinforce the step-wise instruction correction capability within a generation trajectory using GRPO. Since a single interleaved generation trajectory may involve over 25 generator calls, optimizing the entire trajectory is computationally impractical. Therefore, we propose accuracy reward and step-wise reward, allowing single-step RL to effectively guide the entire generation trajectory. The results show that InterleaveThinker improves performance across various image generators. On interleaved generation benchmarks, it achieves performance comparable to Nano Banana and GPT-5. Surprisingly, it also significantly enhances the base model on reasoning-based benchmarks; for example, on 4-step FLUX.2-klein, we observe substantial gains on WISE and RISE.

</details>


<div id='cs.LG'></div>

# cs.LG [[Back]](#toc)

### [77] [Quickest Detection of Hallucination Onset: Delay Bounds and Learned CUSUM Statistics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12476)
*Igor Itkin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Viewing token-level hallucination detection as a quickest change detection problem reveals a fundamental delay in detection not captured by token-AUC metrics. A causal recurrent labeler approximates a CUSUM with learned increments, achieving faster detection than token-wise baselines, but a theoretical information-rate bound explains residual gaps; sequential analysis exposes delay structure obscured by classification metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Token-level AUC is insufficient for streaming monitoring of hallucinations. The paper reframes onset detection as a change-point problem to quantify reaction delay under a false-alarm constraint and to assess how close learned scoring approaches approach information-theoretic limits.

Method: 1) A first-order Markov model of latent faithful/hallucinated state (validated on RAGTruth) to cast onset detection as a change-point problem; 2) apply Lorden’s lower bound to obtain a delay bound (~1.3 tokens at FAR=0.01); 3) show a causal recurrent labeler acts like a CUSUM with learned increment; compare detection delay to a linear per-token baseline; 4) decompose performance to identify the source of advantage; 5) prove an information-rate optimality (Donsker-Varadhan style) bound explaining remaining gap.

Result: - Lorden’s lower bound yields about 1.3 tokens delay at FAR 0.01. - The causal recurrent labeler detects in 11–13 tokens; linear baseline ≈ 31 tokens. - Most of the gain comes from improved per-token scoring, not temporal accumulation. - An information-rate theorem explains the remaining gap: the learned score captures only ~1/4.5 of the divergence carried by features; recalibration cannot close the gap; finite-horizon effects also contribute.

Conclusion: Classification metrics like AUC conceal the delay; sequential, change-detection analysis makes the delay measurable and highlights the limits of learned scores under information-rate constraints. This work stresses the importance of evaluating streaming detectors with reaction-time metrics alongside traditional classifier metrics.

Abstract: Token-level hallucination detectors are evaluated as classifiers, by AUC over all tokens, yet a streaming monitor is judged by its reaction time: the number of tokens that pass between the onset of a hallucination and the alarm. We formulate hallucination onset detection as a quickest change detection problem. A first-order Markov model of the latent faithful/hallucinated state, validated on RAGTruth, places the task inside classical change-point theory and yields Lorden's lower bound on detection delay: about 1.3 tokens at a false-alarm rate of 0.01. We then show that a causal recurrent labeler acts as a CUSUM with a learned increment; at a matched false-alarm rate it detects in 11-13 tokens, against 31 for a linear per-token baseline, and a controlled decomposition attributes most of this advantage to a better per-token score rather than to temporal accumulation. An information-rate optimality theorem of Donsker-Varadhan type explains the remaining order-of-magnitude gap: the learned score realizes only 1/4.5 of the divergence the features carry, a deficit that recalibration cannot remove, with the remainder a finite-horizon effect. Classification metrics conceal this delay structure; sequential analysis makes it measurable

</details>


### [78] [Boltzmann Attention: Learnable Ising Couplings for Cooperative Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12478)
*Gilhan Kim,Daniel K. Park*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Boltzmann attention: an energy-based attention that adds learnable pairwise couplings (Ising model) to capture inter-position interactions beyond softmax; yields consistent improvements, especially for longer sequences, and can be trained via diabatic quantum annealing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard attention relies on individual query–key similarities and softmax, lacking explicit modeling of cooperative/antagonistic inter-position interactions. This limits modeling of co-attention structures within the attention mechanism.

Method: Augments the usual data-dependent local fields with learnable pairwise couplings in an Ising energy model to govern attention patterns. Uses Boltzmann distribution for attention; evaluates on character-level language modeling and synthetic bracket matching; includes a four-way ablation to isolate impact of couplings; explores diabatic quantum annealing for training.

Result: Boltzmann attention consistently improves over standard softmax attention in a Transformer across tasks, with larger gains for longer sequences. Four-way ablation attributes improvement to the learnable pairwise couplings. Ising formulation enables practical training via diabatic quantum annealing while maintaining competitive performance with exact Boltzmann computation.

Conclusion: Explicit inter-position interactions enhance attention-based sequence modeling. The Ising formulation provides a principled, potentially quantum-sampling-friendly extension of attention that scales with sequence length and offers a pathway to alternative training strategies.

Abstract: Attention mechanisms are central to modern sequence models, yet standard attention computes relevance primarily through individual query--key similarities. Although softmax normalization introduces competition among positions, a standard attention layer does not explicitly parameterize learnable interactions between attention decisions. This limits its ability to directly model cooperative or antagonistic co-attention structure within the attention mechanism itself. We propose Boltzmann attention, an energy-based generalization in which attention patterns are governed by an interacting Ising model. The method augments the usual data-dependent local fields with learnable pairwise couplings, allowing the model to represent inter-position correlations beyond those captured by softmax or sigmoid attention. Experiments on character-level language modeling and synthetic bracket matching show that Boltzmann attention consistently improves over standard softmax attention within a standard Transformer architecture, with the advantage becoming more pronounced as sequence length increases. A four-way ablation confirms that the improvement arises from the learnable pairwise couplings. These results suggest that explicit inter-position interactions provide a principled enhancement for attention-based sequence modeling. Moreover, the Ising formulation opens a natural path toward quantum-computing-based sampling strategies: we demonstrate that diabatic quantum annealing provides a practical training method while maintaining competitive performance with exact Boltzmann computation.

</details>


### [79] [ReCal: Reward Calibration for RL-based LLM Routing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12479)
*Qihang Yu,Hanwen Tong,Zhengqi Zhang,Bo Zheng,Feng Wei,Shengyu Zhang,Zemin Liu,Fei Wu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ReCal is a reward-calibration framework for RL-based LLM routing that decomposes rewards hierarchically and uses variance-aware optimization to stabilize learning across heterogeneous tasks, improving routing performance and training stability across seven datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current RL-based LLM routing aggregates multiple objectives into a single scalar reward, causing ambiguous credit assignment, and exhibits high reward variance across instances, risking optimization bias toward trivial samples.

Method: Introduce a hierarchical reward decomposition with component-wise advantage estimation, and a distribution-aware optimization strategy featuring variance-aware reweighting and per-dataset normalization.

Result: Empirical evaluation on seven datasets shows that ReCal consistently improves routing performance and training stability over baselines.

Conclusion: ReCal effectively calibrates rewards for RL-based LLM routing, yielding more reliable improvement across diverse tasks; code is available.

Abstract: Large language model (LLM) routing has emerged as an effective paradigm for leveraging the complementary strengths of multiple LLMs through dynamic model and reasoning-strategy selection. Recent reinforcement learning (RL)-based routing methods further improve routing quality by optimizing routing policies from interaction feedback. However, they still struggle to provide informative and comparable learning signals under heterogeneous tasks with varying difficulty. In practice, multiple objectives (e.g., correctness, format behavior) are aggregated into a single scalar reward, leading to ambiguous credit assignment and conflicting optimization signals. Moreover, reward signals exhibit significant variability across instances, where some instances produce higher or more variable rewards, introducing optimization bias that favors trivial samples over informative ones. To address these issues, we propose \textbf{ReCal}, a \textbf{\underline{Re}}ward \textbf{\underline{Cal}}ibration framework for RL-based LLM routing. We first introduce a hierarchical reward decomposition mechanism with component-wise advantage estimation. We further propose a distribution-aware optimization strategy that calibrates optimization variability through variance-aware reweighting and per-dataset normalization. Experiments on seven datasets demonstrate that ReCal consistently improves routing performance, and training stability over baselines. Code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/ReCal.

</details>


### [80] [Representing Time Series as Structured Programs for LLM Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12481)
*Jaeho Kim,Changhun Oh,Seokhyun Lee,Irina Rish,Changhee Lee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A training-free Time-Series-to-Structured-Program (T2SP) representation converts time series into a deterministic symbolic program, enabling LLMs to reason about time-series data efficiently by offloading temporal-structure extraction to the representation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs struggle with time-series due to modality mismatch; existing approaches either serialize numbers or fine-tune models, which add overhead or degrade long-sequence reasoning. A representation aligned with textual/code modalities can leverage LLM reasoning more effectively.

Method: Propose T2SP: decomposes time series into trends, periods, salient events; expresses as a structured program in a deterministic, training-free way; offloads temporal-structure extraction to the representation; compatible with off-the-shelf LLMs.

Result: Empirical evaluation on three reasoning tasks (editing, captioning, question answering) shows T2SP improves performance, reduces reasoning time, and lowers failure rates relative to raw-string representations.

Conclusion: T2SP provides an effective interface between time series and LLMs, enabling robust, efficient time-series reasoning without model fine-tuning.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated strong reasoning and instruction-following capabilities, making them potentially powerful tools for time-series analysis. However, time series lie outside their native textual modality, raising a fundamental question: how should time series be represented so that LLMs can reason about them effectively? Existing work typically serializes raw numerical sequences or fine-tunes pre-trained LLMs on time-series data. These approaches place the burden of extracting temporal structure directly on the LLM, creating a modality mismatch that often degrades performance on long sequences and introduces substantial computational overhead. In this work, we introduce Time-Series-to-Structured-Program representation (T2SP), a deterministic, training-free method that represents a time series as a structured symbolic program. T2SP decomposes time series into trends, periods, and salient events, expressing them in a program-friendly format aligned with the textual and code-like modalities on which LLMs are natively trained. By shifting temporal-structure extraction from the model to the representation itself, T2SP enables off-the-shelf LLMs to leverage their existing reasoning capabilities for time-series understanding. We evaluate T2SP on three reasoning tasks -- editing, captioning, and question answering -- where it consistently improves performance, reduces reasoning time, and lowers failure rates compared with raw-string representations. Our results demonstrate that T2SP provides an effective interface between time series and LLMs.

</details>


### [81] [Scalable anomaly detection via a univariate Christoffel function](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12483)
*Florian Grivet,Didier Henrion,Jean-Bernard Lasserre,Louise Travé-Massuyès*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Univariate Christoffel-based anomaly detector (UCF) to overcome Christoffel function's dimensionality bottleneck; uses squared distance to support points; claims better average precision on ADBench and retains key theoretical properties.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mitigate exponential-in-dimension matrix inversion required by multivariate Christoffel function-based anomaly detectors while keeping their theoretical guarantees (on-off support and accurate support shape).

Method: Introduce UCF, a univariate Christoffel function built on squared distance to the support points. Evaluate on the ADBench anomaly detection benchmark and compare against 14 state-of-the-art baselines.

Result: UCF consistently outperforms 14 baselines in Average Precision on ADBench, addressing scalability while preserving core theoretical properties of Christoffel-based methods.

Conclusion: By resolving the scalability bottleneck, UCF broadens the applicability of Christoffel-function anomaly detection with a robust, theoretically grounded approach.

Abstract: Anomaly detection plays a critical role in identifying unusual patterns across domains such as fraud detection, network intrusion, and system fault diagnosis. Recently, Christoffel function-based methods, rooted in polynomial optimization, have emerged as promising alternatives to deep learning due to their strong mathematical foundations and computational frugality. However, their practical applicability is hindered by the need to invert a matrix whose size grows exponentially with the data dimension, rendering the method intractable even for moderate-dimensional datasets. This paper addresses the dimensionality limitations of Christoffel function-based anomaly detection while preserving its key theoretical properties, i.e., the on-off support dichotomy behavior and the accurate support shape capture. We introduce UCF, a univariate Christoffel function which is based on the squared distance between the query point and the support points. Extensive experiments on the ADBench benchmark demonstrate that UCF consistently outperforms 14 state-of-the-art baselines in terms of Average Precision. By resolving the scalability bottleneck of the Christoffel Function, this work expands the toolkit of anomaly detection methods with a robust, theoretically grounded, and universally applicable approach.

</details>


### [82] [Speculative Rollback Correction for Quality-Diverse Web Agent Imitation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12485)
*Longkun Hao,Hongyu Lin,Hao Li,Zhichao Yang,Haojie Hao,Dongshuo Huang,Haitao Yang,Hongyu Ge,Ming jie Xie,Yanjun Wu,Zi Hao Yin,Yan Bai,Yihang Lou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Speculative Rollback Correction (SRC) is a branch-level imitation framework for resettable environments that uses fixed-horizon speculative rollouts, verifier-based filtering, and a quality-diversity trajectory archive to improve data efficiency and the recovery-versus-query tradeoff in interactive imitation learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In interactive imitation learning for web agents, choosing when to intervene with expert input is critical. Delayed interventions allow error accumulation; premature or excessive intervention causes overreliance on expert policies and convergence to a single rigid trajectory.

Method: SRC performs fixed-horizon branch review: the student runs a short speculative segment, then the teacher localizes the first harmful deviation when local progress breaks. Rollback preserves useful prefixes; a hard verifier filters successful rollouts; verifier-passing trajectories are stored in a lightweight quality-diversity archive. Next-action supervised fine-tuning is performed on localized corrections and verifier-passing trajectories.

Result: Applied to WebArena-Infinity, SRC yielded 977 verifier-passing trajectories and 9,183 next-action examples. Fixed-horizon review improved the recovery-versus-query tradeoff relative to step-level review while preserving verifier-passing solution variants.

Conclusion: SRC provides a robust, data-efficient framework for interactive imitation learning by balancing speculation, rollback, and verifier-based filtering, enabling diverse, recoverable trajectories and effective next-action supervision; code is available.

Abstract: Training interactive web agents through imitation learning from expert trajectories has emerged as a highly effective approach. However, determining the optimal timing for expert intervention presents a critical challenge in this context. Delayed intervention often leads to the accumulation of early-stage errors, pushing the page state into an irrecoverable regime. Conversely, premature or excessive intervention causes the agent to become overly reliant on expert policies, trapping the model in local optima characterized by a single, rigid trajectory. We propose Speculative Rollback Correction (SRC), a branch-level imitation framework for resettable agent environments. Instead of requesting teacher labels at every visited state or correcting only after a completed trajectory, SRC uses fixed-horizon branch review: the student executes a short speculative segment before teacher review, and the teacher localizes the first harmful deviation only when local progress breaks. Rollback preserves useful prefixes, while successful rollouts are filtered by a hard verifier and retained in a lightweight quality-diversity archive. The resulting data supports next-action supervised fine-tuning on both localized corrections and verifier-passing trajectories. On WebArena-Infinity, SRC collects 977 verifier-passing trajectories and 9,183 next-action examples; fixed-horizon review improves the recovery-versus-query tradeoff over step-level review while retaining verifier-passing solution variants. Code is available at https://github.com/LongkunHao/SRC_gui_agent.

</details>


### [83] [An Empirical Study on Predictive Maintenance for Component X in Heavy-Duty Scania Trucks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12486)
*Valeriu Dimidov,Sasan Jafarnejad,Raphaël Frank*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A condition-based predictive maintenance approach for trucks converts wear-related time series into recent-tabular data for ML classification, assuming monotonic wear, yielding cost reductions on a Scania dataset and leveraging AutoML to simplify modeling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the data throughput and detection challenges in condition-based PdM for large truck fleets by focusing on the most recent wear observations and enabling scalable, autoML-driven modeling.

Method: Assume wear state is monotonically non-decreasing. Select the most recent observations from the wear time series, convert them into a tabular format, and apply ML models designed for tabular data, with AutoML aiding model selection/training.

Result: The proposed method reduces costs on the Scania Component X dataset compared to current SOTA approaches.

Conclusion: A lightweight, AutoML-enabled, recent-observation-based PdM pipeline can achieve cost savings with simplified modeling, though the efficacy depends on the monotonic wear assumption and data characteristics.

Abstract: Condition-based Predictive Maintenance (PdM) for truck fleets has gained momentum in recent years. This maintenance strategy aims to minimize unplanned downtimes and reduce costs by monitoring the health status of vehicles and taking proactive action based on their condition. However, the implementation of condition-based PdM systems is challenging due to the large volume of data generated by the trucks, the inherent complexity of detecting failures through sensor data and the difficulties in finding cost-effective trade-offs in the solution's implementation. In this paper, we define and validate a condition-based PdM methodology built on the assumption that the wear-and-tear state of the monitored component can be represented as a monotonically non-decreasing time series. It involves selecting only the most recent observations from the time series and transforming them into a tabular format for classification using machine learning (ML) models designed for tabular data. Our results indicate that the proposed methodology reduces costs on the Scania Component X dataset compared to current state-of-the-art (SOTA) approaches, while also simplifying the modeling process through AutoML.

</details>


### [84] [DynamicPTQ: Mitigating Activation Quantization Collapse via Residual-Stream Dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12487)
*Zimo Zhao,Maolin Wang,Bowen Yu,Bowen Liu,Xiao Han,Xiangyu Zhao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DynamicPTQ presents a phase-aware dynamic post-training quantization for LLMs, selectively using 8-bit activations on quantization-sensitive residual layers while keeping 4-bit elsewhere, improving accuracy and throughput under W4A4KV4 with modest memory cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To tackle quantization instability in 4-bit PTQ for LLMs caused by massive activations and cross-layer residual changes that static transformation-based smoothing cannot handle.

Method: Analyze residual-stream dynamics using Jump Ratio and Historical Feature SNR to identify quantization-sensitive layers; apply 8-bit activation quantization to those layers only, keep weights, KV caches, and other activations at 4-bit; integrate with strong PTQ baselines such as QuaRot, SpinQuant, and FlatQuant; validated on LLaMA-2/3 under W4A4KV4.

Result: DynamicPTQ consistently improves perplexity and zero-shot QA performance on LLaMA-2/3 under W4A4KV4; yields 1.05–1.07x throughput gains with modest memory overhead.

Conclusion: Phase-aware dynamic activation quantization offers a practical and effective route for robust low-bit LLM inference, outperforming static smoothing approaches by addressing cross-layer residual dynamics.

Abstract: Post-training quantization (PTQ) is essential for efficient large language model inference, but reliably quantizing activations remains challenging when weights, activations, and KV caches are all quantized to 4-bit precision. A key difficulty lies in massive activations, whose extreme values dominate the activation range and amplify quantization errors. State-of-the-art methods mainly mitigate massive activations through transformation-based smoothing, such as orthogonal rotations and affine scaling, but overlook the cross-layer dynamics of the residual stream. In this paper, we show that massive activations emerge and disappear in a phase-wise pattern across network depth, triggering large residual changes. These changes cause newly injected layer-wise updates to dominate the 4-bit quantization scale and weaken historical residual information. To characterize this behavior, we introduce Jump Ratio and Historical Feature SNR. This suggests that static transformation-based smoothing cannot fully resolve dynamic quantization instability caused by cross-layer residual changes. Based on this analysis, we propose DynamicPTQ, a Dynamic Post-Training Quantization policy for phase-aware mixed-precision activation quantization. DynamicPTQ identifies quantization-sensitive layers from residual-stream dynamics and assigns 8-bit activation precision only to these layers, while keeping weights, KV caches, and other activations in 4-bit precision. It can be directly integrated with strong PTQ baselines such as QuaRot, SpinQuant, and FlatQuant. Experiments on LLaMA-2 and LLaMA-3 show that DynamicPTQ consistently improves perplexity and zero-shot QA performance under W4A4KV4 quantization, while achieving 1.05 to 1.07 times throughput improvement with modest memory overhead. These results demonstrate a practical path toward robust low-bit LLM inference.

</details>


### [85] [A Stationary (and Therefore Compatible) Representation is All You Need](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12488)
*Niccolò Biondi,Federico Pernici,Simone Ricci,Alberto Del Bimbo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Stationary representations from d-Simplex fixed classifiers guarantee compatibility during sequential model updates; a convex combination of cross-entropy and a contrastive loss captures higher-order dependencies and remains equivalent to cross-entropy under compatibility constraints; empirical results show uninterrupted retrieval and improved performance during updates and replacements; code is released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable continuous model lifecycle updates (fine-tuning and replacements) without reprocessing large gallery data, by ensuring representations stay compatible over time.

Method: Train with a d-Simplex fixed classifier using cross-entropy to align first-order statistics. To capture higher-order dependencies, blend cross-entropy with a contrastive loss in a convex combination. Prove the blend is equivalent to cross-entropy under compatibility constraints. Validate via extensive experiments, including sequential fine-tuning and scenarios where a pre-trained model is replaced by an improved one.

Result: Demonstrates that stationary representations under a d-Simplex fixed classifier achieve compatibility. The convex-combo objective captures higher-order relations and remains compatible, yielding improved retrieval performance and enabling uninterrupted service during model updates or replacements; results reach state-of-the-art.

Conclusion: d-Simplex fixed classifiers provide a principled foundation for compatible representations in evolving models. The proposed training loss can be directly used in practice and supports scenarios with sequential fine-tuning and occasional upgrades; the approach opens avenues for robust, update-friendly deployment.

Abstract: Learning compatible representations aims to learn feature representations that can be used interchangeably over time whenever a model undergoes updates. In this paper, we demonstrate that stationary representations learned by d-Simplex fixed classifiers imply compatibility as in its formal definition. This result establishes a foundation for future works and can be directly exploited in practical learning scenarios. We address the challenge of learning compatibility using $d$-Simplex fixed classifiers when the model is sequentially fine-tuned. Learning according to a d-Simplex fixed classifier with the cross-entropy loss aligns feature distributions at the first-order statistics. Consequently, it may not fully capture higher-order dependencies in the representation between model updates. To address this issue, we demonstrate that training the model using a $d$-Simplex fixed classifier through a convex combination of the cross-entropy loss and a contrastive loss not only captures higher-order dependencies, but is also equivalent to learning with the cross-entropy under the compatibility constraints. We confirm our findings with extensive experiments also considering a new scenario where a pre-trained model is sequentially fine-tuned and occasionally replaced with an improved model. We show that stationary representations enable uninterrupted retrieval services (without reprocessing gallery images) while improving performance during model updates and replacements, achieving state-of-the-art. Code at https://github.com/miccunifi/iamcl2r.

</details>


### [86] [Robustness Verification of Recurrent Neural Networks with Abstraction Refinement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12490)
*Li-Jen Lin,Chih-Duo Hong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: An abstraction-refinement framework for RNNs that partitions activation intervals to remove dominant relaxation error; on refined branches, ReLU is exact and tanh/sigmoid have tighter envelopes; SHAP-guided timestep selection targets only the most critical timesteps to control combinatorial cost; shows improved verification success and robustness margins on CIFAR10 and MNIST stroke with trade-offs between ReLU and tanh models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robustness verification for RNNs is hindered by error accumulation from nonlinear relaxations across time, making scalable linear bound propagation overly conservative and often unable to certify truly robust inputs.

Method: Partition activation intervals that cross zero to remove dominant relaxation error (abstraction-refinement). On each refined branch, ReLU becomes exact; use tighter linear envelopes for smooth activations (tanh, sigmoid). To control combinatorial growth for long sequences, apply a SHAP-guided timestep selection that ranks hidden states by their contribution to the verification objective and refines only the most critical timesteps in temporal order.

Result: Empirical evaluation on CIFAR10 and MNIST stroke benchmarks shows consistent improvements in verification success and robustness-margin tightness over abstraction-only baselines; reveals runtime trade-offs between ReLU and tanh models.

Conclusion: Abstraction-refinement significantly improves local robustness verification for RNNs by reducing relaxation error through targeted interval partitioning and activation-specific envelopes; SHAP-guided timestep refinement yields a scalable approach by focusing on the most influential timesteps, though activation choice (ReLU vs tanh) affects runtime and accuracy trade-offs.

Abstract: Certified local robustness verification for recurrent neural networks (RNNs) is challenging because approximation errors introduced by nonlinear relaxations can propagate through recurrent connections and accumulate over time. As a result, scalable linear bound propagation methods often become overly conservative and fail to certify inputs that are in fact robust, especially when many pre-activation intervals cross zero. We propose an abstraction-refinement framework for RNN verification that partitions such intervals to remove the dominant relaxation error: on each refined branch, ReLU becomes exact, and smooth activations such as tanh and sigmoid admit substantially tighter linear envelopes. To control the combinatorial cost of splitting in long sequences, we introduce a SHAP-guided timestep selection strategy that ranks hidden states by their contribution to the verification objective and refines only the most critical timesteps in temporal order. Experiments on CIFAR10 and MNIST stroke benchmarks demonstrate consistent improvements in verification success and robustness-margin tightness over abstraction-only baselines, while exposing clear runtime trade-offs between ReLU and tanh models.

</details>


### [87] [Net-Ev$^2$: A Generative Simulator for Network Event Evolution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12494)
*Guangyu Wang,Zhaonan Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Net-Ev2 is a two-stage generative simulator for network-event evolution that preserves topology, supports language-only inputs, and introduces a new multimodal benchmark (Net-Ev2-6.5M) and a topology-aware metric (JL-MMD), reporting SOTA performance and strong generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Decision-support in real-world networks requires simulating how disturbance events propagate while respecting network topology and event semantics. Current methods struggle to jointly model structured network attributes and unstructured event semantics, hindering realistic, scalable simulations.

Method: Two-stage framework: (1) structure-guided masked pre-training to align event cues with network structure; (2) topology-aware diffusion using a U-Net-like graph downsampling/upsampling during denoising. Inference accepts natural-language event input. Introduces Net-Ev2-6.5M benchmark across four large road networks and a topology-aware JL-MMD metric for evaluating topological fidelity.

Result: Empirical evaluation shows state-of-the-art performance and strong generalization across networks and events. Code is released for reproducibility.

Conclusion: Net-Ev2 provides a flexible and effective generative simulator for network-event evolution that fuses semantic event cues with topology-aware dynamics, supported by a new multimodal benchmark and a topology-focused evaluation metric.

Abstract: Reducing real-world trial and error has long been a central goal of decision making, and generative simulators advance this goal by modeling the evolution of future states. An even more challenging yet meaningful task is simulating how disturbance events (e.g., accidents) propagate their impacts across real-world networks. The existing approaches fall short of modeling both structured attributes and unstructured semantics of events, and capturing topological structures in simulating network event evolution. Therefore, we are motivated to propose Net-Ev$^2$ ($\underline{\textbf{Net}}$work $\underline{\textbf{Ev}}$ent $\underline{\textbf{Ev}}$olution), a novel generative simulator that jointly leverages event cues while preserving network topology in simulations. Specifically, the framework consists of two stages, namely structure-guided masked pre-training and topology-aware diffusion process, which is achieved by U-Net-like graph downsampling and upsampling during denoising. At inference time, Net-Ev$^2$ can generate simulations using natural-language event input only, with greater flexibility for practical usage. Furthermore, we introduce Net-Ev$^2$-6.5M, a multimodal benchmark of aligned event and network traffic data across four large-scale road networks, as well as a new topology-aware metric, namely JL-MMD, to evaluate topological fidelity in generated network dynamics. Extensive experiments demonstrate the state-of-the-art performance and strong generalization ability of Net-Ev$^2$. Code is made available at https://github.com/Guangyu4/Net-Ev-2.

</details>


### [88] [$μ$VLA: On Recurrent Memory for Partially Observable Manipulation in VLA Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12497)
*Egor Cherepanov,Nikita Kachaev,Daniil Zelezetsky,Aydar Bulatov,Artem Pshenitsyn,Yuri Kuratov,Alexey Skrynnik,Aleksandr I. Panov,Alexey K. Kovalev*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Minimal recurrence via learnable memory tokens in VLA backbones improves performance under partial observability, isolating recurrence from other memory-related components.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To isolate the contribution of recurrence in vision-language-action models, disentangled from other memory/retrieval components.

Method: Augment transformer with a small set of memory tokens carried across timesteps, updated via self-attention; train end-to-end with truncated BPTT; no auxiliary losses or architectural changes; μVLA variants with memory width m, TBPTT length K, and two memory update rules (cross-step gradient or detached EMA).

Result: On MIKASA-Robo: training tasks average success up from 0.42 to 0.84; held-out tasks 0.23 vs 0.07 baseline. On LIBERO: 96.2% average success with strongest recurrence; no regression under full observability. For tasks requiring different memory structure, performance near baseline.

Conclusion: Results delineate the regime where minimal intrac-backbone recurrence suffices and where additional memory structures are necessary; provide a calibration of the recurrence capability envelope for VLA models.

Abstract: Vision-language-action (VLA) models predict chunks of future actions from the current observation, an assumption that fails under partial observability, where decisions depend on information no longer visible. Existing memory-augmented VLAs simultaneously introduce recurrence, retrieval, compression modules, auxiliary objectives, hierarchical memory, or task-specific architectural changes, so the contribution of recurrence itself remains entangled with surrounding machinery. We present a controlled isolation study of recurrence in a strong pretrained VLA backbone. Our formulation augments the transformer with a small set of learnable memory tokens carried across timesteps and updated through self-attention, trained end to end with truncated backpropagation through time, with no auxiliary losses and no architectural changes. We instantiate this as $μ$VLA, a family of OpenVLA-OFT variants parameterized by memory width m, TBPTT length K, and the memory update rule (cross-step gradients or a detached EMA), so that recurrence is the only varying factor. On MIKASA-Robo, $μ$VLA improves average success rate on five training tasks from 0.42 to 0.84 at the strongest setting and reaches 0.23 on held-out tasks with the same memory structure versus 0.07 for the memoryless baseline. On tasks requiring different memory structure, performance remains near baseline. On LIBERO, the strongest recurrent variant achieves 96.2% average success, indicating no regression under full observability. We interpret these results as a calibration of the capability envelope of minimal in-backbone recurrence, identifying the regime in which it is sufficient and the regime where additional memory structure is required. Demos and videos can be found in https://avanturist322.github.io/mu-vla/.

</details>


### [89] [Improving Crash Frequency Prediction from Simulated Traffic Conflicts Using Machine Learning Based Microsimulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12500)
*Xian Liu,Carlo G. Prato,Gustav Markkula*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ML-based traffic microsimulation improves crash-frequency prediction from simulated conflicts compared with rule-based models; however, ML-generated crashes are not yet realistic. The approach may reduce location-specific calibration needs but requires broader validation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address limitations of rule-based behavioural models in microsimulation for surrogate safety analysis by leveraging ML models trained on large-scale trajectory data to better capture human driving behavior.

Method: Simulate five real-world signalised intersections in Leeds using both a standard rule-based model and a state-of-the-art ML model within a microsimulation framework. Extract vehicle trajectories and compute 2D Time-to-Collision to identify conflicts. Use Extreme Value Theory to model conflict extremes and predict crash frequency. Also assess crash frequency by directly using ML-generated simulated crashes.

Result: ML-based conflicts yielded crash predictions aligned with observed real crashes, while rule-based predictions failed due to calibration issues. Directly using ML-generated crashes for prediction performed poorly, indicating current ML models realistically reproduce conflicts but not crashes. Overall, ML-based behavior models show promise for improving crash prediction without location-specific calibration.

Conclusion: ML-based driving behavior models are a promising direction for enhancing crash prediction from simulated conflicts; however, they do not yet generate realistic crashes and require further development and validation across diverse sites.

Abstract: Traffic microsimulation combined with surrogate safety measures has increasingly been used as a proactive alternative to historical crash data for predicting crash frequency for current or planned road infrastructure designs. However, existing microsimulation-based safety studies have adopted simplified rule-based behaviour models, which reproduce traffic flow reasonably well but often fail to generate realistic conflict dynamics, limiting crash prediction accuracy. Recent advances in machine learning (ML)-based behaviour models offer a promising opportunity to potentially improve microsimulation realism and crash frequency predictions by learning human driving behaviour directly from large-scale trajectory datasets. To investigate this possibility, traffic microsimulation was conducted for five real-world signalised intersections in Leeds, UK, using both a standard rule-based model and a state-of-the-art ML model. Simulated vehicle trajectories were analysed using a two-dimensional Time-to-Collision metric to identify simulated conflicts, which were then modelled using Extreme Value Theory to predict crash frequency. Results show that conflicts from the ML model yielded crash predictions in line with the real-world crash data, whereas the rule-based model did not permit meaningful predictions, presumably due to a lack of model calibration to the specific simulated intersections. Directly using ML-generated simulated crashes to predict real-world crash frequency also yielded poor results, suggesting that while current ML models can realistically reproduce conflicts, they are not yet able to generate realistic crashes. Overall, the findings demonstrate that ML-based behaviour models are promising for improving crash prediction from simulated conflicts, without a need for location-specific model calibration, and suggest clear future directions for ML-based traffic microsimulation.

</details>


### [90] [Policy-driven Conformal Prediction for Trustworthy QoT Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12501)
*Kiarash Rezaei,Omran Ayoub,Paolo Monti,Carlos Natalino*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Conformal QoT is a policy-driven framework that uses conformal prediction to provide statistically guaranteed QoT estimates and policy-based decision rules, enabling robust lightpath feasibility under domain shift and achieving high accuracy (92% → 99.6%).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Quality-of-Transmission (QoT) estimation in optical networks suffers from domain shift and uncertainty; existing models lack reliable guarantees, risking provisioning errors. A framework that couples rigorous statistical guarantees with practical decision policies can improve reliability under changing conditions.

Method: A policy-driven framework that integrates statistically guaranteed QoT estimation (via conformal prediction) with operational decision policies to select feasible lightpaths under domain shift; evaluated on open datasets showing accuracy gains.

Result: Accuracy improved from 92% to 99.6% on open datasets; provides reliable lightpath-feasibility predictions despite domain shift.

Conclusion: The approach delivers high-probability validity guarantees for QoT estimates and robust provisioning decisions, improving reliability under domain shift and likely generalizable to similar network inference problems.

Abstract: We propose Conformal QoT, a policy-driven framework that combines statistically guaranteed QoT estimation with operational decision policies, enabling reliable lightpath-feasibility predictions under domain shift and improving accuracy from 92\% to 99.6\% on open datasets.

</details>


### [91] [Dolph2Vec: Self-Supervised Representations of Dolphin Vocalizations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12503)
*Chiara Semenzin,Faadil Mustun,Roberto Dessi,Pierre Orhan,Alexis Emanuelli,Yair Lakretz,Gonzalo de Polavieja,German Sumbre*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Large-scale, species-specific SSL model Dolph2Vec trained on a five-year dolphin dataset improves signature whistle classification and whistle detection, revealing interpretable acoustic units and validating SSL as a scientific tool for animal communication.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fill the gap in SSL for intra-species, fine-grained dolphin communication analysis and leverage a longitudinal, multi-dolphin dataset to study unit structure rather than only cross-species generalization.

Method: Adapt the Wav2Vec2.0 architecture to dolphin vocalizations; train Dolph2Vec on a five-year longitudinal dataset from five dolphins in a semi-natural environment; evaluate on signature whistle classification and whistle detection; analyze learned embeddings and codebook for interpretable acoustic units and potential sub-whistle structure.

Result: Dolph2Vec outperforms general-purpose baselines on both tasks; embeddings and codebooks reveal interpretable acoustic units aligned with dolphin whistle categories and possible sub-structure; dataset released for the community.

Conclusion: SSL is a dual-purpose tool: a scalable model for animal vocalization analysis and a scientific instrument enabling fine-grained, hypothesis-driven study of dolphin communication.

Abstract: Self-supervised learning (SSL) has opened new opportunities in bioacoustics by enabling scalable modeling of animal vocalizations without the need for expensive manual annotation. However, current SSL models in this domain prioritize broad generalization across species and are not optimized for uncovering the fine-grained structure of individual communication systems. In this work, we collect and release a novel dataset of over five years of longitudinal recordings, from five known dolphins in a semi-naturalistic marine environment, an unprecedented resource for studying dolphin communication. We adapt the Wav2Vec2.0 Baevski et al. (2020) architecture to this domain and introduce Dolph2Vec, the first large-scale, species-specific SSL model trained exclusively on this data. We benchmark our model on two biologically relevant tasks: signature whistle classification and whistle detection. Dolph2Vec significantly outperforms general-purpose baselines in both tasks. Beyond performance, we show that learned embeddings and codebook structure capture interpretable acoustic units aligned with dolphin whistle categories and possibly sub-whistle structure, enabling fine-grained analysis of communication patterns. Our findings demonstrate how SSL can serve as both a model and a scientific tool to explore hypotheses in animal communication research.

</details>


### [92] [Boosting Direct Preference Optimization with Penalization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12505)
*Pengwei Sun*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DPOP augments DPO with a gated penalty on reference-greedy responses, improving length-controlled offline preference optimization; it outperforms DPO, SimPO, and AlphaDPO on AlpacaEval 2.0 for Llama-3-8b-it and Gemma-2-9b-it, with SimNPO-style length normalization driving key gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To exploit the otherwise unused signal from the reference model's own responses in offline preference optimization and to prevent misalignment when the policy still ranks the reference-greedy option unfavorably; a gated penalty activates only when the current policy underestimates the preferred option relative to the rejected one, encouraging better alignment under length control.

Method: Extend DPO by adding a penalization term on the reference-greedy response that is gated by the condition P_theta(preferred) < P_theta(rejected). The penalty uses a SimNPO-style length-normalized formulation, and is contrasted with NPO and token-level unlikelihood. Evaluations are conducted on AlpacaEval 2.0 across Llama-3-8b-it and Gemma-2-9b-it, comparing against DPO, SimPO, and AlphaDPO.

Result: DPOP yields improvements in length-controlled win rate: +5.3 percentage points for Llama-3-8b-it and +4.4 percentage points for Gemma-2-9b-it over the baselines. Ablations indicate the SimNPO-style length-normalized penalty outperforms NPO and token-level unlikelihood in this setting.

Conclusion: DPOP effectively leverages the reference-response signal via a gated, length-normalized penalty, improving offline preference optimization performance, with the length-normalized penalty emerging as a particularly strong component.

Abstract: Offline preference optimization has become a practical substitute for reinforcement learning from human feedback, but pairwise objectives such as Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) and its variants use only the chosen and rejected responses stored in a static dataset. This leaves a useful signal unused: the response that the reference model itself would generate for the same prompt. We propose Direct Preference Optimization with Penalization (DPOP), a simple extension of DPO that augments the base preference loss with a gated penalty on reference-greedy responses. DPOP activates this penalty only when the current policy still assigns a lower likelihood to the preferred response than to the rejected response. On AlpacaEval 2.0, DPOP improves length-controlled win rate over DPO, SimPO, and AlphaDPO on both Llama-3-8b-it and Gemma-2-9b-it, achieving relative gains of 5.3\% and 4.4\% over baselines on the two models, respectively. Ablations further show that a SimNPO-style length-normalized penalty is stronger than NPO and token-level unlikelihood in this setting.

</details>


### [93] [Rubric-Guided Self-Distillation: Post-Training Without Rubric Verifiers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12507)
*MohammadHossein Rezaei,Anas Mahmoud,Zihao Wang,Utkarsh Tyagi,Advait Gosai,Razvan-Gabriel Dumitru,Aakash Sabharwal,Bing Liu,Yunzhong He*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Rubric-Guided Self-Distillation (RGSD) trains a verifier-free rubric-conditioned teacher to distill into a student token-by-token, yielding dense per-token signals and comparable rubric satisfaction to verifier-based GRPO with zero verifier calls.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Eliminate training-time overhead, verifier bias, and sparse feedback in rubric-based RL in open-ended tasks where ground-truth final answers are unavailable.

Method: Use a rubric-conditioned base policy as the teacher to distill into an unconditioned student, performing token-by-token distillation to replace trajectory-level rewards with dense per-token learning signals and removing the LLM judge from training.

Result: RGSD achieves rubric satisfaction comparable to GRPO across Qwen-2.5 (3B, 7B) and Qwen3-Thinking (4B, 8B) in medical and science domains, using only one on-policy rollout per prompt and no training-time verifier calls. Ablations show rubrics provide stronger teacher signals than self-generated references; in some settings a stronger GRPO judge can outperform RGSD.

Conclusion: RGSD offers a competitive verifier-free alternative when verifier cost or reliability is a bottleneck and can complement verifier-based methods like GRPO.

Abstract: Rubrics have emerged as an alternative to RLVR in open-ended domains where a single ground-truth final answer is not available. Existing rubric-based training methods rely on an LLM verifier that scores each rollout against rubrics. This introduces substantial training-time overhead, exposes optimization to verifier-specific biases, and reduces rubric feedback to a sparse end-of-trajectory signal. We propose Rubric-Guided Self-Distillation (RGSD), a verifier-free training method in which the base policy, conditioned on the rubric, serves as the teacher for the unconditioned student. RGSD distills the rubric-conditioned teacher distribution into the student token-by-token, replacing sparse trajectory-level rewards with dense per-token learning signals and removing the LLM judge from the training loop entirely. Across Qwen-2.5 (3B, 7B) and Qwen3-Thinking (4B, 8B) models on medical and science domains, RGSD achieves rubric satisfaction comparable to judge-based GRPO while using one on-policy rollout per prompt and no training-time verifier calls. Ablations show that raw rubrics provide a stronger teacher enrichment signal than self-generated reference responses, while a stronger GRPO judge can outperform RGSD in some settings, positioning RGSD as a complementary verifier-free alternative when verifier cost or reliability is the bottleneck.

</details>


### [94] [Crossing the Validation Crisis: Cross-Validation Reduces Benchmarking Variance Surprisingly Well](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12552)
*Célestin Eve,Gaël Varoquaux,Thomas Moreau*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Cross-validation substantially reduces benchmarking variance by effectively augmenting data; introduces a 'sample gain' metric to quantify this virtual data increase from multiple CV splits, with diminishing returns and a dynamic early-stop criterion to avoid unnecessary folds.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Benchmarking ML methods is hampered by stochasticity and limited test samples, making performance estimates unreliable. A formal measure of how much CV reduces variance (sample gain) and practical strategies to push CV without excessive cost are needed.

Method: Define sample gain as the variance-reducing effect of adding more CV splits; propose a dynamic early-stopping rule that estimates early, from initial folds, whether future folds will yield substantial gains; conduct experiments on synthetic data and real-world tasks (histopathology images and NLP fine-tuning) to compare different numbers of CV splits and assess reliability of performance estimates.

Result: Using multiple CV splits yields substantially more reliable performance estimates; sample gain captures virtual data augmentation from CV, with gains saturating later than expected; the early-stopping rule can identify when additional folds are unlikely to yield meaningful improvements, reducing computational cost.

Conclusion: Pushing cross-validation on all available samples enhances robust benchmarking. The combination of sample gain and dynamic early stopping offers practical guidance for designing CV-based evaluation protocols in ML, balancing reliability with computational efficiency.

Abstract: Modern machine learning progresses through empirical work, benchmarking new methods to evaluate relative performance. However, the statistical variability inherent to evaluation - exacerbated by the stochastic nature of many algorithms - often makes performance estimation unreliable due to the limited test samples available, leading to a validation crisis in which genuine advances are difficult to discern. In this work, we show that cross-validation improves markedly confidence when evaluating and comparing learning algorithm performances. We introduce the concept of sample gain, which quantifies the virtual data augmentation achieved by using multiple cross-validation splits to reduce benchmarking variance. Experiments on both synthetic and real-world datasets (histopathologic scans and NLP fine-tuning) demonstrate that multiple splits can substantially improve the reliability and stability of performance estimates, with diminishing returns often setting in later than expected. We also introduce a procedure to dynamically early-stop cross-validation by estimating from the first few folds if subsequent folds will bring large sample gains. Our findings highlight the value of pushing cross-validation on available samples to achieve robust and reliable benchmarking.

</details>


### [95] [Emerging Flexible Designs for Geospatial Multimodal Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12595)
*Philipe Dias,Waqwoya Abebe,Abhishek Potnis,Aristeidis Tsaris,Dan Lu,Xiao Wang,Dalton Lunga*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Apples-to-apples comparison of foundation model (FM) architectures for geospatial multimodal reasoning under standardized pretraining, highlighting design trade-offs and offering practical guidance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the lack of fair, cross-architecture evaluation in Earth observation due to architectural diversity (encoder-only, encoder-decoder, MAE) and varied spectral configurations; aim to guide next-generation geospatial FMs.

Method: Standardize pretraining with identical self-supervised objectives and training data; evaluate encoder-only, encoder-decoder, and masked autoencoding models under a fixed parameterization on the GEOBench benchmark for classification and segmentation, including varied spectral band configurations.

Result: Reveals trade-offs between model flexibility, modality alignment, and downstream performance; identifies architectural strengths and limitations under controlled conditions.

Conclusion: Provides practical guidance for building robust multimodal geospatial foundation models and informs architecture choice given task requirements and spectral configurations.

Abstract: Foundation models are rapidly transforming Earth observation by enabling scalable pretraining across diverse unlabeled geospatial modalities. However, their architectural diversity ranging from encoder-only to encoder-decoder and masked autoencoding paradigms makes it challenging to assess performance trade offs in a consistent manner. In this work, we present an apples-to-apples comparison of leading FM architectures designed for geospatial multimodal reasoning, with a particular focus on flexibility across varied spectral band configurations. We standardize pretraining using identical self supervised learning objectives and training datasets, and evaluate all models under consistent parameterization on the GEOBench benchmark across classification and segmentation tasks. Our results offer new insights into the design trade-offs between model flexibility, modality alignment, and downstream task performance. By highlighting architectural strengths and limitations under controlled conditions, this study provides practical guidance for building next generation geospatial foundation models capable of robust multimodal reasoning.

</details>


### [96] [Viral Proteins Reveal Geometry of Protein Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12609)
*Arthur Bigot,Harmon Bhasin,Core Francisco Park,Eugene Shakhnovich,Dianzhuo Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: pLM embeddings reveal a dominant nativeness axis aligned with reconstruction perplexity, ordering sequences by biological authenticity; viral signals persist but vary across families; nativeness is a general structuring principle with family-specific information retained.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how protein language models represent underrepresented biological sequences (viral proteins) and whether embeddings encode a notion of nativeness or authenticity across diverse protein sets.

Method: Empirical analysis across multiple ESM model families, examining embedding space structure, perplexity-aligned axes, and linear separability of viral proteins beyond standard perplexity thresholds and shallow sequence features.

Result: Identified a dominant nativeness axis in embedding space that correlates with masked reconstruction perplexity and orders sequences from cellular proteins to viral proteins to shuffled/random. Scaling contracts this axis unevenly across viral families. Viral proteins remain linearly separable beyond zero-shot perplexity and shallow features, indicating preservation of viral-specific signals within pLM embeddings.

Conclusion: pLM representations are organized by a general notion of nativeness but retain informative structure specific to distinct biological groups (e.g., viral families), suggesting robustness of pLMs to underrepresented sequences and potential for targeted analyses across biological classes.

Abstract: Protein language models are trained on highly imbalanced datasets, raising the question of how they represent underrepresented biological sequences. Using viral proteins as a case study across ESM model families, we identify a dominant nativeness axis in embedding space, aligned with masked reconstruction perplexity, that orders sequences from well-modeled cellular proteins through viral proteins to shuffled and random sequences. Scaling contracts this axis unevenly across viral families. Despite this, protein language model embeddings retain viral-specific signal: viral proteins remain linearly separable beyond zero-shot perplexity and shallow sequence features. Together, these results suggest that pLM representations are structured by a general notion of nativeness while preserving information specific to distinct biological groups.

</details>


### [97] [The Mathematics of AI Winters: The mathematical Taxonomy of Paradigm Fragility in AI Winter](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12610)
*Miquel Noguer i Alonso,David Pacheco Aznar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper argues that AI winters were shaped not only by hype and failure but also by enduring formal bottlenecks in representation, optimization, complexity, learnability, and high-dimensional approximation, evidenced by key theoretical results and later mitigations rather than eliminations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To offer a formalist, complementary explanation for the AI winters by linking historical disappointments to mathematically precise barriers, enriching the traditional engineering/commercial narrative.

Method: Synthetic, cross-disciplinary analysis that maps historical episodes of AI disappointment to established theoretical limits (e.g., perceptron impossibility, hardness of training, minimax rates, vanishing gradients, VC theory) and analyzes how later breakthroughs mitigated these bottlenecks.

Result: Demonstrates alignment between early AI disappointments and mathematically precise bottlenecks; shows that breakthroughs mitigated rather than fully resolved these barriers, reshaping the trajectory of AI progress.

Conclusion: A refined historical account: formal barriers influenced the timing and nature of AI advances; understanding these bottlenecks clarifies the pattern of progress and offers insight into how current and future limits may shape AI development.

Abstract: Two major periods of reduced funding and confidence in artificial intelligence research, commonly called the first and second AI winters, are usually explained through engineering failure, commercial disappointment, and inflated expectations. This article develops a complementary thesis: that the dominant paradigms of those periods also met genuine formal barriers, including limitations of representation, optimisation, computational complexity, statistical learnability, and high-dimensional approximation. The contribution is synthetic rather than archival. We do not claim that particular theorems mechanically caused the winters; rather, we show that several central disappointments of early AI were aligned with mathematically precise bottlenecks. We analyse these bottlenecks through the perceptron impossibility results of Minsky and Papert, the complexity-theoretic hardness of exact neural-network training established by Blum and Rivest, minimax rates for nonparametric estimation in high dimension due to Stone, vanishing-gradient analyses by Hochreiter and by Bengio and collaborators, and classical statistical learning theory in the tradition of Vapnik and Chervonenkis, Valiant, and Blumer and collaborators. We then relate these barriers to the later breakthroughs that mitigated, rather than eliminated, them.

</details>


### [98] [Evaluation of AutoML Frameworks for IDS under Imbalanced Data Conditions of the NSL-KDD Dataset](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12611)
*Wiliane Carolina Silva,Evandro César Vilas Boas,Felipe A. P. de Figueiredo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: We evaluate nine AutoML frameworks for multiclass IDS on NSL-KDD under severe class imbalance, preserving five classes. PyCaret leads macro-F1, AutoGluon second; imbalance-aware ensemble methods improve minority-class detection; several frameworks fail without balancing; establishes a benchmark and highlights need for native imbalance-handling in AutoML pipelines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide a realistic assessment of AutoML in intrusion detection under multiclass extreme imbalance, avoiding binary simplification, and to understand how framework design (ensembles, validation, hyperparameter optimization, imbalance handling) affects minority class performance.

Method: A unified experimental protocol evaluating nine open-source AutoML frameworks on NSL-KDD with original five-class distribution, analyzing architectural design, ensemble strategies, validation, hyperparameter optimization, and imbalance-handling mechanisms; metrics focus on macro-F1 and minority-class performance; reproducible, open-source code.

Result: PyCaret achieves 66% macro-F1; AutoGluon 55%; frameworks lacking native balancing show significant minority-class degradation; balancing-aware and ensemble-based frameworks perform better on rare classes; accuracy-only optimization not sufficient for highly imbalanced IDS tasks.

Conclusion: Provides a standardized benchmark for AutoML-based IDS under severe multiclass imbalance; identifies architectural limitations; calls for native integration of imbalance-aware optimization, resampling, and stratified evaluation in AutoML pipelines; source code released.

Abstract: This work investigates the impact of severe class imbalance on the performance of automated machine learning (AutoML) frameworks for multiclass network intrusion detection using the NSL-KDD dataset. Unlike previous studies that simplify the problem through binary classification or minority-class removal, we preserve the original five-class distribution, including highly underrepresented attacks such as R2L and U2R, enabling a realistic evaluation of imbalance-sensitive learning behavior. Nine open-source AutoML frameworks were analyzed under a unified and reproducible experimental protocol, considering differences in architectural design, ensemble strategies, validation procedures, hyperparameter optimization, and imbalance-handling mechanisms. The results demonstrate that frameworks incorporating ensemble learning and imbalance-aware optimization achieve better minority-class discrimination. PyCaret obtained the best overall performance, reaching 66\% macro-F1, followed by AutoGluon with 55\%, whereas frameworks lacking native balancing support exhibited significant degradation in minority-class detection capability. The analysis further shows that accuracy-oriented optimization alone is insufficient for highly imbalanced IDS scenarios, since high-weighted metrics may coexist with poor generalization on rare attack categories. As a contribution, this work establishes a standardized benchmark for AutoML-based intrusion detection under severe multiclass imbalance, highlighting current architectural limitations and the need for native integration of imbalance-aware optimization, resampling, and stratified evaluation strategies into automated learning pipelines. The source code is publicly available.

</details>


### [99] [Towards Provably Fair Machine Learning: Bayesian Approaches For Consistent and Transparent Predictions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12615)
*Owen O'Neill,Fintan Costello*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Fair Bayesian classifier enforces determinism and Bayesian consistency across all subgroups, abstaining when no consistent prediction exists, achieving zero consistency error and improved accuracy and multicalibration on benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prediction quality varies across subgroups; regularization worsens minority representation by collapsing small subgroups; need principled framework for consistent and fair predictions.

Method: Define determinism and statistical consistency; derive Fair Bayesian classifier that enforces consistency across all groups and subgroups; abstains when inconsistent; rely on subgroup-specific Bayesian target distributions.

Result: Standard classifiers show substantial inconsistent predictions; the proposed method achieves zero consistency error by construction; outperforms baselines in accuracy and multicalibration on Adult, COMPAS, Bank Marketing.

Conclusion: Statistical consistency provides a principled fairness foundation; addressing inference in small subgroups is necessary for fair ML; exhaustive subgroup fairness with principled abstention is feasible.

Abstract: ML classifiers deployed in high-stakes domains produce predictions whose quality varies systematically across subgroups. For granular subgroups defined by intersections of multiple features, predictions are often inconsistent with the observed data: the model's outputs contradict the evidence available for that subgroup. This problem is exacerbated by regularisation, which improves aggregate performance by collapsing small subgroups into larger groups, disproportionately affecting demographic minorities. We define two requirements for consistent prediction: determinism (identical individuals receive identical predictions) and statistical consistency (we cannot reject, at significance level alpha, the hypothesis that the predictions for a subgroup were drawn from the Bayesian optimal target distribution inferred for that subgroup). From these requirements we derive the Fair Bayesian classifier, which enforces both across every group and subgroup simultaneously and abstains whenever no consistent deterministic prediction is possible. On three benchmark datasets (Adult, COMPAS, and Bank Marketing), standard classifiers produce statistically inconsistent predictions for a substantial proportion of subgroups. Our classifier achieves zero consistency error by construction while exceeding baseline accuracy and multicalibration on every dataset tested. Statistical consistency provides a principled foundation for prediction quality with direct implications for algorithmic fairness. Minority demographics are disproportionately concentrated in small subgroups, precisely where frequentist inference is least reliable; addressing this inference problem is therefore a necessary step toward fair ML. By enforcing Bayesian consistency at the finest resolution the data supports, the our classifier demonstrates that exhaustive subgroup fairness with principled abstention is achievable in practice.

</details>


### [100] [Bag of Dims: Training-Free Mechanistic Interpretability via Dimension-Level Sign Patterns](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12629)
*Varun Reddy Nalagatla*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The standard transformer hidden-state basis encodes semantic content per-dimension via signs and magnitudes, enabling training-free feature reading across multiple models. A large, interpretable feature set emerges unsupervised (up to ~1500 features) with minimal cross-dimension coupling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine whether the hidden-state basis of transformers is already a training-free, architecture-general feature space that supports zero-shot interpretability and rapid feature extraction across model families.

Method: Four progressive experiments across Qwen 3.5-4B, Gemma 3-4B, and Mistral 7B. Analyze sign-patterns (magnitude-ablated) for content and confidence; single-token-type cache to discover categories from per-dimension sign consistency (no training); trained probe to assess cross-dimension structure and axis-alignment; extend to attention (K and V projections); write-side analysis linking features to writer neurons; top-k coalitions and majority vote; fully unsupervised discovery with random seeds to scale features; measure mutual information to assess inter-dimension coupling.

Result: Sign-patterns alone yield 72–93% top-5 accuracy via the LM head with magnitudes replaced by unity; pure Hamming scoring without decoder achieves 80–90% top-4096. Identifies 175 semantic categories (mean AUC 0.80) from 50 anchors with zero training; trained probe adds only +0.018 AUC and converges to axis-aligned weights. Categories persist in K and V projections. 20% of features map to writer neurons (agreement >0.70; random 0%). Top-200 coalitions produce >0.70 agreement on 99.9% of prototypes via majority vote. Unsupervised discovery scales to ~1500 features at 100% yield and 99% sparsity across Qwen/Gemma/Mistral; pairwise MI = 0.0014 bits, indicating low inter-dimension coupling. Overall, the standard basis suffices for feature reading through the transformer compute path with no training or GPU-days beyond a single forward pass per vocabulary token.

Conclusion: The standard basis functions as a universal, training-free feature basis in transformers; semantic structure is distributed across dimensions, detectable via sign-consistent features; robust zero-shot interpretability and feature reading are feasible without training.

Abstract: We show that the standard basis of transformer hidden states already provides a training-free, architecture-general feature basis. Individual dimensions encode semantic content via their signs and confidence via their magnitudes, functioning as independent binary registers. We validate this Bag of Dims framework across three model families (Qwen 3.5-4B, Gemma 3-4B, Mistral 7B) through four progressive experiments. Sign patterns alone carry predictive content: replacing all magnitudes with unity achieves 72-93% top-5 next-token accuracy through the LM head, and pure Hamming scoring without any decoder reaches 80-90% top-4096. These sign patterns organize into semantic features: using a single-token type cache (one forward pass per vocabulary token, no context), we discover 175 categories via per-dimension sign consistency (mean AUC 0.80) from 50 anchors with zero training. A trained probe adds only +0.018 AUC and converges to axis-aligned weights, confirming negligible cross-dimension structure. This structure extends to attention: all 175 categories remain discoverable in K and V projections. On the write side, static FFN weight inspection links 20% of features to individual writer neurons (>0.70 agreement; random controls: 0%), with top-200 neuron coalitions achieving >0.70 agreement on 99.9% of prototypes via majority vote. Fully unsupervised discovery (random seeds, no labels) scales to 1500 features at 100% yield and 99% sparsity across all three models, with pairwise MI of 0.0014 bits confirming low inter-dimension coupling. These results establish that the standard basis already suffices for feature reading throughout the transformer compute pathway, requiring no training, no optimization, and no GPU-days beyond a single forward pass per vocabulary token.

</details>


### [101] [Keep Policy Gradient in Charge: Sibling-Guided Credit Distillation for Long-Horizon Tool-Use Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12634)
*Tianyu Ding,Jianhong Xin,Juan Pablo De la Cruz Weinstein*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SGCD introduces sibling-guided credit distillation to stabilize credit assignment in long-horizon tool-use RL, showing improvements over GRPO baselines on AppWorld and τ^3-airline without relying on external LLMs at deployment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Direct token-level self-distillation can amplify both desirable tool-use actions and harmful shortcuts because it bypasses verifier rewards; there is a need to reframe distillation as credit assignment rather than an actor loss.

Method: Dynamic sampling of mixed successful/failed sibling rollouts; an external LLM computes a contrast-based, training-only stepwise credit reference; dense teacher/student divergence triggers credit reassignment; bounded detached credit weights reshape token-level advantages; the deployed student operates without external LLMs, sibling evidence, or oracle.

Result: SGCD yields improvements: AppWorld TGC from 42.9 to 45.6 on test_normal and 24.7 to 27.0 on test_challenge; τ^3-airline pass@1 from 0.583 to 0.602; outperforms matched GRPO comparators.
