Conclusion: SGCD demonstrates that using distillation to reallocate credit, guided by contrasting sibling rollouts, can improve long-horizon tool-use RL without exposing deployment to external evaluators.

Abstract: Long-horizon tool-use reinforcement learning can learn from outcome verification, but its
  trajectory-level advantage is broadcast across many reasoning, API, and answer tokens.
  Self-distillation promises a denser signal by reusing a policy's own rollouts or a privileged
  teacher. We show, however, that direct token-level self-distillation can silently destroy tool use:
  it rehearses teacher behavior without knowing which actions the verifier rewards, so useful skills
  and harmful shortcuts are amplified together. We introduce Sibling-Guided Credit Distillation
  (SGCD), which uses distillation for credit assignment rather than as a competing actor loss.
  Dynamic sampling produces mixed successful and failed sibling rollouts; an external LLM summarizes
  their contrast into a training-only stepwise credit reference; dense teacher/student divergence
  drives credit reassignment; and bounded detached credit weights reshape GRPO token advantages. The
  deployed student sees no external LLM, sibling evidence, or oracle. Across AppWorld and
  $τ^3$-airline, SGCD improves over matched GRPO comparators: AppWorld TGC $42.9 \to 45.6$ on
  test_normal and $24.7 \to 27.0$ on test_challenge, and $τ^3$-airline pass@1 $0.583 \to 0.602$.

</details>


### [102] [The Metric Picks the Winner: Evaluation Choice Flips Model Rankings for Drug-Response Prediction in Unseen Chemistry](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12639)
*Dhruv Agarwal,Riya Bisht*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Simple baselines can beat complex models under proxy metrics, but the contest's active-set metric reverses the result; a fusion model with chemistry embeddings plus retrieval outperforms a linear fingerprint baseline; demonstrates metric-calibration effect on real drug-chemistry data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evaluating transcriptional response to unseen drugs is hard and benchmarks show naive baselines can win when test compounds are held out by chemistry; understanding metric impact and improving robust predictions is needed.

Method: Staged approach: (1) dumb baselines (untreated control and mean training-compound response); (2) non-parametric retrieval using a Tanimoto-weighted average of nearest training compounds; (3) a fusion stage combining a frozen chemistry embedding with retrieval-support features to predict residual over the mean, with an uncertainty head and gene programs. Evaluated on VCPI THP-1 DRUG-seq data with Bemis-Murcko scaffold split (14,026 training compounds).

Result: Under inverse-variance per-gene proxy, regularized linear regression on Morgan fingerprints wins over deep models, retrieval, and ChemBERTa. Under the contest's true active-set metric (per-(gene, compound) Mejia weights; mean baseline 0.535 vs organizers’ 0.507 reference), that reverses: deep models win; the fusion decoder significantly beats the linear fingerprint baseline (−0.012 wMSE, paired bootstrap p < 1e-4); the proxy-based winner becomes the worst chemistry-aware predictor.

Conclusion: The winner is metric-dependent; evidences metric-calibration effect in real held-out drug chemistry; provides a reproducible pipeline aligned to the official scorer; underscores the need for metric-consistent evaluation in computational pharmacogenomics.

Abstract: Predicting how a cell's transcriptome responds to a drug it has never seen is a core, hard problem in computational cell biology: recent benchmarks show complex models often fail to beat trivial baselines once test compounds are held out by chemistry. We study one cell line and assay, THP-1 cells profiled by DRUG-seq, scored by the active-compound weighted MSE(wMSE) of the VCPI prediction contest. We propose a staged approach: dumb baselines (untreated control and mean training-compound response) that the field keeps failing to beat; non-parametric retrieval (a Tanimoto-weighted average of a held-out compound's nearest training compounds); and a fusion stage combining a frozen chemistry embedding with retrieval-support features to predict the residual over the mean, with an uncertainty head and gene programs. On the released VCPI THP-1 drug-seq data (14,026 training compounds), under a Bemis-Murcko scaffold split, the model ranking inverts depending on the metric. Under an inverse-variance per-gene proxy, a regularized linear regression on Morgan fingerprints appears to win over the deep models, retrieval, and ChemBERTa -- the textbook "simple baselines win" result. But under the contest's true active-set metric (per-(gene, compound) Mejia weights, validated against the official scorer; mean baseline 0.535 vs the organizers' 0.507 reference), that reverses: the deep models win, our fusion decoder significantly beats the linear fingerprint baseline (-0.012 wMSE, paired bootstrap p < 10^-4), and the proxy's winner becomes the worst chemistry-aware predictor. Picking the metric picks the winner -- to our knowledge the first demonstration on real held-out drug chemistry of the metric-calibration effect established largely on genetic perturbation. We release a reproducible pipeline
  wired to the official scorer that emits a valid submission over the real 1064 x 12,995 grid.

</details>


### [103] [Individual Control Barrier Functions-Guided Diffusion Model for Safe Offline Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12640)
*Qingyun Guo,Junyi Shi,Jianuo Huang,Tianyu Shi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes a diffusion-model-based offline multi-agent RL method augmented with individual control barrier functions to enforce safety, with policies recovered via inverse dynamics; shows enhanced safety with competitive rewards across benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Safety challenges in multi-agent offline RL and the opportunity to leverage diffusion models' capacity for complex data distributions; existing work mainly addresses single-agent settings.

Method: Integrates neural individual control barrier functions into a diffusion model for safe trajectory generation; uses inverse dynamics to recover control policies; evaluated on diverse benchmarks.

Result: Significant safety improvements demonstrated while maintaining competitive rewards across tasks.

Conclusion: The approach achieves safer offline multi-agent reinforcement learning without sacrificing performance, indicating potential for safer deployment in multi-agent environments.

Abstract: Offline reinforcement learning allows control policies to be learned directly from data without online interaction, making it suitable for safety-critical tasks. Recent studies have applied diffusion models to offline reinforcement learning to leverage their strong capacity for modeling complex data distributions. However, existing approaches primarily focus on single-agent settings, leaving the safety challenges in multi-agent environments largely unexplored. In this work, we propose a safe offline multi-agent reinforcement learning algorithm that embeds neural individual control barrier functions into the diffusion model to enhance safety during trajectory generation, with control policies recovered through inverse dynamics. We evaluate our algorithm across diverse benchmarks, demonstrating substantial safety improvements while maintaining competitive rewards.

</details>


### [104] [TEDD: Robust Detection of Unstable Temporal Features](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12643)
*Ricardo Ribeiro Pereira,Bruno Casal Laraña,Nádia Soares,Miguel Araújo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TEDD identifies temporal feature drift and flags features contributing to model instability using a regression model predicting timestamp; supports numerical and categorical features, detects multivariate drifts, parameter-free, and scalable.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world temporal data exhibit distribution shifts over time, causing ML model performance to degrade if not detected and mitigated. Automatic detection of changing features enables robust modeling and timely interventions.

Method: Train a regression model to predict the instance timestamp from feature values; analyze feature contributions to timestamp predictions to identify drift-affected features; compare against baselines on real and synthetic data across simple change patterns; provide per-feature drift scores; claim no parameter tuning and scalability.

Result: TEDD detects all basic change types for numerical and categorical features, can detect multivariate drifts, returns a per-feature drift magnitude, requires no parameter tuning, and scales to large feature sets and datasets; shown to be competitive with existing methods across tested scenarios.

Conclusion: TEDD offers automatic, scalable feature-level drift detection that helps maintain ML robustness by identifying when and which features drive instability and guiding appropriate data transformations.

Abstract: When working with real-world temporal data, it is common to encounter features whose distribution is changing over time. The naive employment of Machine Learning models on this unstable data might lead to rapidly degrading performance, especially if the new distribution is much different from what was previously seen during training. In order to cope with this problem, it is critical to automatically identify features that are changing over time. With these features detected, data scientists and other practitioners will be able to mitigate the issue (for instance, by applying data transformations), deploying more robust models that retain high performance for longer periods of time. In this paper, we describe which temporal changes a feature should not suffer from, and propose TEDD, a technique to a) identify when a dataset might lead to an unstable Machine Learning model and b) automatically detect which features cause such lack of robustness. In order to achieve it, we leverage a regression model to highlight which features contribute to a good prediction of an instance's timestamp. We compare our approach to other methods in real and synthetic data, testing their detection capability on all simple change patterns. We show that our method: detects all types of basic changes, both for numerical and categorical features; can detect multivariate drifts; returns a comparable value measuring the amount of change of each feature; requires no parameter tuning; and is scalable both on number of features and instances of the dataset.

</details>


### [105] [Physics-Aware Auxiliary Losses Improve Out-of-Distribution Generalization of a GNN Synthesizability Filter](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12651)
*Riya Bisht,Dhruv Agarwal*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Physics-informed auxiliary losses on a GNN yield modest but significant OOD gains for SAScore-based drug-likeness filtering; topological complexity (Bertz index) and MMFF94 strain energy penalties each help, with the best result when combined; results are robust across seeds but sensitive to seed initialization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Out-of-distribution generalization is a major challenge for generative drug-discovery pipelines. Purely statistical filters degrade on OOD data; cheap, closed-form physical priors may provide transferable supervision to improve robustness.

Method: GINE-based graph neural network with two auxiliary losses: (1) topological complexity regression using Bertz index; (2) MMFF94 strain-energy penalty. Train on a 65,177-molecule corpus labeled by SAScore thresholds (drug-like HIV+Tox21) and evaluate on an OOD split (COCONUT natural products). Four ablations (baseline; +complexity; +strain; +both) tested with 5 seeds and bootstrap CIs. Evaluation metric: AUC for SAScore-label discrimination.

Result: Three physics-aware variants achieved small but statistically significant OOD improvements over baseline. Reported mean OOD AUC ~0.9774 with deltas: complexity +0.0060 (95% CI [0.0023, 0.0102]); strain +0.0032 (CI [0.0008, 0.0052]); both +0.0066 (CI [0.0038, 0.0093]). All CIs exclude zero; the combination is the best. In-distribution performance is unchanged across variants. A notable methodological point: single-seed results can mislead; multi-seed evaluation is essential.

Conclusion: Incorporating inexpensive physical priors as auxiliary supervision provides modest but robust OOD improvements for GNN-based drug-likeness filtering, with the combination of complexity and strain penalties delivering the strongest gain. Results underscore the importance of multi-seed robustness checks to avoid spurious conclusions.

Abstract: Machine-learning drug-discovery pipelines increasingly rely on generative models that propose molecules far from the data used to train downstream synthesizability filters. Existing filters (SAScore, SCScore, RAscore, DeepSA) are purely statistical and degrade in exactly this out-of-distribution (OOD) regime. We ask whether cheap, closed-form physical priors, used as auxiliary supervision on a graph neural network (GNN), improve OOD generalization. We add two auxiliary losses to a GINE backbone: a topological complexity regression supervised by the Bertz index, and a strain-energy soft penalty supervised by MMFF94 force-field energy. On a 65,177-molecule corpus (HIV, Tox21, COCONUT) labeled by SAScore thresholds we reproduce a strong in-distribution baseline, then evaluate a 4-way ablation (baseline / +complexity / +strain / +both) on a single-source OOD split (train on drug-like HIV+Tox21, test on COCONUT natural products), repeated over 5 seeds with paired bootstrap confidence intervals. All three physics-aware variants give a small but statistically significant OOD improvement over the baseline (mean OOD AUC 0.9774): +complexity Delta = +0.0060 (95% CI [+0.0023, +0.0102]), +strain Delta = +0.0032 ([+0.0008, +0.0052]), +both Delta = +0.0066 ([+0.0038, +0.0093]); every interval excludes zero, and the combination is best. The variants are indistinguishable in-distribution, so the effect is visible only under OOD evaluation. We are explicit that the effects are modest, and we report a cautionary methodological finding: a single-seed version of this experiment produced a qualitatively different (non-monotone) story that did not survive multi-seed evaluation.

</details>


### [106] [Physics-Informed Neural Networks for Chemotherapy Pharmacokinetics: Benchmarking the Clinical Estimator and Exposing Parameter Identifiability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12658)
*Riya Bisht,Dhruv Agarwal*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PINNs offer a uniform framework for PK problems with partial observability, matching classical estimators on linear models, exposing identifiability issues in nonlinear extensions, and integrating heterogeneous data in a single loss.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess whether physics-informed neural networks can handle partial-observation pharmacokinetic problems, compare with standard nonlinear least-squares (NLS) and a data-only MLP, and investigate (structural) identifiability and data integration using tissue and plasma measurements.

Method: Benchmark three approaches (PINN, NLS with biexponential plasma solution, and a data-only MLP) on two compartment PK models: (i) linear two-compartment with biexponential plasma solution; (ii) Michaelis–Menten saturable elimination extension. Evaluate performance on plasma and tissue concentrations, investigate identifiability under plasma-only data, and test the effect of adding sparse tissue observations across multiple random seeds.

Result: On the linear two-compartment problem, NLS is near-optimal; PINN matches NLS within a small constant factor and provides the tissue curve in one pass; the data-only MLP fails on tissue by ~10x. In the Michaelis–Menten extension, the closed-form NLS is mis-specified and yields meaningless rate constants; PINN reveals non-identifiability from plasma alone, converging to a basin with k12→0. Adding two sparse tissue observations improves identifiability: k21 and Vmax/Km recovered with ~1% and ~one-standard-deviation accuracy; k12 moves toward the truth but remains offset, a recovery unattainable by NLS due to the biexponential-only plasma model.

Conclusion: PINNs do not necessarily beat NLS but provide a uniform framework that (i) aligns with textbook estimators on classic problems, (ii) uncovers structural identifiability limitations hidden by traditional estimators, and (iii) accommodates heterogeneous measurements within a single loss, thus enhancing model interrogation and data integration in PK problems.

Abstract: Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) are an attractive tool for partial-observation problems in biology, where the governing dynamics are known but some compartments cannot be measured. Chemotherapy pharmacokinetics (PK) is a clean instance: drug concentration in plasma is routinely measured, but concentration in tissue -- which determines tumour kill and off-target toxicity -- is not. We benchmark a PINN against the standard clinical baseline (nonlinear least-squares on the analytical biexponential plasma solution, hereafter NLS) and a physics-agnostic neural baseline (a data-only MLP) on two PK problems. On the linear two-compartment problem, NLS is near-optimal; the PINN matches it to within a small constant factor while also producing the tissue curve in a single training pass, whereas the data-only MLP fails on tissue by roughly 10x. On a Michaelis-Menten extension (saturable elimination), the biexponential closed form no longer exists, so NLS is mis-specified and silently returns meaningless rate constants. The PINN instead exposes a deeper fact: the Michaelis-Menten two-compartment model is non-identifiable from plasma alone, and the PINN reports this honestly by converging to a basin with k12 -> 0. Adding two sparse tissue observations largely resolves identifiability: across five seeds the PINN recovers k21 to within 1% of truth and Vmax, Km to within one standard-deviation bar, while k12 moves in the correct direction (0.02 -> 0.82) but remains ~2 sigma below truth -- a recovery the closed-form NLS estimator cannot attempt at all, because its biexponential ansatz describes only plasma. Our claim is not that PINNs beat NLS. It is that PINNs offer a uniform recipe that ties the textbook estimator on the textbook problem, exposes structural identifiability that
  the textbook estimator hides, and absorbs heterogeneous measurements within a single loss.

</details>


### [107] [A Zero-shot Generalized Graph Anomaly Detection Framework via Node Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12673)
*Phan Nguyen,Dat Cao,Hien Chu,Khue Hoang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A zero-shot generalized GAD framework (AlignGAD) that unifies heterogeneous graph features, builds cluster-aware views, and scores anomalies via reconstruction discrepancy to detect abnormal nodes in unseen target graphs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cross-domain graph anomaly detection struggles to generalize due to dataset-specific feature semantics and structural patterns; a zero-shot, domain-general framework is desirable for unseen heterogeneous graphs.

Method: Global Unification Module aligns heterogeneous node features and normalizes graph signals in the spectral domain; Clustering Module constructs cluster-aware graph views to capture group-level abnormal patterns; Node Discrepancy Scoring Module measures reconstruction discrepancy and aggregates anomaly evidence across graph views.

Result: Empirical evaluation on multiple real-world datasets demonstrates AlignGAD's effectiveness under the zero-shot GAD setting.

Conclusion: AlignGAD provides a modular, zero-shot capable framework for cross-domain graph anomaly detection, enabling robust generalization to unseen heterogeneous graphs.

Abstract: Cross-domain graph anomaly detection (GAD) aims to identify abnormal nodes in unseen target graphs, showing strong potential in real-world applications with heterogeneous graph data. However, existing methods often depend on dataset-specific feature semantics and structural patterns, which limits their ability to generalize across different domains. To address this challenge, we propose AlignGAD, a zero-shot generalized graph anomaly detection framework. Our framework is built upon three key components: a Global Unification Module that aligns heterogeneous node features and normalizes graph signals in the spectral domain; a Clustering Module that constructs cluster-aware graph views to capture group-level abnormal patterns; and a Node Discrepancy Scoring Module that measures reconstruction discrepancy and aggregates anomaly evidence from different graph views. Experiments on multiple real-world datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of AlignGAD under the zero-shot GAD setting.

</details>


### [108] [Fed-FBD: Federated Functional Block Diversification for Isolation, Privacy, and Surgical Unlearning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12679)
*Weijie Chen,Alan B. McMillan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Fed-FBD decomposes a ResNet backbone into six functional blocks and uses a warehouse of color variants built from independently tracked, contributor-stamped blocks to enable block-level isolation, privacy-by-design, and fast unlearning, with competitive accuracy and robust attack containment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard federated learning (FedAvg) treats clients as black boxes and lacks mechanisms to isolate adversarial contributors, audit per-client influence, or honor forgetting requests. There is a need for architecture-level isolation, privacy safeguards, and efficient per-client unlearning.

Method: Decompose the ResNet backbone into six blocks (stem, four residual groups, classifier head). Maintain a warehouse of N color variants, each assembled from independently tracked and contributor-stamped blocks. Provide block-level isolation, integrate privacy-by-design such that membership inference advantages are at chance before applying privacy mechanisms, and enable surgical unlearning of a departed participant’s contribution without retraining. Evaluate on six MedMNIST-2D datasets, PathMNIST (224x224), and CIFAR-10, comparing against FedAvg under Dirichlet distributions (alpha=1.0) and testing against six adversarial attacks.

Result: Fed-FBD incurs a modest IID accuracy gap of 0.3%–3.1% for the integrity guarantees and stays within 0.8%–4.0% of FedAvg on three of four datasets at Dirichlet alpha=1.0. All six adversarial attacks are confined to the poisoned client’s blocks, with at most ±0.01 AUC drift on the clean colors.

Conclusion: Fed-FBD offers architecturally guaranteed block-level isolation, privacy-by-design, and fast per-client unlearning with only modest accuracy trade-offs, and demonstrates robust protection against adversarial contributors while maintaining competitive performance compared to FedAvg.

Abstract: Federated learning (FL) enables collaborative model training without sharing raw patient data, but standard approaches such as FedAvg treat each client as a black box and provide no mechanism for isolating an adversarial contributor, auditing per-client influence, or honoring a departed participant's right to be forgotten. We present Fed-FBD (Federated Functional Block Diversification), a modular federated architecture that decomposes a ResNet backbone into six functional blocks (the stem, four residual groups, and the classification head) and maintains a warehouse of N color variants, each assembled from independently tracked and contributor-stamped blocks. Fed-FBD provides three capabilities absent in FedAvg: (i) architecturally guaranteed block-level isolation, so that an adversarial or mislabelled client cannot contaminate the clean colous; (ii) privacy-by-design, where membership inference advantage is already indistinguishable from chance before any privacy mechanism is applied; and (iii) surgical machine unlearning of a departed participant's contribution at sub-second cost and without retraining. Experiments on six MedMNIST-2D datasets, PathMNIST at 224x224, and CIFAR-10 show that Fed-FBD trades a modest 0.3%-3.1% IID accuracy gap on the adequately sized datasets for these guarantees, remains within 0.8%-4.0% of FedAvg at Dirichlet alpha=1.0 on three of four datasets, and confines all six adversarial attacks we study to the poisoned client's own blocks with at most +/-0.01 AUC drift on the clean colors.

</details>


### [109] [How Useful is Causal Invariance for Domain Adaptation in Finite-Sample Settings?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12680)
*Julia Kostin,Kasra Jalaldoust,Elias Bareinboim,Samory Kpotufe,Fanny Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Finite-sample gains from using causal invariances in supervised domain adaptation (sDA) for linear regression depend on target-risk margins between candidate predictors and target sample size; an adaptive aggregation can match the best candidate while avoiding negative transfer when margins are large; if margins are small, no algorithm can improve finite-sample rates; margins relate to structural shift magnitudes in linear SCMs; validated on causal benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge the gap between population-level causal invariances and finite-sample performance in domain generalization under labeled target data, by quantifying when causal knowledge helps and how much gain to expect.

Method: Derive matching upper and lower finite-sample bounds for linear regression with (possibly) invariant feature subsets; propose an adaptive aggregation over source-trained predictors; analyze success conditions in terms of target-risk margins and target sample size n_Q; relate gains to estimation error and invariance structure.

Result: Provided sharp bounds showing finite-sample gains are governed by target-risk margins and estimation error; with margins large relative to n_Q, adaptive aggregation attains the best candidate and avoids negative transfer; with small margins, no algorithm can achieve faster finite-sample rates; connections made to structural shift magnitudes in linear SCMs and validated on real-world causal benchmarks.

Conclusion: Structural margins and finite-sample estimation error determine when causal invariances yield benefits in sDA; the work clarifies regimes where leveraging causal structure is advantageous and provides a principled aggregation strategy, with empirical validation on causal benchmarks.

Abstract: Machine learning models often degrade when they are deployed on a target distribution that differs from the source distributions they were trained on. Recent work in causality-based domain generalization has shown how shared causal structure between domains can induce invariant predictors, e.g., models on a subset of features which have stable risk across structured domain shifts. However, the extent to which such population-level causal invariances can lead to gains in finite-sample settings remains underexplored. In particular, in practice we often have access to a few labeled target samples, a setting called supervised domain adaptation (sDA). In this paper, we explore when (full or partial) causal knowledge can provably improve supervised domain adaptation.
  As a first step, we study linear regression, where full or partial causal knowledge specifies a collection of invariant or possibly invariant feature subsets, each yielding a source-trained candidate predictor. We derive matching upper and lower bounds showing that finite-sample gains are governed by the target-risk margins separating the candidates, together with the finite-source estimation error. When these margins are sufficiently large relative to $n_Q$, an adaptive aggregation procedure can match the best candidate predictor while avoiding negative transfer relative to target-only learning. On the other hand, when the margins are too small, no algorithm can reliably exploit the candidate collection to obtain faster finite-sample rates. We further connect these margins to structural shift magnitude in linear SCMs and validate the theory on real-world causal benchmarks.

</details>


### [110] [Forecasting Is Not Attribution: Localizing Decoder Bypass in Graph-Based Neural Marketing Mix Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12687)
*Yunbo Wang,Bolbi Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DICE-MMM reveals that low forecasting error in graph-based MMM can mask attribution bypass, where the decoder uses non-graph channels to reduce error without proper graph-based attribution. It introduces a two-stage bounded diagnostic to separate graph recovery, forecasting, and attribution alignment, demonstrating improved graph recovery and localization of the attribution bottleneck to graph-support selection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Graph-based MMM conflate three distinct objectives: recovering the underlying graph, achieving good forecasting accuracy, and ensuring that counterfactual sensitivity (attribution) really flows through the graph. This work targets attribution bypass, a failure mode where high-capacity decoders attain low error without proper graph-mediated attribution.

Method: Propose DICE-MMM, a bounded diagnostic/training framework. Stage 1 trains a graph encoder with a restricted graph-mediated decoder. Stage 2 freezes the selected encoder and trains a graph-safe latent decoder whose cross-node communication must pass through the supplied graph. Decoder use is evaluated with CIG, AR-CIG, and graph-swap tests. Evaluations include controlled R/d/T swaps and an external multi-graph rawlog stress test, comparing against CausalMMM.

Result: DICE improves stable graph recovery over CausalMMM across stress tests. In sparse-target benchmarks, no-graph and full-graph decoders achieve MSE@7 ~0.004 while AR-CIG nAUPRC remains near or below zero; an oracle graph reaches 0.807 ± 0.129 in AR-CIG at similar MSE. Frozen graph-swap localizes the bottleneck: the DICE-hard-trained decoder moves from nAUPRC -0.044 ± 0.006 with learned graph inputs to 0.894 ± 0.027 with the oracle graph. This shows that low MSE can hide attribution bypass and that the bottleneck lies in graph-support selection rather than forecasting or decoder capacity.

Conclusion: DICE provides a stress test and failure-localization framework that demonstrates attribution bypass in graph-based MMM and separates three intertwined questions—graph recovery, forecasting accuracy, and graph-aligned counterfactual perturbation—revealing that robust attribution requires careful graph-support selection beyond forecasting performance or decoder capacity.

Abstract: Marketing mix models are used to forecast business outcomes and to attribute those outcomes to marketing channels, but these goals are not equivalent. We study a failure mode in graph-based neural MMM called attribution bypass: a high-capacity decoder can obtain low forecasting error through target autoregression, dense communication, co-movement, context, or latent memory while failing to route counterfactual sensitivity through the graph used as the attribution object. We introduce DICE-MMM as a bounded diagnostic and training framework. We do not claim that observational neural MMM identifies causal effects. Instead, DICE separates three questions often conflated in graph-based MMM: graph recovery, forecasting accuracy, and whether the trained decoder's perturbation-induced influence is graph aligned. Stage 1 trains a graph encoder with a restricted graph-mediated decoder. Stage 2 freezes the selected encoder and trains a graph-safe latent decoder whose cross-node communication must pass through the supplied graph. Decoder use is evaluated with CIG, AR-CIG, and graph-swap tests. Across controlled R/d/T swaps and an external multi-graph rawlog stress test, DICE improves stable graph recovery over CausalMMM. The experiments show that forecasting accuracy is not an attribution certificate: in a sparse-target benchmark, no-graph and full-graph decoders achieve MSE@7 around 0.004 while AR-CIG nAUPRC remains near or below zero, whereas an oracle graph reaches 0.807 +/- 0.129 at comparable MSE. Frozen graph-swap localizes the bottleneck: the same DICE-hard-trained decoder moves from nAUPRC -0.044 +/- 0.006 under learned graph inputs to 0.894 +/- 0.027 with the oracle graph. The contribution is a stress test and failure-localization framework showing that low MSE can hide attribution bypass and that the unresolved bottleneck is graph-support selection, not forecasting or decoder capacity.

</details>


### [111] [M*: A Modular, Extensible, Serving System for Multimodal Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12688)
*Atindra Jha,Naomi Sagan,Keisuke Kamahori,Irmak Sivgin,Rohan Sanda,Steven Gao,Mark Horowitz,Luke Zettlemoyer,Olivia Hsu,Jure Leskovec,Baris Kasikci,Stephanie Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: M* is a universal, dataflow-based serving system for composite AI models, introducing a Walk Graph abstraction to flexibly compose and deploy diverse model components, achieving significant latency/throughput improvements over baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current model-serving frameworks assume narrow, fixed model structures and lack support for highly composite, multimodal architectures; a flexible, modular runtime is needed to efficiently serve such models.

Method: Represent models as dataflow graphs processed as traversals (Walk Graph); modular abstraction enabling arbitrary component composition, flexible physical placement, and model-agnostic optimizations within a distributed runtime; instantiate on representative models and evaluate against benchmarks.

Result: M* achieves 20% lower end-to-end latency than vLLM-Omni for text-to-image workloads on BAGEL; up to 2.9x lower real-time factor and 2.7x higher throughput for text-to-speech on Qwen3-Omni; up to 12.5x improvement for robotic planning over V-JEPA 2-AC rollout baseline.

Conclusion: M* enables more efficient serving of complex composite models with minimal developer effort and broad applicability across multimodal model families.

Abstract: We are entering a new era of composite model architectures that integrate diverse components such as vision encoders, language backbones, diffusion and flow heads, audio codecs, action generators, and world-model predictors. Such architectures underpin a broad class of multimodal models, including unified multimodal models, omni models, speech-language models, vision-language-action policies, and world models. However, existing model serving frameworks were built on narrow assumptions about model structure, making them ill-suited to accommodate this new architectural diversity. Here we present M*, a universal serving system for efficient serving of composite AI models. M* represents models as dataflow graphs, processing requests spanning diverse modalities and tasks as traversals over these graphs. The core insight is a modular abstraction that supports arbitrary composition of model components, flexible placement onto a physical cluster, and model-agnostic optimizations within a distributed runtime. We call this abstraction the Walk Graph and show how it can concisely capture composite models from a broad range of families. We instantiate M* on representative models and find that it achieves, on average, 20% lower end-to-end latency than vLLM-Omni for text-to-image workloads on BAGEL, while delivering up to 2.9x lower real-time factor and 2.7x higher throughput for text-to-speech workloads on Qwen3-Omni. M* also outperforms the V-JEPA 2-AC rollout baseline for robotic planning by up to 12.5x. Thus, our work paves the road towards more efficient serving of complex models with minimal developer effort.

</details>


### [112] [Two-Layer Linear Auto-Regressive Models Estimate Latent States](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12691)
*Yahya Sattar,Sunmook Choi,Leo Maynard-Zhang,Yassir Jedra,Maryam Fazel,Sarah Dean*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Two-layer linear autoregressive models trained with empirical risk on data from partially observed linear dynamical systems learn latent representations that align with Kalman filter state estimates (up to a similarity transform). The analysis shows Kalman filtering can be well approximated by such AR models with bounded truncation error, and demonstrates a benign non-convex optimization landscape with finite-sample guarantees for prediction, parameter estimation, and latent state recovery, supported by simulations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address why auto-regressive models learn meaningful latent representations and how these representations relate to classical state estimation (Kalman filtering) in linear dynamical systems.

Method: Theoretically relate Kalman filtering to two-layer linear AR models by proving bounded truncation error in approximating the Kalman filter, showing the optimization landscape has no bad local minima (only strict saddles or global minima), and deriving finite-sample guarantees for prediction error, parameter estimation error, and latent state recovery; corroborate with numerical simulations.

Result: The learned hidden representation coincides, up to a similarity transformation, with the Kalman filter's state estimates; the Kalman filter can be well approximated by a truncated AR model; the two-layer optimization landscape is benign; finite-sample guarantees are established for prediction, parameter estimation, and latent state recovery; simulations validate latent-state recovery by AR models.

Conclusion: Auto-regressive models can implicitly recover Kalman-filter-like latent states in partially observed linear dynamical systems, providing theoretical insight into representation learning for sequential models and justification for using AR-based approaches in state estimation tasks.

Abstract: Auto-regressive models have emerged as powerful tools for sequential data, from language to video. Understanding how and why these models learn latent representations remains an open theoretical question. In this work, we demonstrate that when trained by empirical risk minimization on data from partially observed linear dynamical systems, two-layer linear auto-regressive models naturally learn to approximate Kalman filtering. In particular, we show that the learned hidden representation coincides, up to a similarity transformation, with the state estimates produced by the optimal (Kalman) filter, even though the model has no explicit knowledge of the underlying dynamics or state. The result follows from three main insights. First, we establish that the Kalman filter is well approximated by an auto-regressive model with bounded truncation error. Second, we show that despite non-convexity, the two-layer optimization landscape is benign, i.e., all stationary points are either strict saddles or global minima. Finally, as our main contributions, we provide finite-sample guarantees on prediction error, parameter estimation error, and latent state recovery. Numerical simulations support the theoretical results and demonstrate that the latent representations of auto-regressive models recover state estimates.

</details>


### [113] [LLM-Powered Personalized Glycemic Assessment in Type 2 Diabetes with Wearable Sensor Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12699)
*Yifan Gao,Yanmin Gong,Yun Shi,Yuanxiong Guo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GlyLLM uses an LLM-powered framework to fuse CGM data with wearable sensor data and structured metadata for personalized glycemic assessment, achieving notable improvements over traditional ML on AI-READI data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the limitations of traditional ML approaches that rely mainly on historical glucose and lack personalized context, by leveraging LLMs' capacity for multimodal data integration and modeling sequential dependencies for individualized diabetes care.

Method: Propose GlyLLM, an LLM-driven framework that ingests CGM time-series, wearable sensor features, and structured metadata. It leverages pre-trained LLMs for sensor-text semantic abstraction at decision time, and is evaluated on the AI-READI dataset against traditional ML baselines with ablations to identify critical data modalities (diabetes surveys and biometric tests).

Result: GlyLLM outperforms traditional ML methods by an average of 13.66% in RMSE for glucose forecasting and 13.08% in AUROC for diabetes categorization. Ablation indicates diabetes surveys and biometric tests are disproportionately informative for glycemic assessment.

Conclusion: Demonstrates the potential of LLMs to enhance personalized glycemic assessment by integrating diverse data sources; suggests a path forward for personalized T2D care using multimodal, semantically-aware models, while highlighting data modality importance.

Abstract: Type 2 Diabetes (T2D) poses an increasing global health threat, demanding effective glycemic assessment to support personalized and improved diabetes care. Wearable sensors such as continuous glucose monitors (CGM) and fitness trackers offer many valuable insights for glycemic assessment. However, effectively analyzing these data requires integration with essential individual-level context. Existing methods are often based on traditional machine learning (ML) and rely primarily on historical blood glucose measurements and overlook personalized information, which limits their performance across diverse diabetes populations. Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated their ability to integrate diverse data modalities while modeling sequential dependencies, motivating the exploration of their potential for personalized glycemic assessment.
  In this paper, we propose GlyLLM, an LLM-powered framework for modeling CGM-based glycemic dynamics through the integration of wearable sensor data and structured metadata. GlyLLM can leverage the extensive prior knowledge of pre-trained LLMs and achieve sensor-text semantic abstraction at decision time. Experiments on two related tasks on the AI-READI dataset demonstrate that our model outperforms traditional ML methods by an average of 13.66\% in Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) for glucose forecasting and 13.08\% in Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic (AUROC) for diabetes categorization. Additionally, our ablation study shows that diabetes surveys and biometric tests are more critical than other health information for glycemic assessment. Our work presents a promising step toward harnessing the power of LLMs to advance personalized glycemic assessment in T2D care.

</details>


### [114] [A Stabilized Path-Space Approach to Diffusion-Based Posterior Sampling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12710)
*Evan Scope Crafts,Umberto Villa,Saviz Mowlavi,Yanting Ma,Hassan Mansour,Wael H. Ali*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A stabilized path-space diffusion posterior sampler that frames Bayesian inverse problems as stochastic control on trajectory space, with time reparameterization, trust-region learning, and importance-sampling corrections, yielding improved accuracy and uncertainty quantification over prior diffusion-based samplers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address limitations of diffusion posterior samplers that rely on heuristic guidance and struggle with nonlinear operators and multimodal posteriors; provide principled, uncertainty-aware posterior sampling.

Method: Define base diffusion with terminal prior; construct likelihood-weighted trajectory target; learn a controlled SDE whose path measure matches target; apply time reparameterization to remove initial-value bias; optimize control via trust-region path-space optimization with log-variance objective; connect to guidance-based samplers; provide importance sampling corrections for asymptotically exact expectations.

Result: Evaluated on benchmark inverse problems with known or high-quality posteriors; demonstrates principled assessment of sampling accuracy and uncertainty; shows improved accuracy and robustness versus leading approaches.

Conclusion: Unifies diffusion posterior sampling with stochastic optimal control, preserving Bayesian structure for uncertainty quantification; provides a well-posed, tractable path-space framework; enables principled error quantification and asymptotically exact expectations via importance sampling; shows practical improvements on benchmark problems.

Abstract: Diffusion models provide expressive data-driven priors for Bayesian inverse problems, but many diffusion posterior samplers rely on heuristic guidance approximations that can fail for nonlinear operators and multimodal posteriors. In this work, we develop a stabilized path-space framework for diffusion-based posterior sampling. Starting from a base diffusion process whose terminal marginal represents the prior, we define a likelihood-weighted target measure on trajectories and cast posterior sampling as learning a controlled stochastic process whose path measure matches this target. This formulation connects diffusion posterior sampling to stochastic optimal control while preserving the Bayesian structure needed for uncertainty quantification. We introduce a time reparameterization that makes the path-space control problem well posed by removing the bias induced by the unknown initial value function, without auxiliary training. We then learn the control via a trust-region path-space optimization method with log-variance objectives. The path-space perspective also unifies our learned control approach with existing guidance-based samplers, quantifies the sampling error induced by approximate controls, and yields importance sampling corrections for asymptotically exact posterior expectations. We evaluate the proposed framework on a suite of benchmark inverse problems with analytically characterized or high-quality reference posteriors, enabling principled assessment of sampling accuracy and uncertainty quantification. These experiments provide insight into the behavior of diffusion-based posterior samplers and demonstrate improved accuracy and robustness over leading approaches.

</details>


### [115] [Out-of-Distribution (OOD) Detectors for Open-Set RF Fingerprinting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12718)
*Sudeepta Mondal,Ganesh Sundaramoorthi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces OOD detection methods for open-set RF fingerprinting using an information-theoretic framework; shows tuning without OOD data; strong POWDER dataset results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RF fingerprinting in open-world settings suffers distribution shifts due to unknown transmitters and temporal drift. Traditional OOD detectors require OOD tuning data, which is hard to collect in RF environments. The work aims to adapt ML OOD detectors to RF fingerprinting within a principled information-theoretic framework.

Method: Proposes a unified information-theoretic framework for open-set RF fingerprinting and incorporates a set of ML-based OOD detectors; demonstrates tuning without given OOD tuning data to accommodate real-world RF constraints; analyzes detectors systematically and develops potential new methods.

Result: On the POWDER RF fingerprinting dataset, detectors tuned without any OOD tuning data achieve performance comparable to baselines that do have true OOD tuning data, and significantly outperform baselines without access to OOD tuning data.

Conclusion: Bridges ML OOD detection with RF fingerprinting in open-world scenarios, provides a rigorous framework for analysis and method development, and demonstrates practical viability of tuning-free OOD detectors in RF environments.

Abstract: Radio-frequency (RF) fingerprinting systems must operate in open-world environments where signals from unknown transmitters and temporal drift introduce distribution shift at test time. Out-of-distribution (OOD) detection provides a natural framework for this problem, yet its application to RF fingerprinting (RFF) remains limited. A key barrier to their adoption is that most OOD detectors require auxiliary OOD data for parameter tuning, an assumption that is difficult to satisfy in RF environments where representative OOD data is impractical to collect. In this work, we introduce a promising set of OOD detection methods from the machine learning literature to open-set RFF domain. We present these methods within a unified mathematical framework based on information theory, which is a natural framework for communication systems. Our framework allows for the systematic analysis of methods and development of new methods. We further demonstrate the applicability of recent work on tuning OOD detectors without given OOD tuning data for open-set RFF. We evaluate on the POWDER RF fingerprinting dataset, showing that detectors tuned without any given OOD data achieve performance comparable to baselines with access to true OOD tuning data and greatly out-perform baseline approaches without access to true OOD tuning data, showcasing the practical viability for the RFF problem.

</details>


### [116] [Normative Robustness as a Frontier for Non-Verifiable Reasoning in LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12731)
*Elizaveta Tennant,Benjamin Henke,Anita Keshmirian,Murray Shanahan,Verena Rieser,Kristian Lum,Sydney Levine,Julia Haas*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Defines moral robustness in LLMs, presents an adversarial multi-turn evaluation framework, and reports that models can be swayed to align reasoning with a user's moral view, revealing 'moral deliberative sycophancy' as a potential failure mode.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current evaluations focus on verifiable domains (e.g., math, science). There is a gap in assessing non-verifiable, subjective, or value-laden reasoning—crucial for advisory roles, as models may adapt to user morals over time.

Method: Introduces a scalable, adversarial, multi-turn evaluation framework. Simulates 48,000 user–agent moral deliberations across four frontier LLMs while varying premise relevance, premise order, conversation duration, and the user's stated moral view to probe robustness of moral reasoning.

Result: Models ignore morally irrelevant distractors but shift reasoning toward the user's preferred moral view, averaging up to a 6.5% shift. Order effects alter moral judgments in 13–22% of cases; longer multi-turn interactions alter judgments in 10–24%. The model also tailors underlying justifications to the user’s moral viewpoint.

Conclusion: Identifies a concerning failure mode—moral deliberative sycophancy—where models align both verdicts and justifications with user morals. This underscores the need for robust evaluation of non-verifiable reasoning and safeguards against adaptive alignment.

Abstract: As LLMs increasingly serve in advisory and deliberative roles, users rely on them for non-verifiable reasoning in domains lacking objective ground truths. However, traditional evaluations of LLM reasoning focus almost exclusively on fact-based domains, such as mathematics and science, leaving uncertainty over whether and to what degree models can handle ambiguous, subjective, or value-laden problems over time. To address this concern, we propose moral reasoning as a paradigmatic subdomain of non-verifiable reasoning. We define moral robustness as a model's capacity to exhibit sound moral reasoning across time and contexts, and we introduce a scalable, adversarial, multi-turn evaluation framework to empirically measure this capability. We simulate 48,000 user-agent moral deliberations across four frontier LLMs, varying premise relevance, premise order, conversation duration, and the user's stated moral view. We find that models successfully ignore morally-irrelevant distractors, but shift their reasoning by up to 6.5%, on average, towards the user's stated preferred moral view, and varying their reasoning depending on factors such as order (altering moral judgments by order in 13-22% of the cases) and duration (altering moral judgments between single-turn and multi-turn in 10-24% of the cases). Our analysis indicates that models tailor not just their final verdicts but their underlying justifications to align with a user's moral viewpoint - a failure mode we characterize as moral deliberative sycophancy.

</details>


### [117] [Let's Ask Gauss: Improved One-Run Privacy Auditing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12733)
*Adya Agrawal,Yu Wei,Jaspal Singh,Malik Magdon-Ismail,Vassilis Zikas*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A distributional analysis of canary-based signals in white-box DP-SGD enables a one-shot privacy audit: the sum of aligned signals becomes asymptotically Gaussian, allowing a framework that yields tighter DP lower bounds from a single training run.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To ensure that practical differential privacy guarantees hold in ML, there is a need for efficient, informative empirical audits. Existing one-run methods reduce information to binary canary membership, wasting information; this work aims to extract more information from a single run to bound privacy leakage tighter.

Method: Model signals aligned with canaries are treated as a sequence of random variables. The normalized sum of these signals is shown to be asymptotically Gaussian in the white-box DP-SGD setting. This distributional insight is used to construct a DP-auditing framework that infers tighter privacy lower bounds from one training run.

Result: The proposed auditing framework yields tighter (larger) lower bounds on the privacy loss from a single DP-SGD run than prior one-run methods, improving the efficiency and informativeness of empirical audits.

Conclusion: A distributional perspective on canary-aligned signals enables effective one-run DP auditing for DP-SGD, providing tighter privacy guarantees from a single training run and avoiding information loss inherent in binary thresholding approaches.

Abstract: Privacy auditing provides an important safeguard by estimating the actual information leaked by a model, thus ensuring that theoretical privacy guarantees hold in practice. We study empirical privacy auditing for differentially private (DP) machine learning, focusing on efficient one-run methods for mechanisms such as DP-SGD. Prior one-run approaches threshold training examples or "canaries" into binary membership guesses, which discards useful information. We show that, in the white-box DP-SGD setting, canary-aligned signals naturally form a sequence of random variables whose normalized sum is asymptotically Gaussian. Leveraging this distributional perspective, we develop a DP-auditing framework that leads to tighter privacy lower bounds from a single training run.

</details>


### [118] [Physics-Informed Neural Networks and Radial Basis Functions for PDEs with Dirac Delta Sources](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12735)
*Manuel Reyna,Alexandre Tartakovsky*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RBF-RLS outperforms PINNs for PDEs with Dirac delta forcing by directly handling deltas through the weak form; NTK analysis explains the discrepancy; experiments on linear groundwater and transport problems show robust forward/inverse results with RBF-RLS across synthetic, noisy, and real data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address modeling error from smoothing Delta functions in PINNs and provide a direct weak-form treatment; assess whether alternative Residual Least Squares methods, especially RBF-based, offer superior performance for delta-containing PDEs.

Method: Treat PINNs as residual least-squares (RLS) and integrate the weak-form to handle Dirac delta terms. Compare with Radial Basis Function (RBF) expansion-based RLS (single-layer RBF network). Analyze using Neural Tangent Kernel (NTK) theory. Validate on linear PDEs modeling groundwater flow and transport in porous media and rivers. Solve inverse problems with synthetic, noisy, and real data.

Result: Integrating Dirac deltas in PINNs causes residuals to fail to converge to zero; RBF-RLS maintains good forward and inverse accuracy for transport-like PDEs. NTK reasoning explains the observed behavior. Across linear PDE tests with synthetic, noisy, and real data, RBF-RLS provides robust results, while PINN-based approaches under delta smoothing exhibit larger errors.

Conclusion: RBF-RLS offers a more reliable framework than PINNs for PDEs involving Dirac delta terms, due to proper delta handling via weak-form integration and favorable NTK properties. PINNs remain susceptible to modeling errors from delta approximations; RBF-RLS demonstrates consistent forward/inverse performance for groundwater flow and transport problems.

Abstract: Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) are a machine learning method for solving forward and inverse Partial Differential Equations (PDEs). When applied to PDEs with Dirac delta functions in the forcing terms, boundary conditions, or initial conditions, PINNs require approximating them with smooth surrogate functions, a practice that can introduce significant modeling errors. In this work, we exploit the interpretation of PINNs as Residual Least Squares (RLS) methods and show that this perspective enables direct treatment of Dirac delta terms by integrating the weak-form equation. Among RLS formulations other than PINN, we focus on the Radial Basis Function (RBF) expansion (also known as a single-layer RBF Network). We show that while integrating out the Dirac delta in PINNs causes residuals to fail to converge to zero, RBF-RLS consistently provides good forward and inverse solutions to transport problems. We explain this finding using the Neural Tangent Kernel (NTK) theory. We test both approaches on linear PDEs that represent groundwater flow and transport in porous media and rivers. We solve inverse problems to fit synthetic data, noisy synthetic data, and real-world measurements.

</details>


### [119] [Deep Unfolded Latent Optimally Partitioned-l2/l1 Networks for Data-driven Block-Sparse Recovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12740)
*Takanobu Furuhashi,Hidekata Hontani,Qibin Zhao,Tatsuya Yokota*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Two DU-enabled architectures for latent optimal partition (LOP) with l2/l1: a stable implicit-differentiation framework and a flexible deep weight factorization (DWF) variant for unknown partitions and block-sparse recovery; address hyperparameter tuning and proximal-operator instability, achieving competitive performance and robustness to impulsive noise.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Manual hyperparameter tuning and instability in differentiating the proximal operator limit automatic parameter optimization of convex LOP-l2/l1 for block-sparse recovery with unknown partitions.

Method: Introduce two architectures: (1) a stable framework using implicit differentiation; (2) a flexible DWF-based variant that supports nonconvex smooth data fidelity terms and facilitates automatic tuning.

Result: Numerical experiments show that DU-LOP-l2/l1 attains competitive performance and strong resilience to impulsive noise.

Conclusion: The proposed DU-LOP-l2/l1 architectures address tuning and differentiation limitations of LOP-l2/l1, enabling stable automatic tuning and flexible data-fidelity modeling with competitive results.

Abstract: The convex Latent Optimal Partition (LOP)-l2/l1 approach enables block-sparse signal recovery with unknown partitions but relies on manual hyperparameter tuning. Additionally, numerical instability in differentiating its proximal operator prevents its automatic parameter tuning via Deep Unfolding (DU). To address these limitations, we propose two architectures: a stable framework utilizing implicit differentiation and a flexible variant leveraging Deep Weight Factorization (DWF). The DWF-based approach also supports nonconvex smooth data fidelity terms. Numerical experiments demonstrate that DU-LOP-l2/l1 yields competitive performance and high resilience against impulsive noise.

</details>


### [120] [Adaptive Weighted Averaging](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12763)
*Aditya Bhaskara,Ashok Cutkosky,Ravi Kumar,Manish Purohit*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Admits and analyzes strategies for selecting the maximum from n unknown values with one unbiased noisy reading per item; constructs strategies that are not uniformly dominated and are never worse than a baseline (e.g., uniform random). Shows an application to stochastic optimization: online-to-batch bounds with a no-compromise guarantee, i.e., never worse than random selection and potentially much better in benign settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To robustly identify the maximum among noisy evaluations while ensuring that no strategy is uniformly better than all others and that performance is never worse than simple baselines, with practical relevance to stochastic optimization where online-to-batch guarantees are desirable yet safe.

Method: Develop a class of selection strategies (admissible and nondominated) for choosing the largest x_i from unbiased estimates y_i. Analyze performance relative to a baseline using dominance concepts and risk bounds. Extend the approach to an online-to-batch setting in stochastic optimization by deriving bounds that guarantee no worse performance than random iterate selection, while allowing improved performance in favorable (benign) scenarios.

Result: Existence of strategies that are simultaneously admissible and sample-efficient relative to a baseline, plus online-to-batch conversion bounds with a no-compromise guarantee. In benign settings, these bounds can yield substantially better performance than standard random iterate selection while never underperforming the baseline.

Conclusion: The paper provides a robust selection framework under noisy information that preserves baseline safety and avoids domination, with practical implications for stochastic optimization through no-compromise online-to-batch bounds. This invites further exploration of admissible, baseline-guaranteed strategies in online learning and randomized optimization.

Abstract: We study the problem of selecting the largest among $n$ unknown values $x_1,\dots,x_n$ given only a single unbiased estimate $y_i$ for each $x_i$. We design strategies that are simultaneously admissible (not uniformly dominated by any other strategy) and also never worse than a given baseline such as uniform random selection. We provide an application to stochastic optimization, where we obtain online-to-batch conversion bounds with a desirable "no-compromise" guarantee: they are never worse than standard random iterate selection, and yet can be significantly better in benign settings.

</details>


### [121] [Detecting Functional Memorization in Code Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12764)
*Matthieu Meeus,Anil Ramakrishna,Matthew Grange,Zheng Xu,Luca Melis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Evidence of functional memorization in code generation; textual overlap alone underestimates leakage.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the risk that training data could influence code outputs at a functional level, not just through verbatim text; existing audits miss function-level leakage.

Method: Construct counterfactual setup using Olmo-3-32B; compare midtrained model (exposed to target code) with pretrained reference; prompt both with Python function signatures; assess textual similarity and functional similarity via LLM-as-a-judge and execution-based evaluation.

Result: Demonstrates functional memorization: models reveal target code behavior/functionality beyond exact text; textual overlap metrics understate leakage.

Conclusion: Auditing must include functional/tests beyond text overlap; mitigations may be needed to curb functional memorization.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used to generate code at scale. Meanwhile, prior work has investigated whether training data may be recoverable from model outputs, by auditing the textual overlap between training examples and model generations. Code, however, can be functionally equivalent while textually dissimilar. In this work, we study functional memorization: extraction of functional logic beyond what verbatim metrics detect. We construct a counterfactual setup for Olmo-3-32B, comparing a midtrained model (exposed to target code) against a pretrained reference (not exposed). We prompt both models with Python function signatures and measure both textual and functional similarity (i.e., LLM-as-a-judge, execution-based). Our results show clear evidence of functional memorization, highlighting the need for auditing metrics that go beyond textual overlap.

</details>


### [122] [ProPlay: Procedural World Models for Self-Evolving LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12780)
*Yijun Ma,Zehong Wang,Yiyang Li,Ziming Li,Xiaoguang Guo,Weixiang Sun,Chuxu Zhang,Yanfang Ye*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ProPlay introduces a procedural world model with a graph of procedures to preplay and refine for self-evolving agents in partially observable environments; yields consistent performance gains and provides open-source code.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Agents in partially observable environments must explore, learn from sparse feedback, and decide when to rely on past experience. Existing LLM-agent approaches often separate memory and planning without a closed loop to continually refine environment dynamics.

Method: Procedural world model with procedure-level preplay. It abstracts successful trajectories into procedures organized in a procedure graph with causal transitions between task stages. Each transition has a reliability embedding to estimate its contribution from past outcomes. Before episodes, the model simulates future procedural trajectories on known graph structures as structured soft guidance; after execution, it refines the graph using environment feedback.

Result: Experiments on public benchmarks show ProPlay consistently improves environment understanding and self-evolution capability relative to strong baselines. Code released at https://github.com/antman9914/proplay.

Conclusion: ProPlay provides a looped mechanism where planning, memory, and world dynamics are unified into a revisable procedural graph, enabling continual self-improvement in partially observable tasks.

Abstract: Self-evolving agents are expected to improve through interaction without external supervision, but this remains difficult in partially observable environments where agents must explore actively, learn from limited feedback, and decide when to trust prior experience. Existing LLM-agent methods often rely on memory or planning modules, yet they rarely close the loop between them to continually refine an internal understanding of environment dynamics. We introduce ProPlay, a procedural world model that supports procedure-level preplay, where agents can rehearse future procedural paths using the learned world knowledge. Rather than representing experience as isolated rules or low-level action constraints, ProPlay abstracts successful trajectories into procedures and organizes them in a procedure graph that captures causal transitions among task stages. Each transition is associated with a reliability record embedding to estimate its task-specific contribution from past outcomes. Before each episode, ProPlay simulates future procedural trajectories over known graph structures as structured soft guidance; after execution, it refines the graph using environment feedback. Experiments on public benchmarks show that ProPlay consistently improves environment understanding and self-evolution capability over strong baselines. Our code has been released in https://github.com/antman9914/proplay.

</details>


### [123] [SymQNet: Amortized Acquisition for Low-Latency Adaptive Hamiltonian Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12808)
*Yash Vardhan Tomar,Dheeraj Peddireddy,Vaneet Aggarwal*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces SymQNet, an amortized reinforcement-learning approach to low-latency adaptive Hamiltonian learning, achieving large reductions in experiment-design latency on transverse-field Ising benchmarks and enabling practical adaptive workloads.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Adaptive Hamiltonian learning requires frequent design of the next experiment; recomputing Bayesian designs after each update incurs nontrivial wall-clock time, hindering real-time adaptivity in multi-shot experiments.

Method: Train offline a posterior-conditioned acquisition policy via reinforcement learning (amortized). Use a fast online policy forward pass to select the next experiment while maintaining Bayesian posterior feedback. Evaluate on transverse-field Ising benchmarks (five and twelve qubits), comparing to bounded Fisher-information search and bounded two-step BALD.

Result: Significant reductions in acquisition latency: at five qubits, 47.1× and 72.6× faster than the online baselines BALD and Fisher-information methods; at twelve qubits, full simulated steps take 1.02 s (SymQNet) versus 13.27 s (bounded BALD). Overall, demonstrates practicality of low-latency adaptive Hamiltonian learning.

Conclusion: Learned acquisition policies can render adaptive Hamiltonian learning feasible for repeated, low-latency workloads while preserving Bayesian feedback through amortization.

Abstract: Adaptive Hamiltonian learning is central to calibrating and characterizing quantum devices. In an adaptive controller, choosing the next experiment is itself a computation. Bayesian design rules are recomputed after every posterior update, and that step can take seconds. Across hundreds of shots, those seconds become a significant wall-clock cost for adaptivity. We introduce SymQNet, an amortized reinforcement-learning approach for low-latency adaptive Hamiltonian learning. SymQNet learns a posterior-conditioned acquisition policy offline, then uses a fast policy forward pass online while retaining Bayesian posterior feedback. On transverse-field Ising benchmarks, SymQNet substantially reduces acquisition latency relative to bounded Fisher-information search and bounded two-step Bayesian active learning by disagreement (BALD). At five qubits, it reduces acquisition-only decision latency by $47.1\times$ and $72.6\times$ relative to these online baselines; at twelve qubits, full simulated steps take $1.02$ s for SymQNet versus $13.27$ s for bounded two-step BALD. Overall, we show that learned acquisition can make adaptive Hamiltonian learning practical for repeated low-latency workloads.

</details>


### [124] [Scale Buys Interpolation, Structure Buys a Horizon: Certified Predictability for Equivariant World Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13092)
*Hongbo Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Equivariant latent world models enable a computable, symmetry-aware horizon certificate for multi-step predictions, with orbit-wise constancy and Lyapunov-spectrum stratification; empirically validated to recover full spectrum only for Z_N-equivariant networks, and capable of calibration-free auditing across public pretrained models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current practice uses average prediction error; no guarantee about per-prediction trust or horizon length. A structured certificate tied to symmetries offers provable, interpretable bounds on rollout accuracy and a principled way to audit deployed models.

Method: Introduce a T-step rollout error certificate that is constant over symmetry orbits (Theorem A) and stratified by the predictor's Lyapunov spectrum, T_j(ε) ~ log(1/ε)/λ_j. Prove two-sided horizon bounds; show exclusivity to equivariant structure. Empirically evaluate on 40-D Lorenz-96 with Z_N symmetry; compare to dense and recurrent baselines. Extend to scale by calibration-free auditing across pretrained models (TD-MPC2, 1M–317M multitask ladder, V-JEPA 2-AC) and analyze cross-checks.

Result: The certificate achieves orbit-constant error across symmetry orbits and Lyapunov-spectrum–driven horizon scaling; Z_N-equivariant nets recover the full spectrum (R^2=0.98) while dense/recurrent baselines fail. The certificate implies calibration-free budgeting: under fixed sensing budget, c× inflated certificate requires c× budget; equivariant certificates beat inflated dense models. Readouts audit pretrained models training-free and map to a spectrum-consistent taxonomy; cross-checks override misleading tangent spectra in real robot data. Across large multitask models, calibration does not improve with parameters; cross-validated audits provide deployable trust.

Conclusion: Scale alone improves interpolation but does not create a calibrated horizon without exploiting structure. The horizon certificate is a structural property (orbit-constant error) that is not present in non-equivariant models, enabling provable horizon limits and calibration-free auditing for deployed systems.

Abstract: Scale buys interpolation; structure buys a certified horizon. A world model's average error says nothing about whether a particular prediction can be trusted, or for how long. For equivariant latent world models we give a computable, multi-step certificate of the predictable horizon: $T$-step rollout error is provably constant over each symmetry orbit (Theorem A) and stratified channel-by-channel by the predictor's Lyapunov spectrum, $T_j(ε)\sim\log(1/ε)/λ_j$. The horizon is two-sided -- a matching lower bound makes approximate equivariance provably horizon-limited -- and the certificate is exclusive to structure: orbit-constant error characterizes equivariance, so no non-equivariant model has it at any scale. Empirically, on 40-D Lorenz-96 only a $\mathbb{Z}_N$-equivariant network recovers the full Lyapunov spectrum ($R^2{=}0.98$); dense and recurrent baselines fail.
  Because the spectrum is faithful, the certificate acts, a priori: under a fixed sensing budget a $c\times$-inflated certificate provably needs $c\times$ the budget, and the equivariant certificate meets a budget its inflated dense counterpart cannot -- with zero calibration data. The same read-out, unchanged, audits public pretrained world models training-free: TD-MPC2 checkpoints land on the certificate's own scope taxonomy -- calibrated where strongly expansive (ratio 0.94-1.02), optimistic where weakly expansive, correctly abstaining where contracting -- a map a deployed monitor replicates cell-by-cell, out-of-sample. Across the official 1M-317M multitask ladder, calibration does not improve with parameters. On V-JEPA 2-AC (1B, real robot data) the measured cross-check correctly overrides an over-promising tangent spectrum -- the cross-validated audit, not the raw number, is the deployable object. Scale buys interpolation, not a calibrated horizon.

</details>


### [125] [CLARITree: Cholesky and Lookahead Accelerations for Regression with Interpretable Piecewise Linear Trees](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12840)
*Yixiao Wang,Hayden McTavish,Varun Babbar,Margo Seltzer,Cynthia Rudin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Near-optimal, sparse, piecewise linear regression trees using a lookahead search together with rank-one Cholesky updates to the Gram matrix, yielding better accuracy, sparsity, and scalability than greedy or existing optimal methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Regression trees are interpretable but balancing performance with efficiency is hard. Optimal methods are often too slow for general linear regression trees, while lookahead improves performance in classification; there is a need for scalable near-optimal methods for sparse linear regression trees in regression settings.

Method: A novel algorithm that fuses lookahead-style search with efficient rank-one Cholesky updates of the Gram matrix to build sparse, piecewise linear regression trees. The approach enables near-optimal splits and leaf models with linear regressors while maintaining computational efficiency.

Result: Theoretically analyzed and empirically validated to achieve a favorable trade-off among computation, predictive accuracy, and sparsity, and to scale significantly better than current state-of-the-art methods for regression trees.

Conclusion: The proposed lookahead-enabled, rank-one Cholesky-based approach delivers scalable, accurate, and sparse regression trees, outperforming greedy baselines and existing optimal methods in terms of efficiency and performance.

Abstract: Regression trees are among the most interpretable yet expressive model classes in machine learning. Historically, greedy induction has been the dominant approach for constructing well-performing regression trees. While optimal methods based on dynamic programming and branch-and-bound exist, they are computationally prohibitive for general linear regression trees, despite often achieving substantially better performance than greedy approaches. Recent work has shown that specialized lookahead strategies can dramatically improve runtime while maintaining near-optimal performance, primarily in classification settings. In this work, we develop a novel algorithm for near-optimal, sparse, piecewise linear regression trees that combines a lookahead-style search strategy with efficient rank-one Cholesky updates of the Gram matrix. We demonstrate, both theoretically and empirically, that our method achieves a favorable trade-off between computational efficiency, predictive accuracy, and sparsity, and scales significantly better than the current state of the art.

</details>


### [126] [TimeROME-DLM: Temporal Causal Tracing and Low-Rank Inference-Time Knowledge Editing for Masked Diffusion Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12841)
*Zhengtao Yao,Liuyang Song,Hongbo Zhang,Chenhao Wei,Haoyan Xu,Guang Yang,Siheng Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TimeROME-DLM is a training-free, gradient-free framework for editing knowledge in masked diffusion language models (MDLMs). It uses Temporal Indirect Effect (TIE) causal tracing to locate the causal coordinates for each fact and a closed-form, low-rank residual edit memory to apply a single ridge-regularized update at those coordinates during diffusion steps, with sparsification to limit spillover. Backbone weights remain frozen; only three hyperparameters (alpha, lambda, q) are tuned. The approach delivers large forgetting reductions, cross-model transfer, and substantial speedups with negligible extra VRAM.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing knowledge-editing methods (e.g., ROME, MEMIT) target autoregressive transformers and struggle with MDLMs due to iterative denoising, gradient-based updates, and high VRAM costs. There is a need for a training-free, inference-time, gradient-free editing method that works across different MDLMs while preserving unrelated knowledge.

Method: Two components: (1) Temporal Indirect Effect (TIE) causal tracing to identify, for each fact, the coordinate whose intervention most strongly influences later denoising predictions; (2) a closed-form, low-rank residual edit memory that aggregates subject keys and target deltas for all forget facts and applies a single ridge-regularized update at the identified coordinate on every diffusion forward step, with sparsification to minimize utility spillover. Backbone weights are frozen; edits are controlled by three hyperparameters (alpha, lambda, q).

Result: On TOFU forget01 with TOFU-finetuned LLaDA-8B-Base, TimeROME-DLM reduces forget-set log-probability by ~83 nats.
