The same configuration transfers to LLaDA-8B-Instruct, Dream-7B, MMaDA-8B, DiffuLLaMA-7B, and LLaDA-MoE-1.4B. Retain-set log-probability remains nearly flat (within ~1 nat) across 50 sequential edits. The method delivers 4–14x wall-clock speedup with zero additional VRAM and scales sub-linearly to 400 facts, closing the gap between MDLMs and AR LLMs at a fraction of the compute.

Conclusion: TimeROME-DLM demonstrates a training-free, gradient-free approach to inference-time knowledge editing for MDLMs that preserves unrelated knowledge, scales across models, and significantly reduces computation relative to training-based baselines, thereby narrowing the gap with AR LLMs.

Abstract: Masked diffusion language models (MDLMs) such as LLaDA now rival autoregressive (AR) LLMs, but every existing knowledge-editing and unlearning method (ROME, MEMIT, etc.) targets AR transformers and either makes assumptions that fail under iterative denoising, or requires gradient updates whose backward-pass activations cost tens of GB of extra VRAM and which collapse MDLMs at standard learning rates. We introduce TimeROME-DLM, the first training-free, gradient-free, inference-time knowledge-editing framework for MDLMs. It couples two components: a Temporal Indirect Effect (TIE) causal-tracing protocol that identifies, for each fact, the coordinate whose intervention most strongly drives the object prediction at later denoising steps; and a closed-form, low-rank residual edit memory that aggregates subject keys and target deltas across all forget facts and applies a single ridge-regularised update at that coordinate at every diffusion forward, with sparsification to limit utility spillover. Backbone weights stay frozen; only three hyperparameters (alpha, lambda, q) are tuned on a small validation split. On TOFU forget01 with TOFU-finetuned LLaDA-8B-Base, TimeROME-DLM cuts forget-set log-probability by roughly 83 nats. The same configuration transfers to LLaDA-8B-Instruct, Dream-7B, MMaDA-8B, DiffuLLaMA-7B, and LLaDA-MoE-1.4B. It keeps retain-set log-probability nearly flat (within ~1 nat at the utility-safe operating point) across 50 sequentially inserted facts, delivers a four- to fourteen-fold wall-clock speedup with zero additional VRAM over the strongest converged training-time baseline, and scales sub-linearly to 400 facts. TimeROME-DLM closes the locate-then-edit gap between AR LLMs and MDLMs at a fraction of the computational cost.

</details>


### [127] [PolyFlow: Safe and Efficient Polytope-Constrained Flow Matching with Constraint Embedding and Projection-free Update](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13400)
*Jianming Ma,Qiyue Yang,Yang Zhang,Liyun Yan,Zhanxiang Cao,Yazhou Zhang,Yue Gao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A polytope-constrained flow matching framework (PolyFlow) that embeds polyhedral constraints into discrete-time flow dynamics, achieving zero constraint violation with projection-free architecture and reduced inference latency, suitable for safety-critical planning/control; competitive distributional fidelity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Safety-critical physical systems require strict constraint satisfaction; post-hoc corrections are costly and can distort learned distributions; need direct integration of constraints into generative models.

Method: Introduce a discrete-time flow formulation with a projection-free mechanism to enforce arbitrary polyhedral constraints directly in the flow dynamics, eliminating discretization error and avoiding expensive solvers.

Result: Zero constraint violation across tested tasks, high distributional fidelity, significantly reduced inference latency compared to constrained generation baselines, favorable trade-off among safety, efficiency, and generative quality.

Conclusion: PolyFlow provides a practical and efficient solution for hard-constraint generative modeling in safety-critical domains; demonstrates strong safety-accuracy-performance balance and scalable, real-time applicability.

Abstract: While flow-based generative models have demonstrated strong performance across a wide range of domains, deploying them in safety-critical physical systems remains challenging due to strict constraint requirements. Existing approaches typically enforce safety through post-hoc corrections, which incur substantial computational overhead and may distort the learned distribution. We propose PolyFlow, a polytope-constrained flow matching framework that embeds constraints directly into the model and flow dynamics. PolyFlow introduces a discrete-time flow formulation and a projection-free architecture, which eliminate the discretization error and guarantee strict satisfaction of arbitrary polyhedral constraints, without the need for expensive iterative solvers. Experimental results show that PolyFlow achieves zero constraint violation while maintaining high distributional fidelity across a range of planning and control tasks. Compared to state-of-the-art constrained generation baselines, PolyFlow significantly reduces inference latency and demonstrates a favorable trade-off between safety, efficiency, and generative quality. Code is available on https://github.com/MJianM/PolyFlow.

</details>


### [128] [Interpretable Factor Decomposition for Decision Intelligence in Large-Scale Financial Markets: Evidence from China's A-Share Market](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12843)
*Xiao Han,Yao Xiao,Zhen Zhang,Moxuan Zheng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: An interpretable ML pipeline using XGBoost with TreeSHAP to decompose cross-sectional equity return predictability into auditable factor contributions, applied to 3,632 Chinese A-share stocks (2009-2019).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To quantify and audibly attribute predictive power to interpretable factors, separating behavioral signals from valuation signals, using SHAP and ablation analyses, and to test robustness through cross-validation and Carhart-adjusted performance.

Method: XGBoost model with TreeSHAP attribution; rolling 60-month training windows, 55 months of out-of-sample data; 3,632 stocks; stress testing; long-short spread top vs bottom quintiles; evaluation with AUC, monthly returns, Newey-West t-statistics; Carhart four-factor model adjustment; ablation analysis.

Result: Mean out-of-sample AUC 0.547; +2.38%/month long-short return; NW t = 5.94; annualized Sharpe 2.23; after Carhart +2.31%/month; t = 7.48; SHAP attributes 58.2% of predictive attribution to behavioral signals (turnover, momentum) vs 10.7% to valuation ratios; ablation validates the ranking and reveals feature substitutability not captured by either method.

Conclusion: Demonstrates an interpretable ML framework for decomposing cross-sectional equity predictability into auditable factor contributions, highlighting behavioral signals as primary drivers and offering a robust cross-validated approach via SHAP and ablation analysis.

Abstract: We present an interpretable machine learning pipeline to decompose Cross-Sectional Equity Return Predictability into auditable factor contribution. We apply an XGBoost model with TreeSHAP attribution and conduct stress testing on 3632 Chinese A-share stocks from 2009 until 2019. Using 60-month, rolling windows over 55 months of out-of-sample data, XGBoost obtains a mean AUC of 0.547 and +2.38%/month (Newey-West t = 5.94; Annualized Sharpe 2.23) long-short spread for the top vs bottom quintiles. This alpha is persistent after adjusting for the Carhart four-factor model (+2.31%/month; t = 7.48). SHAP Decomposition indicates that behavioral signals (turnover and momentum) account for 58.2% of predictive attribution compared to 10.7% for valuation ratios, on average, across 55 industry groups. Ablation analysis serves to cross-validate this ranking and provides evidence that SHAP and ablation diverge in a manner that highlights feature substitutability structure that is largely invisible to either method used in isolation.

</details>


### [129] [Multimodal Graph Negative Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12863)
*Zhengyu Wu,Xu Wang,Hongchao Qin,Xunkai Li,Guang Zeng,Rong-Hua Li,Guoren Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GraphMNL uses graph-aware negative learning to handle branch semantic imbalance in multimodal attributed graphs (MAGs), achieving state-of-the-art on Grocery and Reddit M datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In MAGs, different modality branches (topology, text, images) vary in reliability. Forced cross-branch imitation can propagate bias from a dominant but biased branch, degrading performance. A decoupled supervision strategy is needed.

Method: Introduce a branch library and graph-aware reliability arbitration to identify dominant vs inferior branches, gate unstable transfer, and apply target-preserving Negative Learning across non-target classes. This decouples target supervision from branch guidance, allowing supervised losses to learn the correct class while Negative Learning suppresses unlikely alternatives when branch agreement is unreliable.

Result: GraphMNL achieves state-of-the-art performance on Grocery datasets (72.47% accuracy) and Reddit M datasets (76.60 F1).

Conclusion: Decoupling target supervision from cross-branch guidance via Negative Learning effectively handles node-level semantic imbalance in MAGs, improving multimodal graph classification performance.

Abstract: Multimodal attributed graphs (MAGs) integrate graph topology with heterogeneous modality attributes, such as text and images, thereby enabling richer modeling of complex relational systems. However, such expressiveness also makes learning on MAGs depend on multiple semantic sources, including structural topology, textual and visual attributes, each of which can be regarded as a branch for node representation. Node-level branch semantic imbalance arises when these branches differ across nodes in semantic informativeness and reliability: a branch that provides discriminative semantics for one node may mislead another due to bias in modality quality or structural context. Existing methods often mitigate such heterogeneity through cross-branch agreement or alignment, implicitly treating the dominant prediction as reliable supervision. When the dominant branch is biased, forced imitation may propagate its bias to other branches and suppress original semantics that are useful for classification. We propose GraphMNL, a graph-aware multimodal negative learning framework that addresses this issue by using Negative Learning as cross-branch guidance. Instead of forcing inferior branches to imitate a teacher prediction, the model teaches them which classes a node is unlikely to belong to. GraphMNL builds a branch library, identifies dominant and inferior branches via graph-aware reliability arbitration, gates unstable transfer, and applies target-preserving negative learning over non-target classes. This design decouples target supervision from branch guidance so that supervised losses learn the correct class, while Negative Learning suppresses unlikely alternatives when branch agreement is unreliable. Through the comprehensive experimental evaluation, GraphMNL achieves the best performance on Grocery datasets with 72.47% accuracy and 76.60 F1 score on Reddit M datasets.

</details>


### [130] [SMGFM: Spectral Multimodal Graph Pretraining for Multimodal-Attributed Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12867)
*Zhengyu Wu,Xu Wang,Hongchao Qin,Xunkai Li,Guang Zeng,Rong-Hua Li,Guoren Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SMGFM is a spectral multimodal graph pretraining framework that decomposes modality signals into graph-frequency bands and uses topology-conditioned routing for cross-modal interaction, achieving state-of-the-art on multimodal attributed graphs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In MAGs, structure-induced semantics and modality-intrinsic semantics can diverge; naive coupling risks over-smoothing or misalignment of semantic information.

Method: Decompose each modality signal into graph-frequency bands via scalable Chebyshev filters; estimate coupling reliability with topology-conditioned routing; conduct band-wise interactions before fusion; optimize frequency-routed objectives to align consensus routes while preserving modality-specific routes.

Result: Empirical results on MAG datasets demonstrate state-of-the-art performance for both graph-level and modality-level tasks.

Conclusion: Frequency-aware, band-level interaction with topology-conditioned routing mitigates spatial-domain entanglement and enhances cross-modal fusion in MAG pretraining.

Abstract: Multimodal-attributed graphs (MAGs) couple graph topology with node semantics from text, images, and other modalities. Traditional graph learning contextualizes node semantics by coupling topology with node features. However, this coupling design becomes troublesome in MAGs, where structure-induced and modality-intrinsic semantics may contribute differently to downstream tasks. Structure-induced semantics promote relational consistency through smooth topological variation, whereas modality-intrinsic semantics often encode local, fine-grained distinctions that should not be uniformly smoothed or aligned. Therefore, the key challenge is to identify semantic roles before cross-modal fusion. To this end, we leverage graph-frequency variation as a prior, where low-frequency components capture topology-consistent semantics and high-frequency components preserve modality-specific semantics. Based on this intuition, we propose SMGFM, a spectral multimodal graph pretraining framework that decomposes each modality-specific node signal into graph-frequency bands and assigns band-level semantic roles before cross-modal interaction. Concretely, SMGFM constructs frequency-resolved modality tokens with scalable Chebyshev filters, estimates their coupling reliability through topology-conditioned routing, and performs band-modality interaction before fusion. Its frequency-routed objectives align smooth consensus routes while preserving modality-specific routes, mitigating spatial-domain entanglement and uniform cross-modal alignment. Extensive experiments conducted on the MAG datasets demonstrate that SMGFM achieves state-of-the-art performance across graph-level and modality-level tasks.

</details>


### [131] [Multi-Bitwidth Quantization for LLMs Using Additive Codebooks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12876)
*Liza Babaoglu,Shuangyi Chen,Ashish Khisti*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Drop-by-Drop is a post-training quantization framework enabling inference-time multi-bitwidth control from a single trained model by learning additive codebooks with Matryoshka-style supervision. The weights are assumed Gaussian and reconstructed with increasing fidelity via successive refinement of encoded bits, optimizing a weighted MSE aligned with LLM loss. It provides efficient multi-bitwidth support without retraining, reducing storage, and preserving accuracy across Qwen, LLaMA, Gemma, and Mistral.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large language models run on heterogeneous hardware with different resource constraints; retraining for each hardware target is impractical. There is a need for a single model that can adapt precision at inference time to balance performance and efficiency.

Method: Post-training quantization using multiple additive codebooks arranged in a Matryoshka-style supervision framework. Weights (assumed Gaussian) are encoded into ordered codebook subsets; a weighted MSE loss guided by LLM loss functions drives learning so that partial reconstructions at each precision level yield accurate performance. The method relies on information-theoretic successive refinement to ensure fidelity improves with added bits, enabling multi-bitwidth inference without retraining.

Result: A single checkpoint can deliver multiple bitwidths by using ordered subsets of codebooks, reducing storage and memory overhead while maintaining competitive perplexity and accuracy across Qwen, LLaMA, Gemma, and Mistral.

Conclusion: Drop-by-Drop demonstrates practical, theory-grounded multi-bitwidth quantization for LLMs, enabling adaptive precision at inference without retraining and maintaining accuracy across diverse architectures.

Abstract: As large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed across heterogeneous hardware with varying resource constraints, the ability to adaptively manage the trade-off between performance and efficiency without retraining is critical. We propose Drop-by-Drop, a novel multi-bitwidth post-training quantization framework that enables inference-time precision control over LLM weights from a single trained model. Our method is theoretically grounded in information theory and successive refinement. We establish that LLM weights, which commonly follow a Gaussian distribution, can be optimally reconstructed with increasing fidelity as additional bits are incorporated, under a weighted mean squared error distortion motivated by LLM loss functions. To realize this in practice, Drop-by-Drop incorporates Matryoshka-style supervision into the loss function, exploiting the structure of additive codebooks. Drop-by-Drop produces a single model where ordered subsets of codebooks yield accurate partial reconstructions at each precision level. This approach significantly reduces storage and memory overhead by allowing a single checkpoint to serve multiple bitwidths, while maintaining competitive perplexity and accuracy across major architectures, such as Qwen, LLaMA, Gemma, and Mistral.

</details>


### [132] [LongSpike: Fractional Order Spiking State Space Models for Efficient Long Sequence Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12895)
*Xinrui He,Qiyu Kang,Xuhao Li,Zheng-Jun Zha*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LongSpike introduces fractional-order state-space modeling (f-SSM) into spiking neural networks to capture long-range dependencies with long-memory kernels while preserving sparse computation; it achieves superior accuracy on long-sequence benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome the memoryless bottleneck of traditional first-order SNNs that hindering long-range dependency modeling; leverage fractional calculus and state-space methods for enhanced temporal dynamics and efficiency.

Method: Extend integer-order state-space models to fractional calculus within the SNN framework; implement LongSpike with hierarchical coupling to embed long-memory kernels; use a state-space formulation to enable parallel training and scalable computation; evaluate on LRA, WikiText-103, and Speech Commands; provide open-source code.

Result: LongSpike outperforms state-of-the-art SNNs in accuracy on challenging benchmarks (LRA, WikiText-103, Speech Commands) while preserving sparse synaptic computation, indicating improved long-range sequence modeling without sacrificing efficiency.

Conclusion: Fractional-order state-space modeling is a viable and effective approach to enhance SNNs’ capacity for long-range dependencies, offering improved accuracy with maintained sparsity; the work provides a scalable, trainable framework and shared code for replication.

Abstract: Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs) are well-regarded for their biological plausibility and energy efficiency in processing sequential data. However, dominant SNN architectures typically rely on first-order Ordinary Differential Equations (ODEs) to govern neuronal state transitions. This first-order assumption imposes a "memoryless" bottleneck, limiting the model's capacity to capture the complex, long-range dependencies inherent in long-sequence tasks. In this work, we propose LongSpike, a novel SNN framework that integrates fractional-order State-Space Modeling, or f-SSM, from control theory into the spiking domain. By extending traditional integer-order SSMs to the fractional-calculus regime, LongSpike enables the hierarchical integration of neuronal dynamics with long-memory kernels. To mitigate the computational overhead and parallelization challenges typically associated with fractional operators, we leverage a state-space formulation that supports efficient, parallel training. Empirical evaluations on challenging benchmarks, including Long Range Arena (LRA), large-scale WikiText-103, and Speech Commands, demonstrate that LongSpike outperforms state-of-the-art SNNs in accuracy while preserving sparse synaptic computation. The code is available at https://github.com/xinruihe389-commits/LongSpike.

</details>


### [133] [PolicyGuard: Towards Test-time and Step-level Adversary Defense for Reinforcement Learning Agent](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12896)
*Junfeng Guo Heng Huang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PolicyGuard is a test-time, step-level backdoor defense for RL based on Gaussian Process posterior variance; it detects backdoors by estimating per-time-step uncertainty via adapted pseudo trajectories, with theoretical justification and strong empirical AUROC ~0.856-0.859 across seven RL games, outperforming existing defenses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Backdoor attacks threaten RL systems; existing defenses have limitations (parameter access, model/trajectory granularity, or limited attack types). A per-time-step, test-time defense is needed to detect malicious triggers in real time.

Method: Compute per-step uncertainty via GP posterior variance by constructing pseudo trajectories to enable per-time-step uncertainty estimation; use this to detect backdoor-triggered actions during test time; provide theoretical analysis for the efficacy of GP posterior variance.

Result: PolicyGuard achieves state-of-the-art detection on seven RL games, with average AUROC of 0.856 for perturbation-based attacks and 0.859 for adversary-agent attacks.

Conclusion: A novel, theoretically-grounded, test-time, step-level backdoor defense for RL that leverages GP uncertainty and pseudo trajectory adaptation; demonstrates strong empirical performance.

Abstract: While real-world applications of reinforcement learning (RL) are becoming increasingly popular, the security of RL systems deserve more attention and exploration. In particular, recent work has revealed that RL agents are vulnerable to backdoor attacks, where a victim agent behaves normally under standard conditions but executes malicious actions when a specific trigger is activated. Existing backdoor defenses for RL either require access to the agent's internal parameters, operate only at the model or trajectory level, or are limited to specific attack types. To ensure the security of RL agents, we propose \texttt{PolicyGuard}, a \textit{test-time step-level} backdoor defense which leverages Gaussian Process (GP) posterior variance and adapts pseudo trajectories to enable uncertainty computation for individual time step. Besides, we also provide theoretical foundations to explain the efficacy of GP posterior variance. Extensive experiments across seven RL games demonstrate that PolicyGuard achieves state-of-the-art detection performance in most cases, with average AUROC of 0.856 for perturbation-based attacks and 0.859 for adversary-agent attacks.

</details>


### [134] [Selecting Samples on Graphs: A Unified Dataset Pruning Framework for Lossless Training Acceleration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12913)
*Dongyue Wu,Zilin Guo,Xiaoyu Li,Jiajia Liu,Jingdong Chen,Nong Sang,Changxin Gao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A unified graph-based dataset pruning approach that models data as a weighted graph and solves a Maximum Weight Clique Problem (MWCP) to select informative samples, with a greedy algorithm offering approximation guarantees and strong empirical gains (e.g., 40%+ training time reduction on ImageNet-1k/ResNet-50) while preserving accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dataset pruning aims to reduce training cost in large-scale datasets; existing criteria capture limited aspects (intrinsic or extrinsic) and may lack robustness across pruning ratios and data distributions; a unified, theory-backed approach is needed.

Method: Construct a weighted data graph where node weights reflect intrinsic sample value and edge weights reflect extrinsic diversity; formulate pruning as MWCP; use a sample-wise marginal-gain greedy algorithm; prove approximation guarantees under mild conditions for a broad class of importance metrics; derive practical design guidelines.

Result: Empirical results show the method outperforms existing DP methods and reduces training time by over 40% on ImageNet-1k with ResNet-50, without sacrificing accuracy.

Conclusion: The unified graph-based DP framework provides both theoretical guarantees and practical gains, offering a robust, scalable approach to dataset pruning across different ratios and distributions.

Abstract: The rapid growth of modern training datasets has significantly increased computational cost, motivating dataset pruning~(DP) methods which retain only a subset of informative samples to reduce training cost.
  Existing pruning criteria typically rely on either intrinsic signals that assess samples independently or extrinsic signals that promote diversity via pairwise relations.
  While effective in their own specific regimes, each captures only one aspect of sample utility and lacks robustness across different pruning ratios or data distribution.
  In this work, we present a unified graph-based DP framework.
  By modeling the dataset as a weighted graph, where node weights encode intrinsic value and edge weights encode extrinsic value, DP can be cast as a Maximum Weight Clique Problem (MWCP).
  Although MWCP is NP-hard, its structure admits a principled greedy solution based on sample-wise marginal gains.
  Under a few mild conditions, we further prove that this unified objective enjoys a formal approximation guarantee, which applies to a broad family of importance metrics and provides practical design guidelines.
  Extensive experiments show that our method outperforms existing DP methods while substantially reducing training cost, reducing training time by over 40\% without sacrificing accuracy on ImageNet-1k with ResNet-50.

</details>


### [135] [Where Computation Lives Inside TabPFN: Causal Localisation of Attention Head Function](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12917)
*Atharva Gupta,Dhruv Kumar,Murari Mandal,Saurabh Deshpande*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: One attention head dominates TabPFN 2.5's computation with layer- and task-dependent dominance; steering via activation does not transfer due to in-context learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand distributed computation and steerability in a tabular foundation model by dissecting attention heads and layers using causal methods on synthetic data.

Method: Activation patching, ablation, and attention entropy analyses across two synthetic regression datasets; evaluation of inference-time steerability via contrastive activation steering.

Result: A single head shows greater causal necessity (2–5×) at a peak layer; the dominant head's key layer shifts with task complexity; other heads show symmetric late-layer activity; attention entropy and patching corroborate active layers; contrastive activation steering fails to transfer across samples.

Conclusion: TabPFN employs in-context learning with task-structured, context-dependent attention rather than stable parametric directions; steering is not easily transferable, highlighting temporal specialization in the model's computation.

Abstract: We present the first causal mechanistic analysis of a tabular foundation model, investigating how TabPFN 2.5's feature wise attention heads distribute computation across layers. Using activation patching, ablation, and attention entropy across two synthetic regression datasets, we find clear temporal specialisation: one head's causal necessity dominates that of the others by 2 to 5 times at peak layer, with its dominant layer shifting across tasks of different complexity, while the remaining heads exhibit symmetric late layer profiles. Attention entropy and patching provide convergent evidence for the computationally active layers of the dominant head. We additionally investigate inference time steerability via contrastive activation steering, which fails to transfer across samples. We attribute this result to TabPFN's in context learning mechanism, which encodes task structure through context dependent attention rather than the stable parametric directions that make steering tractable in language models.

</details>


### [136] [LoRA-Muon: Spectral Steepest Descent on the Low-Rank Manifold](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12921)
*Franz Louis Cesista,Katherine Crowson,Cédric Simal,Stella Biderman*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LoRA-Muon extends the Muon/Shampoo family to low-rank LoRA fine-tuning by applying a spectral steepest-descent update to the factorized weights, with a split weight-decay rule. It yields learning-rate transferability across rank, width, depth, and factor-scaling, is memory-efficient (no QR, no second-moment storage), and can outperform dense baselines in some setups, making it a strong proxy for full-rank optimizers in LoRA contexts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LoRA reduces compute/memory for fine-tuning but is finetuning-sensitive when using factor-wise optimizers (e.g., AdamW): initialization dependence, poor transfer of optimal learning rates across ranks, and frequent underperformance vs dense baselines. The goal is to create a low-rank optimization method that inherits the favorable spectral properties of Muon/Shampoo and yields stable, transferable learning rates across rank and model dimensions.

Method: Apply Muon optimizer's spectral steepest-descent rule to the low-rank LoRA setting, introduce a split weight-decay rule, and show that LoRA-Muon updates can be computed without QR decomposition and without storing second moments. Compare to Spectron (factor-scaling sensitivity) and LoRA-RITE (QR-coordinate core). Evaluate on TinyShakespeare with rank-2 and rank-32 LoRA configurations to assess learning-rate transfer and performance.

Result: LoRA-Muon demonstrates rank-invariant learning-rate transfer across rank, width, depth, and factor-rescaling. In a compute-matched TinyShakespeare study: rank-2 recovers the dense best tested learning rate; rank-32 LoRA-Muon achieves lower seed-averaged mean validation loss than the dense baseline. Spectron's performance depends on factor scaling; LoRA-RITE's QR-coordinate core implements the same spectral update. LoRA-Muon computes the spectral update without QR and avoids storing second moments, improving accelerator-friendliness and memory efficiency.

Conclusion: LoRA-Muon is a viable, memory-efficient proxy for full-rank Muon/Shampoo optimizers in low-rank fine-tuning, with transferring optimal learning rates across rank and model dimensions. Its efficiency and lack of QR/second-moment storage make it attractive for large-scale fine-tuning; however, care should be taken with factor scaling, and further validation on diverse tasks is warranted.

Abstract: Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) significantly reduces compute and memory costs for finetuning Deep Learning models but is often harder to tune than dense training: when using factor-wise optimizers such as AdamW, it is sensitive to initialization choices, its optimal learning rates transfer poorly across ranks, and it often fails to beat dense baselines. We derive LoRA-Muon by applying the Muon optimizer's spectral steepest-descent rule to the low-rank setting. Along with our split weight-decay rule, our main claim is that LoRA-Muon is a good low-rank proxy for full-rank Muon and Shampoo-family optimizers. Its optimal learning rates transfer across rank, width, depth, and factor-rescaling. In our compute-matched TinyShakespeare study, a rank-$2$ proxy recovers the dense best tested learning rate, and a rank-$32$ LoRA-Muon run attains lower mean validation loss than the dense baseline in the seed-averaged sweep. We further show that the Spectron optimizer depends on arbitrary factor scaling, so it would likely be a poor fit when finetuning starts from badly imbalanced factors, and that LoRA-RITE's simplified QR-coordinate core implements the same spectral update. LoRA-Muon computes that update without QR-decomposition and avoids storing second moments, making it more accelerator-friendly and memory-efficient.

</details>


### [137] [Order Is Not Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12923)
*Gareth Seneque,Lap-Hang Ho,Nafise Erfanian Saeedi,Jeffrey Molendijk,Tim Elson*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes a local, driven-dissipative mesoscopic control framework distinguishing order from true control via receiver-gated response laws; evidence across biological, LLM, adapter, and stochastic-operator panels shows measurable, local response operators with high predictive accuracy; notes scope limits.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To separate observed order-inducing effects from actual control mechanisms by formalizing a denominator-indexed, receiver-gated response law and testing its locality across cross-domain panels.

Method: Develop a driven-dissipative response-system model where drives act through prepared media, baths, and receivers to produce admitted movement, impedance, sinks, or overdrive. Analyze panels from biology (mouse ALM, C. elegans, zebrafish) and AI (LLMs, adapters, stochastic-operator), measuring component-sign accuracy and observer prediction accuracy to infer local control and stochastic response operators.

Result: Across four material conditions, response vectors show 72.8–73.7% component-sign accuracy, rising to 84.3–84.8% for nonzero components; held-out observers predict system-effect and target/oracle families with 93.6% and 91.7% accuracy, respectively. Constitution-conditioned adapters reshape susceptibility via prepared media; stochastic-operator panels separate measured opportunity from deployable policies. The account supports a driven-dissipative, mesoscopic, locally admitted control with measurable response operators.

Conclusion: Evidence supports local admitted control and measurable stochastic response operators; however, deployable pre-generation control, hidden/logit causal sufficiency, biological-to-LLM coordinate identity, and literal thermodynamic quantities remain outside scope.

Abstract: AI alignment, interpretability, steering, and neural perturbation studies identify order-inducing objects. We argue that order is not control. Control requires a receiver-gated response law: a denominator-indexed operator mapping material state, action/drive, bath, and receiver state to response displacement, sinks, effort, and basin projection. We identify it across biological, LLM, adapter, and stochastic-operator panels. The laws are local: an intervention can be admitted, saturated, sign-changing, leaky, or overdriven depending on medium, bath, receiver state, action port, and comparator. Control is assigned when finite effort moves a target or outcome-readout class under the same denominator while damage, null/evasive, invalid format, overdrive, and unnecessary effort stay bounded. Mouse ALM, C. elegans, and zebrafish panels provide physical response-operator evidence while excluding coordinate identity and controller conclusions. LLM panels show generated-output response laws: across four material conditions, response vectors are predictable at 72.8-73.7% component-sign accuracy, rising to 84.3-84.8% on nonzero components; held-out observers predict system-effect and target/oracle families at 93.6% and 91.7% accuracy. Constitution-conditioned adapters reshape susceptibility as prepared media, and stochastic-operator panels separate measured opportunity from deployable action policies. This gives a driven-dissipative response-system account at the mesoscopic control level: drives act through prepared media, baths, and receivers, producing admitted movement, impedance, sinks, or overdrive. The evidence supports local admitted control and measurable stochastic response operators, while leaving deployable pre-generation control, hidden/logit causal sufficiency, biological-to-LLM coordinate identity, and literal thermodynamic quantities outside scope.

</details>


### [138] [Is Spurious Correlation Removal Always Learnable?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12930)
*Yibo Zhou,Bo Li,Hai-Miao Hu,Hanzi Wang,Xiaokang Zhang,Ruifan Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Conditional computational barrier in invariant learning: even when invariant structure is identifiable, samplable multi-environment instances with k=1 can be learned by exhaustive search with polynomial samples, but any polynomial-time constant-accuracy method contradicts the primitive; complexity depends on environment diversity γ and sample size, with minimax risk Θ(k(d-k)/(n|E|)) and a phase transition at n* ∝ k(d-k)/(|E| γ^2), with error scaling ~1/γ^2.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge statistical identifiability of invariant structures with computational feasibility in multi-environment learning; quantify how environment diversity (γ) affects identifiability, learning gaps, and sample complexity; propose diversity diagnostics.

Method: Theoretical construction based on a black-box samplable supervised sparse-recovery primitive motivated by average-case sparse-recovery reductions; analyzes multi-environment instances with a one-dimensional invariant subspace (k=1); derives minimax risk under sufficient diversity and local Gaussian regularity; analyzes phase transition under label-induced shifts; validates with synthetic and real datasets and proposes diversity diagnostics.

Result: Shows existence of samplable instances with k=1 learnable by exhaustive search with polynomial samples, while any polynomial-time constant-accuracy recovery would contradict the primitive; minimax risk scales as Θ(k(d-k)/(n|E|)) under adequate diversity and regularity; phase transition at n* ∝ k(d-k)/(|E| γ^2) with estimation error scaling ∝ 1/γ^2; empirical demonstrations reveal gaps and transitions and motivate simple diversity diagnostics.

Conclusion: Diversity of environments, captured by γ and the number of environments |E|, governs identifiability curvature and the computational feasibility of invariant learning. Even statistically identifiable invariants can be computationally intractable under limited diversity; practical guidance includes diagnosing diversity to assess required samples and potential gaps in invariant learning.

Abstract: Invariant learning can fail even when the invariant structure is statistically identifiable. We show a conditional computational barrier: under a black-box samplable supervised sparse recovery primitive motivated by average-case sparse-recovery reductions, there exist \emph{samplable} multi-environment instances with a one-dimensional predictive invariant subspace ($k=1$) that are learnable with polynomial samples by exhaustive search, while any polynomial-time constant-accuracy recovery algorithm would contradict the primitive. We further quantify environment diversity by a separation parameter $γ$, which controls identifiability and the curvature of invariance objectives. Under sufficient diversity and local Gaussian regularity, the minimax risk is $\mathbb{E}[\dist(\hat{V},V_{\mathrm{inv}})^2]=Θ(k(d-k)/(n|\mathcal{E}|))$, and under label-induced shifts a phase transition occurs at $n^*\propto k(d-k)/(|\mathcal{E}|γ^2)$ with refined estimation error scaling proportional to $1/γ^2$. Synthetic and real datasets illustrate the predicted gaps and transitions and motivate simple diversity diagnostics.

</details>


### [139] [Circuit Synchronization Precedes Generalization: Causal Evidence from Fourier Structure in Grokking Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12966)
*Achyuthan Sivasankar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FSD is a robust, early predictor of grokking in modular-addition transformers, identifying Fourier-circuit synchronization 0.5k–3k steps before grokking and revealing a regularization-driven timing law Delta_t ~ C/λ across primes; attention-based circuits exhibit the precursor, while MLPs do not, implying a multi-block, Fourier-like mechanism.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand the timing, causal structure, and controllability of grokking by diagnosing Fourier-circuit synchronization with a metric that requires no prior circuit knowledge.

Method: Define Frequency Synchronization Degree (FSD), a normalized, permutation-tested metric for circuit synchronization. Evaluate nine modular-addition configurations (primes p ∈ {53,71,97,113,131}, three seeds). Track FSD lead time to grokking, compare against Nanda et al.'s excluded loss baseline, and perform causal/ablation experiments: (i) fork training at the FSD ceiling with varying weight decay λ to test impact on Delta_t; (ii) replicate across primes; (iii) architecture ablations: attention-only, MLP-only, single-layer models.

Result: FSD synchronizes 500–3,000 steps before grokking (mean lead +1,722 steps; all nine cases positive; sign-test p ≈ 0.004). It precedes the restricted-logit loss baseline in all cases, making it the earliest predictor. The forking experiment shows strictly monotone earlier grokking with Delta_t ∝ 1/λ, consistent with (1/λ)·log(||W_mem||/τ). Cross-prime replication yields high R^2 (1.00 and 0.99 in two clean cases). Architecture ablations indicate: an attention-only model groks with a strong FSD precursor; an MLP-only model never groks; a single-layer model’s FSD lags, indicating the precursor is a multi-block circuit property.

Conclusion: FSD is an effective, early, model-agnostic predictor of grokking tied to a regularization-driven inter-phase synchronization. The results argue for a multi-block Fourier-circuit mechanism behind grokking and suggest controllability via weight-decay settings; simple architectures (MLP) lack the necessary Fourier synchronization to grok.

Abstract: Grokking -- where a transformer on modular arithmetic suddenly transitions from near-chance to near-perfect validation accuracy -- is attributed to a Fourier circuit, but its timing, causal structure, and controllability remain poorly understood. We introduce the Frequency Synchronization Degree (FSD), a normalised, permutation-tested metric for Fourier circuit synchronisation requiring no prior circuit knowledge. Across nine modular addition configurations (primes p in {53, 71, 97, 113, 131}, three seeds), FSD synchronises 500-3,000 steps before grokking (mean lead +1,722 steps; all nine positive, sign-test p~0.004), and precedes a restricted-logit loss baseline (Nanda et al.'s excluded loss) in all nine cases, making it the earliest available predictor. We provide direct causal evidence that the inter-phase gap is a regularisation phenomenon: forking training at the FSD-ceiling step and varying weight decay lambda produces strictly monotone earlier grokking, with Delta_t proportional to 1/lambda. This law replicates across three primes (p in {53,97,131}; R^2=1.00 and R^2=0.99 for two clean cases), captured as Delta_t ~ C/lambda, consistent with (1/lambda)*log(||W_mem||/tau). Architecture ablations show an attention-only model groks with a strong FSD precursor; an MLP-only model never groks; a single-layer model's FSD lags, confirming the precursor is a multi-block circuit property.

</details>


### [140] [Predicting Cognitive Load from Speech and Interaction Dynamics in Dyadic Conversations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12971)
*Tahiya Chowdhury*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Speech-based cognitive load estimation in natural dyadic conversations benefits from interaction dynamics; time pressure, mental demand, effort, and task performance are reflected in turn-taking, overlap, speaker switches, and participation balance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To assess the reliability of cognitive load estimation from speech outside lab settings and identify which conversational dynamics predict perceived cognitive load.

Method: 53 dyads performing nine collaborative tasks; extract static acoustic, dynamic, and interaction features; train a two-head gated recurrent unit (GRU) encoder to predict cognitive load scores.

Result: Conversational interaction provides signals for cognitive load predictions related to time pressure, mental work, effort, and task performance. Temporal demand relates to turn-taking dynamics (overlap, speaker switches); mental demand relates to imbalanced participation.

Conclusion: Task structure and conversational interaction are important for modeling cognitive load in natural collaborative settings.

Abstract: Estimating cognitive load from speech has largely been studied in controlled laboratory settings, with limited understanding of its reliability in natural collaborative conversations. We investigate whether speech and interaction dynamics predict perceived cognitive load during dyadic conversations. We analyze audio from 53 dyads performing nine collaborative tasks and extract static acoustic, dynamic, and interaction features to train a two-head Gated Recurrent Unit encoder to predict cognitive load scores. Results show conversational interaction provides useful signals for predicting cognitive load related to time pressure, mental work, effort, and task performance. Temporal demand is associated with turn-taking dynamics such as overlap and speaker switch, while mental demand is linked to imbalanced participation between speakers. These findings highlight the importance of task structure and conversational interaction for modeling cognitive load in natural collaborative settings.

</details>


### [141] [EPM-JEPA: Operator-Side Experience Modulation in JEPA-Family World Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12979)
*Vedant Pandya*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Comparative study of two JEPA memory integration mechanisms under distribution shift: EI-JEPA (operand-side residual injection) vs EPM-JEPA (operator-side LoRA-based weight modulation). On a pre-registered Moving MNIST gravity-shift task, EI-JEPA yields higher performance (0.8238) than EPM-JEPA (0.7848), a relative delta of about 4.7%; the authors designate this primary comparison as a null result per their criterion. A secondary finding shows EPM-JEPA exceeds a no-memory baseline (0.8000) by ~1.9% (consistent across seeds), while EI-JEPA underperforms the baseline. The primary contribution is a mechanism analysis attributing the D_shift^{n=50} dynamics to three independent processes—buffer cycling, EMA target drift, and an intrinsic LoRA settling transient—rather than convergence to equilibrium, motivating PEM-JEPA, a physics-grounded successor addressing this dynamical peak limitation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the rigidity of a static JEPA predictor under test-time distribution shifts and evaluate whether accumulated experience can be fused into predictions via two distinct integration strategies (operand-side residuals vs operator-side LoRA modulation). The study uses a pre-registered protocol to compare mechanisms and a secondary non-pre-registered observation to probe robustness.

Method: Pre-registered comparison on Moving MNIST with gravity shift (three seeds, n=50 trajectory), comparing EI-JEPA (residual addition) vs EPM-JEPA (LoRA-based weight deltas). Report D_shift^{n=50} values; assess relative performance. A secondary non-pre-registered analysis compares to a no-memory baseline. Mechanism-level analysis decomposes the observed dynamics into buffer cycling, EMA target drift, and LoRA settling transient (~+0.021). Proposes PEM-JEPA as a physics-informed successor.

Result: EI-JEPA outperforms EPM-JEPA on the primary metric (0.8238 vs 0.7848; ~4.7% difference). The authors label this a null result under their criterion. Secondary result: EPM-JEPA improves over a no-memory baseline (0.8000) by ~1.9% with consistent gains across seeds, while EI-JEPA underperforms the baseline. The trajectory D_shift^{n=50} is argued to reflect three independent dynamical processes plus a LoRA settling transient, not convergence to equilibrium.

Conclusion: The work clarifies how two memory-integration strategies behave under distribution shift and emphasizes dynamical components in the shift trajectory. It motivates PEM-JEPA, a physics-grounded successor designed to mitigate the observed dynamical-peak limitations and to improve robustness to test-time dynamics.

Abstract: JEPA-family world models use a static predictor whose weights do not adapt when test-time dynamics diverge from training. We compare two mechanisms for incorporating accumulated experience into a JEPA predictor under distribution shift: operand-side injection, where a compressed experience representation is added as a residual to the predictor's hidden state (EI-JEPA), and operator-side modulation, where the same representation generates low-rank weight deltas via LoRA applied to the predictor's weights (EPM-JEPA). On a pre-registered comparison (Moving MNIST, gravity shift), EPM-JEPA (D_shift^{n=50} = 0.7848 +/- 0.0078, three seeds) differs from EI-JEPA (0.8238) by delta = 4.74% - Outcome C: a null result - by our stated criterion, a valid outcome. As a secondary, non-pre-registered observation, EPM-JEPA improves 1.90% over a no-memory baseline (0.8000), consistently across seeds, while EI-JEPA underperforms the baseline, indicating the benefit is specific to weight-level modulation. Our primary contribution is a mechanism analysis: the D_shift^{n=50} trajectory reflects three independent dynamical processes - buffer cycling, EMA target drift, and an intrinsic LoRA settling transient of +0.021 - rather than convergence to equilibrium. These findings motivate PEM-JEPA, a physics-grounded successor addressing this dynamical-peak limitation.

</details>


### [142] [Exposure Bias as Epistemic Underidentification in Recursive Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12990)
*Riku Green,Zahraa S. Abdallah,Telmo M Silva Filho*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Recursive multi-step forecasting under partial observability is an epistemic underidentification problem due to self-induced states during rollout; induced states Z and provenance P explain why exposure bias occurs and how provenance-aware corrections can help in some regimes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reframe exposure bias not only as distribution shift but as a self-induced epistemic uncertainty arising from recursive rollout under partial observability; identify how induced states and provenance affect what targets are learned and predicted.

Method: Theoretical development introducing induced states Z and provenance variables P; decomposition of induced-state error into three components (teacher-forcing/rollout mismatch, representation–class approximation, provenance information gaps); empirical validation showing rollout enters distinct induced-state regime and potential corrections using a binary provenance encoding.

Result: Rollout dynamics enter a regime where fixed induced states create a separate local correction task; gains in performance stem from both local adaptation and changes in induced states visited during rollout; provenance-aware corrections can improve performance but are conditional rather than universal.

Conclusion: Exposure bias is reframed as reasoning under self-induced epistemic uncertainty; understanding and manipulating induced states and provenance can yield targeted improvements, though benefits depend on regime and encoding.

Abstract: Recursive multi-step forecasting is usually framed as distribution shift: models are trained on observed histories but deployed on their own predictions. We show this framing is incomplete by proving that, under partial observability or state truncation, recursive rollout is also an epistemic underidentification problem. Even with deterministic latent dynamics, one-step Bayes supervision identifies behavior only on observed contexts and need not identify the deployed recursive predictor once rollout queries self-generated induced states whose correct local targets are not determined by numeric state alone. We formalize this with induced states $Z$ and provenance variables $P$, and derive a decomposition of induced-state error into teacher-forcing/rollout mismatch, representation--class approximation, and provenance information gaps. Empirically, we show that rollout enters a distinct induced-state regime, that fixed induced states define a distinct local corrective task, and that closed-loop gains arise not only from local adaptation but also from changing the induced states visited during rollout. Using a simple binary provenance encoding, provenance-aware correction can further improve performance, though gains are conditional rather than uniform. These results recast exposure bias as reasoning under self-induced epistemic uncertainty.

</details>


### [143] [DeepJEB++: Foundation Model-Driven Large-Scale 3D Engineering Dataset via 2D Latent Space Augmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12994)
*Soyoung Yoo,Leekyo Jeong,Jinsu Ra,Dongeon Lee,Sunwoong Yang,Hyogu Jeong,Namwoo Kang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A foundation-model-driven, three-stage data augmentation framework (DeepJEB++) expands a small seed set of jet-engine brackets into a large, simulation-labeled 3D dataset by augmenting in 2D latent space, lifting to 3D, and automatically assigning FE labels; achieves 40x expansion (400 seeds to 15,360 labeled 3D brackets) with a single GPU per stage, aiming for manufacturability-preserving and label-faithful data for engineering AI; dataset will be public.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The field lacks large-scale 3D datasets pairing geometry with physics-based performance labels. Existing 3D augmentation methods struggle to preserve subtle geometric variations and automating simulation-labeling is hard due to varying boundary conditions across generated geometries.

Method: Stage 1: fine-tune a pretrained 2D latent diffusion model on multi-view renders; synthesize novel views via latent interpolation; apply a vision-language-model quality filter to retain manufacturable designs. Stage 2: lift validated images to 3D meshes using a domain-adapted generative foundation model. Stage 3: automatically recognize load/bolt interfaces and assign FE labels (mass, stress, displacement) without manual intervention. Evaluation along manufacturability, label fidelity vs. SimJEB ground truth, and distributional consistency.

Result: From fewer than 400 seed designs, DeepJEB++ yields 15,360 simulation-labeled 3D brackets (40x expansion) using a single GPU per stage. The dataset will be publicly released to support reproducible engineering-AI research.

Conclusion: The approach demonstrates a foundation-model-driven data augmentation pathway that leverages 2D latent-space augmentation and automated 3D lifting with FE-labeling to create large, labeled 3D datasets from small seeds, enabling scalable data-driven design in constrained resources.

Abstract: Data-driven engineering design is constrained by the lack of large-scale 3D datasets that pair geometry with physics-based performance labels. In particular, existing 3D data augmentation techniques have limitations in preserving subtle and diverse geometric variations, and it remains difficult to automate the subsequent simulation-labeling process, where boundary conditions vary depending on the generated geometry. We present DeepJEB++, a foundation-model-driven data-augmentation framework that expands a small seed set of jet engine brackets into a large, simulation-labeled 3D dataset under constrained resources. Our key idea is to augment in the data-rich 2D latent space, then transfer to 3D. In Stage 1, we fine-tune a pretrained 2D latent diffusion model on multi-view renders and synthesize novel views by latent interpolation, retaining manufacturable designs through a vision-language-model (VLM) quality filter. In Stage 2, the validated images are lifted to 3D meshes by a domain-adapted generative foundation model. In Stage 3, an automated pipeline recognizes the load and bolt interfaces on each mesh and assigns finite-element labels -- mass, stress, and displacement -- without manual intervention. We assess augmentation quality along three intrinsic axes: manufacturability, label fidelity against the SimJEB ground truth, and distributional consistency. Starting from fewer than 400 seed designs, DeepJEB++ yields 15,360 simulation-labeled 3D brackets -- a 40x expansion -- using a single GPU per stage. The dataset will be made publicly available to support reproducible engineering-AI research.

</details>


### [144] [Reliability of Probabilistic Emulation of Physical Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12997)
*Sam F. Greenbury,Radka Jersakova,Paolo Conti,Marjan Famili,Christopher Iliffe Sprague,Edwin Brown,Jason D. McEwen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CRPS-trained ensembles generally deliver more reliable uncertainty estimates with faster inference than latent-space generative models; ambient-space generative models can match coverage but with higher latency; latent-space CRPS ensembles preserve coverage and efficiency; both approaches achieve good predictive accuracy; AutoCast and AutoSim are released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Systematically evaluate the reliability of uncertainty quantification for two dominant probabilistic forecasting paradigms (generative models vs CRPS-trained ensembles) across diverse 2D spatiotemporal systems under matched resources.

Method: Develop a framework to compare generative models (diffusion/flow matching) and CRPS-trained ensembles across multiple 2D spatiotemporal physical systems with matched model size and compute. Measure empirical predictive-interval coverage, accuracy, and computational efficiency for single-step forecasts and autoregressive rollouts. Compare ambient-space vs latent-space training, and report latency. Release AutoCast (modular framework) and AutoSim (dataset generator).

Result: CRPS-trained ensembles yield more reliable uncertainty, with superior predictive-interval coverage on both single-step and autoregressive rollouts, compared to generative models trained in a latent space. Generative models trained in ambient space achieve comparable coverage but at substantially higher inference latency; CRPS ensembles trained in latent space maintain good coverage with no marked degradation versus ambient training. Both approaches deliver strong predictive accuracy. Overall, CRPS-trained ensembles provide faster inference and more reliable uncertainties; ambient-space generative models offer coverage parity but with latency costs. The work provides a modular AutoCast framework and AutoSim dataset generator to facilitate future research.

Conclusion: For reliable uncertainty quantification under resource constraints, CRPS-trained ensembles are preferred. If using generative models, training in ambient space can recover coverage at the cost of slower inference, while latent-space CRPS ensembles remain robust in coverage and efficiency. The release of AutoCast and AutoSim supports continued benchmarking and rapid prototyping.

Abstract: Two dominant approaches have emerged for generating probabilistic forecasts of physical systems: generative models, such as diffusion or flow matching; and ensembles of deterministic models with stochasticity injected, trained using the continuous ranked probability score (CRPS) loss. While both approaches have demonstrated strong predictive accuracy, the reliability of their uncertainties has not been systematically assessed. We address this gap by developing a framework to evaluate both approaches across diverse 2D spatiotemporal physical systems, under matched model size and computational budget. We assess the reliability of probabilistic emulation by inspecting the empirical coverage of predictive intervals, while also considering accuracy and computational efficiency metrics. CRPS-trained ensembles typically achieve more reliable uncertainties on both single-step prediction and autoregressive rollouts, demonstrating better coverage than the standard alternative of training generative models in a latent space. Moreover, the CRPS approach offers significantly faster inference. When generative models are trained in ambient rather than a compressed latent space, which is often infeasible for high-dimensional problems, they exhibit comparable coverage to CRPS-trained ensembles, though with substantially larger inference latency. In contrast, when CRPS-trained ensembles are trained in latent space they do not show a marked degradation in coverage with respect to ambient space. Both generative models and CRPS-trained ensembles demonstrate good predictive accuracy. To facilitate future research and application, we release AutoCast, a modular framework implementing both generative models and CRPS-trained ensembles, alongside AutoSim, a flexible dataset generation package for rapid prototyping.

</details>


### [145] [scLLM-DSC: LLM-Knowledge Enhanced Cross-Modal Deep Structural Clustering for Single-Cell RNA Sequencing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13007)
*Ping Xu,Pengjiang Li,Tian Du,Zaitian Wang,Jiawei Gu,Ziyue Qiao,Pengfei Wang,Yuanchun Zhou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes scLLM-DSC, a cross-modal LLM-based clustering framework for scRNA-seq that fuses gene knowledge priors and graph topology with a cross-modal contrastive loss, outperforming baselines on clustering accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address semantic neglect in scRNA-seq clustering and the misalignment between generative LLM pretraining and discriminative downstream tasks; leverage biological gene semantics and structural information.

Method: Two-view representation: Knowledge-Driven Semantic View from NCBI gene priors and Cell2Sentence embeddings; Structure-Aware Topological View from a graph-guided encoder; cross-modal contrastive alignment between semantic and transcriptomic representations; evaluation against 11 baselines.

Result: Significant improvement in clustering accuracy over 11 baselines across extensive benchmarks.

Conclusion: Semantically-grounded, cross-modal representation improves clustering in scRNA-seq; demonstrates the value of integrating biological semantics with transcriptomic structure and LLMs for downstream clustering tasks.

Abstract: Clustering is fundamental to scRNA-seq analysis, serving as a cornerstone for identifying cell populations and resolving tissue heterogeneity. However, existing methods focus on mining numerical statistical patterns, suffering from semantic agnosticism by neglecting the intrinsic biological functions encoded by genes. While Large Language Models (LLMs) offer promising semantic capabilities, their direct adaptation to cell clustering is hindered by the structural mismatch between generative pre-training objectives and discriminative downstream tasks. To bridge this gap, we propose scLLM-DSC, a novel LLM-Knowledge Enhanced Cross-Modal Deep Structural Clustering framework. Diverging from data-driven paradigms, scLLM-DSC establishes a semantically-grounded representation by synergizing two views: a Knowledge-Driven Semantic View derived from NCBI gene priors and contextualized Cell2Sentence embeddings, and a Structure-Aware Topological View extracted via a graph-guided encoder. Crucially, we introduce a cross-modal contrastive alignment mechanism to enforce consistency between biological semantics and transcriptomic features within a unified latent space. Extensive benchmarks demonstrate that scLLM-DSC significantly outperforms eleven state-of-the-art baselines in clustering accuracy.

</details>


### [146] [CausalMoE: A Billion-Scale Multimodal Foundation Model for Granger Causal Discovery with Pattern-Routed Heterogeneous Experts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13024)
*Bo Liu,Di Dai,Jingwei Liu,Jiarui Jin,Xiaocheng Fang,Guangkun Nie,Hongyan Li,Shenda Hong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CausalMoE introduces a billion-scale multimodal foundation model for Granger causal discovery that handles patch-level heterogeneity with a Pattern-Routed Mixture of Heterogeneous Experts, enforces sparse causal graphs via causality-aware self-attention, and leverages LLMs/VLMs as priors to regularize estimation; achieves state-of-the-art and strong few-shot generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Neural GCD methods struggle with distribution shifts, dynamic regime changes, and entangled representations leading to spurious graphs; there is a need for regime-aware, interpretable, and multimodal causal discovery.

Method: Pattern-Routed Mixture of Heterogeneous Experts to route patch-level temporal patterns to specialized domain experts; causality-aware self-attention across variables with proximal optimization to yield sparse Granger graphs; integration of LLMs and VLMs to align numerical signals with textual/visual priors to regularize causal estimation.

Result: Extends state-of-the-art on fully supervised benchmarks and demonstrates robust generalization to few-shot settings where existing methods fail.

Conclusion: CausalMoE effectively decouples regime-specific mechanisms from shared dynamics, yielding interpretable sparse causal graphs and scalable, multimodal Granger causal discovery with strong generalization.

Abstract: Granger Causal Discovery (GCD) is fundamental for analyzing temporal dependencies in complex systems. However, existing neural GCD methods predominantly rely on a "one-size-fits-all" paradigm, struggling to capture distribution shifts and dynamic regime changes inherent in real-world time series. This often leads to entangled representations and spurious causal graphs. In this paper, we propose CausalMoE, a billion-scale multimodal Granger causal foundation model that explicitly models patch-level heterogeneity. CausalMoE introduces a Pattern-Routed Mixture of Heterogeneous Experts, which dynamically identifies latent temporal patterns and routes patches to specialized domain experts, effectively decoupling regime-specific mechanisms from shared dynamics. To ensure interpretable graph recovery, we design a Causality-Aware Self-Attention mechanism operating across variables, yielding sparse Granger causal graphs via proximal optimization. Furthermore, CausalMoE is the first to integrate LLMs and VLMs to align numerical signals with textual and visual priors, regularizing causal estimation in complex scenarios. Extensive experiments demonstrate that CausalMoE establishes a new state-of-the-art on fully supervised benchmarks, while effectively generalizing to few-shot settings where traditional methods fail.

</details>


### [147] [TWLA: Achieving Ternary Weights and Low-Bit Activations for LLMs via Post-Training Quantization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13054)
*Zhixiong Zhao,Zukang Xu,Zhixuan Chen,Xing Hu,Zhe Jiang,Dawei Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TWLA is a post-training quantization framework for LLMs achieving 1.58-bit weight and 4-bit activation quantization with preserved accuracy, via three components: E2M-ATQ (Euclidean-to-Manifold ternary quantization), KOTMS (Kronecker-structured orthogonal rotation to shape weights), and ILA-AMP (inter-layer aware mixed-precision activation), enabling end-to-end acceleration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs incur large memory and compute costs; existing quantization methods struggle with heavy-tailed activation distributions, limiting end-to-end speedups. PTQ with very low-bit weights and activations aims to reduce both storage and compute without retraining.

Method: (1) E2M-ATQ: two-stage optimization from Euclidean initialization to manifold relocation to minimize layer-output error under weight ternarization. (2) KOTMS: applies a Kronecker-structured orthogonal rotation to reshape weights into ternary-friendly tri-modal distributions; the shared rotation helps suppress activation outliers. (3) ILA-AMP: jointly optimizes bit allocation across layers by considering adjacent-layer second-order interaction costs, leveraging the shared transform to prevent cascades from weak layers.

Result: Extensive experiments show TWLA maintains high accuracy at W1.58A4 while delivering substantial end-to-end inference acceleration; code is available at https://github.com/Kishon-zzx/TWLA.

Conclusion: TWLA provides an effective PTQ pipeline for LLMs that addresses heavy-tailed activations and inter-layer quantization dynamics, achieving aggressive weight and activation quantization with minimal accuracy loss and practical deployment potential.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) exhibit exceptional general language processing capabilities, but their memory and compute costs hinder deployment. Ternarization has emerged as a promising compression technique, offering significant reductions in model size and inference complexity. However, existing methods struggle with heavy-tailed activation distributions and therefore keep activations in high precision, fundamentally limiting end-to-end inference acceleration. To overcome this limitation, we propose TWLA, a post-training quantization (PTQ) framework that achieves 1.58-bit weight compression and 4-bit activation quantization while maintaining high accuracy. TWLA comprises three components: (1) Euclidean-to-Manifold Asymmetric Ternary Quantizer (E2M-ATQ) minimizes layer-output error under weight ternarization via a two-stage optimization from Euclidean initialization to manifold relocation; (2) Kronecker Orthogonal Tri-Modal Shaping (KOTMS) applies a Kronecker-structured orthogonal rotation to reshape weights into ternary-friendly tri-modal distributions, while the shared rotation statistically suppresses activation outliers; and (3) Inter-Layer Aware Activation Mixed Precision (ILA-AMP) explicitly introduces adjacent-layer second-order interaction costs in bit allocation and jointly optimizes for the layer-wise disparity of activation quantization gains induced by the shared orthogonal transform, preventing cascades triggered by a few weak layers. Extensive experiments demonstrate that TWLA maintains high accuracy under W1.58A4, while delivering significant inference acceleration. The code is available at <https://github.com/Kishon-zzx/TWLA>.

</details>


### [148] [A green solvent screening tool for emerging materials via uncertainty aware, transformer enhanced transfer learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13060)
*Ioannis Kouroudis,Simon Ternes,Zhaosu Gu,Gohar Ali Siddiqui,Marina Ustinova,Angelo Lembo,Alessio Gagliardi,Aldo Di Carlo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Transfer learning from a QM9-based pre-trained model to predict solubility descriptors with limited data, incorporating uncertainty quantification, achieving strong performance on Hansen solubility parameters, Dielectric Constant, and molecular donor/acceptor numbers, enabling high-throughput solvent screening and discovery of green solvents.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the data scarcity and need for accurate solubility descriptors to enable greener solvent replacement in materials science and sustainable chemistry.

Method: Fine-tune a pre-trained QM9-founded model on solubility targets with minimal data; integrate uncertainty quantification; establish baselines on Hansen parameters and Dielectric Constant; evaluate on Gutmann Donor and Acceptor numbers; augment data with high-quality model predictions; deploy an accessible screening tool for solvent substitution.

Result: High predictive performance on Hansen parameters and Dielectric Constant; meaningful results on Donor/Acceptor numbers despite limited data; data on solubility descriptors effectively augmented by model predictions; successful rediscovery of known green solvents and identification of new candidates.

Conclusion: Demonstrates that transfer learning from quantum-chemical datasets, paired with uncertainty quantification, can rapidly expand solubility-descriptor coverage and enable eco-friendly solvent screening in high-throughput settings.

Abstract: Accurate prediction of solubility remains a central challenge across materials science and sustainable chemistry. In particular due to emerging technologies like organic and hybrid photovoltaics, batteries, and catalysis, solvent usage is expected to increase significantly within the coming years. Therefore, substituting solvents with greener alternatives is vital. This is where machine learning can have substantial impact. However, the limited data on critical parameters of solubility significantly constraints machine learning efficacy. In this work, we transfer a pre-trained foundational model on QM9 targets to our application with minimal data requirements. Additionally, the pipeline integrates uncertainty quantification, allowing the user to gauge the confidence of the predictions. As baseline, we succeed in predicting the Hansen solubility parameters and Dielectric Constant for which extensive databases exist. Importantly, we achieve high model performance on additional targets, such as Gutmann Donor and Acceptor numbers, where the available data is extremely limited. Overall, we augment data on solubility descriptors by orders of magnitude with high quality predictions. For effective dissemination, we deploy easy-to-use, easily integrateable with high throughput labs, customizable tool for ranking and screening possible solvent substitutes. Finally, we rediscovered known green solvent alternatives and proposed new candidates proving its relevance for finding eco-friendly solvents.

</details>


### [149] [Limits of spectral learning under noise](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13067)
*Sabin Roman,Ljupco Todorovski,Saso Dzeroski,Marta Sales-Pardo,Roger Guimera*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Noise in supervised regression with sparse spectral representations induces a predictable drift in coefficient estimates; after whitening, there is a universal degradation curve controlled by an intrinsic noise scale, with a threshold beyond which learning becomes unstable.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Quantify stability of coefficient learning in spectral methods under additive label noise, across multiple bases and dimensions; identify intrinsic limits to recovering functional structure from noisy data.

Method: Use sparse spectral expansions across Fourier, Legendre, Bessel, and Haar bases; analyze additive label noise; whiten empirical feature geometry; derive closed-form overlap between noisy and noiseless coefficient vectors; obtain a universal degradation curve parameterized by an intrinsic noise scale; validate with numerical experiments.

Result: Derived a closed-form expression for the overlap between noisy and noiseless coefficient vectors after whitening; established a universal degradation curve governed by a single intrinsic noise scale; identified a noise threshold where coefficient estimates become unstable; validated predictions across multiple bases (Fourier, Legendre, Bessel, Haar).

Conclusion: Spectral learning under noise has intrinsic limits; beyond a certain noise level, coefficient estimates destabilize, constraining the recovery of functional structure from data; the results provide a unified framework for predicting stability across bases and dimensions.

Abstract: Learning functional relationships from noisy data is a central problem in scientific inference. Spectral methods approximate unknown functions by expanding them in a basis and estimating the corresponding coefficients from data, but the stability of these coefficients under noise remains poorly understood. Here we study supervised regression with additive label noise using sparse spectral representations across multiple bases and dimensions. We show that noise induces a predictable drift in the learned coefficient vector whose magnitude depends on the effective number of active spectral modes. After whitening the empirical feature geometry, we derive a closed-form expression for the overlap between noisy and noiseless coefficient vectors, revealing a universal degradation curve governed by a single intrinsic noise scale. Numerical experiments across Fourier, Legendre, Bessel, and Haar bases confirm the theoretical prediction. The results demonstrate that spectral learning exhibits a fundamental noise threshold beyond which coefficient estimates become unstable, placing intrinsic limits on recovering functional structure from noisy data.

</details>


### [150] [Emotional regulation improves deep learning-based image classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13081)
*Riccardo Emanuele Landi,João M. F. Rodrigues,Marta Chinnici*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes Emotional Regulation, a pre-training framework that injects artificial subjective emotion into DL models to balance affective and non-emotional responses, improving image classification on CIFAR and large vision datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current emotion-inspired DL relies on objective physiological factors; incorporating subjectivity may enhance optimization and generalization.

Method: Pre-train on affective stimuli to balance emotional vs non-emotional responses; downstream task fine-tuning; evaluate ResNet and ViT on four emotion datasets with CIFAR-10/100 as targets.

Result: Reported performance gains over baseline backbones; evidence of emotion-augmented learning benefits; claims state-of-the-art on CIFAR-based emotion-augmented learning for large-scale vision datasets.

Conclusion: Emotional Regulation is a promising approach for emotion-augmented DL via artificial subjective experience, warranting further exploration.

Abstract: Emotion significantly influences cognition, enhancing memory and learning under certain conditions. Drawing on this principle, emotion-augmented deep learning investigates how affective states can improve neural network architectures and learning paradigms, achieving better generalization than non-emotional models. However, existing methods often rely solely on objective neurophysiological factors, neglecting the role of subjectivity in emotion. To bridge this gap, the present study introduces Emotional Regulation, a novel framework for modeling emotion in deep learning through artificial subjective experience. The method employs pre-training based on affective stimuli, balancing non-emotional and emotionally-influenced responses in downstream task optimization. Extensive experimentation was conducted in image classification, pre-training ResNet and ViT architectures on four emotional datasets, using CIFAR-10 and -100 as target benchmarks. Results reveal improvements over the aforementioned backbones, providing evidence of Emotional Regulation as a promising method for defining emotion-augmented deep learning through artificial subjective experience. Furthermore, the proposed approach overcomes the related work in image classification based on CIFAR, revealing Emotional Regulation as the new state-of-the-art in emotion-augmented deep learning for large-scale vision datasets. The study also enforces evidence of the impact of affective states in improving machine learning tasks' optimization, encouraging further investigation on emotion-inspired architectures.

</details>


### [151] [Authority, Truth, and Citation Bias: A Large-Scale Multi-Domain Benchmark for Studying Epistemic Susceptibility in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13104)
*Aryan Khurana,Aravind Ramana RN,Dhruv Kumar*

Main category: cs.LG
