TL;DR: AuthorityBench shows that citation signals increase LLM hallucinations, regardless of factual content, with the effect strongest when fabricated citations accompany true claims; general knowledge is most affected, legal domain is more robust, and venue prestige/author demographics have little impact.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how external citation signals, independent of factual accuracy, shape epistemic behavior in LLMs and to quantify risks of citation-augmented prompting across domains.

Method: A 220,564-prompt, fully factorial benchmark (2x2: claim veracity × citation veracity) across four domains (general knowledge, science, law, medicine), with 40 prompt templates, four venue prestige tiers, and a country-coded author name dataset. Seven models evaluated on 12 research questions.

Result: Citation presence (real or fabricated) consistently increases hallucination rates versus a no-citation baseline. The increase is largest when fabricated citations accompany true claims: +3 to +22 percentage points overall, and 35%–77% hallucination rates in general knowledge. Legal claims show robustness; venue prestige and author demographics have negligible impact.

Conclusion: Citation signals can exacerbate epistemic failures in LLMs, with domain-specific sensitivity. Caution is warranted in citation-augmented deployments; the study provides datasets and code for further investigation.

Abstract: Large language models are increasingly deployed in citation-augmented settings, yet the effect of citation presence on model behavior independent of factual content remains poorly understood. We introduce AuthorityBench, a 220,564-prompt multi-domain benchmark that isolates how citation-based authority signals influence epistemic behavior in LLMs. The benchmark uses a fully balanced 2x2 factorial design crossing claim veracity with citation veracity, the first to do so, across four domains (general knowledge, science, law, and medicine), with controlled variation over 40 prompt templates, four venue prestige tiers, and a country-coded author name dataset. Evaluating seven models on 12 structured research questions, we find that citation presence, whether real or fabricated, consistently increases hallucination rates relative to a no-citation baseline. The effect is strongest when fabricated citations accompany true claims, raising hallucination rates by 3 to 22 percentage points and reaching 35 to 77% in the general knowledge domain, while legal claims are comparatively robust and venue prestige and author demographics show negligible impact. All datasets and evaluation code are available at: https://github.com/floating-reeds/AuthorityBench

</details>


### [152] [Disparate Impact in Synthetic Data Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13105)
*Paul Andrey,Michaël Perrot,Batiste Le Bars,Marc Tommasi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper redefines disparate impact in synthetic data generation (SDG) as the condition where synthetic data matches the real data distribution, analyzes why SDG often fails to preserve this equality due to approximation, sampling, and DP errors, and proposes learning group-wise SDG models using probabilistic graphical models to improve utility and fairness parity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fair SDG is typically aimed at correcting observed biases in the real data; this work questions the goal of deviating from the real distribution, arguing that non-disparate SDG occurs when synthetic equals real, and investigates the sources of disparate impact (model expressiveness, sampling, privacy noise) to guide better SDG design.

Method: Critical analysis of SDG with probabilistic graphical models; examination of distribution complexity, sampling error from group proportions, and estimation error from differential privacy; proposal of learning separate SDG models for each group to improve overall utility and fairness parity; empirical illustration on artificial and real-world data.

Result: Highlights conditions under which SDG can exhibit disparate impact due to estimation and privacy errors; shows how group-wise modeling can mitigate these issues and improve both utility and parity across groups; provides theoretical and empirical insights into the limitations of current fair SDG approaches.

Conclusion: Non-disparate SDG corresponds to faithfully matching the real distribution, but achieving this is challenging due to approximation, sampling, and privacy-induced errors. Group-wise SDG modeling offers a practical strategy to enhance utility and parity, especially when groups differ in distributional characteristics.

Abstract: We revisit the fairness notion of disparate impact for synthetic data generation (SDG), that assesses whether the utility of generated records is the same across sensitive groups. Our approach departs from existing work on fair SDG, that address the problem of correcting for undue biases in the observed distribution, hence redefining SDG as learning a distribution that is not that of the real data. By contrast, non-disparate impact is notably achieved when the synthetic and real distributions are the same. We expose reasons why SDG may fail to reach that solution and discuss why approximation and estimation errors occur and can be disparate across groups. We notably look into the expressive power of SDG methods relative to distribution complexity, sampling errors due to group proportions, and estimation errors induced by differential privacy mechanisms. We illustrate cases of disparate impact on both artificial and real-world data, focusing on SDG methods that rely on probabilistic graphical models. We also introduce a strategy of learning group-wise SDG models and illustrate how it can improve both the overall utility and its parity in many settings.

</details>


### [153] [Demystifying Hidden-State Recurrence: Switchable Latent Reasoning with On-Policy Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13106)
*Jiayu Yang,Chao Chen,Shengen Wu,Yinhong Liu,Yuxuan Fan,Lujundong Li,Songning Lai,Chengwei Qin,Zhijiang Guo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A boundary-token latent reasoning framework (SWITCH) enabling on-policy RL training and mechanistic analysis via discrete entry/exit markers (<swi>, </swi>) for latent mode; yields improved performance and interpretable, localized latent computation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Latent chain-of-thought with recurrent hidden states is hard to optimize with standard on-policy RL and difficult to interpret causally. Discrete boundary anchors can align latent reasoning with RL and provide a natural handle for mechanistic analysis.

Method: Introduce SWITCH: a switchable latent reasoning framework that uses <swi> to enter latent mode and </swi> to exit. Boundaries are discrete tokens so the GRPO policy ratio is well-defined at all decision points. Train with a visible-to-latent curriculum and a Switch-GRPO objective that propagates gradients through recurrent latent computation. The boundaries enable direct probing and causal intervention of latent steps.

Result: SWITCH consistently outperforms prior hidden-state-recurrence latent reasoning approaches at similar scale. Mechanistic analysis reveals: (i) <swi> is a localized switching policy; (ii) the latent step performs problem-specific, causally important computation rather than an inert placeholder; (iii) computation concentrates at a single hidden-state transition on entry.

Conclusion: Hidden-state-recurrence latent reasoning is trainable with on-policy RL and amenable to direct mechanistic analysis via boundary tokens, providing insight into how on-policy RL improves the model from within.

Abstract: Latent chain-of-thought compresses reasoning by replacing visible reasoning traces with continuous hidden-state recurrence, but existing formulations are difficult to optimize with standard on-policy reinforcement learning (RL) and hard to interpret causally. Our key insight is that a single pair of explicit boundary tokens can address both issues at once: discrete entry and exit anchors make the latent block compatible with standard on-policy RL, and the same anchors offer a natural foothold for mechanistic analysis. Motivated by this, we propose SWITCH, a switchable latent reasoning framework. The model emits <swi> to enter latent mode and </swi> to exit. Because the boundaries are ordinary discrete tokens, the GRPO policy ratio is well-defined at every decision point. The same anchors also expose the latent steps to direct probing and causal intervention. We train the model with a visible-to-latent curriculum and a Switch-GRPO objective that propagates gradients through recurrent latent computation. SWITCH consistently outperforms prior hidden-state-recurrence latent reasoning approaches at similar scale. Mechanistic analysis through the boundary tokens further reveals three findings: (i) <swi> is a sharply localised, learned switching policy rather than a stylistic artefact; (ii) the latent step it opens performs problem-specific, causally important computation rather than acting as an inert placeholder; and (iii) that computation is concentrated at a single hidden-state transition on entry. Together, these results show that hidden-state-recurrence latent reasoning is both RL-trainable and open to direct mechanistic analysis, including of how on-policy RL itself improves the model from the inside.

</details>


### [154] [MP3: Multi-Period Pattern Pre-training forSpatio-Temporal Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13119)
*Lilan Peng,Yandi Liu,Qingren Yao,Chongshou Li,Tianrui Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MP3 is a plug-in pre-training module that learns multi-period patterns and cross-period interactions to mitigate temporal mirage in STGNNs, improving forecasting performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Spatio-temporal forecasting with short-window inputs suffers from temporal mirage due to incomplete period observation, heterogeneous global spatial correlations, and cross-period superposition causality; existing STGNNs fail to identify such mirages.

Method: Introduce Multi-Period Pattern Pre-training (MP3) comprising: (1) multi-period temporal modeling via edge convolution to identify distinct multi-period patterns; (2) multi-period spatial modeling using a bottleneck projection and a global memory bank to capture heterogeneous global spatial relations efficiently; (3) cross-period pattern interaction using a causality-enhanced Transformer; (4) plug-and-play integration into existing STGNN backbones.

Result: Evaluated on five STGNN baselines across five real-world datasets (including a large-scale CA dataset); MP3 yields consistent, robust improvements with average MAE reduction of 4.7% and RMSE reduction of 5.0%; demonstrates superior scalability and adaptability.

Conclusion: MP3 effectively strengthens STGNN forecasting, offering a scalable, adaptable pre-training module that improves performance across diverse backbones and datasets; code available at the provided GitHub link.

Abstract: Spatio-Temporal forecasting is crucial in diverse fields, such as transportation, climate, and energy. Urban spatio-temporal data exhibits temporal mirage: similar short-window inputs have divergent future trends, and vice versa. Existing spatio-temporal graph neural networks (STGNNs) cannot effectively identify such mirages. We argue that the core reason lies in the short-window inputs that have incomplete period observation, heterogeneous global spatial correlation, and cross-period superposition causality. To bridge this gap, we develop a novel Multi- Period Pattern Pre-training (MP3), a plug-and-play pre-training plugin for distinguishing temporal mirages. MP3 presents two core innovations: (1) The multi-period pattern learning is designed to learn multi-period patterns from long time series. Specifically, multi-period temporal modeling leverages edge convolution to identify different multi-period patterns. Multi-period spatial modeling uses a bottleneck project and a global memory bank to capture heterogeneous global spatial relations efficiently. Cross-period pattern interaction employs a causality-enhanced Transformer to capture dependencies across different period patterns. (2) This plugin can seamlessly integrate into existing STGNN backbones to strengthen their forecasting performance. The experiment on five STGNN baselines across five real-world datasets (including a large-scale dataset CA) verify the effectiveness, superior scalability and strong adaptability of MP3, which brings consistent and robust performance improvements across all evaluated baselines. On average, MP3 reduces the MAE 4.7% and the RMSE 5.0%. The code can be available at https://github.com/YAN-outlook/MP3.

</details>


### [155] [Select and Improve: Understanding the Mechanics of Post-Training for Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13125)
*Akshay Krishnamurthy,Audrey Huang,Nived Rajaraman*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RL training reveals two mechanistic pathways for improving reasoning in coding models: strategy selection and strategy improvement. These are activated by different data regimes—supervised fine-tuning (SFT) for strategy selection and increasingly difficult RL data for strategy improvement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand the mechanistic processes by which reinforcement learning post-training enhances reasoning capabilities in large coding models, and identify actionable interventions to scale these capabilities.

Method: Controlled math reasoning experiments using the Qwen-2.5-1.5B model to analyze how RL post-training and data types (SFT vs RL data) activate distinct mechanisms; assessment of how diverse reasoning strategies and data difficulty influence outcomes.

Result: Identified two core mechanisms: strategy selection (enabled by supervising diverse reasoning strategies via SFT) and strategy improvement (enabled by increasing difficulty in RL data). Demonstrates how SFT and RL data synergistically activate these mechanisms and provides mechanistic insight into RL training.

Conclusion: RL training drives distinct, identifiable mechanisms for enhancing reasoning: strategic selection from curated strategies and strategic improvement from harder RL data; insights point to practical interventions for scaling reasoning capabilities via targeted data curricula.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning has rapidly emerged as a key component in the training of reasoning and coding models, yet it remains poorly understood from a mechanistic perspective. We study how and through what underlying processes capabilities are acquired or enhanced via reinforcement learning post-training. Our analysis, based on controlled math reasoning experiments with Qwen-2.5-1.5B, reveals two core mechanisms: strategy selection and strategy improvement. Our results highlight the role of SFT data and reinforcement learning data in activating these mechanisms, in particular showing how supervising the model on diverse reasoning strategies can enable strategy selection and how increasing difficulty in reinforcement learning data can enable strategy improvement. Taken together, our results provide mechanistic insight into RL training and suggest practical interventions to continue scaling reasoning capabilities.

</details>


### [156] [MiniPIC: Flexible Position-Independent Caching in <100LOC](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13126)
*Nathan Ordonez,Thomas Parnell*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MiniPIC enables lightweight, flexible position-independent caching for vLLM by storing unrotated K vectors and applying RoPE inside attention per-request, exposed through three primitives (block-aligned padding, span separator, prompt depend) to realize multiple PIC strategies with minimal code changes; shows substantial throughput and latency improvements for cached spans.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Retrieval-augmented and agentic workloads reuse recurring structured inputs, but existing prefix caches in engines like vLLM are limited to identical prefixes or require invasive changes or external KV state, hindering efficient reuse. There is a need for a minimal, flexible PIC design that integrates with CPU offload and supports multiple caching strategies.

Method: Introduce positional-encoding-free KV cache and per-request RoPE-based attention. Store unrotated K vectors; apply RoPE to K tiles inside attention using per-request logical positions. Provide three user-facing token-level primitives: block-aligned padding, span separator (SSep), and prompt depend (PDep) to modify hashing and effective block-level causal structure. Implement multiple PIC methods (Block-Attention, EPIC, Prompt Cache) within a single vLLM instance with <100 lines of core-engine changes plus a custom attention backend; native integration with KV cache CPU offloads.

Result: On 2WikiMultihopQA with interleaved scheduling, MiniPIC improves prefill throughput by 49% over baseline vLLM, reduces cached-span time-to-first-token by up to two orders of magnitude, preserves linear prefill scaling for uncached spans, and incurs only 5.7% worst-case overhead.

Conclusion: A minimal, flexible PIC design is feasible with small-engine changes, enabling multiple caching strategies in one runtime and delivering significant throughput and latency benefits for cached spans while maintaining compatibility with existing KV offload infrastructure.

Abstract: Retrieval-augmented and agentic workloads repeatedly prefill recurring predictable structured inputs (which we call "spans") such as documents and code files. Yet, prefix caching in engines such as vLLM cannot reuse their KV entries unless they share identical prefixes with another request, while Position-Independent Caching (PIC) implementations within production-grade inference servers typically either require substantial server code changes or keep KV state outside the server, incurring host-to-device transfer overhead. We present Minimalistic PIC (MiniPIC): a minimal, flexible and fast vLLM design built from two ingredients: positional-encoding-free KV cache and user-controlled cache-reuse primitives. MiniPIC stores unrotated K vectors in the KV cache, applies RoPE to K tiles inside attention using per-request logical positions, and exposes three user-facing and token-level primitives: block-aligned padding, span separator (SSep), and prompt depend (PDep), that modify hashing behavior and effective block-level causal attention structure. With fewer than 100 lines of core-engine changes plus a custom attention backend, these primitives are sufficient to realize multiple PIC methods, including Block-Attention, EPIC, and Prompt Cache, within the same running vLLM instance, while natively integrating with KV cache CPU offload implementations. On 2WikiMultihopQA, MiniPIC with interleaved scheduling improves prefill throughput by 49% over baseline vLLM, reduces cached-span time-to-first-token by up to two orders of magnitude, preserves the linear prefill scaling of uncached spans, and incurs only 5.7% worst-case overhead.

</details>


### [157] [When Does Routing Become Interpretable? Causal Probes on Block Attention Residuals](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13168)
*Aydin Javadov*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Exposing depth routing via Block AttnRes aids interpretability but does not alone yield mechanistic understanding; structured routing emerges only when routing is learned, and causal testing is needed to validate any explanations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evaluate whether making cross-layer routing decisions observable in Block AttnRes enables reliable mechanistic interpretation of information flow in large language models.

Method: Compare two ~0.6B Block AttnRes Qwen3 checkpoints under routing ablations: (i) a vanilla inference-wrapped baseline with a fixed recency-bias routing schedule, (ii) a Block AttnRes model trained with routing as part of optimization. Analyze routing weights and interventions to identify motifs and causal contributions across layers.

Result: Three localized routing motifs emerge: (a) embedding-source pathway via early-layer MLP, (b) current-state pathway via early-layer attention/MLP, (c) older-history pathway via late-layer attention. A strong dissociation appears between average routing mass and causal importance—the largest mass slice is not the largest causal contributor, and a source family can have substantial mass yet minimal causal effect under intervention.

Conclusion: Architectural exposure of routing is necessary but not sufficient for mechanistic interpretation. Mechanistic insights about depth routing require training-embedded routing and causal validation; descriptive routing summaries should be treated as hypotheses to be tested rather than definitive evidence of mechanism.

Abstract: Block Attention Residuals (Block AttnRes) by replace fixed additive residuals with a learned softmax over earlier depth-source representations, surfacing cross-layer routing as an inspectable tensor in the forward pass. This is a tempting interpretability target: information flow normally inferred indirectly is now directly observable. We ask whether such exposure suffices for mechanistic interpretation. We probe two same-scale ($0.6$B) Block AttnRes checkpoints under identical routing-ablation interventions: a vanilla Qwen3 inference-wrapped through a deterministic recency-bias schedule that the codebase admits as a routing-equivalent loading path, and a Block AttnRes Qwen3 trained from scratch with routing as part of optimisation. The wrapped baseline's routing weights are content-independent and reproduce the schedule's analytic prediction. The trained AttnRes checkpoint instead exhibits three localised routing motifs: an embedding-source pathway through early-layer MLP, a current-state pathway through early-layer attention and MLP, and an older-history pathway through late-layer attention. Beyond this stratification, we find a sharp dissociation between average routing mass and causal importance: in both sublayers, the largest mass slice is not the largest causal contribution, and one source family carries appreciable mass with no detectable causal role under intervention. Architectural exposure of routing is therefore necessary but not sufficient for mechanistic interpretation: structured depth routing emerges only when routing has been part of training, and even then, descriptive routing summaries should be treated as candidate hypotheses to be tested by causal interventions, not as evidence of mechanism in their own right.

</details>


### [158] [Detecting Explanatory Insufficiency in Learned Representations: A Framework for Representational Vigilance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13172)
*Jacques Raynal,Pierre Slangen,Elsa Raynal,Jacques Margerit*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: VER is a diagnostic framework to monitor and detect persistent residual structures indicating explanatory insufficiency in learned representations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Representations can be operationally adequate while missing long-lived residual structures that conventional metrics fail to capture; a formal diagnostic process is needed to assess representational adequacy.

Method: Defines a non-learning diagnostic sequence (representation identification, explanatory-domain delimitation, residual-structure detection, explanatory-resistance evaluation, vigilance signaling) to identify persistent residual structures and distinguish representational inadequacy from ordinary prediction error, uncertainty, noise, or distribution shift; proposes representational-vigilance benchmarks as empirical evaluation paths.

Result: Provides a structured framework to diagnose when learned representations are inadequately organized, separating representational insufficiency from other error sources and outlining a path to empirical evaluation via benchmarks.

Conclusion: VER complements existing evaluation methods by treating representational adequacy as an explicit object of inquiry and can motivate empirical representational-vigilance benchmarks without altering learning algorithms.

Abstract: Learned representations are central to modern machine learning and are commonly evaluated through predictive performance, robustness, uncertainty estimation, or generalization. However, a learned representation may remain operationally successful while progressively failing to organize persistent residual structures that are not fully captured by conventional evaluation metrics. This article introduces VER, the Vigilant Evaluator of Representations, a conceptual framework for monitoring representational adequacy in learned representations. VER does not propose a new learning algorithm, loss function, or model architecture. Instead, it formalizes a diagnostic process through which persistent residual structures may be identified, analyzed, and interpreted as potential indicators of explanatory insufficiency. The framework distinguishes representational inadequacy from ordinary prediction error, uncertainty, noise, and distribution shift. It introduces a monitoring sequence based on representation identification, explanatory-domain delimitation, residual-structure detection, explanatory-resistance evaluation, and vigilance signaling. VER is intended as a contribution to representation diagnostics in machine learning. Its objective is not to replace existing evaluation methods but to complement them by treating representational adequacy as an explicit object of inquiry. A path toward empirical evaluation through representational-vigilance benchmarks is also outlined.

</details>


### [159] [Distributional Loss for Robust Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13223)
*Kathleen Anderson,Thomas Martinetz*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A soft-target loss using a bimodal Gaussian over classifier outputs models class ambiguity, improving robustness and generalization, especially in low-data regimes, with minimal training changes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional single-label targets can cause overfitting and brittle boundaries, particularly with limited data. Introducing a probabilistic, ambiguity-aware target aims to regularize learning without extra labels.

Method: Replace standard hard-label targets with a bimodal Gaussian objective defined over the output space of the classifier. The loss integrates over predictions with respect to this distribution, encouraging the model to accommodate ambiguity and learn smoother decision boundaries with negligible changes to training pipelines.

Result: The approach yields consistent robustness gains, notably in low-data settings, while requiring only minor modifications to typical supervised training procedures.

Conclusion: Modeling label ambiguity via a bimodal Gaussian soft target is an effective, low-overhead regularization that improves robustness and generalization without additional annotation.

Abstract: This paper proposes a novel loss concept for supervised classification tasks. Rather than enforcing a direct mapping from each input sample to a single assigned label, we define an optimization objective over all classifier outputs as a bimodal Gaussian distribution. This softer target formulation implicitly captures class ambiguity, mitigates overfitting, and encourages the learning of more robust decision boundaries, all without requiring additional label information. Experimental results demonstrate consistent improvements in robustness, with particularly pronounced gains in low-data regimes, while requiring only minimal modifications to standard training pipelines.

</details>


### [160] [Getting Better at Working With You: Compiling User Corrections into Runtime Enforcement for Coding Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13174)
*Yujun Zhou,Kehan Guo,Haomin Zhuang,Xiangqi Wang,Yue Huang,Zhenwen Liang,Pin-Yu Chen,Tian Gao,Nuno Moniz,Nitesh V. Chawla,Xiangliang Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TRACE converts user corrections into atomic runtime enforcement rules and compiles them into a skill-layer pipeline to reduce preference-violation across sessions in interactive LLM agents.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses the persistent gap between accessible preferences and compliant behavior: memory alone (Mem0) fails to preserve corrections across sessions, causing repeated friction.

Method: Introduce Test-time Rule Acquisition and Compiled Enforcement (TRACE): mine user corrections from chat, rewrite as atomic rules, and compile them into runtime checks that must pass before completing future tasks; deployed as a drop-in skill-layer for coding-agent runtimes.

Result: TRACE substantially reduces preference-violation rates: on ClawArena (in-distribution 100% → 37.6%; out-of-distribution 100% → 2.0%). On MemoryArena-derived tasks (in-distribution 100% → 60.5%), with task pass comparable or superior to memory-based baselines.

Conclusion: Compiling user corrections into runtime enforcement effectively mitigates repeated friction beyond memory alone, decreasing the need to repeated corrections across sessions; TRACE provides a practical, deployable solution with available code and deployment assets.

Abstract: Interactive LLM agents are becoming part of daily work, but they do not reliably become easier to work with over time: a correction remembered in one session may still be violated in the next. We study this gap between preference access and preference compliance. In tasks derived from anonymized real-user friction cases, Mem0 memory still leaves 57.5% of applicable preference checks violated. We introduce Test-time Rule Acquisition and Compiled Enforcement (TRACE), a drop-in skill-layer pipeline for coding-agent runtimes that mines user corrections, rewrites them as atomic rules, and compiles them into runtime checks that must pass before an agent completes future tasks. Unlike runtime checks written ahead of time by developers, TRACE skills come from the user's own chat corrections. We evaluate TRACE with simulated user-in-the-loop experiments on ClawArena coding-agent tasks and MemoryArena-derived memory-intensive tasks. On ClawArena, TRACE reduces held-out preference violation from 100.0% to 37.6% on in-distribution tasks and from 100.0% to 2.0% on out-of-distribution tasks. On MemoryArena-derived tasks, TRACE reduces in-distribution violation from 100.0% to 60.5% while matching or exceeding the strongest memory baseline on task pass. These results suggest that compiling corrections into runtime enforcement can address a repeated-friction failure mode that memory alone does not reliably solve, reducing the need for users to restate the same correction across future sessions. Experiment code is available at https://github.com/YujunZhou/TRACE_exp, and the deployable skill is available at https://github.com/YujunZhou/tellonce.

</details>


### [161] [Towards More General Control of Diffusion Models Using Jeffrey Guidance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13240)
*Raphaël Razafindralambo,Rémy Sun,Frédéric Precioso,Jes Frellsen,Pierre-Alexandre Mattei*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Jeffrey guidance extends diffusion-model control by using Jeffrey's rule to steer target marginal distributions while preserving conditional structure, enabling targeted embeddings and fairness constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion models offer sampling-time control but many target distributions are implicit; a principled method to align marginals with a prescribed target is needed.

Method: Introduce Jeffrey guidance, applying Jeffrey's rule to update marginal distributions toward a prescribed target within the diffusion framework, preserving conditional dependencies; demonstrate with embedding-targeting and attribute-independence fairness constraints.

Result: Achieves substantial improvements (FID reductions on CIFAR-10 and FFHQ) and enforces independence between attributes on CelebA-HQ.

Conclusion: Jeffrey guidance provides a flexible, principled framework for diffusion-model control beyond conventional guidance, enabling targeted embedding control and fairness constraints while maintaining the joint structure.

Abstract: A key strength of diffusion models lies in their flexibility, since their outputs can be controlled at sampling time through guidance. However, beyond simple cases such as conditional sampling, the target distribution is often left implicit, defined only through a sampling rule or a heuristic energy function. To address this, we propose Jeffrey guidance, a principled framework that extends diffusion-model control to applications beyond what standard guidance can express. It leverages Jeffrey's rule of conditioning to update marginal distributions towards a prescribed target, preserving the conditional structure and minimally perturbing the joint distribution. We first demonstrate Jeffrey guidance by targeting a prescribed embedding distribution. With Inception embeddings as the target, this leads to substantial reductions in FID on both CIFAR-10 and FFHQ. We further apply Jeffrey guidance to fairness on CelebA-HQ, updating an unconditional diffusion model to enforce independence between attributes.

</details>


### [162] [Loss-Shift Transfer via Bayes Quotients](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13178)
*Vasileios Sevetlidis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces loss shift, a loss-driven failure mode with fixed data distribution, formalized via Bayes quotients; shows an obstruction to refining representations across losses and an exact identity for finite-output log loss; experiments confirm that classification-equivalent representations can differ in log-loss under the same distribution.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how representations should depend on the target loss, not just on distribution shifts, revealing an orthogonal failure mode for transfer learning.

Method: Define Bayes quotients to compare losses by refinement; show that a source-minimal representation for a coarse loss cannot be optimal for a strictly finer loss; for finite-output log loss, obstruction becomes an exact quantitative identity; relate excess risk to the conditional information about Y discarded by the representation; validate with controlled, learned, synthetic-image, and real-image experiments.

Result: Theoretical obstructions arise from loss refinement via Bayes quotients; for log loss with finite outputs, the obstruction yields a precise identity linking excess risk to information discarded by the representation (I(Y; X | Z)); empirically, classification-equivalent representations can have different optimal log-loss under a fixed distribution.

Conclusion: Loss shift constitutes an orthogonal failure mode to distribution shift in transfer learning; representations must be aligned with the target loss; Bayes-quotient formalism provides a principled, loss-aware framework for analyzing and guiding representation learning.

Abstract: Transfer learning is usually studied as a consequence of distribution shift. This paper identifies an orthogonal failure mode in which the data distribution is fixed and the loss changes. This setting is called \emph{loss shift}. A loss determines which information in \(X\) is Bayes-relevant, and two losses may therefore require different representations even under the same joint law \(P(X,Y)\). The idea is formalized using Bayes quotients, which allow losses to be ordered by refinement. In the Bayes-quotient formulation, strict refinement gives an immediate qualitative obstruction. A source-minimal representation for a coarser loss is insufficient for a strictly finer target loss. For finite-output log loss, this obstruction becomes an exact quantitative identity. The excess risk is the conditional information about \(Y\) discarded by the representation. Experiments in controlled, learned, synthetic-image, and real-image settings show the predicted effect, i.e., classification-equivalent representations can have different optimal log-loss performance under a fixed data distribution.

</details>


### [163] [VideoMDM: Towards 3D Human Motion Generation From 2D Supervision](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13364)
*Amir Mann,Gal Michael Harari,Merav Keidar,Or Litany*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Diffusion-based video 3D motion priors learned from 2D poses via a noisy 3D teacher and 2D reprojection supervision, enabling 3D coherence without 3D ground truth.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Eliminate reliance on ground-truth 3D data for training 3D human motion priors by leveraging accurate 2D keypoints from monocular videos and learning a coherent 3D motion manifold.

Method: Train a diffusion model that denoises approximate 3D poses produced by a pretrained 2D-to-3D lifter (noisy teacher). Supervise via reprojected 2D keypoints, using a depth-weighted 2D reprojection loss. Adapt 3D regularizers (velocity consistency, over-parameterized representation alignment) to the 2D-supervision setting, enabling learning of a coherent 3D motion manifold during training rather than lifting only at inference.

Result: On HumanML3D, the method nearly closes the gap to fully 3D-supervised MDM (FID 0.88 vs 0.54). On real datasets Fit3D and NBA, motions are consistently preferred by humans with strong quantitative results.

Conclusion: A diffusion-based approach can learn 3D human motion priors from accurate 2D poses with a noisy 3D teacher and 2D reprojection supervision, achieving competitive 3D performance and human-preferred motion on real data.

Abstract: We introduce VideoMDM, a diffusion-based framework that trains 3D human motion priors directly from accurate 2D poses extracted from monocular videos, without any 3D ground truth. A pretrained 2D-to-3D lifter provides approximate 3D pose sequences that serve as a noisy teacher: these are diffused, denoised by the model in 3D, and supervised in 2D by reprojecting the prediction and comparing against accurate keypoints. We show that, under mild assumptions, a depth-weighted 2D reprojection loss is equivalent in expectation to direct 3D supervision, and we adapt standard 3D motion regularizers - velocity consistency and over-parameterized representation alignment - to this 2D setting. Unlike methods that lift 2D to 3D only at inference, VideoMDM learns a coherent 3D motion manifold during training. On HumanML3D it nearly closes the gap to fully 3D-supervised MDM (FID 0.88 vs 0.54); On real video datasets Fit3D and NBA the method learns to generate motions consistently preferred by humans, with strong quantitative results.

</details>


### [164] [The Geometry of Phase Transitions in Generative Dynamics via Projection Caustics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13191)
*Ryosuke Sakamoto,Kotaro Sakamoto*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A geometric explanation for abrupt, phase-transition-like changes in diffusion/flow-based samplers via projection caustics; introduces the Critical Boundary Detector (CBD) to diagnose score-direction instability and enable targeted control.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand and characterize sudden qualitative transitions in continuous-state generative samplers and relate data geometry to diffusion generation dynamics.

Method: Frame denoising as gradient descent on a free-energy landscape; analyze projection caustics where nearest-point projection to data support is non-unique; define CBD and validate across toy models, standard diffusion models, and latent text-to-image diffusion models.

Result: CBD localizes mode commitment, predicts intervention-sensitive windows, and enables targeted control in geometrically sensitive regions; demonstrates a link between data geometry and diffusion dynamics.

Conclusion: The geometry of data governs diffusion generation behavior; projection caustics underpin phase-transition-like transitions; CBD offers practical diagnostics for score instability and controllability in generative sampling.

Abstract: Continuous-state generative samplers, including diffusion and flow-matching models, evolve through continuous reverse-time dynamics, yet their samples often undergo abrupt qualitative changes: trajectories commit to modes, semantic alternatives collapse, and small perturbations in narrow time windows can produce large downstream effects. This paper develops a geometric account of such phase-transition-like behaviour. We view denoising as gradient descent on a free energy landscape and show that sharp transitions arise near projection caustics, where the nearest-point projection onto the data support ceases to be unique. Motivated by this perspective, we introduce the Critical Boundary Detector (CBD), as practical diagnostics for score-direction instability. Across toy models, standard diffusion models, and latent text-to-image diffusion models, CBD localises mode commitment, predicts intervention-sensitive windows, and supports targeted control in geometrically sensitive regions. Our results connect geometry of data and dynamics of diffusion generation.

</details>


### [165] [WHAR Arena: Benchmarking the State of the Art in Efficient Wearable Human Activity Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13194)
*Maximilian Burzer,Tobias King,Till Riedel,Michael Beigl,Tobias Röddiger*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Open-source, large-scale WHAR benchmark with 30 datasets; standardized processing; evaluate 17 architectures across 4760 training runs; deploy metrics; results show no single dominating model; predictive performance plateau; deployment efficiency matters; framework released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address comparability crisis in wearable HAR due to inconsistent datasets, processing, and evaluation; need reproducible baselines and fair comparisons.

Method: Assemble 30 diverse datasets with standardized preprocessing; unify model interfaces; implement cross-subject evaluation; evaluate 17 architectures; run 4,760 training runs; measure accuracy (macro-F1) and on-device metrics (latency, memory, size) on Android; analyze Pareto frontier.

Result: No single architecture dominates; CNN-HAR has highest mean macro-F1; performance converges near a ceiling; compact models (TinierHAR) and Random Forests define the practical Pareto frontier; larger RNN/hybrid models incur high hardware costs with limited gains.

Conclusion: Predictive performance has plateaued; progress should focus on deployment efficiency and domain adaptation; full framework released to enable transparent reuse and extension.

Abstract: Deep learning has become the dominant paradigm in Wearable Human Activity Recognition (WHAR), yet progress is obscured by a comparability crisis. Results are often reported using inconsistent datasets, custom data processing, and varying evaluation protocols, making state-of-the-art claims fragile. We address this with a large-scale, open-source benchmark that integrates 30 diverse datasets under standardized processing, unified model interfaces, and a shared cross-subject evaluation protocol. Evaluating 17 representative architectures across 4760 training runs, we jointly measure predictive performance alongside on-device latency, peak memory, and model size on an Android reference device. Our results reveal that the WHAR state of the art is distributed rather than dominated by a single architecture. While CNN-HAR achieves the highest mean macro-F1, top-performing models cluster tightly, indicating contemporary architectures have converged near a predictive performance ceiling. When accounting for deployment efficiency, compact neural models, such as TinierHAR, and classical Random Forests define the practically relevant Pareto frontier, whereas larger recurrent and hybrid models incur high hardware costs without corresponding performance gains. Consequently, while predictive performance has plateaued, substantial potential for future progress remains in optimizing deployment efficiency and improving adaptation to domain shifts. We release our full framework to support transparent reuse and extension.

</details>


### [166] [Reinforcement Learning for Neural Model Editing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13461)
*Shaivi Malik*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes an RL-based neural model editing framework with two environments—MaskWorld (multiplicative weight scaling) and ShiftWorld (additive updates)—to learn editing policies via reward signals, applied to text bias mitigation and image unlearning, achieving strong forgetting retention and bias improvements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcomes the need for bespoke editing algorithms per task by learning editing behavior through reinforcement learning.

Method: Formulates editing as RL; defines two environments with action spaces (multiplicative or additive), a composite reward balancing utility preservation and task edits; trains agents to modify pretrained models; evaluates on text classification bias mitigation and image classification unlearning.

Result: Unlearning: forget set accuracy near 0%, retain set accuracy >90%. Bias mitigation: bias-related metrics improve by >5% while preserving overall utility.

Conclusion: Demonstrates that neural model editing can be effectively reframed as RL with reward-driven policies, enabling generalizable editing strategies and reducing reliance on hand-engineered methods.

Abstract: Editing pretrained neural networks requires specialized algorithms tailored to specific objectives. Designing such algorithms is often time-consuming and demands significant effort. We present an exploratory framework that formulates neural model editing as a reinforcement learning problem, where agents modify models using reward feedback. We introduce two environments: MaskWorld, where agents scale weights multiplicatively, and ShiftWorld, where agents apply additive weight updates. The reward function combines a utility-preservation objective with a task-specific editing objective, enabling agents to learn targeted modifications while maintaining overall model performance. We evaluate the framework on bias mitigation in text classification and machine unlearning in image classification, both of which traditionally rely on specialized algorithms. Our results show that the learned policies reduce forget set accuracy to nearly 0% while preserving over 90% retain set accuracy on the unlearning task. In the bias mitigation setting, the learned policies improve bias-related performance by more than 5% while maintaining general classification utility. Our findings show that neural model editing can be cast as a reinforcement learning problem, allowing editing policies to be learned from reward feedback rather than manually engineered for each task.

</details>


### [167] [Understanding helpfulness and harmless tension in reward models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13209)
*Eshaan Tanwar,Pepa Atanasova*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Multi-objective reward models in RLHF exhibit interference effects: mixed objectives underperform single-objective models due to shared neurons that causally influence both objectives and drive alignment tension.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand why combining helpfulness and harmlessness in reward models leads to alignment tension and to uncover the neural mechanisms underpinning objective representation and interference.

Method: Train reward models under helpfulness-only, harmlessness-only, and mixed objectives. Apply activation-based analyses to identify neurons associated with each objective. Use targeted ablations to test the causal role of these neurons. Examine the overlap between objectives and the impact of shared neurons on behavior.

Result: Mixed-objective models underperform compared with single-objective models, indicating interference. Identified neurons are causally linked to their corresponding objective; many neurons are shared between helpfulness and harmlessness and have outsized influence on behavior, contributing to alignment tension. Ablations reveal that enabling a neuron to support one objective can adversely affect the other.

Conclusion: The study provides a mechanistic view of how alignment objectives are represented in reward models, explains why multi-objective alignment remains challenging, and motivates future work on disentangled and controllable alignment methods.

Abstract: Reward models are a key component of reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF), aligning language models toward both helpful and harmless behaviour. However, the internal mechanisms underlying these objectives and their conflicts remain poorly understood. We study alignment tension in reward models trained under helpfulness-only, harmlessness-only, and mixed-objective settings. We find that mixed-objective models often underperform single-objective models, indicating interference between objectives. Using activation-based methods, we identify neurons associated with each objective and study their functional roles via targeted ablations. We find that these neurons causally support their corresponding objectives while often negatively affecting the opposing one. We find that a substantial proportion of neurons are shared between helpfulness and harmlessness, and that these shared neurons exert a disproportionate influence on model behaviour, contributing to alignment tension. Additionally, our results provide insights and mechanistic interpretation into how alignment objectives are represented in reward models and why multi-objective alignment remains challenging, motivating future work on disentangled and controllable alignment methods.

</details>


### [168] [From Uncertain Judgments to Calibrated Rankings: Conformal Elo Estimation for LLM Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13221)
*Bora Kargi,David Salinas*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Two-layer calibrated evaluation framework (local calibrated Elo via Bradley-Terry with win-probabilities and global split-conformal intervals on Elo residuals) for low-cost, uncertainty-aware LLM evaluation; achieves 17.9 Elo MAE vs human ratings on LMArena; code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM-based judging lowers annotation cost but introduces systematic biases (e.g., position bias, self-preference, intransitivity) that distort rankings. There is a need for scalable, calibrated evaluation with honest uncertainty bounds that align with human judgments.

Method: Local level: replace hard labels with calibrated win probabilities and propagate them into Bradley-Terry to estimate per-battle uncertainty, improving Elo estimation. Global level: apply split conformal prediction to the residual gap between LLM-derived and human Elo ratings across held-out models to produce distribution-free prediction intervals with marginal coverage guarantees.

Result: Achieves Elo MAE of 17.9 against human ratings on 55 held-out models (LMArena). Provides prediction intervals with distribution-free coverage, accounting for irreducible LLM-human disagreement.

Conclusion: A two-layer approach yields low-cost, calibrated Elo estimates and honest uncertainty bounds without large-scale human annotations; reproducible via the authors’ code.

Abstract: Evaluating new large language models typically requires costly human annotation campaigns at scale. LLM-as-a-judge offers a cheaper alternative, but judge scores carry systematic errors - such as position bias, self-preference, or intransitivity - that can strongly miscalibrate the resulting rankings. We quantify the resulting judge-human disagreement at two complementary levels. At the local level, we estimate per-battle uncertainty from the judge's own score differences by propagating calibrated win probabilities rather than hard labels into the Bradley-Terry procedure. This alone provides a drastic improvement to Elo estimation accuracy, bringing LLM-derived ratings within 17.9 Elo MAE of human-derived ones when averaged over 55 held-out models on LMArena. At the global level, we apply split conformal prediction to the residual gap between LLM-derived and human-derived Elo ratings across held-out models, producing prediction intervals with distribution-free marginal coverage guarantees that account for irreducible LLM-human disagreement. Together, these two layers yield a low-cost evaluation tool that provides developers with calibrated Elo estimates and honest uncertainty bounds, without access to large-scale human annotations.To facilitate reproducibility, we release our code at https://github.com/kargibora/SoftElo .

</details>


### [169] [ReSET: Accurate Latency-Critical NVFP4 Reasoning via Step-Aware Temperature Scaling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13233)
*Sihwa Lee,Janghwan Lee,Donghoon Yoo,Jae Gon Kim,Hanyul Ryu,Soojung Ryu,Jungwook Choi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Entropy-aware ReSET improves NVFP4 reasoning by adaptive temperature leveraging token- and step-level entropy, plus a latency-optimized CUDA kernel; achieves about 2-point accuracy gains and up to 2.5x kernel-level speedup (≈2x end-to-end) on reasoning benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large reasoning models incur high compute due to long intermediate reasoning traces. NVFP4 quantization reduces compute but degrades accuracy and latency benefits in small-batch autoregressive decoding; there is a need for online uncertainty-aware decoding and hardware-optimized kernels.

Method: Introduce ReSET, an entropy-based temperature-scaling method that online estimates step-level uncertainty via reasoning-step entropy and adapts decoding temperature using both token-level and step-level entropy signals. Also design a CUDA-core small-M NVFP4 kernel to improve latency for autoregressive decoding.

Result: Across reasoning benchmarks and model scales, ReSET improves NVFP4 reasoning accuracy by up to ~2 points over the NVFP4 baseline. The proposed CUDA-core small-M kernel yields up to 2.5× kernel-level speedup over NVFP4 vLLM and roughly 2× end-to-end decoding speedup over BF16.

Conclusion: ReSET effectively mitigates accuracy loss from NVFP4 quantization while delivering latency gains for small-batch autoregressive decoding, enabling more practical efficient reasoning with large models. Code is available at the provided URL.

Abstract: Large reasoning models (LRMs) improve complex problem-solving by generating long intermediate reasoning traces, but this substantially increases inference costs. NVFP4 inference offers a promising approach to reduce both computational and memory costs through hardware-supported low-precision execution. However, directly applying NVFP4 to LRMs introduces two practical limitations: reasoning accuracy degrades under quantization, and existing NVFP4 kernels do not fully realize latency benefits in small-batch autoregressive decoding. In this work, we analyze the effect of NVFP4 quantization on token-level uncertainty during reasoning. We show that quantization increases incorrect sampling at low-entropy symbolic tokens, while causing over-concentration on a small set of tokens in high-uncertainty reasoning steps. Based on this observation, we propose \textbf{ReSET}, a reasoning-step entropy-based temperature-scaling method that estimates step-level uncertainty online and adapts the decoding temperature using both token-level and step-level entropy signals. To address the latency gap, we further design a CUDA-core small-$M$ NVFP4 kernel for latency-critical autoregressive decoding. Across reasoning benchmarks and model scales, ReSET improves NVFP4 reasoning accuracy by up to $\sim\!$2 points over the NVFP4 baseline. Our CUDA-core small-$M$ kernel further improves latency-critical decoding, delivering up to $2.5\!\times$ kernel-level speedup over NVFP4 vLLM and approximately $2\!\times$ end-to-end decoding speedup over BF16. Code is available at https://github.com/aiha-lab/ReSET.

</details>


### [170] [Decoding Insect Song: A Multitask Semisupervised Orthoptera Bioacoustic Classifier](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13236)
*Olga Isupova,Danil Kuzin,Ella Browning,Tom Mills,Steven Reece*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PULSE is a semi-supervised, multi-task framework for Orthoptera bioacoustics that combines weakly-supervised species classification, self-supervised learning on unlabeled field audio, and knowledge distillation from a general-purpose bioacoustic model. It yields a domain-adapted specialist model that outperforms a state-of-the-art general model on all metrics, and active learning further improves performance; learned embeddings enable ecologically meaningful discovery via an interactive visualization tool.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Automated passive acoustic monitoring tools often struggle to generalize across species and settings due to narrow training, limited labeled data, and domain shift. There is a need for transferable, data-efficient approaches that leverage unlabeled data and prior general models to improve species classification and ecological insight in Orthoptera bioacoustics.

Method: A semi-supervised, multi-task architecture (PULSE) that integrates weakly-supervised species classification, self-supervised representation learning on unlabelled field audio, and knowledge distillation from a general-purpose bioacoustic model. The model is domain-adapted to Orthoptera, with active learning to further refine performance. Outputs include classification and ecologically meaningful embeddings, presented via an interactive visualization tool for discovery.

Result: Domain-adapted specialist model outperforms a state-of-the-art general model across metrics: macro F1 0.21 vs 0.07; AUC 0.74 vs 0.45; AP 0.32 vs 0.19. Active learning raises F1 to 0.34 and AUC to 0.84. The learned embeddings reveal ecologically meaningful structure and are accessible through an interactive visualization tool for ecological discovery.

Conclusion: The study demonstrates that a semi-supervised, multi-task, domain-adapted approach with knowledge distillation and active learning improves transferability and performance for Orthoptera bioacoustics, and that ecologically meaningful embeddings can support discovery beyond classification.

Abstract: Passive acoustic monitoring holds great promise for ecological inference, yet existing automated tools are typically narrowly trained and non-transferable. We address these limitations with PULSE, a semi-supervised, multi-task framework for Orthoptera bioacoustics, combining weakly-supervised species classification, self-supervised learning on unlabelled field audio, and knowledge distillation from a general-purpose bioacoustic model. Our domain-adapted specialist model outperforms a state-of-the-art general model across all metrics (macro F1: 0.21 vs. 0.07; AUC: 0.74 vs. 0.45; AP: 0.32 vs. 0.19), with active learning further raising F1 to 0.34 and AUC to 0.84. Beyond classification, the learned embeddings encode ecologically meaningful structure, exposed through an interactive visualisation tool for ecological discovery.

</details>


### [171] [To GAN or Not To GAN: Segmentation Analysis on Mars DEM](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13252)
*Douglas Dziedzorm Agbeve,Aditya V. Handrale,Salim Fares,Seif E. Idani*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Automatic Martian mound detection via neural network semantic segmentation; evaluated supervised segmentation and GAN-based approaches; synthetic data augmentation did not improve performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable rover navigation and scientific analysis by reliably locating Martian mounds; manual mapping of morphological parameters on DEMs is time-consuming, motivating automation.

Method: Neural network-based semantic segmentation using a supervised model, plus a generative adversarial approach; comparative evaluation showing augmentation with artificial data had no performance gains.

Result: Automatic detection/prediction of mounds achieved; adding extra artificially generated data did not improve results.

Conclusion: Synthetic data augmentation did not help in this setting; suggests exploring alternative modeling strategies, data sources, or evaluation approaches to improve mound detection on Mars.

Abstract: To better understand Martian Surface, which is needed to enable Rovers navigate Mars with ease, it is necessary to be able to determine the location of mounds. Detecting and studying these morphologies can also help us find evidence of extraterrestrial life, in this case, more specifically, water or signs of life conducive environments. Detection of mounds was done by manually mapping morphological parameters onto Digital Elevation Models. This paper solves the problem by automatically detecting and or predicting mounds on Mars using Neural Network based Semantic Segmentation methodologies. This is done by using supervised semantic segmentation model and generative adversarial approach. A comparison of the approaches shows that adding extra artificially generated data did not improve the result.

</details>


### [172] [Extracting Governing Equations from Latent Dynamics via Multi-View Contrastive Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13260)
*Paolo Muratore,Mackenzie Weygandt Mathis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DYSCO is a multi-view temporal contrastive learning method that recovers latent trajectories and underlying dynamics from noisy, high-dimensional measurements by exploiting multiple independent views, enabling symbolic recovery of governing equations within an affine gauge, with identifiability guarantees and robust empirical validation across chaotic, oscillatory, and metastable regimes under Gaussian and Poisson noise.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Identify latent dynamical systems from noisy, high-dimensional, nonlinear measurements where noise obscures the true dynamics. Leverage multiple independent views to disentangle signal from noise and enable both trajectory recovery and symbolic dynamical discovery.

Method: DYSCO uses multi-view temporal contrastive learning to jointly recover latent trajectories and flow fields. Dynamics are parameterized in a structured functional basis, enabling symbolic recovery of governing equations within an affine gauge. The framework provides identifiability guarantees up to an affine indeterminacy, and handles noisy nonlinear observations.

Result: The approach yields theoretical guarantees for strong identification up to affine indeterminacy. Empirically, it accurately recovers latent trajectories and flow fields across dynamical regimes (chaotic, oscillatory, metastable) under Gaussian and Poisson observation noise, with Poisson noise being particularly relevant for neural recordings.

Conclusion: DYSCO offers a principled, identifiable framework for extracting latent dynamical systems and their governing equations from noisy multi-view observations, uniting representation learning with dynamical systems identification and proving robustness across noise models and dynamical regimes.

Abstract: Identifying latent dynamical systems from noisy, high-dimensional measurements is a central problem at the intersection of representation learning, system identification, and scientific discovery. We present DYSCO, a multi-view temporal contrastive learning algorithm that jointly recovers latent trajectories and the governing dynamics from such observations, by leveraging multiple independent noisy views of the same underlying process to disentangle signal from noise. By parameterizing the dynamics in a structured functional basis, our framework further enables symbolic recovery of the governing equations within an affine gauge. We offer theoretical guarantees for strong identification up to an affine indeterminacy, extending prior identifiability results to the realistic setting of noisy nonlinear observations. Empirically, we demonstrate accurate recovery of both latent trajectories and flow fields across a diverse set of dynamical regimes (e.g., chaotic, oscillatory, and metastable) under both Gaussian and Poisson observation noise, the latter being particularly relevant for neural recordings.

</details>


### [173] [Different Layers, Different Manifolds: Module-Wise Weight-Space Geometry in Transformer Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13276)
*Kirato Yoshihara*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Layer-wise manifold constraints in Transformers reveal module-specific geometry preferences: applying Stiefel constraints to attention and DGram constraints to MLP blocks yields best performance, while uniform or inverted constraints cause instability due to singular-value growth and softmax saturation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To test whether different modules within transformer architectures benefit from different weight-space geometries rather than applying a uniform constraint across all weights.

Method: Using Manifold Muon on GPT-2 pretraining, assign Stiefel constraints to attention layers and DGram constraints to MLP layers (and compare alternative configurations). Evaluate performance and stability under shared hyperparameters and analyze singular-value dynamics and attention logits to diagnose instability.

Result: There is a clear asymmetry: the Stiefel-constraint attention with DGram-constrained MLPs delivers the best performance among tested configurations. The inverted assignment and the all-DGram setup become unstable under shared hyperparameters. The instability is linked to singular-value growth in DGram-constrained attention weights, which amplifies attention logits and causes softmax saturation.

Conclusion: Geometry-aware optimization for transformers should be module-specific rather than uniform; symmetry-aware, geometry-aware constraints can improve stability and performance if aligned with the distinct roles of attention and MLP blocks.

Abstract: Weight-space geometry plays a central role in neural network optimization, yet manifold constraints are often applied uniformly across all weight matrices. In this work, we ask whether different transformer modules prefer different manifold geometries. We study Manifold Muon for GPT-2 pretraining and compare layer-wise assignments of Stiefel and DGram constraints across attention and MLP blocks. Our results show a clear asymmetry: constraining attention layers with Stiefel geometry while assigning DGram geometry to MLP layers gives the best performance among the tested configurations, whereas the inverted assignment and all-DGram configuration become unstable under the shared hyperparameter setting. We trace this failure to singular value growth in DGram-constrained attention weights, which can amplify attention logits and induce softmax saturation. These findings suggest that symmetry-aware and geometry-aware optimization for transformers should be module-specific rather than uniform.

</details>


### [174] [Once-for-All: Scalable Simultaneous Forecasting via Equilibrium State Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13285)
*Beinan Xu,Andy Song,Jiti Gao,Feng Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Equilibrium State Estimation (ESE) is a fast, scalable multi-system forecasting framework that estimates a common equilibrium state across interacting systems and then forecasts by the deviation from equilibrium. It processes all systems in a single pass, achieving at least SOTA accuracy with linear-time complexity and substantial speedups (10-70x when paired with standard predictors).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a need for simultaneous, coordinated forecasting across multiple interacting systems (e.g., economics, healthcare) where predicting each system separately is inefficient and may miss cross-system dynamics.

Method: Estimate a joint equilibrium state across all systems; generate forecasts from the difference between the current state and this equilibrium; integrate with conventional predictors to boost accuracy while maintaining fast, linear-time processing; demonstrated on synthetic and real-world datasets including currency exchange and COVID-19 spread.

Result: ESE achieves accuracy comparable to or better than state-of-the-art methods while being significantly faster. When combined with conventional predictors, it yields 10-70x speedups. It scales linearly with the number of systems and remains robust under perturbations.

Conclusion: ESE is a fast, generalizable, robust, and scalable multi-prediction paradigm that can be integrated with existing predictors to deliver accurate forecasts with substantial efficiency gains across diverse multi-system domains.

Abstract: We introduce Equilibrium State Estimation (ESE), a novel paradigm for simultaneous prediction, where multiple interacting systems require separate yet coordinated forecasts. Such scenarios often arise in real-world settings such as economics and healthcare modeling. Unlike existing approaches that predict one system at a time, ESE forecasts all systems in a single pass. It first estimates the equilibrium state across systems, then generates holistic forecasts based on the difference between the current state and the estimated equilibrium. Extensive experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets, including currency exchange and COVID-19 spread modeling, demonstrate that ESE is at least as accurate as state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods while being significantly faster. In addition, ESE integrates seamlessly with conventional predictors, combining their accuracy with its exceptional efficiency and delivering a 10-70x speedup. With linear-time complexity, ESE scales far better than SOTA methods as the number of systems increases. Moreover, it remains accurate under diverse perturbations, establishing ESE as a fast, generalizable, robust, and scalable multi-prediction method.

</details>


### [175] [Clipping Makes Distributed and Federated Asynchronous SGD Robust to Stragglers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13287)
*Samuel Erickson,Mikael Johansson*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Gradient clipping in asynchronous SGD removes the dependence of the oracle complexity on the maximum delay under a sub-Weibull gradient-noise model, enabling convergence in both expectation and, for the first time in ASGD, high-probability convergence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable scalable parallel training with asynchronous updates while mitigating degraded convergence caused by slow workers; to theoretically justify the empirically observed stabilizing effect of gradient clipping under heavy-tailed gradient noise.

Method: Theoretically analyze ASGD with constant step sizes under a sub-Weibull noise model for gradients. Introduce gradient clipping and compare oracle complexity and convergence bounds with/without clipping. Prove convergence in expectation and establish high-probability convergence in asynchronous optimization.

Result: Demonstrates that gradient clipping removes the dependence of the maximum delay on the oracle complexity. Establishes convergence in expectation and, for the first time in ASGD, convergence with high probability under sub-Weibull gradient noise.

Conclusion: Gradient clipping provides a robust mechanism to stabilize ASGD under heavy-tailed noise, decoupling delay from convergence guarantees and yielding both expected and high-probability convergence results in asynchronous training.

Abstract: In modern machine learning, parallelization of training is an important strategy for increasing scale. Asynchronous stochastic gradient descent (ASGD), which maximizes the utilization of available hardware by avoiding waiting for slow workers. However, with constant step sizes, the convergence of ASGD is nonetheless affected negatively by slow workers due to large delays in updates. At the same time, it has been empirically observed in asynchronous training of deep learning models that gradient clipping "stabilizes" training. In this work, we provide a theoretical justification for this behavior, as we show that clipping removes the dependence of the maximum delay in the oracle complexity. We employ a sub-Weibull model of gradient noise which generalizes sub-Gaussian and sub-exponential distributions to more heavy-tailed distributions, motivated by empirical observations in deep learning. We show convergence in expectation, and the first time in asynchronous optimization, convergence with high probability.

</details>


### [176] [Quantizing Time-Series Models As Dynamical Systems: Trajectory-Based Quantization Sensitivity Score](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13300)
*Mariya Pavlova,Harrison Bo Hua Zhu,Elizsveta Semenova,Yingzhen Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes Trajectory-based Quantization Sensitivity Score (TQS) to model quantization effects as a discrete dynamical system, enabling a priori sensitivity estimation decoupled from specific quantizers and bit-widths; introduces TQS-PTQ mixed-precision without calibration data, showing robust low-precision deployment in resource-constrained settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome limitations of traditional PTQ sensitivity analyses that tie sensitivity to particular quantizers and require calibration data or costly approximations; enable budget-aware, data-free deployment for black-box or fused-operator networks.

Method: Model the network rollout under quantization as a discrete-time dynamical system; define TQS to quantify error propagation and amplification over rollout horizon; develop TQS-PTQ as a flexible mixed-precision framework that does not require calibration data or second-order approximations.

Result: Empirical results indicate the dynamical-systems perspective yields robust, high-performance low-precision deployment; TQS provides a priori sensitivity estimates and supports budget planning for black-box/compiled networks.

Conclusion: A dynamical-systems viewpoint offers a robust, scalable pathway for efficient low-precision inference, enabling decoupled sensitivity from quantizer design and facilitating budget-aware PTQ in constrained settings.

Abstract: We introduce the Trajectory-based Quantization Sensitivity Score (TQS), a metric that reframes post-training quantization (PTQ) through the lens of dynamical-systems stability. By modeling the network's rollout as a discrete-time dynamical system, TQS characterizes how quantization-induced errors propagate and amplify over the rollout horizon. Unlike conventional PTQ methods, where sensitivity analysis is often coupled to the quantization procedure, TQS enables a priori sensitivity estimation decoupled from quantizer selection and bit-width assignment. This separation allows for quantization budget planning even for black-box or compiled networks with fused operators. Building on this, we present TQS-PTQ, a flexible mixed-precision framework that requires no calibration data or costly second-order approximations. Our experiments show that a dynamical-systems perspective provides a robust, high-performing pathway for low-precision deployment in resource-constrained settings.

</details>


### [177] [Rarity-Gated Context Conditioning for Offline Imitation Learning-Based Maritime Anomaly Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13311)
*Yongmin Kim,ByeongHoon Jeon,Sungil Kim*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes RGFiLM, a rarity-aware FiLM modulation for contextual anomaly detection to address context-imbalance; shows improved F1–FPR trade-off in maritime AIS with environmental context.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Contextual anomaly detection is undermined by highly imbalanced context distributions; rare regimes can be critical but cause instability and false alarms if not explicitly modeled.

Method: Introduce rarity-gated Feature-wise Linear Modulation (RGFiLM): a context-conditioned scaling/shifting of hidden features gated by a data-driven rarity score derived from the empirical context distribution, making modulation stronger in rare contexts and more conservative in frequent ones; evaluated in a sequential anomaly scoring pipeline on AIS trajectories with ERA5 context in an environment-sensitive detour scenario.

Result: RGFiLM achieves the best mean F1–False Positive Rate trade-off among context-agnostic and context-conditioned baselines in the maritime setting.

Conclusion: Explicitly accounting for context rarity improves false alarm control in context-sensitive anomaly detection.

Abstract: Contextual anomaly detection aims to identify abnormal behavior conditional on context variables, but practical deployments often face highly imbalanced context distributions where rare regimes can be critical information. Under such frequency bias, context-conditioned models can produce unstable decisions and excessive false alarms in rare contexts. We propose Rarity-Gated Feature-wise Linear Modulation (RGFiLM), a rarity-aware conditioning module that combines feature-wise modulation (i.e., context-conditioned scaling and shifting of hidden features) with a gate controlled by a data-driven rarity score. The rarity score is estimated from the empirical distribution of context variables and regulates how strongly context modulates intermediate representations: the gate becomes more decisive under rare contexts while remaining conservative under frequent contexts. We evaluate RGFiLM on maritime trajectory anomaly detection using AIS motion sequences with ERA5 environmental context in an environment-sensitive detour scenario. When instantiated in a sequential anomaly scoring pipeline, RGFiLM achieves the best mean F1--False Positive Rate (FPR) trade-off among the compared context-agnostic and context-conditioned methods. These results suggest that explicitly accounting for context rarity is an effective approach for reducing false alarms in context-sensitive anomaly detection.

</details>


### [178] [Navigating the Safety-Fidelity Trade-off: Massive-Variate Time Series Forecasting for Power Systems via Probabilistic Scenarios](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13338)
*Kaijie Xu,Anqi Wang,Xilin Dai*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces PowerPhase, a scalable probabilistic forecasting benchmark for multivariate power grids (2k–36k channels) with constraint-aware metrics; proposes PowerForge, a scenario-based quantile forecaster with decoding heads and causal bridges, achieving best average rank across grids and highlighting a safety–fidelity trade-off in probabilistic forecasts under physical constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the lack of scalable, probabilistic, constraint-aware evaluation for multivariate power-grid forecasting. Existing benchmarks cap around 2,000 channels and often miss temporal structure or probabilistic evaluation.

Method: PowerPhase assembles six transmission grids (2k–36,964 channels) with AC power-flow outputs; evaluates models using constraint-aware metrics (Safety_mBrier, NECV, CVaR-α) alongside CRPS and Distortion; analyzes eight baselines across three seeds to reveal a safety–fidelity trade-off. PowerForge introduces a scenario-based quantile forecaster with type-specific decoding heads and a causal bridge between variable groups.

Result: Models show differing rankings when considering distributional accuracy versus constraint satisfaction, revealing a safety–fidelity trade-off. PowerForge achieves the best average rank on every grid.
