Conclusion: Large-scale, constraint-aware probabilistic forecasting is feasible; benchmark and model design (PowerPhase and PowerForge) enable scalable evaluation and improved performance, with a consistent emphasis on balancing predictive distribution quality with physical constraint satisfaction.

Abstract: Probabilistic forecasting models are increasingly deployed on multivariate systems with distinct channel physics and operational constraints, but existing benchmarks evaluate neither property at scale. Public canonical multivariate benchmarks cap out at 2,000 channels, while power-system benchmarks either lack temporal structure or probabilistic evaluation. We introduce PowerPhase, a probabilistic forecasting benchmark built on six transmission grids ranging from 2,000 to 36,964 jointly forecasted channels, more than an order of magnitude beyond popular canonical multivariate benchmarks. Each target trajectory is the output of an AC power-flow solve, and PowerPhase ships with constraint-aware metrics, including Safety_mBrier, NECV, and CVaR-alpha, that complement CRPS and Distortion. Across eight baselines and three seeds, distributional accuracy and constraint satisfaction rank models differently, a trade-off we term safety-fidelity. We further propose PowerForge, a scenario-based quantile forecaster with type-specific decoding heads and a causal bridge between variable groups, which achieves the best average rank on every grid.

</details>


### [179] [Enhanced Low-Density Region Exploration in Classifier-Guided Diffusion Models Through Modified Reverse Diffusion Sampling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13347)
*Jagriti Singh,Shekhar Verma,Muneendra Ojha*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Density-aware sampling-time extension of classifier-guided diffusion that targets low-density regions without extra training; combines low-confidence guidance with predicted real-image guidance to improve long-tail coverage while preserving image quality, demonstrated on ImageNet with ADM models (64x64 recall improved; 256x256 qualitative results).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the long-tail coverage gap in classifier-guided diffusion, where high-density regions are favored; achieve better coverage of low-probability samples without training additional models or discriminators.

Method: Starting from a pretrained conditional diffusion model and classifier, modify the guided reverse diffusion by steering trajectories toward low-confidence regions via a modified classifier gradient, and at each step guide toward the predicted real image; apply guidance on noisy images rather than predicted noise; perform dual guidance to explore low-density regions and stay near the real data manifold.

Result: Consistent improvement in ADM model recall at 64×64 while maintaining FID; qualitative results at 256×256 show perceptual quality with combinations of both guidance; standard ADM classifier guidance plus predicted-real-image guidance yields high perceptual quality at 256×256.

Conclusion: A purely sampling-time, density-aware extension can enhance long-tail coverage without extra training, leveraging dual guidance to explore low-density regions and steer samples toward the data manifold; applicable to existing pretrained diffusion models to boost recall without sacrificing fidelity.

Abstract: Diffusion models have emerged as state-of-the-art generative models for high-fidelity image synthesis, particularly in their classifier-free guided and classifier-guided forms. However, standard classifier guidance concentrates probability mass around high-density class mean, leading to poor coverage of rare samples in the tails of the class-conditional distributions. Recent work on diffusion-based tail sampling mitigates this by training an additional low-density-seeking classifier with a synthetic-vs-real discriminator, at the cost of additional networks and training. In parallel, a number of samplers and distillation techniques accelerate or refine diffusion sampling, but do not explicitly address long-tail coverage. We propose a purely sampling-time, density-aware extension of classifier-guided conditional diffusion model that targets low-density regions without any additional training. We have applied guidance at noisy images not on predicted noise like most diffusion models. Starting from a pretrained conditional diffusion model and classifier on ImageNet, we modify the guided reverse dynamics by steering trajectories toward low-confidence regions via the modified classifier gradient, and at each time step, we also guide the sampling process toward the predicted real image. 1st guidance helps explore low-probability samples, and 2nd guidance helps to generate samples to be close to the real data manifold. The proposed sampler consistently improves ADM model recall at 64x64 resolution while maintaining a comparable FID, and with a 256x256 ADM model, we showed the results visually with different combinations of both guidance. We also showed that standard ADM classifier guidance, combined with predicted real image guidance, helps generate high perceptual quality samples with a 256x256 ADM model on ImageNet.

</details>


### [180] [Positional Encoding in the Context of Memristor-Based Analog Computation for Automatic Speech Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13379)
*Benedikt Hilmes,Nick Rossenbach,Ralf Schlüter*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Memristor-based analog neural computation for NLP suffers from large output values in transformed positional encodings causing ADC degradation. By tuning the ADC weight/precision bit allocation in selected memristor layers, degradation is reduced by ~50% while energy remains stable; if ADC modification is impossible, removing encoding-related linear transformations reduces degradation by ~30%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address performance and reliability challenges in analog memristor accelerators for NLP due to large dynamic range and non-idealities in ADCs and weight programming; enable more accurate and energy-efficient vector-matrix multiply on memristor hardware.

Method: 1) Adjust the proportion of weight bits and ADC precision bits in specific memristor layers to mitigate ADC degradation attributed to large transformed positional encodings. 2) In scenarios where ADC parameters cannot be modified, remove or simplify encoding-related linear transformations that contribute to the large dynamic range.

Result: Degradation (loss of execution quality) is reduced by approximately 50% relative when ADC parameters can be tuned, with energy estimates remaining stable. When ADC tuning is not possible, removing encoding-related linear transformations yields about a 30% degradation reduction.

Conclusion: Hardware-aware optimization—tuning ADC bit allocation and/or removing encoding transforms—can substantially mitigate analog execution degradation in memristor-based NLP accelerators, offering a practical path to more reliable and energy-stable analog NLP computation.

Abstract: Memristors provide a new chance for resource-efficient computation of neural models for natural language processing by enabling analog execution of vector-matrix-multiplication. Yet, computations on these devices are currently subject to larger distortion, both in weight programming and execution. In this work, we identify large output values of transformed positional encodings to cause major degradation within analog-to-digital conversion (ADC) as part of memristor-based computation. By adjusting the proportion of weight and precision bits of the ADC of specific memristor layers, we reduce the degradation of the execution by ~50% relative, while keeping the estimated energy consumption stable. Additionally, we investigate scenarios where the ADC cannot be modified. In that case the degradation can be reduced by ~30% relative after removing encoding-related linear transformations.

</details>


### [181] [Hölder++: Improving the Quality-Coherence Trade-off in Multimodal VAEs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13381)
*Huyen Vo,María Martínez-García,Isabel Valera*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Hölder++ is a multimodal VAE that uses exact Hölder pooling, separates shared and private representations, and employs hierarchical inference to improve the generative quality–coherence trade-off and latent disentanglement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MMVAE approaches struggle to simultaneously achieve realistic, diverse generations and cross-modal semantic coherence. A simple Hölder pooling approximation improved coherence but reduced diversity, motivating a method that preserves diversity while enhancing coherence.

Method: 1) Implement Hölder pooling without approximation for multimodal VAEs. 2) Extend the architecture to model distinct shared and private (modality-specific) representations (Hölder+). 3) Introduce hierarchical inference to further disentangle shared vs private representations (Hölder++).

Result: Empirical results show Hölder++ consistently improves the generative quality–coherence trade-off, yields more structured latent spaces, and learns shared representations that are informative for downstream tasks.

Conclusion: Hölder++ advances the state of MMVAE by jointly improving coherence and generative quality while enabling disentangled, task-relevant shared representations.

Abstract: Existing approaches for multimodal variational autoencoders (VAEs) face a trade-off between generative quality and coherence-i.e., they struggle to generate realistic and diverse samples that, at the same time, are semantically consistent across modalities. A recent work shows that using a simple approximation to Hölder pooling as an aggregation method improves coherence over the SOTA MMVAE+, despite assuming a single shared representation across all modalities. Yet, it slightly compromises sample diversity. Inspired by this insight, we propose Hölder++, a novel multimodal VAE that improves the generative quality-coherence trade-off through: (i) the first implementation of Hölder pooling without any approximation for multimodal VAEs; (ii) an extended architecture that models distinct shared and private (i.e., modality-specific) representations (Hölder+); and (iii) hierarchical inference that further enhances the disentanglement between the shared and private representations (Hölder++). Our experiments corroborate that Hölder++ consistently improves the generative quality-coherence trade-off, yields more structured latent spaces, and learns shared representations that are informative for downstream tasks.

</details>


### [182] [Accelerating Speculative Diffusions via Block Verification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13426)
*Alexander Soen,Hisham Husain,Valentin De Bortoli,Arnaud Doucet*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Efficient speculative decoding for diffusion models via an adapted residual-sampling scheme and block verification; introduces Free Drafter, a training-free self-speculative method, achieving up to 6.3% speedup with minimal overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion model inference remains slow. Speculative decoding speeds up token generation in discrete models by using a draft; however, extending this to continuous diffusions requires sampling from a residual distribution, which is nontrivial and currently leads to inefficient or alternative schemes.

Method: Propose an efficient implementation of the original speculative sampling for diffusion models, incorporating block verification from LLMs to improve draft acceptance. Formalize and analyze Free Drafter, a heuristic self-speculative drafter that requires no training, and leverage the existing parallel verification pass.

Result: Demonstrates up to 6.3% speedup over existing speculative methods with no extra training and negligible overhead beyond the parallel verification pass; improved acceptance of drafts due to block verification.

Conclusion: Extends speculative decoding to continuous diffusion models effectively; block verification is a key contributor to speedups; Free Drafter offers a practical, training-free option with tangible performance gains.

Abstract: Speculative decoding speeds up LLM inference by using a draft model to generate tokens, with an acceptance-rejection scheme that ensures that the output matches the target distribution. Adapting this to continuous diffusions is difficult because speculative sampling requires drawing from a residual distribution. While straightforward in discrete spaces, efficiently sampling this residual in continuous space is non-trivial. Consequently, existing diffusion adaptations either use computationally inefficient sampling techniques or rely on an alternative scheme. In this work, we introduce a novel scheme that efficiently implements the original speculative sampling mechanism for diffusion models. Our approach offers a critical advantage over current methods: it enables us to adapt block verification from LLMs to diffusions -- which provably improves the acceptance rate of drafts. Furthermore, we formalize and analyze the Free Drafter, a heuristic self-speculative drafter for diffusions that requires no training. By enabling block verification, our Free Drafter yields up to a 6.3% speedup over existing speculative methods with no additional training and negligible overhead beyond the existing parallel verification pass.

</details>


### [183] [How Much Memory Do We Need? Adaptive Memory Gate for Neural Operators](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13443)
*Jihyeon Hur,Yongseok Kwon,Min-Gi Jo,Jeongwhan Choi,Noseong Park*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Summary generation failed


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Motivation analysis unavailable

Method: Method extraction failed

Result: Result analysis unavailable

Conclusion: Conclusion extraction failed

Abstract: Neural operators have emerged as a powerful data-driven approach for solving time-dependent PDEs. Among recent advances, memory-augmented neural operators explicitly incorporate past states and have achieved remarkable performance under low-resolution observation settings. However, existing approaches apply a fixed memory weight regardless of observation conditions, such as resolution or physical parameters, limiting their adaptability. Our preliminary experiments reveal that optimal memory weight varies with resolution and viscosity, implying that a fixed memory weight cannot simultaneously optimize performance across diverse settings. We propose AMGFNO, which dynamically modulates memory weight through a learnable gate. On the Kuramoto-Sivashinsky and Burgers' equations, AMGFNO achieves 55-79% nRMSE reduction over at low resolution, with the learned gate value automatically decreasing from $\bar{g} \approx 0.7$ to near-zero as resolution increases.

</details>


### [184] [Clustering Node Attributed Networks with Graph Neural Networks and Self Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13444)
*Rodrigo de Sapienza Luna,Daniel Ratton Figueiredo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: An unsupervised multi-round GNN framework for attributed graph clustering that alternates representation learning and clustering, leveraging both edges and node attributes, with iterative refinement and competitive results for balanced real data clusters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Clustering nodes when they carry informative attributes in addition to graph structure, addressing limitations of methods that rely solely on edges or solely on attributes and improving over single-round GNN clustering.

Method: In each round: (i) a GNN learns node representations from the current graph; (ii) the node embeddings are clustered; (iii) the resulting cluster assignments influence the graph used in the next round; (iv) a context graph built from the original graph is also used to generate representations. The process repeats for multiple rounds in a fully unsupervised, self-learning loop.

Result: On synthetic data, the framework leverages both edges and attributes to outperform methods that use only network or attributes when neither source is highly informative. Multiple rounds consistently outperform a single long training round. On real datasets, the method is competitive with state-of-the-art methods when cluster sizes are balanced.

Conclusion: The proposed multi-round, self-learning framework effectively fuses network structure and node attributes for clustering, with iterative refinement yielding gains over single-round approaches; balanced cluster sizes enhance performance on real data, suggesting areas for handling imbalance.

Abstract: Graph clustering - partitioning the node set of a graph into disjoint subsets that reflect some latent information - is a fundamental problem as it finds applications in a myriad of different scenarios. While this classic problem has been tackled for decades by different communities, a recent variation of the problem driven by real data considers the scenario where nodes have attributes that are also informative. This has triggered novel methods that simultaneously leverage network information (edges) and node information (attributed) in the design of novel clustering algorithms. This work proposes a novel framework that builds on prior works that have applied graph neural networks (GNN) to graph clustering. The proposed framework operates in rounds of self learning in a fully unsupervised setting. In each round, a GNN generates representations for nodes that are used to cluster the nodes. This clustering influences the graph used to generate the node representation in the next round. Moreover, a context graph built in each round using the original graph is used to generate the node representations. Empirical results show that the proposed methodology extracts information from both network edges and node attributes in synthetic data, outperforming algorithms focused solely on the network or attributes when neither are very informative. Multiple rounds of learning also improve the performance and always outperforms a long single round of training (i.e., classic GNN graph clustering). When considering real datasets, empirical results indicate that the proposed methodology is competitive to state-of-the-art methods when cluster sizes are balanced.

</details>


### [185] [Uncertainty Estimation for Molecular Diffusion Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13451)
*Paul Seij,Christian A. Naesseth,Stephan Mandt,Metod Jazbec*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Post-hoc per-sample uncertainty for pretrained molecular diffusion models via Laplace approximation of the denoising network; uncertainty correlates negatively with sample quality and can be used to filter samples and improve performance with test-time scaling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion-based 3D molecular generators lack a principled signal indicating when a generated molecule is likely to be low quality; a per-sample uncertainty estimator would enable filtering and quality control.

Method: Apply a Laplace approximation to the denoising network to quantify the variability of noise predictions along the generation trajectory, yielding a per-sample uncertainty score.

Result: The uncertainty score is informative of sample quality, showing a negative correlation with established sample-level quality metrics; using this score to filter/generated samples at test-time (scaling) improves model performance.

Conclusion: A practical post-hoc uncertainty signal for molecular diffusion models that supports sample filtering and improves performance via test-time adjustments.

Abstract: Diffusion models have seen wide adoption for 3D molecular generation, yet they offer no principled signal of when a generated molecule is likely to be of low quality. We propose a post-hoc method for estimating per-sample uncertainty in pretrained molecular diffusion models. Building on a Laplace approximation of the denoising network, we measure the variability of the noise prediction across the generation trajectory. Empirically, we show that the resulting uncertainty score is informative of sample quality, exhibiting a negative correlation with established sample-level quality metrics. We further study how the proposed uncertainty score can be used to filter generated samples, improving model performance via test-time scaling.

</details>


### [186] [MaxProof: Scaling Mathematical Proof with Generative-Verifier RL and Population-Level Test-Time Scaling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13473)
*Jiacheng Chen,Xinyu Zhang,Shunkai Zhang,Yanmohan Wang,Lin Li,Tiancheng Qin,Qin Wang,Zhengmao Zhu,Tianle Li,Jingyang Li,Zehan Li,Binyang Jiang,Jin Zhu,Han Ding,Fei Yu,Chenyu Du,Zijian Song,Jiayuan Song,Zhi Zhang,Yunan Huang,Weiyu Cheng,Pengyu Zhao,Yu Cheng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MaxProof is a population-level test-time scaling framework for contest-grade mathematical proofs that combines generation, verification, and critique-conditioned repair in an M3 model; at test time it searches a population of proofs and uses tournament selection to produce a final proof, achieving high scores on IMO/USAMO datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: to boost automated mathematical proof systems to tackle high-difficulty competition problems by enabling scalable, test-time optimization over numerous candidate proofs.

Method: train three proof-oriented capabilities (proof generation, proof verification, critique-conditioned proof repair) within a defense-in-depth generative verifier; deploy as M3; at test time, treat M3 as generator, verifier, refiner, and ranker; perform population-based search and tournament selection to select final proof.

Result: reported results: 35/42 on IMO 2025; 36/42 on USAMO 2026; exceed human gold-medal threshold on both.

Conclusion: the approach demonstrates potential for scalable, contest-grade automated theorem proving, with strong empirical gains; further work needed to validate robustness, reproducibility, and generalization, and to examine potential overfitting or data leakage concerns.

Abstract: We present MaxProof, a population-level test-time scaling framework for competition-level mathematical proof in the MiniMax-M3 series. M3 first trains three proof-oriented capabilities -- proof generation, proof verification, and critique-conditioned proof repair -- using a defense-in-depth generative verifier engineered for low false-positive rate. These capabilities are merged into a single released M3 model. At test time, MaxProof treats the model as a generator, verifier, refiner, and ranker, searches over a population of candidate proofs, and returns one final proof through tournament selection. With MaxProof test-time scaling, the M3 model reaches 35/42 on IMO 2025 and 36/42 on USAMO 2026, exceeding the human gold-medal threshold on both.

</details>


### [187] [SupraBench: A Benchmark for Supramolecular Chemistry](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13477)
*Tianyi Ma,Yijun Ma,Zehong Wang,Weixiang Sun,Ziming Li,Connor R. Schmidt,Chuxu Zhang,Matthew J. Webber,Yanfang Ye*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces SupraBench, the first benchmark to evaluate LLMs on supramolecular chemistry reasoning across four core tasks (binding affinity prediction, top-binder selection, solvent identification, host-guest description) plus a vision-based molecular-ID task, and SupraPMC, a 16M-token domain-specific corpus; results show substantial remaining headroom for LLMs, with domain-adaptation pretraining improving regression performance but compromising strict letter-format outputs; datasets and code released for community use.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the bottleneck in designing host-guest supramolecular systems, which is time-consuming in dry-lab verification, and the lack of systematic benchmarks to assess LLMs' chemistry reasoning in this domain.

Method: Define four fundamental tasks plus an auxiliary vision task; curate SupraPMC (16M tokens) from Europe PMC; benchmark a broad set of open and proprietary LLMs; analyze performance, failure modes, and the effect of domain adaptation pretraining on SupraPMC; provide results and release code/data at the project repository.

Result: LLMs exhibit substantial headroom across the tasks; domain-adaptation pretraining on SupraPMC transfers well to in-distribution regression but reduces fidelity of strictly formatted outputs; task difficulty varies across task families, revealing distinct failure modes and gaps in supramolecular reasoning.

Conclusion: SupraBench and SupraPMC fill a critical gap for evaluating and advancing LLM-based reasoning in supramolecular chemistry; while progress is evident, targeted domain adaptation and improved reasoning strategies are needed to close remaining gaps. The work provides a foundation for future research and practical benchmarking in this domain.

Abstract: Supramolecular chemistry, which includes the study of non-covalent host-guest assemblies, has advanced various applications. However, designing host-guest systems remains time-consuming, requiring days of dry-lab verification per candidate pair. Although LLMs have emerged as a fast alternative with strong performance on molecular binding tasks, no benchmark currently systematically evaluates LLMs for host-guest reasoning across fundamental supramolecular chemistry tasks, e.g., binding affinity prediction. To this end, we collaborate with domain experts to release the first Supramolecular Benchmark, called SupraBench, to evaluate LLMs in chemistry reasoning. Specifically, we design four fundamental tasks, i.e., binding affinity prediction, top-binder selection, solvent identification, and host-guest description, plus an auxiliary vision-based task for molecular identification. We also release SupraPMC, a curated 16M-token corpus of Supramolecular chemistry articles distilled from Europe PMC, to support the adaptation to the supramolecular domain. We benchmark a broad range of open and proprietary LLMs and find that LLMs leave substantial headroom across all tasks. Domain adaptation pretraining over SupraPMC transfers cleanly to in-distribution regression but trades off against strict letter-format output. Moreover, the difficulty profile differs sharply across task families, revealing distinct failure modes that indicate specific gaps in current supramolecular chemistry reasoning. Our source codes and benchmark datasets are available at https://github.com/Tianyi-Billy-Ma/SupraBench.

</details>


### [188] [CRAFTIIF: Cross-Resolution Analytic Four-Type Interpretable Isolation Forest for Multivariate Time Series Anomaly Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13486)
*William Smits*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CRAFTIIF is a fully unsupervised framework that detects point, distributional, temporal, and collective anomalies in multivariate time series using cross-resolution analytic wavelet features feeding five Isolation Forests (four per type plus a meta-IF); adaptive thresholding; interpretable by branch attribution; strong results on mTSBench with ablation showing necessity of components; code available.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Unsupervised detection across diverse anomaly types with dataset-agnostic tuning and interpretability; existing methods typically target a single type and lack interpretable attribution.

Method: Generate K=500 random analytic wavelet feature drawings across Morlet, DOG, Haar, Coiflet; each targets a specific anomaly type; train five isolation forests; meta-IF for compound anomalies; adaptive Otsu/MAD threshold calibrates across anomaly rates from 0.1% to 69.2%; branch firing indicates anomaly type.

Result: On mTSBench 19 datasets: mean F1=0.228 across all datasets; F1=0.322 on 13 detectable datasets; ranks first on VUS-PR (0.463 vs. 0.329); ablation shows adaptive thresholding adds +38% F1, four-branch structure +20%, meta-IF +23%; oracle framework identifies 6/19 undetectable datasets.

Conclusion: CRAFTIIF provides four-type interpretable unsupervised anomaly detection, with component contributions validated; code in GitHub.

Abstract: Anomaly detection in multivariate time series is challenged by four structurally distinct anomaly types -- point (isolated spikes), distributional (level shifts), temporal (rhythm changes), and collective (inter-sensor correlation breakdowns) -- each requiring different feature representations. Most unsupervised methods target only one or two types and provide limited interpretability. We present CRAFTIIF (Cross-Resolution Analytic Four-Type Interpretable Isolation Forest), a fully unsupervised framework targeting all four types without dataset-specific tuning. CRAFTIIF generates K=500 random analytic wavelet feature draws across four families (Morlet, DOG, Haar, Coiflet), each targeting a specific anomaly type, feeding five structured Isolation Forests -- one per type plus a meta-IF for compound anomalies. An adaptive Otsu/MAD threshold calibrates detection automatically across anomaly rates from 0.1% to 69.2%. Because each IF is trained exclusively on type-specific features, branch firing provides direct anomaly-type attribution by construction, without post-hoc explanation. Evaluated on all 19 datasets of the mTSBench benchmark (Zhou et al., TMLR 2026), CRAFTIIF achieves mean F1=0.228 (all 19 datasets) and F1=0.322 (13 detectable datasets), ranking first among all 25 evaluated methods on VUS-PR (0.463 vs. previous best 0.329, +40.7%). A diagnostic framework -- oracle F1, detectability limits, and branch separation ratios -- identifies 6 of 19 datasets as fundamentally undetectable by any unsupervised method. Ablation over 11 conditions confirms adaptive thresholding (+38% F1), four-branch structure (+20%), and meta-IF (+23%) are each essential. Code: https://github.com/smitswil/craftiif

</details>


### [189] [A2D2: Fine-Tuning Any-Length Discrete Diffusion for Adaptive Decoding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13565)
*Sophia Tang,Yuchen Zhu,Molei Tao,Pranam Chatterjee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A2D2: Fine-Tuning Any-Length Discrete Diffusion for Adaptive Decoding; joint optimization of insertion and unmasking policies with a quality-based inference schedule; leverages Radon-Nikodym derivatives to converge to reward-tilted distributions without target samples; introduces Adaptive Joint Decoding (AJD) loss; shows empirical gains over fixed-length and guidance baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fill the gap in principled reward-guided fine-tuning for any-length discrete diffusion, enabling flexible, high-quality sequence generation with adaptive decoding.

Method: Derive the Radon-Nikodym derivative for joint insertion-unmasking path measures; optimize unmasking and insertion quality; propose AJD loss that yields the optimal path measure for reward tilting; integrate with a quality-based inference schedule; training jointly for insertion and unmasking.

Result: Empirical improvements in reward optimization and generation quality/flexibility compared with prior fixed-length fine-tuning and inference-time guidance methods.

Conclusion: A2D2 provides a theoretically grounded framework for reward-guided fine-tuning of any-length discrete diffusion, achieving convergence to the reward-tilted distribution and improved practical performance.

Abstract: Discrete diffusion models offer a simple and stable likelihood-based framework for sequence generation, recently extended to any-length settings via token insertion. Principled reward-guided fine-tuning for any-length discrete diffusion, however, remains largely unexplored. We introduce Fine-Tuning Any-Length Discrete Diffusion for Adaptive Decoding (A2D2), a unified framework for reward-guided fine-tuning of any-length discrete diffusion models via joint optimization of the insertion and unmasking policies together with a quality-based inference schedule. We derive the Radon-Nikodym derivative for the joint insertion-unmasking path measures, enabling theoretically guaranteed convergence to the intractable reward-tilted sequence distribution without requiring target samples. Building on this, we establish unmasking and insertion quality as tractable approaches for minimizing decoding error and introduce the Adaptive Joint Decoding (AJD) loss, which provably yields the optimal path measure that generates the reward-tilted distribution. Empirically, A2D2 improves reward optimization while enhancing generation flexibility and accuracy over prior fixed-length fine-tuning and inference-time guidance methods.

</details>


### [190] [Adjusted Cup-Product Neural Layer](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13568)
*Snigdha Chandan Khilar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Adjusted cup product neural layer yields a gauge-invariant readout; the gauge-invariant signal comes solely from an adjustment coefficient; zero coefficient nullifies the output; the observable is a nonzero quadratic form invariant under one and two gauge transformations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Many observables in physics and geometry arise from cup products of cochains; designing neural readouts that are gauge-invariant by construction is desirable. The paper introduces an adjustment term from higher gauge theory to enforce gauge invariance.

Method: Define an adjusted cup product neural layer that couples the cup product with a higher-gauge-theory adjustment term; analyze its behavior on closed cycles; prove the output depends only on the adjustment coefficient; show that setting the coefficient to zero eliminates the output regardless of other parameters; establish that the observable is a nonzero quadratic form and invariant under one- and two-gauge transformations;

Result: On a closed cycle, the output relies entirely on the adjustment coefficient; setting the coefficient to zero removes the output; the adjustment is the only source of gauge-invariant signal; the observable is a nonzero quadratic form and is exactly invariant under one- and two-gauge transformations.

Conclusion: The adjustment term is the sole source of gauge-invariant signal in this construction; the adjusted cup product neural layer yields a gauge-invariant observable grounded in cup products and higher gauge theory; the result has implications for constructing invariant features in physical and geometric contexts.

Abstract: Many important observables in physics and geometry are cup products of cochains. The adjusted cup product neural layer has been introduced in this paper. It is a neural primitive that hard wires the cup product with an adjustment term from higher gauge theory. This creates a readout that is gauge invariant by design. Their main theoretical result shows that on a closed cycle the output relies entirely on the adjustment coefficient. Setting this coefficient to zero removes the output completely regardless of other parameters. Thus the adjustment is the only source of gauge invariant signal. They prove this observable is a nonzero quadratic form and is exactly invariant under one and two gauge transformations.

</details>


### [191] [Existence Precedes Value: Joint Modeling of Observational Existence and Evolving States in Time Series Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13571)
*Yifan Hu,Hongzhou Chen,Peiyuan Liu,Yiding Liu,Zewei Dong,Jiang-Ming Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Timeflies reframes forecasting with incomplete/irregular time series as a joint problem of predicting future observability and values. It introduces a dual-stream architecture (observation and value streams) with reliability-aware embedding, observation-guided dependency modeling, and joint prediction, plus a new benchmark (Shadow) and an OVJE metric to evaluate coupled predictability, showing empirical improvements over prior methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world time series are frequently incomplete and irregular due to sensor dormancy, transmission delays, and event-driven sampling. Existing approaches either impute first or rely on continuous-time models that assume future observation timestamps are known (oracle). This assumption undermines practical applicability since forecasting should also account for whether a valid observation will occur.

Method: Propose Timeflies, a unified framework with two interacting streams: an observation stream and a value stream. It couples them via three modules: reliability-aware embedding (to assess observation reliability), observation-guided dependency modeling (to capture how observations influence state evolution), and joint prediction (to estimate both future observability and value). Additionally, construct Shadow, a benchmark combining public data missingness with real-world industrial data, and introduce OVJE (Observation-Value Joint Entropy) to quantify the predictability of the coupled task.

Result: Empirical evaluations show Timeflies consistently outperforms existing methods, highlighting the benefit of modeling future observability in forecasting with missing values.

Conclusion: Explicitly modeling future observability is crucial for accurate forecasting under incomplete data. Timeflies provides a practical framework, supported by a new benchmark and metric to evaluate the coupled predictability of observations and values.

Abstract: Real-world time series are often highly incomplete and irregular due to sensor dormancy, transmission delays, and event-driven sampling, making reliable forecasting fundamentally challenging. Existing methods have evolved from impute-then-forecast pipelines to continuous-time models such as Neural ODEs and continuous-time graph networks. While these approaches improve the modeling of historical irregularity, they still rely on an implicit oracle assumption at inference time: the timestamps of future valid observations are presumed to be known in advance. This assumption limits practical relevance, since in many real systems the more fundamental question is not only what the future value will be, but also whether a valid observation will occur at all. In this paper, we propose Timeflies, a unified framework that reformulates forecasting as a joint problem of future observability inference and value estimation. To explicitly model the interaction between observation dynamics and state evolution, Timeflies adopts an observation stream and a value stream, coupled through three dedicated modules for reliability-aware embedding, observation-guided dependency modeling, and joint prediction. We further construct Shadow, a benchmark that combines natural missingness from public datasets with real-world industrial data, and introduce the Observation-Value Joint Entropy (OVJE) metric to comprehensively evaluate this coupled predictability. Extensive experiments show that Timeflies consistently outperforms existing methods, highlighting the importance of explicitly modeling future observability in time series forecasting with missing values. Code and dataset are available in https://github.com/ant-intl/Timeflies.

</details>


### [192] [Learning with Simulators: No Regret in a Computationally Bounded World](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13576)
*Sasha Voitovych,Abhishek Shetty,Noah Golowich,Alexander Rakhlin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces simulatable processes to extend VC-based generalization guarantees to dependent data via a data-generating simulator, yielding i.i.d.-like error bounds; presents conditional sampling advantages and a universal, time-bounded-Kolmogorov-complexity–based algorithm for all polynomial-time simulatable processes, broadening PAC.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Generalization under data dependency remains challenging; need a framework that leverages simulators to recover classical learning guarantees and analyze computational-statistical gaps.

Method: Define simulatable processes with access to a data simulator; prove VC-dimension–based generalization bounds in this setting; analyze conditional sampling; construct a single algorithm that learns any VC class for processes simulatable in bounded polynomial time; relate regret to time-bounded Kolmogorov complexity.

Result: 1) Recovery of classical VC bounds under simulatable processes; 2) Demonstration of strict benefits of conditional sampling; 3) A universal algorithm for all polynomial-time simulatable processes with regret controlled by the process's time-bounded Kolmogorov complexity.

Conclusion: Broadens the classical PAC framework to dependent data contexts by leveraging simulators, enabling i.i.d.-like guarantees and highlighting computational-statistical trade-offs via time-bounded complexity.

Abstract: Understanding the minimal assumptions necessary for generalization is the fundamental question in learning theory. Unfortunately, most results rely heavily on independence (or some proxy thereof) of the data-generating process, while results for strongly dependent data are far more limited. Towards addressing this gap, we introduce the framework of simulatable processes, where the learner has access to a simulator that approximates the distribution generating the data (which may be an arbitrarily complex and dependent process). Surprisingly, given access to such a simulator, we show that we can recover the same learning guarantees as in the classical setting with independent data, namely, error bounds that depend on the VC dimension. Further, we use this framework to study the power of conditional sampling and show strict statistical and computational advantages in this setting. As a highlight of our framework, we exhibit a single algorithm that simultaneously learns any given VC class under all processes samplable in bounded polynomial time, with regret controlled by the time-bounded Kolmogorov complexity of the process. This provides a significant conceptual broadening of the classical PAC model.

</details>


### [193] [Simplex-Constrained Sparse Bagging: Transitioning from Uniform Priors to Sparse Posteriors in Ensemble Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13589)
*Meher Sai Preetam,Meher Bhaskar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SCSB uses simplex-constrained optimization with a concave quadratic penalty to prune and calibrate bootstrap ensembles, achieving up to 96% compression with faster inference and improved calibration while preserving accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address overconfidence and inefficiency in traditional bagging due to uniform voting power; provide a model-agnostic method for post-training pruning and probability calibration.

Method: Formulate ensemble pruning and calibration as a joint optimization on the probability simplex by minimizing Out-Of-Bag loss; address the L1-simplex paradox with a concave quadratic penalty to induce sparsity; model-agnostic, post-training approach.

Result: Achieves up to 96% ensemble compression, leading to linear inference speedups and improved probability calibration (lower Expected Calibration Error); maintains or improves generalization accuracy.

Conclusion: SCSB offers a rigorous, model-agnostic framework for efficient, well-calibrated bagging ensembles through simplex-constrained pruning and calibration, substantially reducing computation without sacrificing performance.

Abstract: We present Simplex-Constrained Sparse Bagging (SCSB), a mathematically rigorous framework for post-training compression and probability calibration of bootstrap-based bagging ensembles. Standard bagging ensembles (such as Random Forests, Bagged SVMs, and Bagged Neural Networks) assign uniform voting power to all constituent estimators. However, this naive uniform prior ignores the varying local competence of base estimators and contributes to model overconfidence. We formulate ensemble pruning and calibration as a joint optimization problem over the probability simplex by minimizing the Out-Of-Bag (OOB) loss. To induce sparsity, we address the theoretical "L1-simplex paradox" -- the mathematical reality that the L1 norm is constant on the simplex and fails to prune -- by introducing a concave quadratic penalty. SCSB is model-agnostic and achieves up to 96% ensemble compression, yielding linear inference speedups and superior probability calibration (lowered Expected Calibration Error) while preserving or enhancing generalization accuracy.

</details>


### [194] [Beyond the Commitment Boundary: Probing Epiphenomenal Chain-of-Thought in Large Reasoning Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13603)
*Daniel Scalena,Sara Candussio,Luca Bortolussi,Elisabetta Fersini,Malvina Nissim,Gabriele Sarti*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CoT reasoning exhibits a commitment boundary with a rapid transition to the final answer; many steps after are epiphenomenal; early exit of CoT at boundary reduces length and maintains performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how individual reasoning steps causally influence final answers and to improve efficiency by shortening reasoning traces.

Method: Quantify step-wise causal importance via early exit across multiple tasks and model families; analyze using attention probes to identify answer-formation stages; implement early-exit at the commitment boundary and evaluate CoT length and task performance.

Result: A single-step commitment boundary is common; later CoT steps do not affect final probabilities; answer-formation can be decoded from intermediate steps; early-exit reduces CoT length up to 55% with negligible performance loss.

Conclusion: Reasoning unfolds in distinct stages; exploiting early commitment signals enables effective pruning of CoT without compromising accuracy, suggesting robust, generalizable efficiency gains.

Abstract: Chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning is the dominant paradigm for inference-time scaling in language models, yet the causal influence of individual steps on the final answer poorly understood. We estimate each step's causal importance via early exit and use this measure to study how answers form across the reasoning traces of several model families. Across diverse tasks, we find that reasoning typically crosses a \emph{commitment boundary} -- a sharp transition from transient intermediate guesses to a stable, high-confidence answer. This transition often happens in a single step, well before the model's reasoning block ends, and is followed by \emph{epiphenomenal} CoT steps that leave the final answer probability unaltered. Using attention probes, we show that answer-formation stages can be linearly decoded from intermediate reasoning steps with high accuracy and generalize robustly to unseen reasoning tasks. We exploit this signal to early-exit reasoning blocks at the commitment boundary, reducing the length of CoTs up to 55\% on average with negligible impact on model performance.

</details>


### [195] [The Stable Recovery Manifold: Geometric Principles Governing Recoverability in Continual Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13637)
*Ayushman Trivedi,Bhavika Melwani*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Recovery Subspace Dimensionality kt stabilizes across sequential tasks in continual learning, indicating stable recoverability despite representational drift; a simple geometric model explains most recoverability variance and supports the Stable Recovery Manifold hypothesis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand why forgetting occurs in continual learning by examining the geometric structure of recoverability and whether forgotten knowledge becomes irrecoverable or remains decodable within a stable subspace.

Method: Sequential training on Split CIFAR-100 using a ResNet-18; measure recoverability, representational drift, and recovery complexity across ten tasks. Introduce kt as the minimum number of singular directions to preserve 90% of full probe performance. Analyze principal-angle drift and develop a geometric model to predict recoverability.

Result: kt is stable with mean 8.0 despite substantial drift. Principal-angle drift correlates with recoverability (r = -0.862). The geometric model explains 82.2% of recoverability variance.

Conclusion: Forgotten knowledge remains compactly decodable within a stable recovery manifold; catastrophic forgetting is primarily an accessibility/manifold-alignment issue rather than destruction of information.

Abstract: Catastrophic forgetting is often viewed as the destruction of previously learned knowledge during sequential learning. Building on the Accessibility Collapse framework, we investigate the geometric structure of recoverability in continual learning. Using Split CIFAR-100 and a sequentially trained ResNet-18, we analyze recoverability, representational drift, and recovery complexity across ten tasks. We introduce Recovery Subspace Dimensionality (k_t), a measure of the minimum number of singular directions required to preserve 90 percent of full probe performance. Contrary to our Recoverability Diffusion hypothesis, recovery dimensionality remains stable throughout training (mean k_t = 8.0) despite substantial representational drift. Principal-angle drift strongly predicts recoverability (r = -0.862), and a simple geometric model explains 82.2 percent of recoverability variance. These findings support the Stable Recovery Manifold hypothesis, suggesting that forgotten knowledge remains compactly decodable despite representational reorganization. The results indicate that catastrophic forgetting is primarily an accessibility and manifold-alignment problem rather than information destruction.

</details>


### [196] [Dense Supervision, Sparse Updates: On the Sparsity and Geometry of On-Policy Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13657)
*Guo Yu,Wenlin Liu,Yulan Hu,Hao-Xuan Ma,Jun-Peng Jiang,Han-Jia Ye*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: OPD yields small, coordinate-sparse, FFN-heavy updates distributed across layers; sparse subnetworks can achieve near-full OPD performance. Dense teacher supervision preserves heterogeneous gradient scales, making AdamW preferable to a sparsity-inducing SGD.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how on-policy distillation (OPD) shapes parameter updates in large language and vision-language models, and what this implies about the underlying geometry and sparsity.

Method: Empirical analysis across multiple model pairs and use cases, examining update sparsity, layer distribution, optimization behavior (SGD with sparsity-promoting updates vs AdamW), and the spectral geometry of updates relative to source weights.

Result: Updates are small and coordinate-sparse, distributed across layers and FFN-heavy. Training only the discovered subnetwork recovers nearly the same performance as full OPD. The sparsity-inducing SGD underperforms AdamW due to preservation of heterogeneous gradient scales by dense teacher supervision. Updates are numerically full-rank but spectrally concentrated, lying away from the source weights’ principal singular subspaces and concentrating on coordinates where source weights are near zero.

Conclusion: Dense teacher supervision in OPD does not produce plain dense parameter rewriting; OPD retains important geometric signatures of on-policy post-training.

Abstract: On-policy distillation (\textsc{OPD}) has recently become a prominent post-training recipe as it combines two desirable ingredients: on-policy student trajectories and dense teacher supervision, yet how this hybrid changes a model's parameters remains unclear. Across several language and vision-language model pairs and use cases, our analysis yields two main findings. On sparsity, \textsc{OPD}-style updates are small and coordinate-sparse. They are distributed across layers and are usually FFN-heavy. This sparse structure is operationally useful: training only the discovered subnetwork recovers nearly the same performance as full \textsc{OPD}. However, the sparsity-inducing SGD optimizer underperforms AdamW in our optimizer ablation, likely because dense teacher supervision preserves heterogeneous coordinate-wise gradient scales where AdamW's adaptive scaling remains useful. On geometry, the updates are numerically full-rank but spectrally concentrated; they lie mostly away from the principal singular subspaces of the source weights and fall disproportionately on coordinates where the source weights are close to zero. These findings suggest that dense teacher supervision does not turn \textsc{OPD} into ordinary dense parameter rewriting; instead, \textsc{OPD} retains important geometric signatures of on-policy post-training.

</details>


### [197] [Understanding Truncated Positional Encodings for Graph Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13671)
*James Flora,Mitchell Black,Weng-Keen Wong,Amir Nayyeri*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Truncating spectral and walk-based positional encodings changes their expressive power: the previously claimed equivalence breaks under truncation, with truncated spectral PEs no longer exceeding 1-WL. The study also analyzes k-harmonic distances to illustrate nuanced differences among truncated PEs, and empirically shows that combining multiple truncated PEs outperforms any single family on real data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Practitioners use truncated encodings for efficiency, but the theoretical equivalence of PE families assumes complete encodings. Understanding how truncation affects expressivity and performance is essential for effective GNN design.

Method: Theoretically analyze the expressive power of truncated spectral and walk-based PEs, show that truncation yields fundamental differences between PE families, derive a corollary that truncated spectral PEs are not stronger than 1-WL, examine the k-harmonic distances to highlight differences between closely related truncated PEs, and perform experiments on real-world datasets comparing mixed vs single truncated PEs.

Result: Under truncation, several PE families become distinct in expressive power; truncated spectral PEs do not surpass 1-WL; k-harmonic distances reveal nuanced differences among truncated PEs; empirically, mixing truncated PEs outperforms any single family on real-world data.

Conclusion: Truncation undermines the complete-projection equivalence of PEs; to leverage PEs in practice, using a mix of truncated encodings yields better performance, and care should be taken in choosing truncation schemes (e.g., spectral vs walk-based, and k-harmonic variants).

Abstract: Positional encodings (PEs) enhance the power of graph neural networks (GNNs), both theoretically and empirically. Two of the most popular families of PEs - spectral (e.g., Laplacian eigenspaces, effective resistance) and walk-based (polynomials of the adjacency matrix) - are theoretically equivalent in expressive power, with expressivity between the 1-WL and 3-WL tests. However, this equivalence assumes the GNN uses the "complete" version of these PEs, which requires $O(n^3)$ time and space complexity. Instead, practitioners commonly use truncated variants of these encodings, such as the first $k$ eigenspaces or powers of the adjacency matrix. However, the theoretical properties of these truncated PEs are unknown. In this work, we initiate the study of these truncated PEs. Theoretically, we show that, under truncation, several families of PEs are fundamentally different in expressive power. As a corollary, we show that truncated spectral PEs are no longer stronger than the 1-WL test. We also study a family of spectral PEs, the $k$-harmonic distances, to highlight the differences in expressive power of even closely related truncated PEs. Finally, we experimentally show that a mix of truncated PEs is preferable to any single family on real-world datasets.

</details>


<div id='cs.RO'></div>

# cs.RO [[Back]](#toc)

### [198] [Learning to Assist: Collaborative VLAs for Implicit Human-Robot Collaboration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12475)
*Leo Xu,Letian Li,Alex Cuellar,Michael Hagenow*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: End-to-end vision-language-action policies can enable collaborative manipulation but suffer action leakage across latent task transitions; an inference-time steering method mitigates premature assistance, enabling longer-horizon collaboration with improved performance in a 16-participant study.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve scalability and flexibility of HRC beyond hand-engineered pipelines by leveraging end-to-end imitation learning; understand real-world failure modes of VLA models in collaborative tasks.

Method: Evaluate two state-of-the-art VLA models on collaborative manipulation tasks, identify action-chunking failure where action chunks cross latent task transitions (action leakage); develop an inference-time steering mechanism to curb premature assistive actions; conduct a 16-participant user study on a long-horizon collaborative assembly task comparing steering vs non-steering policy, measuring horizon length, assist timing, collaboration speed, and failure rates.

Result: Inference-time steering reduced action leakage and premature assistance while preserving policy performance, enabling longer execution horizons; in the user study, steering led to faster collaboration and fewer failures compared to a shorter-horizon policy.

Conclusion: End-to-end VLA policies are viable for HRC but require mechanisms to control action-chunk leakage; inference-time steering is an effective solution to enable long-horizon, reliable collaboration in real-world settings.

Abstract: Human-robot collaboration (HRC) combines the complementary strengths of humans and robots to improve task efficiency. However, many existing collaborative systems rely on hand-engineered pipelines, limiting their scalability and flexibility for new tasks. In this work, we show that models trained end-to-end with imitation learning, specifically vision-language-action (VLA) models, can support collaborative manipulation, and characterize the key factors affecting their real-world performance. We evaluate two state-of-the-art models and identify a failure mode of action-chunking policies in implicit HRC, where demonstration action leakage (i.e., action chunks crossing latent task transitions) can cause premature assistive behavior. We find that this issue increases with longer execution horizons and occurs in real-world collaborative VLA systems, such as when a robot attempts to hand over a tool before the person is ready. We propose an inference-time steering method to mitigate these erroneous assistive actions while preserving policy performance. Finally, through a 16-participant user study on a long-horizon collaborative assembly task, we show that steering enables a longer execution horizon while mitigating premature assistance, leading to faster collaboration and fewer failures compared to a shorter-horizon policy.

</details>


### [199] [Action-Effect Memory Pretraining for Robot Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12499)
*Yijing Zhou,Qiwei Liang,Sitong Zhuang,Jiaxi Li,Xianpeng Wang,Boyang Cai,Yunyang Mo,Renjing Xu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Action-Effect Memory (AEM) pretraining learns a compact, action-conditioned temporal history for robot manipulation by masked reconstruction over vision-action sequences, yielding improved policy performance and efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Under partial observability and non-Markovian dynamics in manipulation, single-frame encodings are insufficient; a compact temporal representation is needed for downstream control.

Method: Pretrain with AEM by interleaving visual and action features, applying masked modeling to recover missing content from history; produce a Mamba-encoded output of the final vision token as a compact history representation to serve as global context for decoding and downstream control; preserves a single-vector temporal bottleneck to keep inference efficient; evaluate with Diffusion Policy and Flow Policy; compare to single-frame pretraining and frame stacking; ablations to quantify history-aware benefits and latency.

Result: AEM consistently improves manipulation performance in both simulation and real-world settings across clean, cluttered, and random scenes as well as non-Markovian tasks; outperforms baselines; ablations show history-aware pretraining surpasses single-frame pretraining and direct frame stacking; also reduces inference latency and computational cost.

Conclusion: AEM demonstrates effective temporal representation learning for robot manipulation, providing a compact, action-conditioned history that improves policy generalization and efficiency, with strong potential for real-world deployment and compatibility as a drop-in enhancement for existing policies.

Abstract: We present AEM, an Action-Effect Memory pretraining framework for robot manipulation that learns compact temporal representations from vision-action history. Unlike prior robot representation pretraining methods that mainly focus on single-frame visual encoding, AEM targets the temporal nature of manipulation, where the current observation alone is often insufficient under partial observability. AEM models manipulation as an action-driven interaction process by interleaving visual and action features and applying masked modeling to recover missing content from incomplete histories, thereby learning action-conditioned state evolution. The Mamba-encoded output of the final vision token is used as a compact history representation, serving as the global context for decoding and downstream control. This design preserves a single-vector temporal bottleneck while keeping inference efficient. We evaluate AEM with Diffusion Policy and Flow Policy. AEM consistently improves manipulation performance in both simulation and real-world settings, outperforming baselines across clean scenes, cluttered and random scenes, and non-Markovian tasks. Ablation studies further show that history-aware pretraining surpasses single-frame pretraining and direct frame stacking, while reducing inference latency and computational cost.

</details>


### [200] [Foresight: Iterative Reasoning About Clues that Matter for Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12550)
*Arthur Zhang,Carl Qi,Donne Su,Xiangyun Meng,Amy Zhang,Joydeep Biswas*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Foresight uses a finetuned Vision-Language Model in a test-time plan-critique loop for open-world, mapless navigation, guided by a reward model trained from human feedback, achieving real-world improvements and real-time performance on edge hardware.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Open-world mapless navigation with sparse language instructions suffers from underspecified goals and reliance on fixed navigation factors. Pretrained VLMs can discover instruction-relevant cues but require adaptation to focus on cues that matter for motion planning.

Method: A test-time framework where a finetuned VLM alternates between proposing image-space motion plans and critiquing them with the language goal and visual context. Plans are refined iteratively based on critiques. A reward model is learned from human feedback and used to post-train the VLM with reinforcement learning in the plan-critique loop.

Result: In offline tests and 6 real-world environments, Foresight improves average task success by 37% and reduces interventions per mission by 52% relative to baselines, while running in real-time on a Jetson AGX Orin. Code, data, and training details will be released.

Conclusion: Foresight demonstrates that test-time reasoning with a finetuned VLM and plan-critique reinforcement learning can significantly enhance navigation performance in open-set, environment-rich tasks, with practical real-world deployment and reproducibility.

Abstract: Open-world mapless navigation from sparse language instructions requires resolving underspecified goals and inferring which environmental cues are relevant for reaching the goal. For instance, reaching an out-of-view destination may require interpreting ramps, signs, or detours that reveal where to go or which route to take. Prior works are limited by their reliance on known navigation factors and closed-set factor categories, or identify cues before motion planning and miss plan-dependent cues. We argue that pretrained Vision-Language Models (VLMs) can discover novel instruction-relevant cues, but require adaptation to focus on which cues matter and how they should influence motion planning. We realize these ideas in Foresight, a test-time framework in which a finetuned VLM alternates between proposing image-space motion plans and critiquing them using the language goal and visual context. Subsequent plans are conditioned on prior critiques, enabling iterative motion refinement before execution. To align plan critiques and refinements with open-set behavior preferences, we learn a reward model from human feedback and use it to post-train the VLM with reinforcement learning in the plan-critique loop. In offline evaluations and 6 real-world environments, Foresight improves average task success by 37% and reduces interventions per mission by 52% relative to state-of-the-art test-time reasoning and foundation-model baselines, while running in real-time on a Jetson AGX Orin. We will release code, data, and training details to support future work on test-time reasoning for robot motion refinement. Additional videos at: https://amrl.cs.utexas.edu/foresight

</details>


### [201] [G-MAPP: GPU-accelerated Multi-Agent Planning and Perception for Reactive Motion Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12579)
*Tanmay Bishnoi,Riddhiman Laha,Tobias Löw,Jose Alex Chandy,Luis F. C. Figueredo,Sami Haddadin*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: GPU-accelerated reactive motion planning that speeds up world modeling and vector-field planning, enabling real-time, collision-free navigation in dynamic clutter, achieving up to 5x CPU speedup.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address runtime bottlenecks in high-fidelity environmental modeling and perception-planning coupling that limit reactive motion in unstructured environments.

Method: Implement GPU-accelerated world modeling and GPU-based vector-field planning for quasi-global trajectory exploration; real-time perception-action loop with depth sensors; evaluate on CPU vs GPU; real-world tests on a 7-DoF robot.

Result: Demonstrates up to 5x speedup over CPU version; successful collision avoidance in simple and hard scenarios; improved runtime performance and planning tight coupling in dynamic clutter.

Conclusion: GPU-based framework enables real-time reactive motion in unstructured environments without compromising environment representations, showing feasibility for depth-based manipulation with fast planning.

Abstract: Reactive motion generation in unstructured environments remains an open challenge in robotics. Due to the computational complexity of collision-free motion generation, existing methods either generate global trajectories for static scenarios, or employ models that make conservative assumptions about the environment. This paper identifies the primary bottleneck as the runtime performance demand of planning on high-fidelity environments, and the temporal integration between the perception and planning modules. Therefore, we propose a framework that does not compromise on runtime performance and world representations for perception and planning by accelerating world modeling and vector-field based planning using the GPU. This allows us to achieve faster parallel state exploration for quasi-global trajectory planning, and tighter coupling of the perception-action loop in real-time for dynamic cluttered environments with off-the-shelf depth sensors. We quantitatively evaluate the computation-time and success rate differences for the CPU and GPU versions of our planner, and perform qualitative evaluations of our coupled framework using real-world experiments on a 7-DoF Franka Emika robot. Experimental results demonstrate that our GPU-based framework achieves up to a 5x speedup over the CPU version and successfully avoids collisions across both trivial and challenging physical world scenarios.

</details>


### [202] [From Imitation to Alignment: Human-Preference Flow Policies for Long-Horizon Sidewalk Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12603)
*Honglin He,Zhizheng Liu,Yukai Ma,Bolei Zhou*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: FlowPilot uses anchored flow matching for monocular, mapless sidewalk navigation and a human-in-the-loop preference learning loop to improve social compliance and counterfactual reasoning, yielding moderate simulation performance and enhanced real-world robustness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Autonomous long-horizon sidewalk navigation requires robust, perception-efficient control with social compliance; imitation learning alone suffers compounding errors and weak counterfactual reasoning on dynamic sidewalks.

Method: 1) Pre-train a policy with anchored flow matching on large-scale robot fleet data to capture multimodal sidewalk behaviors; 2) apply a human-in-the-loop preference learning scheme to tune policy with small amounts of intervention data to improve counterfactual reasoning and social compliance; 3) evaluate on simulation and real-world sidewalk environments.

Result: In simulation, FlowPilot achieves 42% success rate and 66% route completion. FlowPilot-HP improves real-world robustness, reducing IR by 40.0% and NIR by 52.1% relative to the base model.

Conclusion: FlowPilot demonstrates effective monocular, mapless long-horizon sidewalk navigation with improved social compliance through human-in-the-loop tuning; the proposed representation and learning scheme yield notable gains in both simulated and real-world settings.

Abstract: Autonomous long-horizon sidewalk navigation is essential for micro-mobility applications such as robotic food delivery and assistive electronic wheelchairs. Unlike autonomous driving on the road, long-horizon sidewalk navigation requires precise maneuvering through unpredictable sidewalk terrains and pedestrians, with a lightweight perception stack as minimal as a single monocular RGB camera. While imitation learning (IL) from demonstrations offers a practical solution, the resulting autopilot policy often suffers from compounding errors, a lack of social compliance on sidewalks, and deficiencies in counterfactual reasoning to handle complex situations. To address these challenges, we introduce FlowPilot, a mapless navigation policy that achieves robust and efficient long-horizon navigation performance using only a monocular RGB camera. We first propose to use anchored flow matching as an action representation for policy pre-training on large-scale robot fleet data and to capture the diverse, complex, multimodal distribution of sidewalk navigation behaviors. To bridge the gap between imitation and alignment, we further design a human-in-the-loop preference learning scheme to tune the policy on a small amount of human intervention data. It strengthens the model's counterfactual reasoning and social compliance on sidewalks. We evaluate FlowPilot through extensive simulation and real-world experiments in diverse sidewalk environments. FlowPilot achieves 42% success rate and 66% route completion in simulation, while FlowPilot-HP further improves real-world robustness and social compliance, reducing IR by 40.0% and NIR by 52.1% relative to the base model.

</details>


### [203] [EgoEngine: From Egocentric Human Videos to High-Fidelity Dexterous Robot Demonstrations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12604)
*Yangcen Liu,Shuo Cheng,Xinchen Yin,Woo Chul Shin,Alfred Cueva,Yiran Yang,Zhenyang Chen,Chuye Zhang,Danfei Xu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: EgoEngine converts egocentric human videos into robot-ready data by replacing the human with a robot video while preserving context and by producing executable robot action trajectories, enabling zero-shot visuomotor dexterous policy learning without real-robot data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large-scale robot dexterous manipulation is limited by costly data collection; egocentric human videos offer scalable data but require bridging visual and action gaps between humans and robots.

Method: Two-step transformation: (i) generate a high-fidelity robot-observation video replacing the human while preserving scene context and temporal alignment; (ii) compute a task-aligned, executable robot action trajectory under feasibility constraints; implemented as EgoEngine; evaluated in simulation and on real robots.

Result: Demonstrates scalable conversion of human videos into robot data and first zero-shot visuomotor dexterous policy learning from egocentric human videos without real-robot demonstrations; validated through experiments in simulation and on real robots; project website provided.

Conclusion: Shows that EgoEngine enables scalable leverage of egocentric videos for dexterous manipulation, reducing reliance on real-robot demonstrations and enabling zero-shot learning; highlights promising direction while leaving room for addressing feasibility constraints, domain gaps, and generalization.

Abstract: Dexterous manipulation is limited by the cost of collecting large-scale robot demonstrations. Egocentric human videos offer a scalable source of diverse manipulation behaviors, but directly using them for robot learning requires bridging two gaps: the visual gap between human and robot observations, and the action gap between human motion and robot-executable action. We propose EgoEngine, a scalable framework for transforming egocentric human manipulation videos into high-fidelity robot data. Given an egocentric RGB video, EgoEngine produces: (i) a high-fidelity robot observation video replacing human with robot while preserving scene context and temporal alignment, and (ii) a task-aligned, executable robot action trajectory under feasibility constraints. Experiments in simulation and on real robots show that EgoEngine enables scalable conversion of human videos into robot data and, to our knowledge, demonstrates the first zero-shot visuomotor dexterous policy learning from egocentric human videos without real-robot demonstrations. Project website: https://egoengine.github.io.

</details>


### [204] [DARRMS -- An Efficient Algorithm for Dynamic Attention Radius in Resource-Constrained Multi-Agent Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12614)
*Benjamin Alcorn,Eman Hammad*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: An adaptive observation approach for resource-constrained multi-agent systems that tunes each agent's attention radius to balance computation with performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the gap between idealized decision-making (full observability, unlimited compute) and real-world constraints by reducing observation scope while preserving or improving performance.

Method: Introduce a new algorithm that jointly optimizes the attention radius and decision policy; agents selectively observe within an attention radius to reduce computation; theoretical analysis and empirical validation.

Result: The adaptive observation framework improves coordination and scalability under uncertainty, achieving robust decision-making with lower computational demand; empirical results validate effectiveness.

Conclusion: Adaptive observation via controllable attention radii is beneficial for resource-constrained multi-agent systems, enabling efficient planning without substantial performance loss.

Abstract: Multi-agent systems are integral tools for various domains such as robotics, cybersecurity, and autonomous vehicle planning. These types of systems often have constraints on the computational resources, leading to a need for efficient lightweight algorithms. Traditional decision making frameworks often assume ideal conditions, such as full observability and unlimited computational capacity, which do not align with real-world challenges. In this paper, we introduce a new algorithm that allows for reduced demand on computational resources without a large cost of other performance metrics. Agents will limit their observability to some attention radius, which intentionally allows them to ignore parts of the environment that might be unnecessary for action planning. By optimizing both the attention radius and decision-making, our approach enhances coordination and scalability in uncertain environments. Through both theoretical analysis and empirical validation, we demonstrate the effectiveness of adaptive observation in improving system performance and maintaining robust decision-making strategies in resource-constrained systems.

</details>


### [205] [EWAM: An Enhanced World Action Model for Closed-Loop Online Adaptation in Embodied Intelligence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12690)
*Xin Zhou,Cong Miao*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: EWAM introduces an inference-time, closed-loop adaptation framework that augments a frozen Cosmos3 backbone with four differentiable, lightweight modules for memory, anomaly detection, policy routing, and action correction. It operates under a zero-shot protocol, avoiding any backbone fine-tuning or task-specific demonstrations, to reduce deployment data needs for adapting to new task layouts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To minimize additional deployment data and avoid backbone fine-tuning or task-specific demonstrations while enabling rapid adaptation to new task layouts through an online, differentiable, co-reasoning pipeline integrated into a frozen pretrained model.

Method: Attach four lightweight neural layers to a frozen Cosmos3 backbone: (1) Neural Experience Memory Layer for task-relevant context, (2) Neural Anomaly Detection Layer that monitors divergence between predicted and actual states, (3) Neural Policy Routing Layer that decides between direct execution, replanning, or rollback based on anomaly severity, (4) Neural Action Correction Layer that refines action chunks using execution diagnostics; training is minimal/auxiliary and most components are integrated differentiably with only the final routing decision being discrete and supervised.

Result: Demonstrates performance gains in a zero-shot setting solely via the co-reasoning modules, without any fine-tuning of the backbone or added task-specific demonstrations; memory, anomaly detection, routing, and correction modules are deeply integrated into the forward path, leading to coherent control flow adjustments at inference time.

Conclusion: EWAM enables effective adaptation to new task layouts with reduced deployment data by leveraging an integrated, differentiable, inference-time co-reasoning mechanism layered on a frozen backbone, while maintaining a purely zero-shot evaluation protocol.

Abstract: In this paper, we propose the Enhanced World Action Model (EWAM), a closed-loop online adaptation architecture built upon a pretrained and fully frozen Cosmos3 backbone network. Evaluated entirely under a zero-shot task protocol, EWAM is centrally focused on reducing the amount of additional deployment data required to adapt to new task layouts. Notably, no extra task-specific demonstration sets were introduced in any of the evaluations, and no fine-tuning was performed on the backbone network.
