Its performance gains stem entirely from an inference-time co-reasoning mechanism composed of four inserted lightweight neural layers: the Neural Experience Memory Layer located in the intermediate layers of the Diffusion Transformer (DiT) provides task-relevant execution context; the Neural Anomaly Detection Layer after the state prediction head monitors the divergence between predicted and actual states in real time; the Neural Policy Routing Layer dynamically selects direct execution, conservative replanning, or rollback recovery based on the anomaly severity; and the Neural Action Correction Layer refines the generated action chunks using execution diagnostics. Unlike naive feature fusion, the memory, anomaly detection, and correction modules are deeply integrated into the Cosmos3 forward path in a differentiable manner, with only the final routing decision being a discrete supervised one.

</details>


### [206] [EquiDexFlow: Contact-Grounded SE(3)-Equivariant Dexterous Grasp Generative Flows](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12728)
*Clinton Enwerem,John S. Baras,Calin Belta*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: SE(3)-equivariant flow-matching model (EquiDexFlow) jointly predicts pose, joints, contacts, normals, and forces for dexterous grasping, enforcing Coulomb friction constraints and achieving physics-consistent, hardware-feasible grasps with zero friction violations and strong simulation-to-hardware validation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current learned dexterous grasp generators often treat contact forces as a downstream verification step, risking unstable grasps despite kinematic plausibility; there is a need for end-to-end physics-aware grasp generation that respects SE(3) symmetry.

Method: An SE(3)-equivariant flow-matching model taking object point clouds as input and outputting wrist pose, joint angles, fingertip contacts, surface normals, and contact forces. Contacts are projected onto the object surface and forces into the Coulomb friction cone by construction. Proves end-to-end SE(3) equivariance. Trains on 8,100 force-closure grasps across 81 objects for a 16-DoF Allegro Hand. Retargets decoded fingertip contacts to a 16-DoF LEAP Hand via per-finger inverse kinematics. Hardware-feasible refinement ensures joints stay at least 5% inside actuator envelopes while preserving wrench balance.

Result: Zero friction violations; best composite score; lowest wrench residual among ablations. Wrist residuals < 0.04 degrees; exactly zero joint deviation. Achieves open-loop pick-and-hold on six test objects for canonical pose and 120-degree co-rotation. On hardware, retargeted grasps complete open-loop trials.

Conclusion: EquiDexFlow demonstrates end-to-end SE(3) equivariance enabling physically consistent, robust dexterous grasp generation with successful simulation-to-hardware transfer and accessible code/resources.

Abstract: Most learned dexterous grasp generators relegate contact forces to a downstream verification step, so a kinematically-plausible pose can still violate the conditions for a stable physical grasp. We address this with EquiDexFlow, an SE(3)-equivariant flow-matching model that jointly predicts wrist pose, joint angles, fingertip contacts, surface normals, and contact forces from an object point cloud. Our architecture projects contacts onto the object surface and forces into the Coulomb friction cone by construction, so placement and friction compliance hold without loss penalties. We prove end-to-end SE(3) equivariance and verify it empirically over 200 rotations, with wrist residuals below $0.04^\circ$ and exactly zero joint deviation. Trained on 8,100 force-closure grasps across 81 objects for the 16-DoF Allegro Hand, our model achieves zero friction violations, the best composite score, and the lowest wrench residual among all ablation variants. We retarget decoded fingertip contacts to a 16-DoF LEAP Hand via per-finger inverse kinematics, and our hardware-feasible refinement places every joint at least 5% inside its actuator envelope while preserving wrench balance. On the physical robot, retargeted EquiDexFlow-decoded grasps complete open-loop pick-and-hold trials on all six test objects, with every asymmetric object succeeding at both the canonical pose and a $120^\circ$ co-rotation. Videos, code, and checkpoints are available at https://equidexflow.github.io.

</details>


### [207] [Sparse2Act: Learning Action-Aligned Sparse 3D Representations for Cross-Domain Robot Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12759)
*Yu Guo,Chang Yu,Siyu Ma,Yunuo Chen,Yin Yang,Ying Nian Wu,Chenfanfu Jiang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Pretraining sparse 3D encoders with task-space end-effector actions yields reusable, geometry-aligned representations across policies and domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Sparse 3D encoders trained for specific downstream tasks overfit to data distributions and action parameterizations; a task-agnostic, geometry-centric pretraining signal is desired.

Method: Mask and align sparse 3D tokens using workspace motions commanded by end-effector actions. Pretrain the encoder with this action-alignment objective; downstream policies reuse only the encoder init while retaining their own architectures and action spaces.

Result: On LIBERO-10: 86.9% average success after 500 fine-tuning steps. LIBERO-to-Meta-World cross-domain: 73.4% average success. Ablations show gains come from the masked action-alignment signal. Real-world: sim-to-real pretraining plus limited real data yields 72.5% average across four tasks.

Conclusion: Robot actions provide compact geometric supervision for reusable sparse 3D representations, enabling cross-domain and sim-to-real transfer with robust downstream performance.

Abstract: Explicit 3D representations are attractive for manipulation because they expose object shape, workspace geometry, and robot-object relations in metric coordinates. However, sparse 3D encoders are often learned through downstream task objectives, tying the representation to a particular data distribution, policy architecture, and action parameterization. We introduce Sparse2Act, an observation-action alignment framework for pretraining sparse point-cloud encoders. The key idea is to use task-space end-effector actions as geometric supervision: masked sparse 3D tokens are trained to organize scene features around the workspace motion paired with the observation. After pretraining, only the encoder initialization is reused by downstream policies, allowing them to retain their own architectures and action spaces, including joint-space commands. On the LIBERO-10 benchmark, our method achieves 86.9% average success after 500 fine-tuning steps. The same pretrained encoder supports LIBERO-to-Meta-World cross-domain transfer, achieving 73.4% average success on the Meta-World-5 benchmark. Ablations on the objective and decoder capacity show that the gains come from the masked action-alignment signal and remain useful across downstream action decoders. In real-world experiments, simulation pretraining followed by limited real-data fine-tuning achieves an average success rate of 72.5% across four tasks, demonstrating effective sim-to-real transfer. These results suggest that robot actions can provide compact geometric supervision for reusable sparse 3D representations.

</details>


### [208] [Stubborn: A Streamlined and Unified Reinforcement Learning Framework for Robust Motion Tracking and Fall Recovery for Humanoids](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12814)
*Xiao Ren,Yuhui Yang,Zongbiao Weng,Zhijie Liu,He Kong*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A unified asymmetric actor-critic RL framework (Stubborn) for robust humanoid motion tracking and fall recovery, leveraging yaw-aligned tracking, probabilistic termination, and adaptive sampling to boost exploration and learning efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the separation of tracking and fall-recovery tasks in RL and to avoid early episode termination that limits recovery exploration, enabling robust, unified learning for humanoid control.

Method: Asymmetric Actor-Critic architecture. Three components: (1) yaw-aligned tracking representation to reduce drift while preserving balance cues; (2) Bernoulli-based probabilistic termination to encourage exploration of fall-recovery behaviors; (3) probabilistic termination with tracking-error-driven adaptive sampling that reshapes the training distribution based on performance, improving efficiency on difficult segments and unstable states.

Result: Compared with state-of-the-art methods and through ablations, Stubborn achieves competitive performance. The probabilistic termination and adaptive sampling contribute to robustness and training efficiency, with real-world demonstrations linked in the paper.

Conclusion: Stubborn offers a streamlined, unified RL framework for robust humanoid motion tracking and fall recovery, leveraging probabilistic termination and adaptive sampling to enhance exploration, learning speed, and resilience in fall-recovery scenarios.

Abstract: Recent reinforcement learning approaches have shown great promise in improving humanoid motion tracking performance and achieving fall recovery under disturbances. However, most existing works treat motion tracking and fall recovery as different tasks and require multi-stage training with specialized recovery rewards and/or separate recovery policies. Moreover, existing reinforcement learning-based methods often terminate training episodes immediately after severe tracking failures, limiting recovery-oriented exploration in unstable or fallen states. To address the above issues, we propose Stubborn, a streamlined and unified reinforcement learning framework to achieve robust humanoid motion tracking and fall recovery. Specifically, Stubborn uses an asymmetric Actor-Critic architecture and consists of three major components. First, a yaw-aligned tracking representation is adopted to reduce sensitivity to global drift and heading disturbances while preserving gravity-related balance information. Second, we introduce a Bernoulli-based probabilistic termination mechanism that enables the policy to encourage exploration of fall-recovery behaviors under varying failure modes. Third, we propose a probabilistic termination and tracking-error-driven strategy that dynamically reshapes the sampling distribution based on tracking performance, increasing the training efficiency for difficult motion segments and unstable states. Extensive comparisons with SOTA methods and ablation studies show that Stubborn achieved competitive performance, and the proposed probabilistic termination mechanism and adaptive sampling strategy contributed to the performance and robustness gains. For real-world demonstrations, please refer to https://aislab-sustech.github.io/Stubborn/.

</details>


### [209] [AIR-VLA+: Decoupling Movement and Manipulation via Cascaded Dual-Action Decoders with Asymmetric MoE for Aerial Robots](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12859)
*Jianli Sun,Bin Tian,Qiyao Zhang,Zijian Liu,Yutong Wang,Zhiyong Cui,Bai Li,Yisheng Lv,Yonglin Tian*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: AIR-VLA+ introduces a flow-matching architecture with cascaded dual-action decoders and an asymmetric feature-level MoE to decouple UAV movement from arm manipulation, achieving state-of-the-art performance on AIR-VLA with substantial gains over baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: End-to-end aerial manipulation suffers from representation coupling: UAV movement and end-effector manipulation operate at different scales, dynamics, and objectives, causing unstable coordination and suboptimal task execution.

Method: Propose AIR-VLA+ with cascaded manipulation and movement decoders, unidirectional observation of manipulator intent to coordinate workflow, and isolation of backprop effects. Incorporate an input feature enhancement module for the UAV movement decoder, including an implicit visual grasp projector and compressed global semantic features. Employ an implicit Mixture of Experts (MoE) within the UAV movement decoder for task-stage adaptive capacity via dense soft blending.

Result: Experimental evaluation on the AIR-VLA benchmark shows the method surpasses all baselines with an overall average score of 48.0. The task completion score improves by 80.2% over the single-head policy, indicating effective mitigation of heterogeneous coordination conflicts in composite robots.

Conclusion: AIR-VLA+ effectively decouples movement and manipulation components, enhances task-stage adaptability via MoE and enhanced features, and yields robust, high-performance aerial manipulation in end-to-end control scenarios.

Abstract: Aerial manipulation systems have long suffered from representation coupling in end-to-end control, as platform-level Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV) movement and end-effector-level arm manipulation differ substantially in action scale, dynamics, and control objectives. In this paper, we propose AIR-VLA+, a flow matching action generation architecture specifically designed for aerial manipulation, featuring cascaded dual-action decoders and an asymmetric feature-level Mixture of Experts (MoE). We construct cascaded manipulation and movement decoders, allowing the UAV to unidirectionally observe the manipulator's intent during movement to achieve workflow coordination, while isolating the impact of UAV movement information backpropagation on arm manipulation stability. Addressing the characteristic that UAV movement is highly dependent on high-level semantics and responsible for task state transitions in aerial manipulation, we design an input feature enhancement module for the UAV movement decoder. This module introduces an implicit visual grasp projector to perceive the interaction state between the gripper and the object, and injects compressed global semantic features. Within the UAV movement decoder, we deploy an implicit MoE architecture, enabling different movement experts to spontaneously exhibit capacity inclinations for various task stages during training. Through dense soft blending computation on the feature manifold, the UAV movement is endowed with stronger task-stage adaptability. Experiments on the standardized AIR-VLA benchmark demonstrate that our method comprehensively surpasses all baselines with an overall average score of 48.0. The overall task completion score improves by 80.2\% compared to the single-head $π_{0.5}$ policy, effectively mitigating the heterogeneous coordinated control conflicts of composite robots.

</details>


### [210] [Learning to Adapt: Representation-Based Reinforcement Learning for Multi-Task Skill Transfer](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12890)
*Aryan Naveen,Haitong Ma,Haldun Balim,Na Li*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: RepMT-SAC introduces spectral MDP decomposition to factor a shared, task-agnostic value function with a minimal task-specific adjustment, enabling zero-shot transfer and rapid few-shot adaptation in multi-task RL; on quadrotor trajectory tasks it outperforms baselines by up to 30%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Sample inefficiency and poor generalization in reinforcement learning limit practical multi-task deployment; there is a need for efficient knowledge sharing of transferable dynamics across related tasks.

Method: Propose RepMT-SAC, a multi-task RL framework that uses spectral MDP decomposition to capture transferable dynamics and decomposes the value function into a task-agnostic core plus a small task-specific adjustment, enabling strong in-distribution zero-shot performance and rapid out-of-distribution adaptation; evaluated on quadcopter trajectory-following across in- and out-of-distribution contexts.

Result: Empirical evaluation shows up to 30% improvement over baselines on quadcopter trajectory-following tasks, with strong zero-shot in-distribution performance and rapid few-shot adaptation to out-of-distribution tasks.

Conclusion: Spectral MDP-based decomposition enables effective knowledge sharing and robust transfer in multi-task RL, with a core task-agnostic value function and minimal task-specific adjustment supporting both zero-shot generalization and fast adaptation in real-world control scenarios.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning has achieved remarkable success in learning complex control policies, yet its applicability remains limited due to sample inefficiency and poor generalization across tasks. In this work, we propose RepMT-SAC, a framework for multi-task RL that enables efficient knowledge sharing and robust transfer to new tasks. RepMT-SAC uses spectral MDP decomposition to capture transferable dynamics, structuring the value function into a task-agnostic core with a minimal task-specific adjustment. This design allows for strong zero-shot performance on in-distribution tasks and rapid few-shot adaptation to out-of-distribution tasks. We evaluate RepMT-SAC on quadcopter trajectory-following tasks across in-distribution and out-of-distribution contexts, demonstrating that it outperforms baselines by up to 30%.

</details>


### [211] [Bounding Boxes as Goals: Language-Conditioned Grasping via Neuro-Symbolic Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12910)
*Allison Andreyev,Landon Eum,Nestor Tiglao,Romel Gomez*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: GRASP enables open-vocabulary tabletop manipulation by grounding NL prompts into neuro-symbolic goal states via a VLM and a bounding-box detector, enabling zero-shot tasks with no task-specific fine-tuning; achieves 73.3% success on 90 real-robot trials.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the need for robots to understand natural-language prompts in real-time without extensive per-task data or training, enabling robust manipulation in home/industrial settings.

Method: Integrates a pretrained VLM to map NL queries to grounded neuro-symbolic goals; uses a bounding-box detection pipeline to ground propositions in the physical world; avoids fixed color lists or hard-coded coordinates; supports abstract spatial concepts; uses symbolic planning for task execution.

Result: Reported 73.3% overall success across 90 real-robot trials at three difficulty levels; requires no task-specific training (no fine-tuning).

Conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility of open-vocabulary, grounded manipulation with minimal task-specific supervision; highlights neuro-symbolic integration and real-world grounding as a path toward scalable VLM-assisted robotics.

Abstract: For robotics to be effectively integrated into household or industrial environments, machines must adapt to natural-language prompts in real time. Although Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have enabled zero-shot generalization in robot task and motion planning (TAMP), current state-of-the-art approaches often remain computationally "heavyweight" or require extensive training on thousands of demonstrations. We present GRASP (Grounded Reasoning and Symbolic Planning), a framework designed as a step toward open-vocabulary tabletop manipulation. Our approach leverages a pretrained VLM to translate natural-language queries into neuro-symbolic goal states, grounded in the physical world via a bounding-box detection pipeline. Unlike methods that rely on fixed color lists or hard-coded coordinates, GRASP enables robots to interpret abstract spatial concepts such as "top shelf" and execute tasks without additional fine-tuning. We achieve 73.3% overall success across 90 real-robot trials at three difficulty levels, requiring no task-specific training.

</details>


### [212] [An Embodied Simulation Platform, Benchmark, and Data-Efficient Augmentation Framework for Wet-Lab Robotics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12936)
*Zhe Liu,Huanbo Jin,Zhaohui Du,Zhe Wang,He Xu,Peijia Li,Jiaming Gu,Quan Lu,Qi Wang,Bin Ji,Ting Xiao*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Pipette offers an embodied simulation platform with extensive open wet-lab assets, a simulation-based data augmentation pipeline, and an 11-task benchmark to enable data-efficient learning for wet-lab robots, plus NL-driven task construction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tackle reproducibility, throughput, and safety in wet-lab robotics by providing customizable simulators and reusable assets that rapidly turn limited demonstrations into usable training data.

Method: Develop Pipette: 43+ open, editable wet-lab assets and asset-building pipeline; a simulation-based augmentation that replays human demonstrations with lighting, camera, speed, and action perturbations, plus automatic task-success filtering; an 11-task benchmark across sample handling, culture-ware manipulation, device operation, and precision placement; NL-driven scene construction and task registration.

Result: With 30 demonstrations per task, ACT achieves 65.5% average success. Simulation augmentation raises SmolVLA from 44.1% to 74.7% and π0 from 40.4% to 46.5%, demonstrating data-efficient VLA training/evaluation. Pipette also enables NL-based task definition and scene setup.

Conclusion: Pipette advances data-efficient wet-lab robot learning by providing open assets, an extensible simulation-augmentation pipeline, and a multi-task benchmark, supporting reproducible research and easier task registration for non-experts.

Abstract: Wet-lab robots can improve the reproducibility, throughput, and safety of biomedical experiments, but scaling their learning requires customizable simulators for safe and reproducible task generation, open editable laboratory assets, and efficient pipelines that turn limited demonstrations into usable training data. We present Pipette, an embodied simulation platform, benchmark, and data-efficient augmentation framework for wet-lab robot learning. Pipette releases over 43 open-source and re-editable wet-lab assets, together with an extensible asset-building pipeline. A key component of Pipette is its simulation-based data augmentation pipeline, replaying human demonstrations in simulation, applies lighting, camera, speed, and action perturbations, and filters generated episodes with automatic task success checks, rapidly expanding usable training data from limited manual demonstrations. We further introduce an 11-task wet-lab embodied benchmark covering sample handling, culture-ware manipulation, device operation, and precision placement. With only 30 demonstrations per task, ACT achieves 65.5% average success rate, while simulation augmentation improves SmolVLA from 44.1% to 74.7% and π0 from 40.4% to 46.5%, validating the effectiveness of Pipette for data-efficient VLA training and evaluation. Pipette also supports natural-language-driven scene construction and task registration, lowering the barrier for non-expert users to define new wet-lab robotic tasks.

</details>


### [213] [Towards Reliable Sequential Object Picking in Clutter: The Runner-up Solution to RGMC 2025](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12954)
*Wei Yu,Xidan Zhang,Ziyi Zheng,Weijie Kong,Huixu Dong*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: An integrated hardware-software pipeline for sequential object picking in clutter, using a multifunctional gripper and novel clutter representations, achieving second place in RGMC 2025 Pick-in-Clutter track.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the challenge of stable, efficient grasping and object search in cluttered environments, essential for industrial robotics, where existing methods struggle with sequential search and sorting of diverse (rigid and deformable) objects.

Method: A unified pipeline combining object recognition, decluttering, and multi-modal grasping, featuring a multifunctional gripper and new representations for object distribution and occlusion in cluttered space to enable efficient recognition, search, and sequential grasping.

Result: Laboratory tests and competition scenarios show strong performance; the approach earned second place in the Pick-in-Clutter track at RGMC 2025.

Conclusion: Hardware-software co-design with novel clutter representations enhances robustness and efficiency of sequential cluttered-object manipulation, demonstrating practical viability in industrial-like tasks.

Abstract: As a long-standing challenge in robotic manipulation, stable and efficient grasping in cluttered environments is of great importance in industrial settings. While recent studies have achieved relatively high success rates in grasping from clutter, there remain few mature solutions for more demanding tasks such as sequential object search and sorting. This work addresses sequential object picking in cluttered environments based on the Cluttered Environment Picking Benchmark (CEPB) and presents our solution to the Pick-in-Clutter track of the 10th Robotic Grasping and Manipulation Competition (RGMC) at ICRA 2025. The task poses several key challenges. First, it requires robust and collision-aware grasping with high success rates across a diverse set of objects, including both rigid and deformable ones. Second, it demands efficient search for target objects, which places stringent requirements on the decluttering and searching strategies of the solution. To address the above challenges, we design an integrated hardware-software pipeline that combines object recognition, decluttering, and multi-modal grasping. The main contributions include the hardware design of a multifunctional gripper and novel representations for object distribution and occlusion relationships in cluttered space. This pipeline enables efficient recognition, search, and sequential grasping of objects in clutter, demonstrating strong performance in both laboratory tests and competition scenarios, and ultimately achieving second place in the Pick-in-Clutter track of the RGMC 2025.

</details>


### [214] [SERF: Spatiotemporal Environment and Robot Feature Map for Long-Horizon Mobile Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12956)
*Sunghwan Kim,Byeonghyun Pak,Kehan Long,Yulun Tian,Nikolay Atanasov*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A spatiotemporal SERF map integrated into a vision-language-action policy improves long-horizon mobile manipulation by maintaining a neural point-based representation of environment and robot, updated online, and enabling multi-frame context; yields better performance on BEHAVIOR-1K vs image-only baselines and improved robustness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-horizon mobile manipulation demands persistent reasoning about localization, environment changes, and task progress, which image observations alone struggle to provide.

Method: Construct SERF: neural points for environment and robot in a shared latent space; online updates via object-level rigid tracking (env) and forward kinematics (robot); integrate tokens from SERF into a VLA model with multi-frame and multi-scale context.

Result: SERF VLA outperforms image-only baselines, reaches subgoals faster with direct trajectories, improves robustness to scene-configuration shifts, and recovers from object-drop failures on BEHAVIOR-1K.

Conclusion: Spatiotemporal neural maps like SERF enable better long-horizon reasoning in mobile manipulation and can enhance policy performance when combined with language-aware perception and decision making.

Abstract: Long-horizon robot mobile manipulation requires continual reasoning about localization, environment changes, and task progress, all of which are challenging to infer from image observations alone. In this paper, we show that conditioning a mobile manipulation policy on a spatiotemporal feature map improves reasoning over long horizons. The map represents the environment and the articulated robot body as neural points in a shared latent space and is updated online from egocentric observations and proprioceptive state. We update the environment neural points using object-level rigid tracking and the robot neural points using forward kinematics. We use our spatiotemporal environment and robot feature (SERF) map as a state input to a vision-language-action (VLA) model by extracting map tokens from multiple reference frames and spatial scales, providing the policy with both local and global context. We demonstrate SERF on BEHAVIOR-1K, a benchmark for long-horizon mobile manipulation in household environments. Experiments show that the SERF VLA policy outperforms image-only baselines, reaches subgoals faster by following more direct trajectories, improves robustness to scene-configuration shifts, and recovers from object-drop failures.

</details>


### [215] [EmbodiSteer: Steering Embodiment-Agnostic Visuomotor Policies with Joint-Space Guidance for Zero-Shot Cross-Embodiment Deployment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12965)
*Shihefeng Wang,Kangchen Lv,Mingrui Yu,Xiang Li*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: EmbodiSteer provides a training-free method to align Cartesian-end-effector policies with embodiment constraints by lifting inference to joint space via FK and Jacobian updates, with collision-aware guidance; achieving substantial improvements in collision avoidance and task success across simulated and real robots.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: End-effector Cartesian policies are embodiment-agnostic but brittle under whole-body constraints; there is a need for zero-shot, embodiment-aware deployment without retraining.

Method: Inference-time lifting from Cartesian to joint space using forward kinematics and Jacobian-based updates; after each denoising step, apply whole-body collision-aware guidance to steer trajectories away from collisions while preserving end-effector behavior; keeps policy learning in Cartesian space.

Result: Simulated: 46.1% reduction in collision rate and 28.5% increase in task success across 9 robots. Physical robots: 90.0% reduction in collision rate and 36.7% increase in task success in highly constrained scenarios.

Conclusion: Demonstrates a scalable, training-free path to embodiment-aware deployment of Cartesian policies, yielding substantial safety and performance gains across diverse robots and enabling zero-shot transfer to real hardware.

Abstract: Scalable robot imitation learning relies on large-scale heterogeneous data from diverse robots or body-free data, making Cartesian end-effector actions a key interface for embodiment-agnostic policy learning. However, end-effector-only abstraction leaves Cartesian policies unaware of the deployed robot body, making them brittle under robot-specific constraints such as whole-body collision avoidance. To overcome this limitation, we present EmbodiSteer, a training-free framework that steers embodiment-agnostic visuomotor policies toward zero-shot, embodiment-aware deployment. EmbodiSteer keeps policy learning in Cartesian space while efficiently lifting inference-time diffusion sampling into the target robot's joint space via forward kinematics and Jacobian-based updates. With whole-body collision-aware guidance over joint trajectories after each denoising step, the arm can be steered away from collisions while preserving learned end-effector behavior. Compared with Cartesian-only execution, EmbodiSteer reduces collision rate by 46.1% and improves task success rate by 28.5% across 9 simulated robots, and further achieves 90.0% collision rate reduction and 36.7% success rate increase on two physical robots in highly constrained scenarios. Our project page is at https://frankwang67.github.io/EmbodiSteer-Page.

</details>


### [216] [Trajectory-Level Redirection Attacks on Vision-Language-Action Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12978)
*Gokul Puthumanaillam,Vardhan Dongre,Pranay Thangeda,Hooshang Nayyeri,Dilek Hakkani-Tür,Melkior Ornik*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A trajectory-level prompt attack on vision-language-action (VLA) policies shows that prompts that superficially preserve the command can redirect the final robot outcome; introduces a command-preserving trajectory redirection threat model and an on-policy prompt search to find such prompts; demonstrated in simulation and hardware.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VLA policies integrate natural language into closed-loop control, making prompts a reusable, influential control interface. A higher-level vulnerability exists beyond adversarial prompts for low-level actions—trajectory outcomes can be steered by near-benign prompts.

Method: Define command-preserving trajectory redirection as an attack model: the attacker picks a fixed prompt before an episode; all policy and environment components remain fixed; the prompt stays close to the benign instruction but omits target words or corrective language. Develop an on-policy prompt search using rollouts to discover perturbations that cause the closed-loop behavior to track a target task while satisfying the prompt constraints.

Result: The study finds that near-benign prompt perturbations can redirect VLA rollouts to attacker-specified targets, confirmed in both simulation and hardware experiments.

Conclusion: This reveals a trajectory-level vulnerability in VLA instruction grounding: seemingly preserving prompts can still enable adversaries to control the final outcome. It highlights the need for defenses targeting prompt design and grounding reliability, and motivates future work on robust prompting and verification mechanisms.

Abstract: Vision-language-action (VLA) policies bring natural language into closed-loop robot control, enabling robots to execute manipulation tasks directly from text instructions. The same interface gives text a recurring role in control because the prompt is reused at every replanning step, and each prompt-conditioned action changes the future observations on which the policy acts. Existing VLA attacks study adversarial prompts that elicit targeted low-level actions or make such actions persist across changing images. We identify a stronger trajectory-level failure mode: a prompt that still $\textit{appears}$ to specify the intended task but redirects the final physical outcome. We mathematically formalize this setting as $\textit{command-preserving trajectory redirection}$, a prompt-only threat model in which the attacker chooses one prompt before the episode, all policy and environment components remain fixed, and the prompt must stay close to the benign instruction while omitting target words and correction language. To find such prompts, we introduce an on-policy prompt search method that uses rollouts to discover perturbations whose closed-loop behavior tracks a target task while satisfying the command-preserving constraints. Experiments in simulation and on hardware show that near-benign prompt perturbations can redirect VLA rollouts to attacker-specified targets. These results expose a trajectory-level vulnerability in VLA instruction grounding: text that appears to preserve the intended command can still give an adversary control over the robot's final physical outcome. Project website: https://vla-redirection-attack.github.io/

</details>


### [217] [GenHOI: Contact-Aware Humanoid-Object Interaction by Imitating Generated Videos without Task-Specific Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12995)
*Zhihai Bi,Qiang Zhang,Guoyang Zhao,Jiahang Cao,Xueyin Luo,Yushan Zhang,Jinglan Xu,Ruoyu Geng,Yulin Li,Andrew F. Luo,Jun Ma*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: GenHOI enables zero-shot humanoid–object interaction by synthesizing a task video from a first frame and command, extracting geometric priors from contact cues, and optimizing/refining a reference trajectory to adapt to unseen poses, with closed-loop execution.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: HOI is limited by tight balance–interaction coupling and reliance on task-specific policy training or rigid motion replay. A zero-shot, data-efficient approach that leverages a generated video to guide planning could generalize to diverse object interactions without physical demonstrations.

Method: 1) Reconstruct the robot–object scene in simulation and render a first frame. 2) Condition a video synthesis on this frame and a language command to generate a task-oriented interaction video. 3) Analyze the video to identify interaction-relevant contact events and estimate hand–object contact regions. 4) Encode these as object-centric geometric constraints that convert visual cues into physically grounded optimization priors. 5) Refine and smooth the reference motion extracted from the video to resolve scale ambiguity, and adapt a single reference trajectory to unseen robot–object poses. 6) Execute the optimized trajectory with a closed-loop tracking controller.

Result: Extensive validation in simulation and real-world experiments across diverse tasks, including box grasping, asymmetric bimanual chair carrying, table lifting from below, and cylindrical-object enveloping.

Conclusion: GenHOI offers a simple yet effective zero-shot framework for varied HOI tasks without task-specific training or physical demonstrations, demonstrating the feasibility of using a single generated video to guide robust, physically grounded humanoid interactions.

Abstract: Humanoid-Object Interaction (HOI) is a fundamental capability for humanoid robots, yet it remains challenging due to the tight coupling between dynamic balance and stable interaction with diverse objects. Existing methods often require time-consuming task-specific policy training or rely on rigid trajectory replay, which limits their ability to accommodate novel interaction scenarios. In this work, we present \textit{GenHOI}, a simple yet effective framework that enables humanoid robots to perform diverse object-interaction tasks in a zero-shot manner by directly imitating a single generated video, without task-specific training or physical demonstration data. GenHOI first reconstructs the robot-object scene in simulation and renders a first-frame image, which, together with the language command, conditions the synthesis of a task-oriented interaction video. The generated video is then analyzed to identify interaction-relevant contact events and estimate hand-object contact regions, which are encoded as object-centric geometric constraints that convert visual interaction cues into physically grounded optimization priors. Guided by these priors, the reference motion recovered from the video is refined and smoothed to resolve the scale ambiguity inherent in 2D video generation, while adapting a single reference trajectory to unseen robot-object relative poses. The optimized trajectory is finally executed by a closed-loop tracking controller. We validate the proposed framework in extensive simulation and real-world experiments across diverse object-interaction tasks, including box grasping, asymmetric bimanual chair carrying, table lifting from below, and cylindrical-object enveloping.

</details>


### [218] [Comparing Commercial Depth Sensor Accuracy for Medical Applications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13028)
*Pit Henrich,Maximilian Weiherer,Franziska Hansen,Bernhard Egger,Franziska Mathis-Ullrich*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Zivid 2M+ 60 shows the strongest overall depth performance across real tissue, phantom, and other tested objects; Stereolabs ZED 2i performs well on real tissue but poorly on phantom; the remaining sensors underperform relative to Zivid and ZED in at least some conditions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Provide an objective benchmark of depth-sensing technologies (stereo, structured-light, ToF) for medical/surgical applications on biologically relevant and phantom materials to guide sensor selection.

Method: Benchmark four depth sensors (Intel RealSense D405, PMD Flexx2, Stereolabs ZED 2i, Zivid 2M+ 60) at approximately 50 cm on three specimens: porcine bone, porcine belly tissue, and a silicone kidney phantom. Ground truth obtained via stylus-based reference sampling. Evaluate across surfaces with homogeneous, specular, and subsurface scattering properties using multiple metrics across the sensor types.

Result: Zivid 2M+ 60 achieved the best performance across all objects and metrics. The ZED 2i ranked second for real tissue but last on the phantom, indicating modality-dependent performance.

Conclusion: The Zivid 2M+ 60 provides the most robust depth sensing for challenging biological surfaces and phantoms in this study; sensor choice may depend on the target tissue type (real tissue vs phantom). The results underscore cross-modality differences among stereo, structured-light, and ToF sensors and suggest that phantom material properties critically influence depth accuracy.

Abstract: Depth estimation has numerous medical and surgical applications. We benchmark four depth sensors on a porcine bone specimen, a porcine belly specimen, and a silicone kidney phantom using stylus-sampled references. These objects contain several real-world challenges, including homogeneous surfaces, specular surfaces, and subsurface scattering. The comparison includes stereo, structured-light, and time-of-flight sensors at a distance of approximately 50 cm. Specifically, the Intel RealSense D405 (Intel RealSense, United States), PMD Flexx2 (pmdtechnologies, Germany), Stereolabs ZED 2i (Stereolabs, France), and Zivid 2M+ 60 (Zivid, Norway) are compared. The Zivid 2M+ 60 performed best across all objects and metrics considered in this work. The ZED ranked second for real tissue, but last on the phantom.

</details>


### [219] [RoboProcessBench: Benchmarking Process-Aware Understanding in Vision-Language Robotic Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13040)
*Dayu Xia,Yue Shi,Yao Mu,Huiting Ji,Chaofan Ma,Yingjie Zhou,Hua Chen,Yang Liu,Jiezhang Cao,Guangtao Zhai*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A process-aware benchmark for robotic manipulation using vision-language models (VLMs). It introduces RoboProcessBench with static monitoring and dynamic reasoning across 12 diagnostic families, built on 58k QA from 260 tasks (ProcessData); shows broad limitations of current VLMs in process understanding, but post-training on ProcessData-SFT improves some models (Qwen2.5-VL-7B, InternVL-3-8B).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing evaluations of VLMs for robotic manipulation mostly assess end-state success; there is a need for fine-grained, temporally-aware judgment of manipulation processes to enable robust monitoring, error detection, and evaluation.

Method: Construct ProcessData from physically grounded execution traces; define 12 diagnostic families covering static and dynamic aspects (phase, contact, motion, coordination, progress, temporal order, outcome, primitive-level transitions); create 58k QA across 260 tasks; split into ProcessData-SFT (for supervised fine-tuning) and ProcessData-Eval (for evaluation); evaluate multiple VLMs and perform post-training on the SFT data.

Result: ProcessData-Eval reveals broad limitations of current VLMs across the 12 diagnostic families; fine-tuning on ProcessData-SFT yields consistent gains for Qwen2.5-VL-7B and InternVL-3-8B in local state, motion, progress, and primitive-aware cues.

Conclusion: RoboProcessBench serves as both an evaluation benchmark and a learnable supervision source to drive development of VLMs capable of monitoring and evaluating robotic manipulation processes.

Abstract: Vision-language models (VLMs) are increasingly explored as visual critics, reward generators, and failure detectors in robotic manipulation. These roles implicitly require models to judge not only final task success, but also how a manipulation execution is physically and temporally progressing. However, existing evaluations fail to test whether VLMs possess fine-grained process understanding. To address this gap, we present RoboProcessBench, a benchmark for process-aware understanding in vision-language robotic manipulation. RoboProcessBench decomposes such capability into two complementary dimensions, \emph{static monitoring} and \emph{dynamic reasoning}, instantiated as 12 diagnostic question families covering phase, contact, motion, coordination, primitive-local progress, temporal order, outcome, and primitive-level transitions. Built from physically grounded execution traces, the curated benchmark corpus ProcessData contains \textasciitilde 58k question-answer pairs across 260 manipulation tasks, which is further split into ProcessData-SFT and ProcessData-Eval for post-training and evaluation purposes. Extensive evaluation of various VLMs on ProcessData-Eval reveals broad limitations across 12 diagnostic task families, suggesting current models still lack robust process-aware understanding of manipulation executions. But with ProcessData-SFT, the post-trained \textit{Qwen2.5-VL-7B} and \textit{InternVL-3-8B} exhibit consistent gains on local state, motion, progress, and primitive-aware cues. These results demonstrate that RoboProcessBench serves as both an evaluation benchmark and a learnable supervision source for developing VLMs capable of monitoring and evaluating robotic manipulation processes. Project webpage: \href{https://processbench-2026.github.io/RoboProcessBench-Web/}{https://processbench-2026.github.io}.

</details>


### [220] [Y-BotFrame: An Extensible Embodied Agent Framework for Quadruped Robot Assistants](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13049)
*Luyao Zhang,Ke Li,Yuan Ding,Xulong Zhao,Guo Yu,Chengwei Yan,Fuyu Dong,Jiawei Hu,Di Wang,Nan Luo,Gang Liu,Quan Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Y-BotFrame presents an extensible quadruped platform that uses an LLM as a cognitive core with multimodal perception to translate natural-language instructions into executable embodied tasks, enabling natural interaction and modular upgrading.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable real-world deployment of embodied agents on mobile ground platforms by integrating perception, natural language interfacing, and cognitive planning into a modular, extensible framework.

Method: Integrates multimodal perception (speech, vision, LiDAR) with an LLM-based cognitive core to understand, reason about, and plan tasks from natural-language instructions; maps NL instructions to executable embodied task units; supports voice and visual feedback; designed as a plug-and-play, extensible architecture.

Result: Proposes a reference implementation and demonstrates the approach via supplementary video; no quantitative experimental results reported in the abstract.

Conclusion: Y-BotFrame offers a practical, extensible foundation for general-purpose, instruction-driven embodied agents on quadruped robots, facilitating natural human-robot collaboration without reliance on a remote controller.

Abstract: Quadruped robots are capable of traversing a wide range of complex terrains with high flexibility. As highly mobile ground-based intelligent platforms, they can be equipped with modules for navigation control, environmental perception, and intelligent interaction, thereby serving as real-world mobile deployment platforms for various algorithms. In this paper, we introduce Y-BotFrame, an extensible embodied platform that turns a robot into an intelligent ground assistant. Y-BotFrame integrates multimodal perception capabilities, including speech, vision, and LiDAR, and employs a large language model as the cognitive core for environmental understanding, contextual reasoning, and task planning. The system maps user natural-language instructions into executable embodied task units that can be carried out by the robot. Y-BotFrame supports natural interaction through voice commands and visual feedback, removing the need for a remote controller and enabling efficient human-robot collaboration. With a highly extensible framework, Y-BotFrame supports plug-and-play integration of new functional modules as well as modular upgrades and iterative development, offering a reference implementation for the real-world deployment of general-purpose, instruction-driven embodied agents.The supplementary video is available at https://xdei-group.github.io/Y-BotFrame/.

</details>


### [221] [EA-WM: Event-Aware World Models with Task-Specification Grounding for Long-Horizon Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13053)
*Kailin Wang,Haoxiang Jie,Yaoyuan Yan,Jiacheng Zhou,Zhiyou Heng*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Event-aware world-model (EA-WM) augments pretrained feature dynamics with task-grounded event prediction and verification, decoding futures into structured event states and scoring them by task progress, semantic/physical consistency, and uncertainty to guide planning in long-horizon manipulation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Visual/latent predictions alone are insufficient for long-horizon manipulation; planners need relational, predicate-level, physically grounded signals (e.g., object movement, state changes, placements) to determine if imagined futures satisfy task requirements.

Method: Extend frozen visual-feature dynamics with an event predictor and verifier. Roll out candidate futures in feature space, decode to structured event states, and score them using task-progress, semantic-consistency, physical-feasibility, and uncertainty terms. The verifier guides sampling-based planning, gates candidate actions, and, in LIBERO wine-rack, selects among PPO-generated proposals.

Result: Across navigation, deformable-object, wall-constrained, and language-described manipulation tasks, event-aware verification improves interpretability and alignment of feature-space world models with task progress, aiding planning.

Conclusion: Event-aware verification makes feature-space world models more interpretable and task-aligned for long-horizon robotic manipulation.

Abstract: Pretrained-feature world models provide a useful substrate for robot imagination, but visual or latent prediction alone does not determine whether an imagined future satisfies task-relevant events. Long-horizon manipulation requires progress signals that are relational, predicate-level, and physically grounded: whether an object has moved, whether a drawer or contact state has changed, whether a placement predicate is satisfied, and whether a candidate future is reliable enough for execution. We introduce EA-WM, an event-aware world-model framework that augments frozen visual-feature dynamics with task-specification-grounded event prediction and verification. EA-WM rolls out candidate futures in pretrained visual-feature space, decodes them into structured event states, and scores them using task-progress, semantic-consistency, physical-feasibility, and uncertainty terms. The verifier guides sampling-based planning, gates candidate actions, and, in the contact-sensitive LIBERO wine-rack setting, selects among PPOgenerated proposals. Across navigation, deformable-object, wall-constrained, and languagedescribed manipulation studies, EA-WM shows that event-aware verification can make featurespace world models more interpretable and better aligned with task progress.

</details>


### [222] [FTP-1: A Generalist Foundation Tactile Policy Across Tactile Sensors for Contact-Rich Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13102)
*Chengbo Yuan,Zicheng Zhang,Mingjie Zhou,Wendi Chen,Yi Wang,Zhuoyang Liu,Dantong Niu,Shuo Wang,Hui Zhang,Wenkang Zhang,Yingdong Hu,Yuanqing Gong,Wanli Xing,Chuan Wen,Cewu Lu,Kaifeng Zhang,Yang Gao*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: FTP-1 introduces a unified tactile foundation model that generalizes across diverse sensors and embodiments by projecting heterogeneous tactile inputs into a common latent space via morphology-aware tokens and a shared Transformer expert.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tactile signals are highly heterogeneous across hardware, which hinders cross-sensor generalization and transfer of tactile manipulation policies.

Method: Use heterogeneous encoders to map image-, array-, and state-based tactile signals into unified morphology-aware latent tokens, which are modeled by a shared tactile Transformer expert. Pretraining on ~3,000 hours of data from 26 sources and 21 sensors to learn transferable tactile skills.

Result: FTP-1 improves contact-rich manipulation on seen sensors by 17.2% during finetuning and transfers to two unseen tactile-sensor setups, achieving a 31% gain in success rate.

Conclusion: Establishes the first unified foundation baseline for tactile manipulation, enabling future tactile policies to start from a shared model, with pretrained models and data publicly available.

Abstract: Despite the success of vision-based generalist robotic policies, existing tactile-based policies remain tied to fixed embodiments and sensor setups. This is because tactile signals are highly heterogeneous across hardware, making cross-sensor generalization difficult. We present FTP-1,the first generalist foundation tactile policy pretrained to acquire transferable tactile manipulation abilities across diverse sensors and embodiments. FTP-1 supports varied tactile inputs, including image-, array-, and state-based signals, by using heterogeneous encoders to project them into unified morphology-aware latent tokens that are jointly modeled by a shared tactile Transformer expert. Pretrained on around 3,000 hours of tactile manipulation data aggregated from 26 data sources, spanning human and robot demonstrations across 21 sensors, FTP-1 learns tactile skills that transfer beyond the sensors seen during pretraining. Across downstream finetuning experiments spanning 5 hardware configurations, FTP-1 improves contact-rich manipulation on seen sensor setups by +17.2% and, surprisingly, transfers to two previously unseen tactile-sensor setups, achieving a +31% gain in success rate. FTP-1 establishes the first unified foundation baseline for tactile manipulation, providing future tactile policies with a shared model-level starting point. Pretrained models, datasets, training code and more visualization at https://ftp1-policy.github.io.

</details>


### [223] [Redesigning Regularization for Effective Policy Smoothing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13169)
*Taisuke Kobayashi,Naoto Yamanaka*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Proposes a revised regularization to smooth policy functions in reinforcement learning, addressing implementation gaps and demonstrating improved smoothness, performance, and sim-to-real robustness for a quadruped robot.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing global Lipschitz regularization struggled to balance smoothness with expressiveness; the original implementation was cumbersome and failed to provide sufficient smoothing. The work targets a practical, theory-consistent regularization that yields smoother policies with broad applicability.

Method: Diagnose three failure reasons of the original implementation, propose remedies, and introduce a modified regularization. Validate the approach across multiple tasks and RL algorithms, showing improved smooth motion and control performance; extend to sim-to-real quadruped to assess robustness to sudden velocity-command changes.

Result: The modified regularization achieves smoother policy motion and improved control performance across tasks and algorithms, and provides robustness to abrupt velocity-command changes in sim-to-real quadruped experiments.

Conclusion: A practical, theory-consistent regularization for smoothing policies in RL. The revised design reconciles implementation with theoretical goals, delivering broad applicability and real-world robustness.

Abstract: This paper proposes a novel regularization design to effectively smooth policy functions in reinforcement learning. While regularization that enhances ``global'' Lipschitz continuity was initially considered, it has been limited to ``local'' Lipschitz continuity due to a tradeoff between smoothness and expressiveness. However, it has become apparent that the original implementation is cumbersome and does not provide sufficient smoothing, leading to a preference for simpler implementations. This stems from a discrepancy between theory and implementation, and a more appropriate implementation can expect to facilitate smoothing. Therefore, this paper identifies three reasons why the original implementation does not function adequately and provide remedies for them. This modified regularization performs well across multiple tasks and algorithms, successfully achieving smooth motion while improving control performance. Furthermore, by applying it to sim-to-real reinforcement learning for a quadruped robot, it is demonstrated that smooth motion provides robustness against sudden changes in target velocity commands.

</details>


### [224] [Multi-Modal Multi-Agent Robotic Cognitive Alignment enabled by Non-Invasive Consumer Brain Computer Interfaces: A Proof of Concept Exploration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13190)
*Nataliya Kosmyna,Liz Jenkins,Anoop K. Sinha*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Proposes a cognitively aligned multi-agent framework that defers non-urgent agent communications during high cognitive load detected via consumer-grade EEG, enabling background processing by secondary agents and release when load normalizes, using an HTTP signaling loop, LLMs, and robotic embodiment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address cognitive overload and suboptimal human-robot collaboration caused by intrusive, untimely communications by multi-agent systems; enable more natural, non-intrusive interaction by aligning agent behavior with the human's cognitive state.

Method: Closed-loop architecture that continuously monitors EEG spectral powers with a consumer-grade BCI while the user performs an engagement task. An engagement-driven pipeline uses HTTP signaling to hold a primary agent's inputs/audio and allow secondary agents to perform delegated tasks in the background; release queued messages when cognitive load decreases. Incorporates real-time signal processing, LLMs, and physical robot embodiments.

Result: Preliminary results demonstrate feasibility of leveraging real-time EEG processing, LLMs, and robotic embodiments to create cognitively-aware, non-intrusive multi-agent systems.

Conclusion: The work establishes a feasible framework for cognitively-aligned multi-agent interactions, enabling deferral of communications during high workload and background task processing, with release upon cognitive load reduction; real-time neurosignal monitoring with consumer-grade EEG is viable for such systems.

Abstract: While non-verbal behaviors and expressive movements are essential for natural human-robot interaction, existing methods often overlook a crucial element: the human's internal cognitive state. Frequently, proactive multi-agent systems can interrupt humans at inopportune moments, leading to cognitive overload and decreased task performance. This paper introduces a framework for generating "cognitively aligned" multi-agent interactions, enhancing the ability of robotic systems to contextually defer communications to the user of an agent system during moments of high human mental workload and engagement. We present the design and implementation of a closed-loop architecture that explores the interplay between autonomous task execution and real-time neurophysiological focus. Using a consumer-grade Brain-Computer Interface (BCI), our approach continuously monitors Electroencephalography (EEG) spectral band powers while a human performs an engagement-inducing task. We propose an engagement-driven pipeline where an HTTP-based signaling mechanism places a primary agent's sensory inputs and audio outputs into a holding state upon detecting high engagement. This allows secondary agents to seamlessly process complex, delegated tasks in the background. Once the human's cognitive state returns to a lower cognitive load baseline, the primary agent releases the queued agent message. Our preliminary results demonstrate the feasibility of leveraging real-time signal processing, Large Language Models (LLMs), and physical robotic embodiments to create cognitively-aware, non-intrusive multi-agent systems.

</details>


### [225] [Embedding ISO 10218 Safety Compliance in Robots via Control Barrier Functions for Human-Robot Collaboration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13203)
*Federico Parma,Cesare Tonola,Nicola Pedrocchi,Manuel Beschi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A predictive safety framework for human-robot collaboration using Control Barrier Functions (CBF) that incorporate human acceleration to forward-predict minimum separation, integrated into an SQP-based controller; two methods: Method I (CBF PD safety filter) and Method II (task-scaling SQP with spatial tube). Method II outperforms the baseline SSM, achieving 63% reduction in mean trajectory error and better throughput while complying with ISO 10218 SSM.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional SSM is conservative by assuming constant human velocity, leading to pessimistic speeds and unnecessary stops; need predictive, acceleration-aware safety to balance safety with task throughput.

Method: Develop a CBF that uses human acceleration to bound minimum separation during worst-case robot stop; embed as inequality constraint in an SQP controller. Propose (I) a CBF-constrained PD safety filter; (II) a task-scaling SQP controller enforcing a spatial tube constraint.

Result: Simulated and real-world tests on UR10e show Method II reduces trajectory error by 63% vs Method I and baseline; confined spatial deviations and avoided excessive evasive maneuvers; improved throughput while meeting ISO 10218 SSM requirements.

Conclusion: A predictive, acceleration-aware CBF framework embedded in SQP provides safer and more efficient HRC than conventional SSM; Method II offers a practical balance of safety and performance.

Abstract: Human-Robot Collaboration (HRC) requires strict adherence to safety standards, such as ISO 10218, to prevent harmful interactions. Standard Speed and Separation Monitoring (SSM) filters calculate safe robotic speeds based on conservative assumptions, such as constant human velocity, which prevents accurate predictions of minimum separation distances and causes unnecessary operational halts. This paper proposes a Control Barrier Function (CBF) that explicitly incorporates human acceleration data to analytically forward-predict the minimum human-robot separation distance during a worst-case robotic stopping trajectory. To guarantee safety at the control level, this predictive CBF is integrated as an inequality constraint within a Sequential Quadratic Programming (SQP) framework. Specifically, two methods are proposed: Method I, a CBF-constrained PD safety filter; and Method II, a task-scaling SQP controller that enforces a spatial tube constraint. Simulated and real-world experiments on a UR10e robot evaluate the two proposed methods against a standard industrial SSM module baseline. Results demonstrate that Method II dynamically modulates execution speed and confines spatial deviations. Compared to Method I, Method II achieves a 63\% reduction in mean trajectory error and avoids excessive evasive manoeuvres, ensuring high task throughput while complying with ISO 10218 SSM guidelines.

</details>


### [226] [Proprioceptive-visual correspondence enables self-other distinction in humanoid robots](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13222)
*Yurun Chen,Tianyuan Gao,Yizhong Ge,Shikun Ban,Yizhou Wang,Hongkai Xiong,Wenjun Zeng,Wentao Zhu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A humanoid robot learns self-model and self/other discrimination from proprioceptive-visual cues without identity labels, builds a 3D body occupancy model, and uses it to support reaching, collision-aware planning, and human-to-robot motion retargeting in multi-agent scenes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the challenge of distinguishing self from others in shared human-robot workspaces, enabling safe coordination and embodied social intelligence without relying on predefined kinematic models or identity annotations.

Method: Unsupervised/self-supervised learning of proprioceptive-visual correspondences to bootstrap a predictive self-model that maps joint configurations to 3D body occupancy. The system operates in multi-agent scenes with humans or morphologically identical robots, learning to identify itself and construct a 3D self-model, then applying this model to downstream tasks.

Result: The approach reliably identifies the robot as self in multi-agent scenes, learns a 3D self-model, and improves performance on target reaching, collision-aware motion planning, and human-to-robot motion retargeting.

Conclusion: Demonstrates a viable route toward bodily self-representation in robots that act and coordinate with others in shared physical environments, with clear implications for embodied social intelligence and safe human-robot collaboration.

Abstract: Distinguishing self from others is a prerequisite for social intelligence, yet humanoid robots that increasingly share workspaces with humans still lack this ability. Here we show that a humanoid robot can learn self-other distinction from proprioceptive-visual correspondence, without any identity labels or kinematic models. Once established, this distinction bootstraps a predictive self-model that maps joint configurations to three-dimensional body occupancy, capturing how the robot's body changes with action. In multi-agent scenes involving humans or morphologically identical robots, the system reliably identifies itself, learns a 3D self-model, and supports downstream tasks including target reaching, collision-aware motion planning, and human-to-robot motion retargeting. Together, these results outline a route toward bodily self-representation in robots that act and coordinate alongside others in shared physical environments. Project page: https://euron-zc.github.io/humanoid-self-model/.

</details>


### [227] [WT-UMI: Tactile-based Whole-Body Manipulation via Force-Supervised Contact-Aware Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13232)
*Jaehwi Jang,Zhaoyuan Gu,Alfred Cueva,Zimeng Chai,Junjie Sheng,Thong Nguyen,Himank Galundia,Yifan Wu,Huishu Xue,Isaac Legene,Ojas Mediratta,Davin Doan,Andrew Collins,Sarah Sadegh,KyoungMok Kim,Rishita Dhalbisoi,Zun Chen,Ye Zhao*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: WT-UMI enables force-aware whole-body manipulation by integrating a wearable tactile interface with learning-based force-conditioned pose corrections and a force-supervised planner, improving performance across diverse contact-rich tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge the gap between human demonstrations and robot execution by leveraging complementary modalities (natural human force regulation and robot teleoperation data) to enhance contact force control and robustness in whole-body manipulation.

Method: Develop WT-UMI, a wearable whole-body tactile interface that records tactile images, contact forces, and end-effector poses across human-demonstration and humanoid-teleoperation modes; introduce a force-conditioned target-pose correction module learned from teleoperation data; propose a force-supervised planner predicting end-effector pose chunks and contact-force trajectories; apply a tactile-based admittance controller using the predicted contact force as the reference.

Result: Across five contact-rich tasks involving deformable and bulky rigid objects and human–humanoid collaboration, WT-UMI increases success rate and reduces contact-position tracking error relative to four baseline policies.

Conclusion: Demonstrates that coupling a wearable tactile interface with force-conditioned planning and tactile-admittance control can effectively leverage cross-modal demonstrations to improve natural and robust whole-body manipulation of challenging objects.

Abstract: Whole-body humanoid manipulation of bulky, deformable, and shared-load objects requires distributed contact sensing and explicit force regulation, yet most imitation policies treat contact force only implicitly. On the other hand, different demonstration sources provide complementary modalities with inherent trade-offs: human demonstrations capture natural contact forces but not robot-executable actions, while teleoperation directly records robot actions but with less natural force regulation. This paper presents \textbf{WT-UMI}, a wearable whole-body tactile interface worn by human operators or mounted on humanoids, providing accurate observations of tactile images, contact forces, and end-effector poses across both human demonstration and humanoid teleoperation modes. We introduce a force-conditioned target-pose correction module that converts measured human poses into contact-aware robot targets by learning corrections from teleoperation data. To leverage the natural force interaction in human data, we propose a force-supervised planner that predicts end-effector pose chunks and contact-force trajectories. The predicted contact force serves as the reference for a tactile-based admittance controller. Across five contact-rich tasks spanning deformable objects, bulky rigid objects, and human--humanoid collaboration, WT-UMI improves success rate and reduces contact-position tracking error over four policy baselines. Our project page is available at https://wt-umi.github.io/WTUMI/.

</details>


### [228] [Humor Style Drives Laughter, Topic Shapes Acceptability: Evaluating Bilingual Personal and Political Robot-Delivered AI Jokes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13256)
*Anna-Maria Velentza,Anne-Gwenn Bosser*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Explores how humor style, joke content, and language preference shape perceptions of robot-delivered humor in a group classroom, using AI-generated jokes in a mixed factorial design.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses the gap in understanding how different humor dimensions (style and content) influence acceptance and perception of robot humor in group HRI settings, leveraging LLM-generated jokes delivered by a robot.

Method: Mixed factorial design manipulating humor type (Affiliative, Self-Enhancing, Aggressive, Self-Defeating) and joke content (person-related vs political); participants in a university classroom evaluate AI-generated jokes delivered by a robot on funniness and appropriateness, with language preference also assessed.

Result: Humor type significantly affects perceived funniness (Aggressive and Affiliative rated higher); joke content mainly affects appropriateness (person-related jokes preferred over political); language preference is shaped by joke content and participants’ self-reported fluency and humor practices.

Conclusion: Findings suggest tailoring humor style and content for group settings, and accounting for audience language preferences; future work should examine broader contexts, ethical considerations, and cross-cultural applicability; limitations include ecological validity and reliance on self-report.

Abstract: Humor plays a central role in human social relationships, and recent advances in computational humor create new opportunities for integrating humor into human-robot interaction (HRI). While large language models (LLMs) can generate diverse forms of humor, it remains unclear how humor style, joke content, and language preference shape perceptions of robot-delivered humor in group settings. In this exploratory study, we employed a mixed factorial design in which participants evaluated AI-generated jokes delivered by a robot in a university classroom. We examined the effects of humor type (Affiliative, Self-Enhancing, Aggressive, Self-Defeating) and joke content (person-related vs. political) on perceived funniness and appropriateness, as well as preferred language. Results show that humor type significantly influences funniness, with Aggressive and Affiliative humor rated higher, while joke content primarily affects appropriateness, with person-related jokes preferred over political ones. Language preference was shaped by both joke content and participants' self-reported fluency and humor practices.

</details>


### [229] [See Selectively, Act Adaptively: Dual-Level Structural Decomposition for Bimanual Robot Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13279)
*Yoon-Ji Choi,Young-Chae Son,Soo-Chul Lim*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A dual-level decomposition for bimanual VLA policies: a View-Selective Visual Router and an Interaction-Aware MoE, improving simulation and real-world success rates over monolithic baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Monolithic Vision-Language-Action policies struggle to handle task-stage dependent visual relevance and variable bimanual interaction structures, hindering robust policy learning.

Method: Propose two modules: View-Selective Visual Router (VSVR) to weight wrist-view inputs for task-relevant visuals; Interaction-Aware Action Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) to decompose action generation into coordinated and arm-wise pathways; evaluate on six RoboTwin 2.0 simulation tasks and three long-horizon real-world tasks.

Result: Average success-rate gains of 27.7% in simulation and 43.3% in real-world evaluation over a monolithic baseline; the proposed method consistently outperforms single-module variants across both settings.

Conclusion: Jointly modeling selective visual processing and explicit bimanual interaction decomposition provides a useful inductive bias for robust bimanual manipulation.

Abstract: In bimanual robotic manipulation, task-relevant visual information varies with the task stage and context, while the interaction of the two arms shifts between independent and coordinated modes, making policy learning challenging. However, existing monolithic Vision-Language-Action (VLA) policies process diverse visual inputs and interaction patterns through a single shared representation and action generation pathway, often failing to separately account for visual relevance and bimanual interaction structure. To address this issue, we propose a bimanual manipulation VLA framework based on Dual-Level Structural Decomposition. The View-Selective Visual Router dynamically adjusts wrist-view contributions to emphasize relevant visual cues, while the Interaction-Aware Action Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) decomposes action generation into coordinated and arm-wise pathways to adapt to varying bimanual interaction modes. We evaluate the proposed method on six simulated bimanual manipulation tasks in RoboTwin 2.0 and three long-horizon real-world tasks. Our model improves the overall average success rate over a monolithic baseline by 27.7% in simulation and 43.3% in real-world evaluation, while consistently outperforming single-module variants across both settings. These results demonstrate that jointly considering selective visual processing and explicit decomposition of bimanual interaction structures provides an effective inductive bias for robust bimanual manipulation.

</details>


### [230] [EMG-Based Adaptation of Anisotropic Virtual Fixtures for Robot-Assisted Surgical Resection and Dissection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13340)
*Dario Onfiani,Michael Dyck,Luigi Biagiotti,Julian Klodmann*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Adaptive anisotropic virtual fixtures with EMG-based intent control for robot-assisted laparoscopic surgery; pilot results show improved accuracy, movement consistency, and reduced cognitive load.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fixed virtual fixture geometries lack flexibility to adapt to surgical workflow and surgeon intent; need real-time, intent-driven constraint modulation.

Method: Proposes adaptive anisotropic virtual fixture and an EMG-based control interface to expand/disengage constraints via forearm muscle contractions; real-time geometry modulation; pilot user study using a standardized surgical training task.

Result: Significant improvements in task accuracy and movement consistency; reductions in perceived cognitive load, effort, and frustration.

Conclusion: The framework effectively tunes guidance to surgeon intent, enabling seamless transitions between guided and free motion; promising for adaptive robotic assistance in surgery; requires further validation.

Abstract: In this paper, we address the development of an adaptive assistance system for robot-assisted laparoscopic surgery, specifically for delicate tasks such as Resection and Dissection. Even if Virtual Fixtures offer significant advantages for guiding a surgeon's movements, conventional Virtual Fixtures are often defined by fixed geometries, lacking the flexibility to adapt to the surgical workflow or the surgeon's immediate intent. To address these limitations, we propose a novel framework for an adaptive and anisotropic virtual fixture. In addition, we introduce an intuitive control interface that modulates the fixture's geometry in real-time based on the surgeon's intent, inferred from EMG signals. This approach allows the surgeon to dynamically expand or disengage the constraint by contracting their forearm muscles, enabling seamless transitions between precise guided motion and free repositioning of the tool. Experimental results from a pilot user study, based on a standardized surgical training task, demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method. The system showed significant improvements in task accuracy and movement consistency, alongside a reduction in perceived cognitive load, effort, and frustration.

</details>


### [231] [Low cost, easily manufactured, highly flexible strain and touch sensitive fiber for robotics applications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13352)
*Christian Diaz Herrera,Srushti Raste,Simin Liu,Miles Modeste,Jiyang,Yin,Katelyn McCall,Yuxing Jared Yao,Roopkamal Chahal,Simon Chidley,Trung Ha,T. David Westmoreland,Sonia Roberts*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Low-cost, fiber-based conductive sensor made from off-the-shelf parts; supports resistive and capacitive sensing; quick fabrication; demonstrated across multiple robotic sensing tasks; discusses scalability and repair.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce material, equipment, and time costs of stretch and touch sensors for robots while maintaining usefulness across gripping, pose, and hand-follow tasks.

Method: Construct a conductive fiber from inexpensive conductive thread, silicone tubing, and a loop-style needle threader; manufacture ~20 cm fibers in ~2 minutes; use as resistive strain sensor and, separately, as a knitted capacitive sensor; demonstrate in pneumatic assistive finger grasping, strap pose sensing, flexible solid pose estimation; include repair technique for cut fibers; discuss manufacturing scalability and cost trade-offs.

Result: Demonstrated functional resistive and capacitive sensing with low-cost parts; rapid fabrication; three resistive sensing applications and two capacitive sensing applications; flexibility through knitting; a practical repair method; insights on scalability and cost trade-offs.

Conclusion: The approach offers a promising route to affordable, flexible sensors for soft/robotic systems, with notable cost and production gains, but requires further work on scaling, durability, and calibration for widespread deployment.

Abstract: Existing stretch and touch sensors for robots are generally expensive with respect to at least one of material costs, required manufacturing equipment, or manufacturing time. We present and experimentally characterize a conductive fiber made using only inexpensive commercial off-the-shelf parts (conductive thread at $0.07/ft, silicone tubing at $0.94/ft) and tools (loop-style needle threader at $2), which can be manufactured quickly (20 cm length in 2 minutes.)
