Result: Current agents contribute effectively to well-specified data-analysis workflows when task structure and evaluation criteria are clear, but show uneven performance across contexts, struggle to generate genuinely novel insights, sustain self-directed exploration, and produce robust solutions for open-ended questions; identified common failure modes and opportunities to improve reliability, autonomy, and scientific reasoning.

Conclusion: SciAgentArena offers a practical framework to measure progress in AI for science and to guide the design of future agents capable of addressing complex scientific challenges; full codes, tasks, and datasets are publicly available at the provided link.

Abstract: AI agents are increasingly being developed to accelerate scientific discovery, yet their practical capabilities in real research settings remain poorly understood. Existing benchmarks for AI agents rarely capture the complexity, heterogeneity, and extended reasoning required by scientific work, whereas benchmarks for scientific tasks often reduce research to static, direct problems and provide limited support for interactive evaluation. Here, we introduce SciAgentArena, a systematic benchmark for evaluating AI agents in real-world scientific research scenarios drawn from emerging needs across multiple domains. SciAgentArena comprises approximately 200 tasks with stepwise verification and an interactive, agent-agnostic environment for assessing diverse AI agents. Using this benchmark, we find that current agents can contribute effectively to well-specified data-analysis workflows, particularly when the task structure and evaluation criteria are clear. However, their performance remains uneven across scientific contexts: agents struggle to generate genuinely novel insights, sustain self-directed exploration, and formulate robust solutions for open-ended research questions. We further characterize common failure modes across agents and identify opportunities for improving their reliability, autonomy, and scientific reasoning. Together, SciAgentArena provides a practical framework for measuring progress in AI agents for science and for guiding the design of future agents capable of addressing complex scientific challenges. Full codes, tasks, and datasets can be accessed via this link: https://sciagentarena.github.io/.

</details>


### [255] [Reducing the Complexity of Deep Learning Models for EEG Analysis on Wearable Devices](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12742)
*Farough Shayeste Roodi,Parham Zilouchian Moghaddam,Mahdi Mohammadi-nasab,Mehdi Modarressi,Mostafa Ersali Salehi Nasab,Masoud Daneshtalab*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Assesses deploying state-of-the-art DNNs for EEG/ECG on resource-constrained wearables, focusing on quantization and electrode reduction to balance accuracy and computation in epileptic seizure detection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Wearable IoT devices have strict energy, compute, and memory constraints. DNNs offer strong performance for EEG/ECG analysis but are often infeasible on wearables. The study seeks practical accuracy-complexity trade-offs for online seizure detection.

Method: Evaluate several state-of-the-art DNN models for EEG-based seizure detection under wearable-like constraints. Apply parameter quantization and electrode reduction techniques and measure accuracy alongside computational metrics (e.g., complexity, energy). Analyze trade-offs.

Result: Quantization and electrode reduction can substantially reduce DNN complexity with only minimal accuracy degradation when applied judiciously; explicit accuracy–complexity trade-offs are quantified for wearable deployment.

Conclusion: DNN-based online EEG analysis is feasible on wearables by carefully combining quantization and electrode reduction, enabling practical deployment with defined accuracy–complexity guidelines.

Abstract: Wearable healthcare devices are the fastest-growing Internet of Things (IoT) sector. Many automated healthcare services rely on two crucial biological signals, namely ECG and EEG, which reflect the activity of the heart and brain, respectively. Although deep neural networks are considered the primary way to process and analyze these signals, the very tight energy and computational power constraints in wearable devices are far below the computational, energy, and memory bandwidth demands of DNN models, thereby impeding the deployment of deep learning in many practical wearable services. This paper investigates the feasibility of deploying state-of-the-art DNN models in resource-constrained wearable devices. Notably, we explore the trade-off between accuracy and computational complexity of DNNs when parameter quantization and electrode reduction methods are used. Our investigation centers on several state-of-the-art DNN models designed for EEG signal analysis, specifically for detecting epileptic seizures. Our findings demonstrate that, when applied judiciously, these techniques can significantly reduce the complexity of the DNNs under consideration with minimal adverse effects on accuracy. These results reveal the explicit trade-offs between accuracy and complexity reduction encountered when adapting DNN-based online EEG analysis for wearable devices.

</details>


### [256] [Prefill Awareness in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12747)
*Andy Wang,Parv Mahajan,David Demitri Africa,Alexandra Souly,Jordan Taylor,Robert Kirk*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Prefill awareness in frontier LMs confounds prefill-based safety/evaluation methods; models can detect and partly resist injected context.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To assess whether models can recognize and react to tampered or inserted prior assistant messages, which could bias alignment and jailbreaking evaluations.

Method: Construct binary preference benchmarks across three prefill mechanisms, filter for consistent stances; test frontier models (e.g., Claude Opus 4.5) for detection of prefills opposing their preferences; perform ablations to separate cues (stylistic mismatch vs preference mismatch); extend to realistic agentic settings (misalignment-continuation evaluations, SWE-bench trajectories).

Result: Frontier models show substantial prefll awareness: detection of prefills opposing their preferences in 9–35% of cases with 0% false positives when prompted; models often revert toward baseline without reporting foreign prefills. Ablations indicate detection (foreignness) and resistance (reversion) rely on different cues. Prefill awareness also affects realistic tasks and datasets, sometimes leading to disavowal of prefilled turns depending on data and hidden artifacts.

Conclusion: Prefill awareness is a substantial confound for prefill-based evaluation methods; developers should monitor this capability in frontier systems and adjust evaluation designs to mitigate confounds and ensure robust safety/alignment assessments.

Abstract: Safety-relevant studies of language models, including alignment and jailbreaking evaluations and AI control protocols, often rely on prefilling model outputs. If AI models can recognize and act on the fact their prior assistant messages have been inserted or edited, the effectiveness and validity of these methods could be compromised. We investigate whether frontier language models can distinguish between tampered and untampered assistant-side context, a capability we call prefill awareness. To do so, we construct a binary preference benchmark across three prefill mechanisms, filtering for cases where models show consistent stances. We find that frontier models show substantial prefill awareness: Claude Opus 4.5 detects prefills opposing its preferences in 9-35% of cases with a 0% false positive rate when prompted; additionally, models often revert towards baseline behavior without explicitly reporting that the prefill was foreign. Controlled ablations later also show that detection and resistance rely on different cues, where stylistic mismatch mainly affects whether models flag a prefill as foreign, while preference mismatch mainly affects whether they revert toward their baseline answer. We also examine more realistic agentic settings such as misalignment-continuation evaluations and SWE-bench trajectories, where frontier models sometimes disavow prefilled assistant turns in ways that depend strongly on dataset, task success, and hidden formatting artifacts. Our results indicate that prefill awareness is already a substantial confound for some prefill-based methods. We recommend that model developers track this capability in frontier systems.

</details>


### [257] [Constructing Evaluation Datasets for Procedural Reasoning: Balancing Naturalness, Grounding, and Multi-Hop Coverage](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12767)
*Sarah Elshabrawy,Rahul K. Dass,Ashok K. Goel*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Strict TMK-based question generation yields the strongest dataset quality for procedural/multi-hop reasoning; transcript-first produces learner-like items with weaker grounding; TMK-aware generates multi-hop coverage but lower grounding, underscoring the need for validation grounded in representations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To create AI-supported learning evaluation datasets that are both learner-like and grounded in the system's instructional knowledge, and to assess how TMK-based generation strategies influence dataset quality for procedural and multi-hop reasoning.

Method: Compare three generation strategies: (1) strict generation directly from Task-Method-Knowledge (TMK) models, (2) transcript-first generation with post-hoc TMK filtering, and (3) TMK-aware generation that couples transcripts with structured guidance. Evaluate on 23 instructional topics yielding 690 Q&A pairs. Use a grounding validation framework based on closed-set evidence units from TMK models to assess: groundedness of answers, self-containment of questions, and multi-hop targeting.

Result: Strict TMK generation achieved the strongest overall quality: 96.5% of questions grounded; 92.6% usable. Transcript-first generation produced more learner-like questions but with context dependence and weaker grounding. TMK-aware generation offered high raw multi-hop coverage but lower grounding.

Conclusion: Procedural richness and natural phrasing do not guarantee representational grounding; explicit representation-aware validation is necessary for evaluation datasets in AI-supported learning.

Abstract: Evaluating procedural reasoning in AI-supported learning systems requires question-answer datasets that are both learner-like and grounded in the instructional knowledge the system is expected to use. We study how TMK-based question generation strategies affect dataset quality for procedural and multi-hop reasoning.
  We compare three strategies: strict generation from Task-Method-Knowledge (TMK) models, transcript-first generation with post-hoc TMK filtering, and TMK-aware generation that combines transcripts with structured guidance. To evaluate generated items, we introduce a grounding validation framework based on closed-set evidence units extracted from TMK models. The framework measures whether answers are supported by the underlying representation, whether questions are self-contained, and whether they target multi-hop procedural reasoning.
  Across 23 instructional topics and 690 generated question-answer pairs, strict TMK generation achieves the strongest overall quality, with 96.5% grounded questions and 92.6% usable questions. Transcript-first generation produces more learner-like questions but more context-dependent or weakly grounded items, while TMK-aware generation yields high raw multi-hop coverage but lower grounding. These results show that procedural richness and natural phrasing do not guarantee representational grounding, motivating explicit representation-aware validation for evaluation datasets in AI-supported learning.

</details>


### [258] [A Tutorial on World Models and Physical AI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12783)
*Il-Seok Oh*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Tutorial proposing a unified predictive framework for world models, integrating explicit and implicit approaches to support prediction, reasoning, and action in intelligent systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Advance robust, scalable decision making in robotics, autonomous driving, and AI by combining structured dynamics with predictive representations, addressing hierarchical reasoning, long-horizon planning, and autonomous goal formation toward AGI.

Method: Presents a coherent framework that unifies diverse world-modeling approaches through shared predictive structure, differentiated by how structure is represented and exploited; discusses hierarchy, long-horizon planning, and goal formation within the unifying view; connects to foundation models for integrated perception-prediction-action.

Result: Conceptual contribution: a framework and taxonomy for world modeling; identifies key challenges and future directions. No empirical evaluation reported.

Conclusion: Progress requires advances in hierarchical reasoning, long-horizon planning, and autonomous goal formation; foundation models may enable unified perception-prediction-action systems; the paper sets a direction rather than presenting tested methods.

Abstract: World modeling is emerging as a central principle for building intelligent systems capable of prediction, reasoning, and decision making. A central distinction can be drawn between explicit world models, which learn structured dynamics for rollout-based reasoning and planning, and implicit world models, which encode predictive structure within scalable learned representations. These complementary paradigms provide a foundation for physical AI in domains such as robotics and autonomous driving, enabling intelligence beyond reactive control under real-world constraints. Recent foundation models further suggest a pathway toward unified systems integrating perception, prediction, and action. Despite rapid progress, major challenges remain in hierarchical reasoning, long-horizon planning, and autonomous goal formation, which are critical for advancing toward artificial general intelligence. This tutorial presents a coherent framework in which diverse world modeling approaches are unified through shared predictive structure and differentiated by how such structure is represented and exploited.

</details>


### [259] [The Containment Gap: How Deployed Agentic AI Frameworks Fail Public-Facing Safety Requirements](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12797)
*Md Jafrin Hossain,Mohammad Arif Hossain,Weiqi Liu,Nirwan Ansari*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Agentic LLM frameworks (LangChain, AutoGPT, OpenAI Agents SDK) lack architectural safety guarantees, particularly memory integrity. A memory-poisoning attack on a government-benefits agent demonstrates persistent, targeted corruption across seeds/backends, increasing wrongful denials significantly. Two lightweight defenses—memory integrity validator and policy gate—mitigate the attack with sub-millisecond overhead. The study calls for secure-by-default interventions for high-stakes deployments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: With agentic LLMs autonomously invoking tools, maintaining persistent memory, and executing multi-step plans in public-facing domains, there is an urgent need to assess whether current frameworks provide architectural-level safety guarantees and containment.

Method: Derived six containment principles from a compositional model of agentic architectures; audited three frameworks (LangChain, AutoGPT, OpenAI Agents SDK) for native compliance; empirically validated vulnerabilities using a simulated government-benefits agent; conducted memory-poisoning write attacks across multiple seeds and backends; evaluated impact under a complex five-factor policy; developed and tested two defenses (memory integrity validator, policy gate) and measured runtime overhead per call.

Result: None of the three frameworks adhered to all six containment principles; memory integrity was absent in all. A memory-poisoning attack caused persistent, targeted corruption across seeds/backends, boosting wrongful denial rates to 88.9% in a targeted subset. Under a five-factor policy, aggregate accuracy remained, but targeted wrongful denials increased by 3.5x. The two proposed defenses eliminated the attack vectors with sub-millisecond overhead (<0.2 ms per call).

Conclusion: Current agentic framework ecosystems likely do not meet secure-by-default expectations for public deployments. The paper advocates priority architectural interventions—like memory integrity validation and policy gating—to enable trustworthy, high-stakes deployments.

Abstract: Agentic large language model systems that autonomously invoke tools, maintain persistent memory, and execute multi-step plans are increasingly deployed in public-facing domains, including government services, healthcare triage, and financial advising. We ask whether the frameworks used to build these systems provide architectural-level structural safety guarantees. Applying six containment principles derived from a compositional model of agentic architectures, we audit three dominant frameworks (LangChain, AutoGPT, and OpenAI Agents SDK) and find no native compliance in any of them. Memory integrity, a defense against one of the most prevalent vulnerability classes, is not observed in any of the three evaluated frameworks. We validate these findings empirically: in a simulated government benefits agent built on LangChain, a single memory-poisoning write induces persistent targeted corruption across all tested seeds and backends, increasing the wrongful denial rate for targeted applicants to 88.9%. Under a complex five-factor policy, the same attack preserves aggregate accuracy while increasing targeted wrongful denials by 3.5x, rendering the corruption difficult to detect through standard monitoring. We then introduce two lightweight containment mechanisms: a memory integrity validator and a policy gate, which eliminate both attack vectors with sub-millisecond overhead (<0.2ms per call). We conclude that the current agentic framework ecosystem may not yet meet secure-by-default expectations for public-facing deployments and outline priority architectural interventions to enable trustworthy deployment in high-stakes, socially impactful applications.

</details>


### [260] [MLUBench: A Benchmark for Lifelong Unlearning Evaluation in MLLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12809)
*He Li,Haoang Chi,Qizhou Wang,Yunxin Mao,Zhiheng Zhang,Jie Tan,Tongliang Liu,Wenjing Yang,Bo Han*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces MLUBench, a large-scale benchmark for lifelong unlearning in multimodal LLMs, reveals severe cumulative degradation of existing methods due to multimodal alignment constraints, and presents LUMoE to mitigate degradation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the need for data removal in multimodal LLMs as lifelong unlearning requests arrive sequentially, while preserving multimodal alignment and model performance.

Method: Proposes MLUBench with 127 entities across 9 classes to simulate lifelong unlearning; analyzes existing unlearning methods; introduces LUMoE, a method designed to mitigate degradation during continual unlearning.

Result: Existing unlearning methods suffer severe cumulative degradation under lifelong requests, especially due to preserving multimodal alignment. LUMoE significantly mitigates degradation on MLUBench benchmarks compared with baselines.

Conclusion: Lifelong unlearning in MLLMs is constrained by multimodal alignment; proposed LUMoE effectively alleviates degradation; dataset and code are released for community use.

Abstract: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) are trained on massive multimodal data, making data unlearning increasingly important as data owners may request the removal of specific content. In practice, these requests often arrive sequentially over time, giving rise to the challenging problem of MLLM Lifelong Unlearning. However, most existing benchmarks are limited in scale and scope, failing to capture the complexities of MLLM lifelong unlearning. To fill this gap, we introduce the MLUBench, a large-scale and comprehensive benchmark featuring 127 entities across 9 classes under lifelong unlearning requests. We perform extensive experiments using MLUBench and reveal that existing unlearning methods suffer from severe, cumulative degradation. More critically, we further identify the unique challenge of this problem: unlike in unimodal models, MLLM lifelong unlearning is constrained by the need to preserve multimodal alignment. Continually unlearning from one modality could degrade the entire model. To alleviate this challenge, we propose LUMoE, an effective method. Experiments demonstrate that LUMoE significantly mitigates the degradation problem faced by baselines. The source code and the MLUBench dataset are open-sourced in https://github.com/lihe-maxsize/Lifelong_Unlearning_main.

</details>


### [261] [Teach-and-Repeat: Accurately Extracting Operational Knowledge from Mobile Screen Demonstrations to Empower GUI Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12817)
*Yudong Zhang,Lei Hu,Daoyang Liu,Jiawei Liu,Yangfan Luo,Xingyu Liu,Zuojian Wang,Zhilin Gao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduce Teach VLM to translate mobile screen trajectories into step-by-step operational knowledge; introduces data flywheel and a Chinese benchmark; pairs with Teach-and-Repeat to improve downstream agent execution; achieves state-of-the-art operation semantics and higher task success rates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mobile UI understanding requires dynamic action comprehension; existing vision-language models struggle with highly heterogeneous mobile UIs and lack scalable, aligned data for operation-level reasoning.

Method: Identify operation-related keyframes from demonstration videos and train Teach VLM to output short natural-language sentences describing action types, target UI elements, textual arguments, and execution order. Use a data flywheel for scalable data collection. Create the Chinese Mobile Screen Teach Benchmark. Propose the Teach-and-Repeat paradigm where generated operation knowledge guides downstream screen-based execution agents.

Result: Teach VLM outperforms strong VLM baselines in operation semantics prediction and achieves state-of-the-art results; Android World experiments show consistent improvements in downstream Task Success Rate.

Conclusion: The framework provides a practical pipeline from raw demonstrations to reusable task automation, bridging perception and action for mobile UI tasks.

Abstract: Understanding the digital world on mobile devices is shifting from static UI perception to dynamic action comprehension. This capability enables models to convert visual state transitions into operational knowledge, defined as short natural-language sentences that describe action types, target UI elements, textual arguments, and execution orders. However, due to the highly diverse and heterogeneous UI designs across applications, existing vision-language models (VLMs) struggle to accurately infer these underlying operations. To bridge this gap, we introduce Teach VLM, a core model designed to translate mobile screen trajectories into step-wise operational knowledge by extracting and analyzing operation-related keyframes from demonstration videos. To address the scarcity of aligned training data, we develop a systematic data flywheel for scalable data acquisition. We further introduce a novel Chinese Mobile Screen Teach Benchmark for fine-grained evaluation. Building upon Teach VLM, we propose the Teach-and-Repeat paradigm, where the generated operational knowledge serves as an interpretable procedural reference to guide downstream screen-based execution agents. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that Teach VLM significantly outperforms strong VLM baselines, achieving state-of-the-art performance in operation semantics prediction. Furthermore, experiments in Android World show that our paradigm yields consistent Task Success Rate improvements for downstream agents. Together, Teach VLM and the Teach-and-Repeat paradigm offer a practical pathway from raw demonstrations to reusable task automation.

</details>


### [262] [GeoNatureAgent Benchmark: Benchmarking LLM Agents for Environmental Geospatial Analysis Across Frontier and Open-Weight Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12821)
*Gabriel Diaz-Ireland,Diego Prieto-Herráez,Mario García Peces,Javier Velázquez,Devika Jain*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces GeoNatureAgent Benchmark: first benchmark for environmental analysis agents using structured tool calls to a production-style geospatial API; 93 tasks across 18 categories; evaluates seven LLMs; finds Claude Sonnet 4 leads ~60.8%, with open-weight models offering strong cost-accuracy trade-offs; structured-tool calling more discriminative than general GIS benchmarks; extensible with BigEarthNet V2; resources publicly available.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the disproportionate data wrangling burden in environmental analysis and the lack of validated AI agents that operate through structured tool calls against real geospatial APIs; provide a benchmark to assess such agents.

Method: Construct a benchmark with 93 tasks across 18 categories including municipality analysis, multi-turn dialog, spatial reasoning, cross-indicator synthesis, and error handling. Use an open, self-hostable API serving three environmental indicators for Spain and Portugal via sixteen tools. Evaluate seven LLMs (Claude Sonnet 4, DeepSeek V3.2, GLM-5, Gemini 2.5 Pro, Qwen3-235B, GPT-OSS-120B, Llama 4 Scout) across three seeds (temperature=1.0). Report capability and per-case cost as orthogonal axes. Analyze results, compare against traditional GIS benchmarks, and test extensibility by adding BigEarthNet V2 and additional indicators; make benchmark and API publicly available.

Result: Key findings: (1) Claude Sonnet 4 leads with 60.8% ±0.8%; DeepSeek V3.2 at 56.3% ±3.1%; no other model above 51%. (2) Cost-accuracy Pareto frontier dominated by open-weight models; DeepSeek V3.2 achieves ~93% of Claude’s capability at 11x lower cost (~$0.011/case). (3) Comparison tasks remain unsolved (0% on close-value comparisons), revealing systematic reasoning limits. (4) Structured tool calling against a real API is more discriminative than general GIS benchmarks, with accuracies 25–35 points lower. Extensibility demonstrated by integrating BigEarthNet V2 for Portugal alongside Spanish CO2 and erosion indicators; benchmark harness and API are publicly available.

Conclusion: The GeoNatureAgent Benchmark provides a discriminative, extensible framework to evaluate environmental AI agents that operate via structured tool calls against production-style geospatial APIs; open-weight models can offer favorable cost-precision trade-offs; it highlights persistent reasoning limitations in comparison tasks and underscores the value of realistic APIs for benchmarking.

Abstract: Environmental scientists spend disproportionate effort on data wrangling rather than analysis, and AI agents that automate geospatial workflows remain unvalidated: no benchmark evaluates agents operating through structured tool calling against real APIs. We introduce the GeoNatureAgent Benchmark, the first benchmark for environmental analysis agents that operate via structured tool calls to a production-style geospatial API. It comprises 93 tasks across 18 categories, covering municipality analysis, multi-turn conversation, spatial reasoning, cross-indicator synthesis, error handling and recovery, ranking, comparison, multilingual understanding, habitat analysis, and task rejection. Tasks are evaluated against an open, self-hostable API serving three environmental indicators across Spain and Portugal via sixteen tools. We evaluate seven LLMs (Claude Sonnet 4, DeepSeek V3.2, GLM-5, Gemini 2.5 Pro, Qwen3-235B, GPT-OSS-120B, Llama 4 Scout) under three temperature-1.0 seeds, reporting capability and per-case cost as orthogonal axes. We find: (1) Claude Sonnet 4 leads at 60.8% +/- 0.8%, followed by DeepSeek V3.2 at 56.3% +/- 3.1%, with no other model above 51%; (2) the cost-accuracy Pareto frontier is occupied mostly by open-weight models, with DeepSeek V3.2 offering 93% of Claude's capability at 11x lower cost ($0.011/case); (3) comparison tasks remain universally unsolved (0% on close-value comparisons), exposing systematic reasoning limits; and (4) structured tool calling against a real API is more discriminative than general-purpose GIS benchmarks, with accuracies 25-35 points lower. We further show extensibility by integrating BigEarthNet V2 land cover for Portugal alongside Spanish CO2 and erosion indicators. The benchmark, harness, and self-hostable API are publicly available.

</details>


### [263] [Topical Phase Transitions in Artificial Intelligence Research: Large-Scale Evidence and an Early-Warning Signature for Emerging Topics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12828)
*Rasul Khanbayov,Hasan Kurban*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AI topics progress via topical phase transitions: long dormancy followed by rapid surges across major venues; LLMs dominate by 2025; a modest early-warning signature is evaluated with reasonable recall but modest precision.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To characterize large-scale dynamics of topic evolution across top AI venues, and to distinguish genuine phase transitions from ordinary growth, enabling proactive monitoring.

Method: Quantitative analysis of 80,814 accepted main-track papers from ACL, CVPR, ICLR, ICML, NeurIPS (2017–2025); tracking cross-venue topic dynamics; identifying phase-transition patterns; proposing an early-warning signature with four publication-dynamics criteria derived from 2017–2021 data; out-of-sample validation on 2023–2025; assessing precision/recall; releasing source code on GitHub.

Result: Found evidence of topical phase transitions: topics stay marginal for years, then surge within 1–3 years across venues. LLMs become the dominant cross-venue topic by 2025; diffusion models rise abruptly; language-model methods spread into vision via vision-language models; reinforcement learning grows smoothly. An early-warning signature yields 27% precision and 63% recall (base rate 13.5%) for predicting future transitions. For 2025 data, topics flagged for monitoring include reasoning and test-time compute, agentic AI, multimodal LLMs, retrieval-augmented generation, and world models.

Conclusion: Provides a large-scale cross-venue map of AI topic reorganization and introduces an experimentally grounded early-warning framework. Public code enables reproducibility and further monitoring of emerging topics.

Abstract: Do research topics in artificial intelligence grow gradually, or do they advance through abrupt, detectable jumps? Analyzing 80,814 accepted main-track papers from five premier AI conferences (ACL, CVPR, ICLR, ICML, NeurIPS) spanning 2017 to 2025, we show major AI topics advance through topical phase transitions: remaining marginal for years, then surging across venues within one to three years. Large language models became the dominant cross-venue topic by 2025, diffusion models rose with comparable abruptness, and language-model methods crossed into computer vision via vision-language models, whereas reinforcement learning compounded smoothly, distinguishing genuine phase transitions from ordinary growth. This structure is our primary contribution: a large-scale, cross-venue characterization of how AI research reorganizes. We then ask whether a transition leaves a detectable footprint before it peaks. We define an early-warning signature, four publication-dynamics criteria frozen on 2017-2021 data, and evaluate it out of sample on 2023-2025 transitions, obtaining a precision of 27% and recall of 63% against a 13.5% base rate. Applied to 2025 data, the signature flags reasoning and test-time compute, agentic AI, multimodal LLMs, retrieval-augmented generation, and world models as topics to monitor over 2026-2028. The source code is also publicly available on GitHub at https://github.com/KurbanIntelligenceLab/ai-phase-transitions.

</details>


### [264] [Fantastic Scientific Agents and How to Build Them: AgentBuild for Rietveld Refinement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12834)
*Woong Shin,Craig A. Bridges,Marshall T. McDonnell,Rafael Ferreira da Silva*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AgentBuild enables contract-driven construction of scientific LLM-based agents, gating a rubric-driven judge that directs a meta-optimizer to edit within declared boundaries; demonstrated on Rietveld refinement of XRD data (GSAS-II behind MCP/A2A), exposing frontier limits as contract failures and re-tuning as needed, with the scientist's contract as the durable asset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Shift scientific workflow automation from opaque model tuning to codified, version-controlled governance by the scientist's judgment, encoded as a contract with rubric, curriculum, and knowledge base; improve reproducibility, safety, and boundary-respecting automation in LLM-based agents.

Method: Define AgentBuild: a contract comprising a rubric-driven, version-controlled rubric; a difficulty-graded curriculum; and a curated external knowledge base. A rubric-driven judge gates a meta-optimizer coding agent that edits the agent within a declared boundary, producing a compiled agent rather than relying on the scientist's direct judgment. Instantiate this for Rietveld refinement of XRD data via GSAS-II behind MCP and A2A, using a blank-harness construction run through a LLZO SNR ladder to reach a 4-hour frontier scan, exposing workflow-scope limits. The rubric also scores trajectory scope, framing frontier outcomes as contract failures rather than mere pattern-fitting failures.

Result: The frontier case reveals the workflow-scope limits and validates that frontier status is a contract failure. Re-running AgentBuild equates to re-tuning, not rebuilding, preserving the scientist-authored contract as the durable asset.

Conclusion: As base models evolve, re-running AgentBuild is a re-tune guided by an enduring scientist-authored contract, ensuring the agent remains within declared boundaries and the contract remains the stable governance artifact.

Abstract: As scientific workflows shift from deterministic executables to LLM-based agents, the development practices on offer, such as fine-tuning, reinforcement learning, and prompt-and-go, bury the scientist's judgment. We propose treating agent construction as a workflow stage and introduce AgentBuild, which builds a scientific agent from a contract the scientist authors. The contract is a version-controlled rubric, a difficulty-graded curriculum, and a curated external knowledge base. A rubric-driven judge gates a meta-optimizer coding agent that edits the agent within a declared boundary, so the build compiles the agent, not the scientist's judgment. We instantiate this for Rietveld refinement of X-ray diffraction data through GSAS-II behind MCP and A2A, where a blank-harness construction run progresses through a lithium lanthanum zirconium oxide (LLZO) signal-to-noise ladder, reaches the 4 hour scan as a frontier case, and exposes the workflow-scope limits that remain. The same rubric that rewards credible fits also scores trajectory scope, making the frontier a contract failure rather than a pattern-fitting failure. As base models evolve, re-running AgentBuild is a re-tune, not a rebuild, and the scientist's authored contract remains the durable asset.

</details>


### [265] [(Human) Attention Is (Still) All You Need: Human oversight makes AI-assisted social science reliable](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12848)
*Chen Zhu,Xiaolu Wang,Weilong Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: HLER structures human–machine research workflows to dramatically reduce failure rates in AI-assisted research, from 72% to 16%, by enforcing pre-commitment, decision gates, and deterministic computation; most improvements occur on underrepresented datasets; interpretable as a research harness with independent contributions and potential complementarity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address reliability concerns in AI-assisted research by exploring how the division of cognitive labor between humans and LLMs affects outcomes, beyond raw model capability.

Method: A 2×4 factorial experiment (280 complete research runs across four datasets) using a single underlying model and shared reasoning agents. The design imposes three architectural commitments: (1) LLMs reason but do not perform data work, (2) data/estimation are computed deterministically, (3) three human decision gates bind the workflow. An ablation phase with 80 runs tests component contributions. Outcomes measured as failure rates; Fisher’s exact test for equality of failure rates (p<0.001).

Result: Unconstrained baseline failures: 72%. With HLER: 16% failures. Deterministic computation and human gates contribute independently; greater reliability gains on the least public dataset (a Qing-dynasty population register). An 80-run ablation provides exploratory evidence of complementarity between components.

Conclusion: HLER functions as a research harness that sharply reduces failures and exposes residual weaknesses, preventing unreliable findings from becoming publication-ready outputs, and reframing AI-assisted research as a human-in-the-loop process with explicit governance.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used for tasks once reserved for trained researchers, including hypothesis generation, specification choice, and drafting conclusions. We argue that the reliability of AI-assisted research depends not only on model capability, but also on how cognitive labour is structured between humans and machines. We study this problem through Human-in-the-Loop Economic Research (HLER), a decision architecture based on pre-commitment, decision sequencing, accountability, and attention allocation. In a pre-specified 2*4 factorial experiment with 280 complete research runs across four datasets, an unconstrained multi-agent baseline produced critical failures in 72% of runs. Using the same underlying model, the same agent decomposition, and identical prompts for the shared reasoning agents, HLER reduced the failure rate to 16% by imposing three architectural commitments: LLMs reason but do not execute data work, data and estimation are handled deterministically, and three human decision gates bind the workflow. Fisher's exact test rejects equality of failure rates at p<0.001. Reliability gains were largest on the least publicly represented dataset, a Qing-dynasty population register, consistent with a task-based production model with Frechet-distributed output quality. An 80-run ablation suggests that deterministic computation and human gates contribute independently, with exploratory evidence of complementarity. We interpret HLER as a research harness rather than an autonomous AI scientist: it sharply reduces failures, makes residual weaknesses more visible, and prevents unreliable claims from being advanced as publication-ready outputs.

</details>


### [266] [WISE: A Long-Horizon Agent in Minecraft with Why-Which Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12852)
*Renmin Cheng,Changhao Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: WISE is a long-horizon embodied agent framework that integrates Which-Why memory with a Causal Event Graph to augment episodic memory, enabling robust recall, opportunistic task scheduling, and multi-scale exploration to improve performance on long-horizon sparse tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses bottlenecks in LLM-augmented agents due to low-level controller failures and decoupled what-where-when from which-why reasoning; aims to unify episodic memory with causal reasoning to guide planning and action.

Method: Introduce Causal Event Graph to link observations to task relevance, enabling robust recall under viewpoint changes; implement Opportunistic Task Scheduler that reorders subtasks based on causal opportunities; apply multi-scale progressive exploration to obtain comprehensive observations; evaluate in long-horizon sparse tasks.

Result: Empirical results show improved task success and efficiency, particularly where adaptive decision-making is required.

Conclusion: Integrating causal memory, opportunistic task scheduling, and multi-scale exploration can alleviate low-level bottlenecks and enhance long-horizon planning in embodied agents.

Abstract: Rapid advances have been made in developing general-purpose embodied agent in environments like Minecraft through the adoption of LLM-augmented hierarchical approaches. Despite their promise, low-level controllers often become performance bottlenecks due to repeated execution failures. We argue that a key limitation is not only the lack of episodic memory, but also the decoupling of \textit{what-where-when} memory from \textit{which-why} reasoning. To address this, we propose \textbf{WISE} (Which-Why Informed Semantic Explorer), a long-horizon agent framework with an enhanced low-level controller equipped with a Causal Event Graph that augments episodic memory with explicit causal structure linking observations to task relevance. Unlike prior work such as MrSteve, which relies on feature similarity for retrieval, WISE enables robust recall under viewpoint changes and supports opportunistic task reordering through causal reasoning. Building on this memory, we propose an Opportunistic Task Scheduler that dynamically re-prioritizes subtasks when causally relevant opportunities are detected. We further equip WISE with a multi-scale progressive exploration strategy to provide spatially comprehensive observations for downstream reasoning. Experiments show that WISE largely improves task success and efficiency on long-horizon sparse tasks, particularly in settings requiring adaptive decision-making.

</details>


### [267] [DailyReport: An Open-ended Benchmark for Evaluating Search Agents on Daily Search Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12871)
*Jingxuan Han,Wei Liu,Mingyang Zhu,Youpeng Wang,Ziwen Wang,Lin Qiu,Xuezhi Cao,Xunliang Cai,Zheren Fu,Licheng Zhang,Zhendong Mao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: DailyReport is an open-ended benchmark for evaluating Search Agents (SAs) across 150 tasks with 3,546 rubrics. It employs cascade rubrics and user-centric aggregation for interpretable, dimension-specific scores, revealing current SAs underperform relative to user expectations; dataset and code are released to the public.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior SA benchmarks focus on narrow tasks and coarse rubrics, limiting interpretability and realism. A realistic, interpretable evaluation framework is needed to advance SA development.

Method: Assemble 150 open-ended tasks; decompose each task into subtasks; apply cascade rubrics across disentangled dimensions; perform cascade performance attribution and user-centric aggregation to derive scores per dimension and a user preference score.

Result: Evaluation on 17 agentic systems indicates current SAs do not meet user expectations. The framework yields highly interpretable scores by dimension and for user preference; the dataset and code are publicly available.

Conclusion: DailyReport provides a realistic, interpretable benchmark for SA evaluation and a resource for future research, facilitating replication and advancement by releasing data and code.

Abstract: Search Agents (SAs) typically leverage large language models (LLMs) to support complex information-seeking tasks by autonomously exploring web sources and synthesizing information into comprehensive responses. For SAs evaluation, prior benchmarks mainly focus on specialized tasks that are unlikely to arise in real-world user scenarios. Moreover, their reliance on coarse task-level rubrics often limits evaluation interpretability. To bridge this gap, we introduce DailyReport, an open-ended benchmark to evaluate SA capabilities on daily search tasks. It contains 150 open-ended tasks with 3,546 associated rubrics, capturing widely discussed and timely information demands of real-world users. Each task is decomposed into subtasks and evaluated with cascade rubrics across disentangled dimensions. Through cascade performance attribution and user-centric aggregation, we derive highly interpretable scores for each dimension, along with a user preference score. Our results on 17 agentic systems show that current systems still fall short of users' expectations. To facilitate future research, our dataset and code are made publicly available at https://github.com/AGI-Eval-Official/DailyReport.

</details>


### [268] [HarnessBridge: Learnable Bidirectional Controller for LLM Agent Harness](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12882)
*Xiaoxuan Wang,Haixin Wang,Alexander Taylor,Jason Cong,Yizhou Sun,Wei Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Learnable harness module HarnessBridge mediates LLM agent–environment interaction via bidirectional projections (observation and action), trained end-to-end with harness supervision, achieving comparable or better performance with reduced token use and trajectory length, and generalizing across model sizes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Manual, handcrafted harnesses for long-horizon LLM tasks are brittle and hard to scale as trajectories grow; a trainable interface could improve efficiency and generalization.

Method: Introduce HarnessBridge, a lightweight controller that parameterizes the agent–environment interface with two bidirectional projections: (1) observation projection to distill raw trajectories into compact, decision-relevant states, and (2) action projection to convert proposed actions into executable transitions or trajectory-grounded rejections. Trained end-to-end on a harness supervision dataset via unified instruction tuning.

Result: Empirical evaluation on Terminal-Bench 2.0 and SWE-bench Verified shows HarnessBridge matches or surpasses strong specialized harnesses while substantially reducing token usage and trajectory length, and generalizes from smaller generators to larger commercial models.

Conclusion: A learnable harness module can effectively mediate agent–environment interaction, reducing compute and data requirements while enabling scalable, generalizable long-horizon performance across model sizes.

Abstract: Large language models are increasingly deployed as agents for long-horizon tasks, yet their performance is shaped not only by model capability and environment design, but also by the harness that mediates agent--environment interaction. Existing harnesses are largely manually engineered, making them difficult to scale as trajectories grow longer and interactions become more complex. In this work, we ask whether harness can be generated by a learnable plug-in module that can be trained in an end-to-end fashion. We introduce HarnessBridge, a lightweight learnable harness controller that parameterizes the agent--environment interface as a bidirectional projection. HarnessBridge learns two bidirectional projections: observation projection, which distills raw trajectories into compact, decision-relevant states, and action projection, which converts proposed actions into executable transitions or trajectory-grounded rejections. We train HarnessBridge on a harness supervision dataset via unified instruction tuning. On Terminal-Bench~2.0 and SWE-bench Verified, HarnessBridge matches or surpasses strong specialized harnesses while substantially reducing token usage and trajectory length, and generalizes from smaller generators to larger commercial models.

</details>


### [269] [The Hidden Power of Scaling Factor in LoRA Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12883)
*Zicheng Zhang,Haoran Li,Jiaxing Wang,Guoqiang Gong,Anqi Li,Yudong Hu,Ting Xiong,Yurong Gao,Junxing Hu,Zhida Jiang,Yifeng Zhang,Pengzhang Liu,Qixia Jiang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper argues that the LoRA scaling factor α is a primary driver of optimization, not a mere companion to the learning rate. α yields gains beyond LR scaling by smoothing the loss landscape, enabling faster convergence. The optimal α scales sublinearly with rank (approximately sqrt(rank) with a large coefficient). A minimal LoRA-α framework is proposed to align α with principled scaling, improving performance and reducing hyperparameter search.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the optimization role of α in LoRA, disentangle its effect from the learning rate, quantify how α interacts with rank, and establish principled scaling laws to guide LoRA usage.

Method: Extensive empirical analysis across diverse tasks; development of a Signal-Drift theoretical framework to separate task signal from optimization drift; analysis of spectral suppression effects; comparison of α scaling with learning-rate scaling; derivation of a sublinear (square-root) scaling law with rank; proposal of LoRA-α.

Result: α acts as the dominant driver of effective optimization and cannot be replicated by learning-rate scaling alone. LoRA’s spectral suppression smooths the optimization landscape, allowing standard hyperparameters to be overly conservative. The optimal α scales sublinearly with rank, following a square-root law with a large coefficient, indicating current rank-tied heuristics under-scale. LoRA-α delivers consistent performance gains across tasks and simplifies hyperparameter search.

Conclusion: Restore α to a principled regime via LoRA-α, making LoRA compatible with small learning rates and improving effectiveness while reducing hyperparameter tuning. This framework reframes how to scale adapters and promises broader learning potential with simpler setups.

Abstract: In Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA), the scaling factor $α$ is often treated as a mere complement to the learning rate, yet its role in optimization remains poorly understood. In this paper, we reveal that the scaling factor $α$ and the learning rate function differently, with $α$ emerging as the dominant driver of effective optimization, delivering gains that cannot be replicated by learning rate scaling alone. Through the synergy of extensive empirical analysis and a theoretical Signal-Drift framework, we uncover three findings into LoRA's scaling mechanism: First, LoRA's spectral suppression smooths the optimization landscape, rendering standard hyperparameters overly conservative and creating an optimization gap. Second, when leveraging this smoothness to accelerate convergence, $α$ outperforms the learning rate by amplifying the task signal without increasing the drift ratio. Third, the optimal scaling factor follows a sublinear relationship with the rank, well characterized by a square-root law with an unexpectedly large coefficient, revealing the insufficient scaling of existing rank-tied heuristics. Based on these insights, we propose LoRA-$α$, a minimalist framework that restores $α$ to its principled regime, making LoRA compatible with standard small learning rates. Extensive evaluations across diverse tasks demonstrate that LoRA-$α$ consistently improves performance while streamlining hyperparameter search, unleashing the learning potential of LoRA.

</details>


### [270] [Zero-source LLM Hallucination Detection with Human-like Criteria Probing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12900)
*Jiahao Yang,Shuhai Zhang,Hailong Kang,Feng Liu,Qi Chen,Mingkui Tan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Presents HCPD, a zero-source hallucination detector for LLMs that uses Human-like Criteria Probing to decompose judgments into interpretable criteria, with reward-based alignment from semantic consistency, multi-sampling inference, and theoretical guarantees; reports state-of-the-art results and provides code for replication.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Hallucinations in LLMs under zero-source constraints are hard to detect since no internal signals or external references are available. There is a need for interpretable, reliable, and model-agnostic detection methods that only rely on the input-output text.

Method: Introduce Human-like Criteria Probing (HCP). An LLM agent adaptively decomposes judgment into a weighted set of interpretable criteria and aggregates criterion-specific scores into a final truthfulness score. Employ a reward-based alignment scheme using weak supervision from semantic consistency. Use multi-sampling aggregation at inference to improve robustness while preserving interpretability. Provide theoretical analysis of reliability.

Result: Empirical evaluation shows HCPD consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on zero-source hallucination detection tasks. The approach is effective and interpretable, with code available for replication.

Conclusion: HCPD offers an effective and explainable solution for zero-source hallucination detection, backed by theoretical guarantees and strong empirical performance.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) often hallucinate by generating factually incorrect or unfaithful content, posing significant risks to their safe use. Detecting such hallucinations is particularly challenging under the zero-source constraint, where no model internals or external references are available, and detection must rely solely on the textual query-answer pair. In this paper, we propose Human-like Criteria Probing for Hallucination Detection (HCPD), a paradigm that emulates the multi-faceted reasoning of human evaluators. Its core is a Human-like Criteria Probing (HCP) mechanism, in which a LLM agent adaptively decomposes its judgment into a weighted set of interpretable criteria and aggregates criterion-specific scores into a final truthfulness measure. To achieve this adaptive capability, we introduce a reward-based alignment scheme using only weak supervision from semantic consistency. At inference, we employ a multi-sampling aggregation strategy to ensure robust decisions while preserving full interpretability. We further provide theoretical analysis supporting the reliability of our approach. Extensive experiments show that HCPD consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, offering an effective and explainable solution for zero-source hallucination detection. Code is available at https://github.com/TRISKEL10N/HCPD.

</details>


### [271] [MDForge: Agentic Molecular Dynamics Pipeline Design under Sparse Simulator Feedback](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12916)
*Zehong Wang,Yijun Ma,Connor R. Schmidt,Tianyi Ma,Weixiang Sun,Ziming Li,Xiaoguang Guo,Chuxu Zhang,Matthew J. Webber,Yanfang Ye*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MDForge uses an LLM agent with multi-agent physics expert debates to auto-design MD pipelines, achieving competitive performance on SAMPL benchmarks and discovering a novel high-affinity CB[7] binder validated by NMR; code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce expert effort and trial-and-error in building MD pipelines for new systems, enabling rapid, automated design of simulation workflows.

Method: Treat pipeline design as open-ended code generation guided by verbal rewards. MDForge employs an in-context update rule that densifies sparse rewards via a multi-agent debate among physics experts to reshape the agent's behavior online. Evaluation on three SAMPL host-guest binding free-energy benchmarks; discovery of a novel binder in a library of unseen candidates.

Result: MDForge designs MD pipelines competitive with human experts on three SAMPL benchmarks. On unseen candidate guests, the CB[7] pipeline uncovers a novel binder with picomolar affinity, confirmed by wet-lab NMR.

Conclusion: Open-ended, reward-driven code generation with multi-agent expert debate is a viable path to automating complex MD pipeline design and can yield practically meaningful discoveries, with demonstrated success on SAMPL benchmarks and experimental validation for a novel binder.

Abstract: Molecular dynamics (MD) is the canonical in-silico method for atomistic molecular science, simulating molecular behavior from first-principle physics. Designing an MD pipeline for a new system requires substantial expert knowledge: running it on even one molecule is expensive, ruling out trial-and-error. We automate this expert pipeline-design process with an LLM agent. Unlike existing MD agents that orchestrate a predefined tool set, we treat pipeline design as open-ended code generation in which the agent's behavior is reshaped online by verbal reward. Specifically, we build MDForge, an LLM agent whose in-context update rule densifies the sparse reward via a multi-agent debate among physics experts. On three SAMPL host-guest binding free-energy benchmarks, MDForge automatically designs MD pipelines competitive with human experts. Deployed on a library of unseen candidate guests, its CB[7] pipeline discovers a novel binder that wet-lab competition NMR confirms is a high-affinity, picomolar CB[7] binder. Our data and code are available at https://github.com/Zehong-Wang/MDForge.

</details>


### [272] [Iterating Toward Better Search: A Two-Agent Simulation Framework for Evaluating Agentic Search Architectures in E-Commerce](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12924)
*Jetlir Duraj,Jayanth Yetukuri,Shuang Zhou,Dhruv Varma,Rui Kong,Ishita Khan,Qunzhi Zhou*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: In a modular two-agent simulation for conversational shopping assistants, rolling-window memory outperforms intent-extraction memory (faster and better across metrics); targeted failure analysis cuts failure rates by 62%; swapping LLM backbones incurs a small score cost (0.16–0.45); frontier judges disagree on evaluation criteria, with Gemini favoring process correctness and Claude favoring concrete outcomes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable controlled, repeatable evaluation of conversational shopping agent architectures by isolating responder design and memory strategies, across fixed buyer personas and an actual e-commerce search API integration.

Method: A two-agent framework with an independent buyer (with personas, missions, patience) and an interchangeable responder integrated with a real e-commerce search API. The buyer remains constant to enable controlled comparisons. Experiments cover 2011 conversations across 14 persona buckets. Memory designs compared: rolling-window versus intent-extraction. Systematic failure analysis used to guide fixes. LLM backbone comparisons: Gemini 2.5 versus Llama 3.3 70B. Evaluation includes frontier LLM judges.

Result: Empirical findings: (1) rolling-window memory beats intent-extraction on all quality metrics and is 35% faster per query; (2) failure analysis reduces failure and near-failure rates by 62% across the full dataset; (3) backbone swap Gemini 2.5 → Llama 3.3 70B costs 0.16–0.45 points despite identical architecture; (4) judges show systematic disagreement: Gemini rewards process correctness while Claude demands concrete outcomes.

Conclusion: The framework enables controlled, repeatable evaluation of conversational shopping assistants, highlighting the impact of memory design, iterative debugging, and backbone choice on performance, while also revealing evaluator bias across frontier LLMs and the need for robust, multi-metric evaluation pipelines.

Abstract: We present a modular two-agent simulation framework for evaluating conversational shopping assistant architectures. An independent buyer agent, configured with personas, missions, and patience levels, is paired with an interchangeable responder that integrates with a real e-commerce search API. Holding the buyer constant across experiments enables controlled comparison of responder designs on identical scenarios. Using 2011 conversations across 14 persona buckets, we establish four empirical findings. First, rolling-window memory outperforms intent-extraction memory on all quality metrics while being 35% faster per query. Second, illustrating rapid evidence-driven iteration, a systematic failure analysis of a responder version enables targeted fixes that reduce failure and near-failure rates by 62% across the full dataset. Third, swapping the responder LLM backbone from Gemini~2.5 to Llama~3.3~70B costs 0.16--0.45 points despite identical architecture. Finally, we document systematic philosophical disagreement between frontier LLM judges: Gemini rewards process correctness while Claude demands concrete outcomes, despite using the same evaluation prompt.

</details>


### [273] [MARS: Margin-Adversarial Risk-controlled Stopping for Parallel LLM Test-time Scaling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12935)
*Wenbo Chen,Puheng Li,Mengyang Liu,Weijie Su,Tianpei Xie*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MARS introduces a margin-adversarial stopping rule for parallel self-consistency in LLM reasoning, enabling early termination of non-critical traces while preserving accuracy; achieves substantial token savings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce computation in multi-trace parallel test-time reasoning by predicting which traces will not change their conclusion and safely cut them off.

Method: Decompose uncertainty into (1) trace-level switch probabilities indicating margin retention, learned via a five-feature logistic model; (2) an adversarial bound on future vote movement calibrated from warmup traces; uses intermediate checkpoints to probe partial traces; leader remains safe under the bound; with true switch probabilities, guarantees about equivalence to full-budget vote with high probability.

Result: Empirical results across three reasoning models and three competition-math benchmarks show MARS saves 25-47% of self-consistency tokens and 14-29% on top of DeepConf Online, while achieving accuracy comparable to full-budget baselines.

Conclusion: MARS provides a principled, data-driven early-stopping mechanism for parallel trace evaluation that reduces computation while preserving accuracy, by separating margin retention uncertainty from adversarial switching uncertainty, and is effective in practice.

Abstract: Parallel test-time scaling samples many reasoning traces and majority-votes their answers, improving LLM accuracy but requiring traces to run to completion, incurring substantial computational overhead. We observe that probing partial traces at intermediate checkpoints can extract current answers without disrupting generation, revealing an evolving aggregate vote. Based on this observation, we introduce MARS, a margin-adversarial stopping rule that estimates which active traces are likely to change their answers and stops once the leader remains safe under a conservative bound on future vote movement. The rule separates two sources of uncertainty. It learns the trace-level switch probabilities that determine how much of the current margin is likely to be retained, while handling the harder question of where switching traces land through an adversarial bound calibrated from warmup traces. With true switch probabilities, MARS guarantees with high probability that the early-stopped answer matches the full-budget vote. In practice, a five-feature logistic model closely matches oracle switching behavior. Across three reasoning models and three competition-math benchmarks, MARS saves 25-47% of self-consistency tokens and 14-29% on top of DeepConf Online, a strong confidence-weighted baseline that already filters and truncates weak traces, while matching the accuracy of the corresponding full-budget baselines.

</details>


### [274] [PRISMR: Overcoming Parse Collapse in Multimodal Listwise Ranking via Parameterized Representation Internalization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12942)
*Hao Jiang,Xin Li,Annan Wang,Zhi Yang,Haoxiang Zhang,Yichi Zhang,Weisi Lin*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PRISMR introduces a parametric, hypernetwork-driven adapter mechanism to internalize list structure for multimodal listwise ranking, mitigating parse collapse and improving performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-context generative listwise ranking with LMMs suffers parse collapse due to limited context utilization; prompt engineering and constrained decoding are insufficient to fix it.

Method: A lightweight hypernetwork encodes multimodal candidates in parallel and generates item-specific LoRA weights; these weights are synthesized into an instance-specific adapter for a LMM, enabling robust internalization of list structure while preserving the base model.

Result: PRISMR substantially reduces parse collapse, improves listwise ranking performance, and transfers effectively across domains and instruction-tuned backbones, as demonstrated on a new large-scale multimodal review-ranking benchmark.

Conclusion: The approach enables robust internalization of list structure in LMM-based ranking without requiring full fine-tuning of the base model, with demonstrated cross-domain applicability.

Abstract: Generative listwise ranking with Large Multimodal Models (LMMs) aims to capture global list context in a single forward pass, but
  its effectiveness degrades in long-context multimodal scenarios. We identify a recurring failure mode, parse collapse, where the
  autoregressive decoder produces fluent yet incomplete rankings by silently omitting candidates and terminating early. This
  failure stems from limited context utilization rather than simple formatting mistakes, making prompt engineering and constrained
  decoding insufficient. We propose PRISMR (Parameterized Representation Internalization for Semantic Multimodal Ranking), a
  framework that replaces transient in-context list processing with parametric structural conditioning. PRISMR uses a lightweight
  hypernetwork to encode multimodal candidates in parallel and generate item-specific LoRA weights, which are synthesized into an
  instance-specific adapter for a LMM. This paradigm enables more robust internalization of list structure while preserving the
  base model. We further introduce a large-scale multimodal review-ranking benchmark for evaluation. Experiments demonstrate that
  PRISMR substantially reduces parse collapse, improves listwise ranking performance, and transfers effectively across domains and
  instruction-tuned backbones.

</details>


### [275] [Learning What to Remember: A Cognitively Grounded Multi-Factor Value Model for Agentic Memory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12945)
*Zhibao Chen,Qian Cheng*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A cognitive-psychology-inspired multi-factor memory value function guides encoding/forgetting/retrieval under a fixed memory budget, with weights learned via a gradient-free optimiser; yields superior gold-evidence retention on LongMemEval compared to baselines, with interpretable results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-running LLM agents accumulate histories larger than context; existing memory strategies (semantic similarity or recency) mis-specify forgetting; a principled memory management approach using interpretable factors and learned weights aims to optimize downstream retrieval under memory constraints.

Method: Define V(m)=sum_i w_i f_i(m) with seven factors derived from cognitive psychology: emotional intensity, goal relevance, value alignment, self/user relevance, task utility, reliability, and usage history. Weights w_i are learned by a gradient-free optimiser using a downstream objective. Evaluate on LongMemEval across 479 usable cases, comparing learned weights to uniform weights, best single factor, and recency. Also train a neural network over the same factors. A controlled synthetic task with planted confounds tests recoverability of the learned weighting. Experiments run on CPU with open-source code.

Result: Learned multi-factor value retains 0.770 ± 0.011 of gold evidence; baselines: uniform 0.657, best single factor 0.518, recency 0.368. All paired differences have 95% bootstrap CIs above zero. A neural network over the same factors ties the linear model. Dominant factors: reliability, emotional intensity, self/user relevance; query-time goal similarity is down-weighted for forgetting. In a synthetic task with confounds, the learner recovers a separating weighting (1.00 retention) where uniform weighting fails (0.62). Substrate is open-source; experiments run on a single CPU with no API calls.

Conclusion: A principled, interpretable, learnable multi-factor memory value function improves memory management for long-running LLM agents, outperforming baselines and yielding interpretable insights into which factors drive encoding/forgetting/retrieval under budget constraints; results are reproducible with modest compute.

Abstract: Long-running LLM agents accumulate interaction histories far larger than any context window, forcing a standing decision: what to encode deeply, what to forget, and what to retrieve under a fixed memory budget. Production systems answer with semantic similarity or recency -- both mis-specified for the forgetting decision, which is made at consolidation time before the future query is known. We propose a multi-factor memory value function V(m)=\sum_i w_i f_i(m) over seven interpretable factors (emotional intensity, goal relevance, value alignment, self/user relevance, task utility, reliability, and usage history) drawn from cognitive psychology, whose weights are learned from a downstream objective by a gradient-free optimiser, and whose single scalar uniformly controls encoding depth, forget risk, and retrieval rank. We make a methodological point: on LongMemEval, scoring goal relevance against the held-out evaluation question saturates gold-evidence retention at \approx 0.98 -- this measures retrieval, not forgetting. In the realistic blind regime, a learned multi-factor value retains 0.770 \pm 0.011 of gold evidence across 479 usable cases, versus 0.657 for uniform weights, 0.518 for the best single factor, and 0.368 for recency; every paired gap's 95% bootstrap CI is above zero, and a neural network over the same factors ties the linear model. The learned weights are interpretable -- reliability, emotional intensity, and self/user relevance dominate, while query-time goal similarity is correctly down-weighted for the forgetting decision. A controlled synthetic task with planted confounds confirms the learner recovers a separating weighting (1.00 retention) where uniform weighting fails (0.62). The substrate is open-source; all experiments run on a single CPU with no API calls.

</details>


### [276] [OpenMedQ: Broad Open Pretraining for Medical Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12953)
*Ibrahim Gulluk,Max Van Puyvelde,Olivier Gevaert*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: OpenMedQ is a large, fully-open medical vision-language model trained on 14 datasets (~3.35M samples) across pathology, radiology, microscopy, and text-only QA. It achieves state-of-the-art BLEU-1 on PathVQA (75.9) and competitive VQA-MED BLEU-1 (64.5); the vision encoder transfers to 8 unseen medical classification benchmarks with the highest average macro-F1 (0.757) among BiomedCLIP, PMC-CLIP, PubMedCLIP, and a scratch baseline (0.616). Code and an interactive demo are released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To build a fully-open, broad-domain medical vision-language model that leverages diverse datasets to improve performance and reproducibility, addressing limitations of closed, large models and narrow-domain training data.

Method: Pretrain on 14 medical datasets totaling ~3.35M samples spanning imaging modalities and text QA; use a unified multimodal architecture. Evaluate on PathVQA BLEU-1 (state-of-the-art) and VQA-MED BLEU-1; transfer the vision encoder to eight unseen medical classification benchmarks under an identical downstream recipe to compare macro-F1 against BiomedCLIP, PMC-CLIP, PubMedCLIP, and a from-scratch baseline. Release code and interactive demo.

Result: OpenMedQ achieves BLEU-1 75.9 on PathVQA (state-of-the-art; surpassing Med-PaLM variants up to 562B parameters) and matches VQA-MED BLEU-1 64.5. Its vision encoder attains macro-F1 0.757 on eight unseen benchmarks, higher than BiomedCLIP (0.745), PMC-CLIP (0.745), PubMedCLIP (0.746), and a scratch baseline (0.616). Code and interactive demo released.

Conclusion: A broad, open medical vision-language model trained on diverse data can deliver competitive to state-of-the-art performance across vision-language tasks while maintaining openness and reproducibility; provides a strong public baseline for the community.

Abstract: We present OpenMedQ, a medical vision-language model pretrained on the broadest fully-open medical mix to date: 14 datasets totaling ~3.35M pretraining samples spanning pathology, radiology, microscopy, and text-only clinical QA. OpenMedQ reaches state-of-the-art BLEU-1 on PathVQA (75.9), beating Med-PaLM M variants up to 562B parameters (~80x larger), and matches the best reported VQA-MED BLEU-1 (64.5). Its vision encoder, transferred to 8 unseen medical classification benchmarks under an identical downstream recipe, obtains the highest average macro-F1 (0.757) among BiomedCLIP (0.745), PMC-CLIP (0.745), PubMedCLIP (0.746), and a from-scratch baseline (0.616). We release our code and an interactive demo is publicly available as a reproducible baseline for the community.

</details>


### [277] [Multi-Modal Agents for Power Distribution Defect Detection: An Evaluation of Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12969)
*Quan Quan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Multimodal foundation models as cognitive engines are evaluated for a power-distribution defect-detection agent, focusing on perception, reasoning, and tool usage; a domain-specific dataset and benchmark are created; results reveal strengths and limitations for autonomous maintenance in high-stakes environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Improve semantic understanding, generalization, and closed-loop automation in power distribution inspection, where traditional methods fall short.

Method: Introduce a Multi-Modal Agent framework; systematically evaluate foundation models on perception, reasoning, and tool usage; develop a domain-specific evaluation dataset and benchmark; conduct experiments to assess capabilities and limitations.

Result: The study reveals varied performance across perception, reasoning, and tool usage, with clear strengths in perception and notable gaps in robust reasoning and autonomous action, providing empirical guidance for deploying autonomous agents in industrial settings.

Conclusion: Foundation models hold promise for automated power-distribution maintenance but require domain-specific benchmarks and enhancements in reasoning and tool-use to ensure safe, effective closed-loop operations.

Abstract: The power distribution network is critical to reliable electricity delivery, yet traditional inspection methods face limitations in semantic understanding, generalization, and closed-loop automation. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a Multi-Modal Agent framework specifically for power distribution defect detection. Central to this study is the systematic evaluation of multimodal foundation models as unified cognitive engines. We rigorously assess their integrated performance across three critical capabilities: (1) Perception, where the model must accurately identify equipment and generate expert-level descriptions of defects; (2) Reasoning, where the model interprets visual findings to diagnose causes, assess severity, and plan maintenance strategies based on domain knowledge; and (3) Tool Usage, where the model acts as an autonomous operator to execute actions -- such as querying knowledge bases or generating work orders -- to achieve closed-loop maintenance. To support this evaluation, a domain-specific evaluation dataset and a comprehensive benchmark are developed. Experimental results demonstrate the strengths and limitations of current foundation models in these three dimensions, providing empirical evidence for deploying autonomous agents in high-stakes industrial environments.

</details>


### [278] [A Mathematical Forum Platform for Collaborative Problem Solving and Dataset Generation for AI Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12976)
*Akbar Erkinov,Nurmukhammad Abdurasulov*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A unified, three-layer in-forum image-to-LATEX pipeline reduces friction by converting uploaded images to LaTeX or Markdown, with live preview and storage, using Mathpix OCR and a patent-pending architecture.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Friction in sharing math in forums: LATEX is error-prone, OCR tools require platform switching, and no integrated path from photo to rendered post; creating a seamless workflow improves usability and can build a community-curated math dataset.

Method: Integrate an image-to-LATEX conversion pipeline directly into a forum posting interface. User uploads/captures an expression image; route through Mathpix OCR API; detect whether output is LaTeX or plain text with inline math; apply delimiter normalization; render live preview in LaTeX or Markdown before posting. Architecture: three loosely coupled layers (image processing, rendering, storage); desktop/mobile support; provisional US patent filed; detailed system design, data schema, and innovations discussed.

Result: Proposes a feasible, deployable system with live rendering and end-to-end workflow. No empirical results reported; demonstrates architectural viability, integration with existing tools, and potential to create a community-generated dataset for AI training and benchmarking.

Conclusion: The approach fills a practical gap in forum platforms by enabling a seamless image-to-LaTeX workflow, offering immediate usability and a growing dataset for AI development in mathematical reasoning.

Abstract: Sharing mathematical content in online forums remains a significant friction point for students and educators: writing raw LATEX is error-prone, standalone optical character recognition tools require platform switching, and current forum software offers no integrated path from a photograph of a formula to a rendered post. We present a unified system that eliminates this friction by embedding an image to LATEX conversion pipeline directly inside a forum posting interface. A user uploads or captures an image of a mathematical expression; the system routes it through the Mathpix OCR API, detects whether the returned output is LATEX or plain text containing inline math, applies the appropriate delimiter normalisation, and renders a live preview in either LATEX or Markdown mode before the post is committed to the database. The architecture is organized in three loosely coupled layers: image processing, rendering, and storage, and supports both desktop and mobile clients. A provisional US patent application has been filed covering the core methods. We describe the full system design, each component in detail, the data schema, and the key technical innovations, and we position the work against existing standalone tools and forum platforms to demonstrate the practical gap it closes.
