Beyond immediate usability, we argue that a deployed platform of this kind constitutes a continuously growing, community-validated dataset of mathematical problems and step-by-step solutions, a resource that can be used to train and benchmark AI systems for accurate mathematical reasoning

</details>


### [279] [Structured Testbench Generation for LLM-Driven HDL Design and Verification-Oriented Data Curation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12983)
*En-Ming Huang,Yu-Hung Kao,Ren-Hao Deng,Wei-Po Hsin,Yao-Ting Hsieh,Cheng Liang,Hsiang-Yu Tsou,Mu-Chi Chen,Yu-Kai Hung,Shao-Chun Ho,Po-Hsuang Huang,Shih-Hao Hung,H. T. Kung*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: STG is a Structured Testbench Generation framework that deterministically generates testbenches by exploiting hardware structure, achieving large speedups over LLM-based generation, improved coverage and compilation success, and benefits for data curation and test-time scaling; released on HuggingFace.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Automated testbench generation with LLMs is stochastic, expensive, and poorly reproducible; large language model driven RTL workflows require rapid, reliable verification to handle many candidate designs.

Method: Leverages inherent hardware structure to generate deterministic testbenches; acts as direct verification tool (fast simulation and higher coverage), data curation engine (faster filtering with energy efficiency), and test-time scaling oracle (reduces node count). Implementation yields STG framework with practical speedups and better compilation/coverage; models released.

Result: Up to 720x faster than iterative LLM-based flow; higher compilation success rate; higher coverage; fewer false passes on incorrect DUTs; identifies benchmark errors; 11x faster data curation and 127x less energy; 14-47% node count reduction; state-of-the-art performance across multi-benchmark evaluation; models available on HuggingFace.

Conclusion: STG demonstrates that structure-aware, deterministic testbench generation can outperform unconstrained LLM synthesis across verification, data curation, and scaling tasks, with practical benefits in speed, accuracy, and energy efficiency; potential for broader adoption in RTL verification workflows.

Abstract: Automated testbench generation has become a critical bottleneck in large language model (LLM)-driven Register Transfer Level (RTL) workflows, where large numbers of candidate designs must be verified rapidly and reliably. Existing prompt-based approaches treat testbench generation as unconstrained code synthesis, yielding stochastic outputs with high token cost, low reproducibility, and insufficient coverage. To address this gap, we present STG, a Structured Testbench Generation framework that exploits the inherent structure of hardware designs to generate deterministic testbenches. As a direct verification tool, STG runs 720x faster than an iterative LLM-based testbench generation flow and higher rate of successful compilation, achieves higher coverage, and reduces false-pass verdicts on incorrect DUTs. STG also helps identify errors in RTL generation benchmarks by exposing faulty benchmark testbenches. As a data curation engine, it is 11x faster than LLM-based filtering on a single CPU core with 127x less energy, and the resulting distilled models provide state-of-the-art performance in our multi-benchmark evaluation. As a test-time scaling oracle, it reduces node count by 14-47\%. Our models are available at https://huggingface.co/collections/AS-SiliconMind/siliconmind-v12.

</details>


### [280] [APCyc: Property-Informed Design of Cyclic Peptides via Automated Cyclization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12991)
*Yifan Zhao,Lang Qin,Jintai Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: APCyc introduces target-aware de novo cyclic peptide generation by expanding the residue vocabulary and encoding cyclization-site/linkage information; it uses Bayesian posterior guidance to achieve multi-property optimization, enabling target-dependent cyclization and controllable design.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cyclic peptides offer therapeutic advantages but de novo design is difficult due to cyclization constraints and the need to optimize multiple drug-relevant properties; existing genomers trained on linear peptides may miss cyclization-specific patterns.

Method: APCyc expands the residue vocabulary, explicitly encodes cyclization-site and linkage-type information to learn cyclization-aware representations, and employs Bayesian posterior guidance to bias sampling toward cyclic peptides that satisfy several property objectives.

Result: The model learns target-dependent cyclization preferences and enables effective and controllable multi-property optimization for cyclic peptide design; source code is available at the provided GitHub link.

Conclusion: APCyc constitutes a target-aware framework for de novo cyclic peptide generation, achieving cyclization-aware representations and multi-property control, with demonstrated potential for practical cyclic peptide design.

Abstract: Cyclic peptides represent a promising class of therapeutic compounds in modern drug discovery, often offering improved stability and binding affinity. However, the de novo design of cyclic peptides remains challenging because methods must identify pocket-adaptive cyclization patterns and linkage sites while simultaneously controlling drug-relevant properties. This challenge is particularly pronounced for recent generative models trained predominantly on linear peptide data, which may fail to capture cyclization-specific constraints. To address the limitation, we introduce APCyc, a target-aware de novo cyclic peptide generation framework that explicitly models cyclization and jointly optimizes multiple essential physicochemical properties. By using an expanded residue vocabulary and explicitly encoding cyclization-site and linkage-type information, APCyc learns cyclization-aware representations and leverages Bayesian posterior guidance to steer sampling toward cyclic peptides satisfying multiple property objectives. Experimental results demonstrate that our model learns target-dependent cyclization preferences, and enables effective and controllable multi-property optimization for cyclic peptide design. The source code of this paper is available at https://github.com/HKUSTGZ-ML4Health-Lab/APCyc.

</details>


### [281] [The Illusion of Multi-Agent Advantage](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13003)
*Prathyusha Jwalapuram,Hehai Lin,Chuyuan Li,Fangkai Jiao,Sudong Wang,Yifei Ming,Zixuan Ke,Chengwei Qin,Giuseppe Carenini,Shafiq Joty*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Automatic MAS designs often underperform strong Chain-of-Thought with Self-Consistency (CoT-SC) baselines despite higher costs. Expert-architected MAS outperform automated designs on both performance and cost-efficiency when subjected to a diagnostic synthetic dataset focusing on explicit task decomposition, context separation, and parallelization. The study suggests current automated design methods lead to architectural bloat with superficial complexity and misalignment with multi-agent principles, masking true architectural gaps in MAS performance under traditional evaluation frameworks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To scrutinize the claimed superiority of multi-agent systems over single-agent systems by evaluating automatically generated MAS against a strong CoT-SC baseline across standard and interactive tasks, and to determine whether automated MAS architectures capture the benefits of multi-agent collaboration or merely introduce unnecessary complexity.

Method: 1) Systematic evaluation of automatic MAS against CoT-SC on standard reasoning datasets and interactive multi-step tasks (e.g., BrowseComp-Plus). 2) Introduction of a diagnostic synthetic MAS dataset emphasizing explicit task decomposition, context separation, and parallelization potential. 3) Comparative analysis between expert-architected MAS and automatically generated MAS on both raw performance and cost-efficiency. 4) Deconstruction of generated architectures to identify architectural bloat and misalignment with multi-agent principles.

Result: Automatic MAS underperform CoT-SC across traditional datasets and interactive tasks, even at higher computational cost (up to 10x). Expert-architected MAS outperform automatic designs on the diagnostic dataset in both accuracy and cost-efficiency. Architectural analysis reveals automated designs prioritize superficial complexity (bloat) and fail to deliver functional gains aligned with multi-agent principles.

Conclusion: Current automatic MAS design paradigms produce bloated architectures that do not translate into the expected utility and cost-efficiency, obscuring fundamental gaps in MAS effectiveness. Evaluation frameworks that neglect the marginal utility of added computation can mask these deficiencies; expert architectural approaches are more aligned with multi-agent principles and yield better performance and efficiency.

Abstract: Prevailing wisdom posits that Multi-Agent Systems (MAS) are superior to Single-Agent Systems (SAS), citing advantages like context protection, parallel processing and distributed decision-making. However, empirical support for this claim relies primarily on comparisons with SAS baselines using benchmarks that prioritize isolated reasoning tasks, which do not adequately assess these advantages. Focusing on automatically generated MAS that are designed for enhanced generalizability over manually-designed counterparts, we perform a rigorous, systematic evaluation against SAS, specifically Chain-of-Thought with Self-Consistency (CoT-SC). Across traditional reasoning datasets and tasks with interactive multi-step workflows (e.g., BrowseComp-Plus), we demonstrate that automatic MAS consistently underperform CoT-SC despite being up to 10x more expensive. To isolate these failures from limitations inherent to task structure, we introduce a diagnostic synthetic dataset tailored for MAS featuring explicit task decomposition, context separation and parallelization potential. We show that expert-architected MAS consistently outperforms automatically generated architectures in both raw performance and cost-efficiency on this dataset, demonstrating that existing evaluation frameworks mask critical architectural gaps and inefficiencies of complex MAS by failing to account for the marginal utility of increased computational cost. Critically, systematic deconstruction of the generated MAS architectures reveals that current automated design paradigms produce architectural bloat that prioritizes superficial complexity which does not translate into functional utility, exposing a fundamental misalignment with multi-agent principles.

</details>


### [282] [Otters++: A Time-to-first-spike Based Energy Efficient Optical Spiking Transformer](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13016)
*Zhanglu Yan,Jiayi Mao,Kaiwen Tang,Fanfan Li,Gang Pan,Tao Luo,Bowen Zhu,Qianhui Liu,Weng-Fai Wong*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Otters++ leverages intrinsic optoelectronic signal decay to perform TTFS computation in spiking Transformers, removing explicit digital decay. It maps to a layer-wise equivalence with a quantized neural network, enabling hybrid training (device-faithful forward pass, QNN backward pass) with distillation and noise modeling, achieving strong GLUE performance with energy advantages over prior TTFS baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: TTFS SNNs offer potentially ultra-low-energy inference since neurons spike at most once, but practical TTFS requires costly decay terms. The authors turn hardware decay, a natural signal loss, into the core computation, and seek scalable, trainable Transformer models that retain energy benefits.

Method: 1) Use measured decay in a custom In2O3 optoelectronic synapse to realize the TTFS temporal term directly. 2) Establish a layer-wise functional equivalence between the Otters++ SNN and a quantized neural network (QNN). 3) Develop a hybrid training regime: forward pass uses device-faithful SNN computation; backward pass uses straight-through gradients through the equivalent QNN path; incorporate model distillation. 4) Model device noise by sampling run-to-run variation; refine system-level energy model for device sharing and multi-hop communication.

Result: On GLUE, Otters++ achieves an average score of 84.17% while maintaining a clear energy advantage over prior spiking Transformer baselines.

Conclusion: Physically grounded TTFS computing via Otters++ can be energy-efficient, trainable, and robust under realistic hardware effects, and the layer-wise equivalence to QNNs enables effective training without differentiating through discrete spikes.

Abstract: Spiking neural networks (SNNs) are promising for energy-efficient inference, and time-to-first-spike (TTFS) coding is especially attractive because each neuron fires at most once. In practice, however, this benefit is often reduced by the cost of computing a temporal decay term and multiplying it by the synaptic weight. We address this issue by turning a physical hardware "bug," the natural signal decay in optoelectronic devices, into the main computation of TTFS, named Otters++. Specifically, we use the measured decay of a custom In$_2$O$_3$ optoelectronic synapse to directly realize the TTFS temporal term, removing the need for explicit digital decay computation. To scale this idea to Transformer models, we establish a layer-wise functional equivalence between the Otters++ and a quantized neural network (QNN), and develop a hybrid training method that uses device-faithful SNN computation in the forward pass and QNN straight-through gradients through the equivalent QNN path in the backward pass, together with model distillation. This avoids differentiation through discrete first-spike events and reduces the over-sparsity problem in direct TTFS-SNN training. We further make training aware of measured device noise by sampling run-to-run variation, and refine the system-level energy model by accounting for device sharing and multi-hop communication. On GLUE dataset, Otters++ improves the average score to 84.17\% while maintaining a clear energy advantage over prior spiking Transformer baselines. These results show that physically grounded TTFS computing can be efficient, trainable, and robust under realistic hardware effects.

</details>


### [283] [SciR: A Controllable Benchmark for Scientific Reasoning in LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13020)
*Pierre Beckmann,Marco Valentino,Andre Freitas*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SciR is a controllable, multi-paradigm scientific reasoning benchmark (deduction, induction, causal abduction) with parametric extraction and inference difficulty, rendering formal tasks into domain-specific scientific discourse; tested on six models, showing difficulty axes degrade performance and revealing extraction/inference profiles.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a need to reliably evaluate LLMs on scientific reasoning across multiple paradigms. Existing benchmarks either rely on costly human annotations with limited ground truth or synthetic tasks that don’t resemble real scientific documents. SciR provides grounded, verifiable tasks and controllable rendering to bridge this gap.

Method: Tasks are generated from formal objects (deduction tree, inductive rule hypothesis, causal graph) to guarantee verifiable answers, then rendered into multi-document scientific discourse via per-track domain-tuned genres. Two independent difficulty axes are varied: extraction difficulty (how hard it is to extract key information) and principled inference difficulty (how hard the inference itself is). Six models are evaluated; results show both axes reduce performance and interact. Rendering degrades neurosymbolic pipelines; inference-extraction profiles emerge per model (e.g., deepseek-r1 excels on inference relative to non-reasoning instruct models). SciR is claimed to be the first benchmark with parametric control on both extraction and inference difficulty.

Result: Across all models, increasing extraction or inference difficulty degrades performance, with effects compounding. Rendering hurts neurosymbolic pipelines that rely on a solver. Different models show distinct extraction-vs-inference profiles; reasoning models like deepseek-r1 outperform non-reasoning instruction models on inference tasks.

Conclusion: SciR offers a parametric, multi-paradigm scientific-reasoning benchmark with independent control over extraction and inference difficulty, enabling more nuanced evaluation of LLMs on scientific reasoning.

Abstract: Three paradigmatic forms of inference recur across scientific reasoning: deduction, induction, and causal abduction. Reliably evaluating LLMs on these in scientific settings is currently out of reach: scientific benchmarks built on human annotations are costly and lack mechanistic ground truth, while synthetic logical-reasoning benchmarks do not resemble real scientific documents. We introduce SciR, a benchmark that combines multi-paradigm reasoning with controllable scientific rendering, anchored on three paradigmatic scientific problems. Tasks are generated from formal objects (deduction tree, inductive rule hypothesis, causal graph) to guarantee verifiable answers, then rendered into multi-document scientific discourse via per-track domain-tuned genres. The construction lets us independently vary two difficulty axes: how hard it is to extract the key information needed for inference, and how hard the principled inference itself is. We test six models. Both axes hurt every model, and their effects compound. The rendering even hurts neurosymbolic pipelines, which hand inference to a verified solver. The two axes yield a per-model extraction-vs-inference profile: for instance, reasoning models like deepseek-r1 mostly surpass non-reasoning instruct models on the inference axis. To our knowledge, SciR is the first multi-paradigm scientific-reasoning benchmark with parametric control on both extraction and inference difficulty.

</details>


### [284] [Nous: An Attempt to Extract and Inject the Cognition Behind Prediction-Market Behavior](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13038)
*Haowei Qian*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Human cognitive diversity can be partially captured from real trading behavior, but prompting LLM agents with that profile does not meaningfully diversify forecasts or reduce ensemble error; deeper interventions beyond prompts are needed.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Investigate whether human cognitive diversity can mitigate cognitive monoculture in LLM-based prediction/decision systems by extracting behavioral profiles from real agents and transferring them to models.

Method: Extract an eight-dimension behavioral profile from 100 Polymarket wallets; assess temporal stability (split-half ICC, bootstrap CI); test identifiability of wallets; correlate some dimensions with future profits; inject structured prompts encoding the profile into LLM agents; evaluate effects on ensemble metrics (error correlation, Brier score) across varying prompts/settings; analyze whether prompts preserve or transmit profile structure.

Result: Extraction shows partial stability: 8 of 14 parameters stable (ICC ≥0.5; bootstrap lower bound >0.3); contrarian score ICC ~0.9; wallets identifiable above chance (top-1 17–22% vs 1%); two of four dimensions correlate with future realized profit but not robust under behavioral controls. Prompt-level injection yields no measurable transmission: no advantage in semantic embedding over controls; no improvement in ensemble diversity or Brier score; effects robust to changes in sampling temperature, profile diversity, and question difficulty. Prompt analysis reveals the translator produces near-uniform prompts; structure spread does not track profile spread.

Conclusion: Prompt-level cures for cognitive monoculture are limited; the problem is measurable and persists despite injection efforts, advocating deeper, below-the-prompt interventions (e.g., fine-tuning, activation steering) to inject human cognitive diversity into LLM-based decision systems.

Abstract: As LLM agents proliferate in prediction markets and collective decision-making, they risk a cognitive monoculture: agents built on shared foundation models produce correlated forecasts, and recent measurement finds frontier-model errors correlated at r ~ 0.77. We ask whether human cognitive diversity can be recovered from behavior and transferred to LLM agents. Nous extracts a structured eight-dimension behavioral profile from real Polymarket trading activity and injects it into agents through prompts. Our central finding is a dissociation between the two halves of that pipeline. Extraction works, partially: across 100 wallets, 8 of 14 parameters are temporally stable (split-half ICC >= 0.5, bootstrap CI lower bound > 0.3; contrarian score reaches ICC ~ 0.9); wallets are identifiable from their profiles well above chance (top-1 retrieval 17-22% vs. 1% chance); and two of four pre-specified dimensions rank-correlate with future realized profit out-of-sample, though the correlations do not survive behavioral-confound controls. Prompt-level injection does not measurably transmit it: on a semantic embedding metric, structured injection shows no significant advantage over a length-matched control on any model, and the diversity it induces neither reduces ensemble error correlation nor improves Brier score -- a null that persists across exploratory checks on sampling temperature, profile diversity, and question difficulty. Measuring the prompts themselves locates the compression before the model: the structure-to-narrative translator emits near-uniform prompts whose spread does not track profile spread. We position Nous as measuring the cognitive-monoculture problem and the limits of a prompt-level remedy, motivating deeper, below-the-prompt injection (fine-tuning, activation steering). Code, frozen profiles, prompts, and model outputs: https://github.com/WillChienT/nous-paper

</details>


### [285] [Augmentation techniques for video surveillance in the visible and thermal spectral range](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13042)
*Vanessa Buhrmester,Ann-Kristin Grosselfinger,David Munch,Michael Arens*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Multispectral CNN-based object detection using co-collected visible and long-wave infrared data is studied, focusing on how augmentation strategies affect CNN decision-making and robustness across spectral modalities with limited thermal IR data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the scarcity of thermal infrared datasets and to improve detection by leveraging visible data, while understanding how CNNs learn from multispectral inputs and how sensor differences influence performance.

Method: Propose and evaluate a multispectral CNN-based detector and investigate the suitability and robustness of different data augmentation techniques for multispectral input (visible + infrared) to study CNN decision processes.

Result: The abstract presents aims and methodological exploration but does not report concrete empirical results or quantitative findings.

Conclusion: The work seeks to provide deeper insights into CNN decision mechanisms when fusing visible and infrared data, highlighting augmentation as a factor in robustness, though no definitive conclusions are stated in the abstract.

Abstract: In intelligent video surveillance, cameras record image sequences during day and night. Commonly, this demands different sensors. To achieve a better performance it is not unusual to combine them. We focus on the case that a long-wave infrared camera records continuously and in addition to this, another camera records in the visible spectral range during daytime and an intelligent algorithm supervises the picked up imagery. More accurate, our task is multispectral CNN-based object detection. At first glance, images originating from the visible spectral range differ between thermal infrared ones in the presence of color and distinct texture information on the one hand and in not containing information about thermal radiation that emits from objects on the other hand. Although color can provide valuable information for classification tasks, effects such as varying illumination and specialties of different sensors still represent significant problems. Anyway, obtaining sufficient and practical thermal infrared datasets for training a deep neural network poses still a challenge. That is the reason why training with the help of data from the visible spectral range could be advantageous, particularly if the data, which has to be evaluated contains both visible and infrared data. However, there is no clear evidence of how strongly variations in thermal radiation, shape, or color information influence classification accuracy. To gain deeper insight into how Convolutional Neural Networks make decisions and what they learn from different sensor input data, we investigate the suitability and robustness of different augmentation techniques...

</details>


### [286] [AAbAAC: An Annotated Corpus for Autoimmunity Information Extraction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13051)
*Fabien Maury,Solène Grosdidier,Maud de Dieuleveult,Adrien Coulet*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AAbAAC provides a small, manually annotated corpus for autoimmunity to improve NER; shows performance gains after finetuning, highlighting the value of domain-specific data for information extraction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the performance gap of generalist models in specialized biomedical domains, specifically autoimmunity, by creating a domain-focused annotated corpus for entity and relation extraction.

Method: Constructed AAbAAC from 115 PubMed abstracts with manual annotations of entities and relationships relevant to autoimmune diseases, autoantibodies, their targets, locations, and clinical signs; evaluated various NER methods and fine-tuned NER models on the corpus.

Result: Demonstrated utility of the corpus for information extraction in autoimmunity; finetuned NER models achieved expected improvements, validating the value of small-scale, domain-specific annotation.

Conclusion: The corpus supports computational studies in autoimmunity and enhances NER performance in a specialized domain; the dataset is publicly available, encouraging replication and further annotation efforts.

Abstract: Despite advances in information extraction driven by deep learning and large language models, performance gaps remain in highly specialized biomedical fields, where domainspecific complexity poses challenges for generalist models. In this work, we focus on the domain of autoimmunity, where the main entities of interest are autoimmune diseases, autoantibodies (i.e., molecules that may mark or cause these diseases), their molecular targets, their location in the body, and their associated clinical signs. Herein, we present AAbAAC (AutoAntibodies and Autoimmunity Annotated Corpus), a corpus of 115 abstracts selected from PubMed, where we manually annotated entities and their relationships. First, AAbAAC was used to evaluate several methods on the task of named entity recognition (NER), and secondly, to fine-tune NER models. Our study demonstrates the utility of AAbAAC for information extraction in the domain of autoimmunity, showing expected improvement in NER performance after finetuning. This illustrates the value of small-scale annotation efforts for specialized domains and contributes to the computational study of autoimmunity. The AAbAAC corpus is available at https://github.com/f-maury/AAbAAC.

</details>


### [287] [Rethinking RAG in Long Videos: What to Retrieve and How to Use It?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13141)
*Yuho Lee,Jisu Shin,Nicole Hee-Yeon Kim,Jihwan Bang,Juntae Lee,Kyuwoong Hwang,Fatih Porikli,Hwanjun Song*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces V-RAGBench and CARVE to advance VideoRAG by enabling faithful, chunk-level retrieval configuration across modalities; CARVE searches over parallel configurations and adaptively selects per-chunk configurations during generation, improving over baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current VideoRAG evaluation often ignores retrieval errors and restricts queries to a single modality-granularity configuration, hindering faithful assessment and flexible retrieval.

Method: V-RAGBench provides a <query, evidence chunk, answer> triplet benchmark for decoupled evaluation of retrieval and generation. CARVE runs parallel retrievers across multiple configurations and uses chunk-adaptive reranking to select a winning configuration per chunk; the generator then uses the per-chunk winning configuration, producing interleaved evidence across stages.

Result: CARVE outperforms eight recent VideoRAG baselines, leveraging chunk-level configuration interleaving to reveal and utilize varying configurations per chunk rather than a single configuration per query.

Conclusion: Benchmarking with V-RAGBench and the chunk-adaptive CARVE approach enable faithful evaluation and improved VideoRAG performance by aligning retrieval configurations with per-chunk content and facilitating inter-stage propagation of decisions.

Abstract: Retrieval-augmented generation is moving beyond text into long, egocentric video, where systems must select query-relevant chunks across multiple modalities and temporal granularities. Yet progress in VideoRAG is limited by two gaps: existing benchmarks allow queries to be answered without the video, obscuring retrieval errors, and prior methods apply a single modality-granularity configuration per query, ignoring chunk-level variability. We address both by introducing V-RAGBench, a benchmark of $\langle$query, evidence chunk, answer$\rangle$ triplets that enables faithful, decoupled evaluation of retrieval and generation, and CARVE, a simple method that runs parallel retrievers across configurations and employs chunk-adaptive reranking to identify the winning configuration for each chunk. Each chunk then enters the generator under its winning configuration selected during retrieval, yielding an interleaved evidence form where the chunk-level decision propagates across both stages. CARVE outperforms eight recent VideoRAG baselines, with the chunks supplied to the generator interleaving multiple configurations rather than sharing a single one, a behavior unattainable by query-level methods.

</details>


### [288] [TerraBench: Can Agents Reason Over Heterogeneous Earth-System Data?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13148)
*Dat Tien Nguyen,Thao Nguyen,Fadillah Adamsyah Maani,Huy M. Le,Muhammad Umer Sheikh,Numan Saeed,Muhammad Haris Khan,Salman Khan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: TerraBench provides a unified, executable benchmark for grounded Earth-science reasoning, combining LLM planning with domain tools to operate on heterogeneous Earth data and evaluate process-level tool use alongside numeric accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the gap between language-based reasoning by LLMs and high-dimensional Earth data manipulation by software tools; enable interactive reasoning and provenance-aware evaluation in Earth science workflows.

Method: Introduce TerraBench and TerraAgent, a ReAct-style framework interleaving reasoning, tool calls, and observations to connect LLM planning with tools for retrieval, geospatial processing, simulation, and artifact-backed computation. Benchmark covers Earth observation imagery, gridded data, GIS reasoning, and simulation within a single executable interface; includes three tracks and 403 tasks across eight domains, with 24,500 verified execution steps.

Result: Demonstrates that effective Earth-science agents must coordinate heterogeneous workflows, carefully parameterize tools, and preserve artifact provenance; benchmarks quantify both tool-use and numerical tolerance aspects.

Conclusion: TerraBench advances grounded Earth-science reasoning by integrating process-level tool use with numeric scoring, highlighting necessary capabilities beyond mere tool access and enabling more reliable autonomous scientific workflows.

Abstract: Climate and environmental decision-making increasingly requires reasoning across heterogeneous inputs, including gridded physical data, satellite imagery, geospatial context, and simulator outputs. Weather and climate foundation models can forecast well, but do not reason interactively in language, while large language models (LLMs) reason in language but cannot operate directly on high-dimensional Earth-system data. As a result, real scientific workflows in Earth-science remain underserved. We introduce TerraBench, a benchmark for grounded Earth-science reasoning, built on TerraAgent, a ReAct-style executable framework that interleaves reasoning, tool calls, and observations to couple LLM planning with scientific tools for environmental retrieval, geospatial processing, simulation, and artifact-backed computation. TerraBench unifies analysis of Earth observation imagery, gridded data, GIS reasoning and simulation in a single executable interface, whereas prior benchmarks isolate these capabilities into narrow individual tasks. It is also the first in this space to pair process-level tool-use metrics with tolerance-aware numeric scoring. The benchmark comprises 403 extensive agentic tasks across three tracks (Fundamentals, Simulator-Grounded, and Document-Grounded Verification) and eight application domains with 24,500 verified execution steps. These results indicate that reliable Earth-science agents must go beyond tool access to coordinate heterogeneous workflows, parameterize tools precisely, and preserve artifact provenance.

</details>


### [289] [Mental-R1: Aligning LLM Reasoning for Mental Health Assessment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13176)
*Xin Wang,Boyan Gao,Yibo Yang,David A. Clifton*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CRPO is a reinforcement learning framework for mental health assessment that uses stage-wise entropy regularization and cognitive appraisal-inspired reasoning stages to improve weighted F1 by 10.4 points over a strong RL baseline; it aims for better reasoning and interpretability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mental health assessment with large language models is hampered by misalignment between general-purpose post-training methods and human cognitive assessment processes; existing methods may yield unreliable reasoning outcomes.

Method: Extend group relative policy optimization with stage-dependent uncertainty modeling via stage-wise entropy regularization; formalize cognitive reasoning stages based on cognitive appraisal theory to guide theory-grounded interpretable inference; evaluate on 8 mental health datasets; demonstrate a model (Mental-R1) with favorable comparisons.

Result: Average improvement of 10.4 percentage points in weighted F1-score over the best reinforcement learning baseline; Mental-R1 outperforms existing large language models on reasoning-intensive cases, suggesting enhanced reasoning capabilities for mental health assessment.

Conclusion: CRPO yields theory-grounded, interpretable, staged reasoning for mental health assessment. Stage-wise uncertainty modeling fosters exploration in early reasoning and confident decisions later, improving performance and suggesting potential for stronger reasoning in domain-specific LLMs.

Abstract: Mental health problems such as anxiety, depression, and suicide remain urgent global challenges, where timely and accurate assessment is critical for effective intervention. Recently, large language models have been explored for mental health assessment. However, existing general-purpose post-training methods do not align with the cognitive processes of human assessment, which may lead to unreliable reasoning outcomes. To bridge this gap, we propose Cognitive Relative Policy Optimization (CRPO), a reinforcement learning framework tailored for the mental health domain. CRPO extends group relative policy optimization by integrating stage-dependent uncertainty modeling into the policy optimization process. Specifically, we introduce a stage-wise entropy regularization mechanism that encourages broad exploration in early reasoning phases and progressively enforces confident decision-making in later stages, mimicking the human cognitive shift from uncertainty to certainty. In addition, inspired by cognitive appraisal theory, we formalize cognitive reasoning stages, thereby guiding theory-grounded interpretable inference. Experiments on 8 mental health datasets show that CRPO achieves an average improvement of 10.4 percentage points in weighted F1-score over the best reinforcement learning baseline. Furthermore, the CRPO-trained model Mental-R1 demonstrates clear advantages compared with existing large language models on reasoning-intensive cases, suggesting that CRPO enhances reasoning capabilities for mental health assessment.

</details>


### [290] [Reasoning for Mobile User Experience with Multimodal LLMs: Task, Benchmark, and Approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13192)
*Ruichao Mao,Zhou Fang,Teng Guo,Hao Yang,Yaping Li,Shaohua Peng,Maji Huang,Xiaoyu Lin,Shuoyang Liu,Xuepeng Li,Yuyu Zhang,Hai Rao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: New UXBench benchmark for UI-based reasoning with 2000 VQA samples; demonstrates MLLMs' limitations and presents UI-UX model with reward routing achieving SOTA.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address immaturity in evaluating UX from UI screenshots; provide a standardized benchmark and an improved MLLM to drive progress in UI-based reasoning.

Method: Construct UXBench with 2,000 real-world UI screenshot VQA samples across 8 tasks targeting layout relationships, visual hierarchy, and content consistency; evaluate mainstream MLLMs; introduce UI-UX based on Qwen3-VL-4B-Thinking with reinforcement learning that uses a reward routing mechanism and an asymmetric transition reward; assess performance and latency.

Result: Mainstream MLLMs show limited UI-based reasoning capability; UI-UX achieves state-of-the-art on UXBench with 0.7963 accuracy, outperforming Claude-4.5-Sonnet (0.6550); demonstrates strong generalization and low inference latency.

Conclusion: UX evaluation from UI screenshots is viable but still challenging; the proposed UI-UX framework and UXBench benchmark meaningfully advance the evaluation and capability of UI-focused MLLMs, signaling a path toward more reliable GUI agents and design-to-code tools.

Abstract: User experience (UX) centered on usability, perceived consistency, and functional clarity is fundamental to real-world user interfaces (UI). The application of
  multimodal large language models (MLLMs) in the field of user interfaces is evolving rapidly, such as visual element grounding, graphical user interface (GUI)
  agents, and design-to-code generation. However, research efforts on evaluating UX based on UI screenshots are still immature. To address this, we propose UXBench,
  a novel multimodal benchmark consisting of 2,000 VQA data samples designed to assess MLLMs' ability to perform UI-based reasoning. UXBench includes 8 tasks based
  on real-world UI screenshots that require fine-grained diagnosis of UX issues across layout relationships, visual hierarchy, and content consistency. Our
  extensive evaluation of mainstream MLLMs shows that they remain fundamentally limited in their capacity for UI-based reasoning. The results underscore the need
  for further advancements in this area. To bridge this gap, we propose UI-UX, an MLLM based on Qwen3-VL-4B-Thinking foundation model and enhanced via reinforcement
  learning with two key innovations: a reward routing mechanism that dynamically balances perceptual understanding and logical reasoning during inference, and an
  asymmetric transition reward that suppresses redundant or insufficient reasoning steps. Experiments demonstrate that UI-UX achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA)
  performance on UXBench, attaining an accuracy of 0.7963 -- surpassing Claude-4.5-Sonnet's 0.6550 -- while exhibiting strong generalization across diverse UI tasks
  and maintaining low inference latency.

</details>


### [291] [Under What Conditions Can a Machine Become Genuinely Creative?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13196)
*Yong Zeng*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Genuine machine creativity is a structural transformation of incomplete situations via recursive intervention dynamics, operationalized by ten requirements under three Designics laws (perception, conflict, capability); ethics is integral, not optional.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To define and assess machine creativity beyond output novelty or performance, preserving human agency within shared cognitive environments and guiding the design of adaptive, ethical AI systems.

Method: Develop a requirement framework derived from Designics: environment representation, scoped perception, conflict identification, intervention capability, consequence observation, knowledge/environment update, rescoping, local-to-global unfolding, value-based scoping, and human-AI co-living; organize under three laws (perception, conflict, capability); illustrate with cyber-physical/biological case studies; analyze open-ended systems, automated discovery, self-modifying agents, foundation models, and agentic workflows as pressure cases; argue for internalization of ethics within creativity.

Result: Ten requirements operationalize genuine machine creativity and are cohesively structured around three Designics laws; demonstrations suggest computational tractability; open-ended systems and related frameworks are powerful but insufficient for genuine creativity on their own; ethics is embedded in the creative process rather than external oversight.

Conclusion: Proactive AI ethics and value-based scoping are essential to genuine machine creativity, shaping how machines perceive, identify conflicts, intervene, observe, and adapt in pursuit of co-living with humans; provides a normative and architectural blueprint for creative AI systems.

Abstract: Recent AI systems can generate texts, software architectures, hypotheses, designs, and scientific workflows that appear creative. This paper asks under what conditions a machine can become genuinely creative, and how human agency can be preserved within shared cognitive and creative environments. It develops a requirement framework derived from Designics, the science of meaning-bearing intentional change. The paper argues that genuine machine creativity should not be defined by output novelty, current performance, or transient architecture alone. Instead, creativity is understood as the structural transformation of incomplete situations through recursive intervention dynamics. On this view, it depends on ten requirements: environment representation, scoped perception, conflict identification, intervention capability, consequence observation, knowledge and environment update, rescoping, local-to-global unfolding, value-based scoping, and human-AI co-living. These are organized through the three laws of Designics: perception, conflict, and capability. The paper illustrates the computational tractability of these requirements through selected cyber-physical and cyber-biological studies, including recursive element extraction, autonomous mesh generation, and neurophysiological and workload analysis. It then treats open-ended systems, automated discovery frameworks, self-modifying agents, foundation models, and agentic workflows as pressure cases: they demonstrate powerful generative means but do not by themselves establish genuine machine creativity. Finally, the paper argues that proactive AI ethics is internal to genuine machine creativity rather than an after-the-fact filter. Value-based scoping and human-AI co-living must shape how creative machines perceive environments, identify conflicts, select interventions, observe consequences, update knowledge, and rescope future action.

</details>


### [292] [ARMOR-MAD: Adaptive Routing for Heterogeneous Multi-Agent Debate in Large Language Model Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13197)
*Fuqiang Niu,Bowen Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ARMOR-MAD is a training-free heterogeneous multi-agent debate framework that uses conditional computation via PAR, EASE, and SOD to improve reasoning accuracy and efficiency by deciding when to debate, when to stop, and how to down-weight outliers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To reduce wasted computation and mitigate correlated errors in fixed-round MAD by leveraging genuine model heterogeneity and agreement-based control.

Method: Three components: (1) Pre-debate Agreement Routing (PAR) decides if Round-0 answers require debate; (2) Early Agreement Stopping Evaluator (EASE) stops debate after convergence; (3) Semantic Outlier Detection (SOD) down-weights abnormal final answers during aggregation. The framework is training-free and utilizes a pool of heterogeneous models.

Result: ARMOR-MAD consistently improves over fixed-round heterogeneous debate across multiple benchmarks (MATH Level 5, GSM8K, MMLU, MMLU-Pro), achieving accuracies of 65.5%, 96.5%, 90.0%, and 81.5% respectively.

Conclusion: Genuine model heterogeneity paired with agreement-based control can make multi-agent debate more accurate and efficient by reducing unnecessary debate and down-weighting aberrant outputs.

Abstract: Multi-agent debate (MAD) can improve large language model reasoning, but fixed debate pipelines often waste computation and can amplify correlated errors among similar agents. We propose ARMOR-MAD, a training-free heterogeneous MAD framework that treats debate as conditional computation. ARMOR-MAD combines three components: Pre-debate Agreement Routing (PAR) decides whether independently generated Round-0 answers require debate; Early Agreement Stopping Evaluator (EASE) stops debate after convergence; and Semantic Outlier Detection (SOD) down-weights abnormal final answers during aggregation. Across MATH Level 5, GSM8K, MMLU, and MMLU-Pro, ARMOR-MAD consistently improves over fixed-round heterogeneous debate with the same model pool, reaching 65.5\%, 96.5\%, 90.0\%, and 81.5\% accuracy, respectively. The results suggest that genuine model heterogeneity and agreement-based control are both important for making MAD more accurate and efficient.

</details>


### [293] [A Minimal Model of Bounded Trade-Off Screening in Multi-Attribute Choice](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13201)
*Manisha Dubey,Anirban Sarkar,Subramanian Ramamoorthy*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A bounded trade-off screening model for multi-attribute choice introduces a trade-off tolerance parameter; simulations show non-compensatory, context-dependent behavior distinct from standard utility models, with testable predictions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Classical models assume full compensatory utility, yet empirical evidence shows people reject options with poor performance on critical attributes; a mechanism that screens attribute imbalances is needed.

Method: Develops a bounded trade-off screening framework where a screening process evaluates gains versus losses across attributes and introduces a trade-off tolerance parameter; uses simulations to illustrate the resulting decision patterns.

Result: Simulations yield preference patterns that differ from utility-based models and capture context-dependent variation in trade-off behavior; the approach is a plausible computational mechanism and generates testable behavioral predictions.

Conclusion: Bounded trade-off screening is a plausible mechanism for multi-attribute choice and offers testable predictions for future behavioral studies.

Abstract: Human decision-making often involves choosing between multi-attribute alternatives, yet classical models assume fully compensatory utility aggregation despite evidence that people reject options with poor performance on critical attributes. We propose a bounded trade-off reasoning framework in which decisions are governed by a screening process that evaluates the balance between gains and losses across attributes. The model introduces a trade-off tolerance parameter that controls acceptable imbalance and can vary across contexts. Through simulation, we show that this mechanism produces preference patterns that differ from standard utility-based models and captures context-dependent variation in trade-off behavior. These results establish bounded trade-off screening as a plausible computational mechanism for multi-attribute choice and generate testable predictions for future behavioral studies.

</details>


### [294] [Hallucination in Medical Imaging AI: A Cross-Modality Analytical Framework for Taxonomy, Detection, and Mitigation under Regulatory Constraints](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13211)
*Omar Alshahrani,Muzammil Behzad*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Cross-modality taxonomy and mitigation of AI hallucination in medical imaging; general-purpose foundation models can outperform medical-specialized models on hallucination benchmarks; regulatory alignment with FDA lifecycle is essential.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Hallucination is a critical failure mode with clinically plausible yet incorrect outputs, threatening biopsy decisions, staging, and treatment planning; a unified taxonomic framework across modalities is lacking, and regulatory oversight requires translation into lifecycle processes.

Method: Synthesis of peer-reviewed studies, benchmark datasets, and FDA regulatory guidance across five imaging modalities to analyze hallucination taxonomy, etiology, detection, and mitigation; evaluation of model types (general-purpose vs medical-specialized) and mitigation strategies; mapping findings to FDA Total Product Lifecycle and Predetermined Change Control Plan.

Result: Three taxonomic frameworks together cover the imaging pipeline more comprehensively than any single framework; general-purpose foundation models outperform medical-specialized models on hallucination benchmarks, suggesting overfitting risks from narrow fine-tuning; a high percentage of AI-generated flags require expert radiologist correction before clinical use; mitigation strategies (physics-informed constraints, Chain-of-Thought prompting, and human-in-the-loop safeguards) are effective when combined; findings are mapped to FDA lifecycle frameworks, treating hallucination management as a continuous lifecycle obligation.

Conclusion: Hallucination management should be treated as a lifecycle obligation rather than a pre-deployment checklist; radiologist oversight remains essential; a multi-faceted mitigation strategy, aligned with FDA Total Product Lifecycle and Predetermined Change Control Plan, is recommended.

Abstract: AI systems are being deployed across medical imaging faster than their failure modes are understood. At this point in time, the failure of greatest clinical concern is hallucination: clinically plausible but factually incorrect outputs, including fabricated anatomical structures, missed findings, incorrect laterality, and invented measurements in generated reports, with direct consequences, for example, for biopsy decisions, staging, and treatment planning. This structured narrative synthesizes peer-reviewed studies, benchmark datasets, and FDA regulatory guidance across five imaging modalities to produce a cross-modality analysis of hallucination taxonomy, etiology, detection, and mitigation. Specifically, we address three questions in this study: (1) how can existing taxonomies be unified across modalities?, (2) how do medical-specialized foundation models hallucinate less than general-purpose ones?, and (3) which mitigation strategies are effective and compatible with FDA lifecycle oversight? We note that three taxonomic frameworks together cover the imaging pipeline in a way no single framework does alone. We also highlight that general-purpose foundation models outperform medical-specialized models on hallucination-specific benchmarks, indicating that narrow domain fine-tuning can introduce overfitting-induced confabulation. At the same time, the oversight of radiologists remains essential; for instance, a very high percentage of of AI-generated flags required expert correction before clinical use. Physics-informed architectural constraints, Chain-of-Thought prompting, and human-in-the-loop safeguards each address different failure modes and is effective when combined. All findings are mapped to the FDA's Total Product Lifecycle and Predetermined Change Control Plan frameworks, which treat hallucination management as a lifecycle obligation rather than a pre-deployment checklist.

</details>


### [295] [LLM-as-an-Investigator: Evidence-First Reasoning for Robust Interactive Problem Diagnosis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13220)
*Fabrizio Marozzo,Pietro Liò*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces LLM-as-an-Investigator: an evidence-first agent that mitigates user-driven sycophancy by iteratively testing hypotheses with clarifying questions; evaluated via a three-agent benchmark showing improved diagnostic accuracy over baselines and reduced user-induced bias.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs can prematurely align with incomplete or plausible user-provided hypotheses, leading to faulty solutions. Need robust problem diagnosis that tests explanations rather than accepting them at face value, especially in technical domains.

Method: Proposes Solution Investigator Agent that (1) estimates problem ambiguity, (2) generates candidate hypotheses, (3) asks targeted clarification questions, (4) updates hypothesis probabilities after each response. The agent withholds immediate answers to pursue evidence-first investigation. Evaluation uses a three-agent pipeline: Problem-Solution Extractor Agent converts solved threads into cases; Ground-Truth Evaluator Agent simulates the user while hiding the solution; tested assistant attempts to recover the solution via dialogue. Benchmarks drawn from solved mechanical, electrical, and hydraulic threads; compares standard, reasoning-oriented, and investigator-based models across LLM backbones.

Result: The investigator-based model achieves higher diagnostic accuracy than direct prompting and reasoning-only baselines. It also reduces user-induced conversational bias by requiring evidence collection before committing to a solution; performance is consistent across multiple LLM backbones.

Conclusion: An evidence-first, investigator-based framework can improve robustness in technical problem solving by mitigating user-driven sycophancy and enhancing hypothesis testing, suggesting a viable path for safer, more reliable LLM-assisted diagnostics.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used as interactive assistants for technical problem solving. However, when users provide incomplete descriptions or plausible but unverified explanations, LLMs may prematurely align with these assumptions and propose solutions before collecting sufficient evidence. We refer to this behavior as user-driven sycophancy: the tendency of an LLM to reinforce a user-provided hypothesis instead of testing alternative explanations. This paper introduces LLM-as-an-Investigator, an evidence-first agentic AI methodology for robust problem diagnosis. The approach is implemented through a Solution Investigator Agent, which estimates the ambiguity of an initial problem description, generates candidate hypotheses, asks targeted clarification questions, and updates hypothesis probabilities after each answer. Rather than producing an immediate response, the agent continues the investigation until the evidence makes one candidate explanation stronger than the alternatives. To evaluate the approach, we build a benchmark from solved technical forum threads in mechanical, electrical, and hydraulic domains. We use a three-agent evaluation pipeline in which a Problem-Solution Extractor Agent converts solved threads into structured cases, a Ground-Truth Evaluator Agent simulates the user while hiding the known solution, and the tested assistant attempts to recover the solution through dialogue. The experiments compare standard assistants, reasoning-oriented LLMs, and the proposed investigator-based model across LLM backbones. In addition to diagnostic accuracy, we analyze how standard assistants follow misleading user hypotheses in diagnostic cases. The results show that the proposed approach identifies the problem more accurately than direct prompting and reasoning-only baselines, while its evidence-first protocol helps reduce user-induced conversational bias.

</details>


### [296] [Brick: Spatial Capability Routing for the Mixture-of-Models (MoM) Paradigm](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13241)
*Francesco Massa,Marco Cristofanilli*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Brick is a multimodal router that scores models on six capabilities and per-query difficulty, using a cost-aware geometric rule and a tunable preference knob to trade quality for cost; it improves accuracy and reduces latency and cost compared to strongest-model routing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM routers rely on surface features and cost-inefficient frontier models; at production scale even small per-request cost savings matter; need to account for within-domain variance to improve success rates.

Method: Score models on six capability dimensions; estimate per-query difficulty; combine via a cost-penalized geometric dispatch rule; expose a continuous preference knob to switch between max quality and max saving; report performance on a 5,504-query benchmark.

Result: Max-quality accuracy 76.98% (vs 75.02% best single) on the benchmark; neutral profile 74.11% accuracy with 4.71x cost savings; min-cost 22.15x cost reduction with 11.85-point accuracy loss; median latency 51.2s -> 22.8s; outperforms tested routers.

Conclusion: A multiparameter, cost-aware routing strategy can achieve higher accuracy than the strongest model while significantly reducing cost and latency, with tunable trade-offs at deployment.

Abstract: Defining query difficulty is one of the hardest problems in deployment engineering. Existing LLM routers rely on surface features such as domain labels, keywords, and token count, ignoring the within-domain variance that actually determines model success. Frontier models cost ten to one hundred times more than local open-weight models, so at production scale even small per-request savings become a direct cloud-bill lever. We present Brick, a multimodal router that scores each model on six capability dimensions, combines this with a per-query difficulty estimate, and dispatches via a cost-penalized geometric rule. A continuous preference knob lets operators slide between max-quality and max-saving profiles at deploy time. On a benchmark of 5,504 queries, Brick at max-quality reaches 76.98% accuracy, beating the best single model (75.02%) and all tested routers. At a neutral cost-quality profile, Brick achieves 74.11% accuracy at 4.71x lower cost than always using the strongest model. At min-cost, it cuts cost 22.15x with 11.85 points accuracy loss. Median latency drops from 51.2s to 22.8s.

</details>


### [297] [EPIG: Emotion-Based Prompting for Personalised Image Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13247)
*Emna Othmen,Mohamed Yassine Landolsi,Lotfi Ben Romdhane*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: EPIG enriches emotion cues at prompt time to improve emotional expressiveness (valence-arousal) in text-to-image diffusion without retraining, leading to better arousal control and preserved valence/semantic alignment; strongest effect on prompts with explicit subjects.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Generic prompts inadequately express emotional intent; need psychologically grounded emotion representations and lightweight, user-friendly methods that don't modify the diffusion backbone.

Method: Pre-generation, structured, role-aware prompt enrichment anchored in valence-arousal space; training-free, augmentation of prompts prior to generation; leverages emotion representations to influence outputs, with emphasis on arousal control.

Result: Reduces mean arousal error by 14% vs naive insertion and 12% vs LLM-based expansion; preserves valence alignment and CLIPScore; ablations support; stronger for prompts with explicit subjects (humans, animals).

Conclusion: EPIG provides a practical, effective, and resource-efficient approach to emotion-aware image synthesis via prompts, suitable for personalization and constrained resources.

Abstract: Text-to-image diffusion models have achieved impressive results in synthesizing high-quality images from natural language prompts. However, commonly used prompting strategies remain relatively generic, limiting the model's ability to accurately express emotional intent and nuanced affective attributes. This work proposes EPIG, a method that enhances emotional expressiveness at the prompt level prior to image generation. Grounded in psychologically informed emotion representations (valence-arousal) and leveraging structured, role-aware prompt enrichment, EPIG enriches emotion-related components of prompts without modifying or retraining the image generation backbone. The resulting emotion-aware prompts guide the generative process toward more emotionally coherent visual outputs, with particular effectiveness in controlling arousal. EPIG is lightweight, training-free, and well suited for resource-constrained and personalized image generation scenarios. Experimental results on a benchmark of 10 diverse prompts show that EPIG reduces mean arousal error compared to strong baselines, including naive insertion and LLM-based prompt expansion, with reductions of 14% and 12%, respectively. These improvements are statistically significant. EPIG also preserves valence alignment and semantic consistency, as measured by CLIPScore and supported by ablation studies. The effect is more pronounced on prompts containing explicit subjects such as humans, children, or animals, where the reduction reaches 17%, highlighting the subject-sensitive behavior of the proposed method.

</details>


### [298] [Multi-Field Hybrid Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Maritime Accident Root Cause Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13249)
*Seongjin Kim,Sungil Kim*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes a field-aware retrieval-augmented generation framework for automated maritime root-cause analysis using incident cards from KMST reports; shows significant retrieval and generation improvements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Maritime accident adjudication archives are large, heterogeneous, and labor-intensive to search and synthesize. A structured, retrieval-grounded approach can improve accuracy, consistency, and efficiency in RCA drafting.

Method: Transforms adjudications into structured 'incident cards' with fields Summary, Causes, Disposition and a hierarchical L1/L2 taxonomy. Employs a field-aware hybrid retrieval that fuses sparse and dense rankings via Reciprocal Rank Fusion. Evaluates with ceiling-normalized recall and nDCG on a metadata-derived proxy relevance due to lack of expert labels. Grounding a generator on retrieved precedents using a KMST corpus of 13,329 reports (1971–2025).

Result: Retrieval performance significantly improves (NormRecall@100 from 0.18 to 0.55). Grounded generation enhances RCA quality (LLM-as-a-judge score 3.34 to 3.72). Demonstrates the effectiveness of field-aware RAG for faster precedent search and more consistent, evidence-based RCA drafting.

Conclusion: Field-aware RAG can streamline maritime safety investigations and RCA drafting. The approach is scalable to large adjudication archives and may generalize to other legal-adjudication domains with structured incident representations.

Abstract: Maritime accident adjudication reports contain critical tribunal findings for root cause analysis (RCA), yet retrieving relevant precedents and drafting consistent reports from decades of records remains labor-intensive. This paper proposes a multi-field hybrid retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) framework for automated maritime RCA, utilizing a comprehensive dataset of 13,329 Korea Maritime Safety Tribunal (KMST) reports (1971-2025). We transform raw adjudications into a structured knowledge base of "incident cards", indexing three distinct fields-Summary, Causes, and Disposition-alongside a hierarchical L1/L2 cause taxonomy. Our retrieval strategy employs a field-aware hybrid approach, fusing sparse and dense rankings via Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF). Given the lack of large-scale expert relevance labels, we evaluate retrieval performance using ceiling-normalized recall and nDCG based on a metadata-derived proxy relevance score. Experimental results demonstrate that our proposed retrieval significantly outperforms baseline methods, improving NormRecall@100 from 0.18 to 0.55. Furthermore, grounding the generator on the retrieved precedents enhances RCA generation quality over an LLM-only baseline, increasing the LLM-as-a-judge score from 3.34 to 3.72. These findings suggest that field-aware RAG can substantially streamline maritime safety investigation workflows by enabling faster precedent search and more consistent, evidence-based RCA drafting.

</details>


### [299] [MOSAIC: Modality-Specific Adaptation for Incremental Continual Learning in Parkinson's Disease Gait Assessment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13258)
*Minlin Zeng,Zhipeng Zhou,Yang Qiu,Martin J. McKeown,Zhiqi Shen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A modality-incremental, continual learning framework (MOSAIC) for multimodal PD gait assessment; introduces modality-specific warm-up, statistics-decoupled MSBN, and a curriculum-guided repulsive objective to mitigate forgetting and improve final performance across three datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Gait-based PD assessment relies on heterogeneous sensors; real-world systems accumulate modalities gradually due to upgrades, protocol changes, or multi-center deployment. Historical data are often unavailable due to privacy/storage limits. The modality-incremental setting creates unreliable cross-modal distillation, modality-specific distribution shifts, and reduced plasticity after preservation.

Method: Three-part solution: (1) Modality-Specific Warm-Up to stabilize representations of a newly added modality before distillation; (2) Statistics-Decoupled MSBN architecture that isolates sensor statistics while preserving a shared semantic backbone; (3) Curriculum-guided repulsive objective to recover plasticity while preserving legacy knowledge for modality-specific components.

Result: Empirical evaluation on three multimodal Parkinson's gait datasets shows MOSAIC improves final performance and mitigates forgetting compared to baselines; code is available at the provided repository.

Conclusion: MOSAIC offers a compact continual learning framework for modality-incremental multimodal PD gait assessment, effectively addressing cross-modal distillation, distribution shifts, and plasticity preservation, enabling more robust deployment.

Abstract: Gait-based Parkinson's disease assessment increasingly relies on heterogeneous sensors, but clinical systems rarely collect all modalities simultaneously. New sensors may arrive through device upgrades, protocol changes, or multi-center deployment, while historical patient data are often unavailable because of privacy and storage constraints. This modality-incremental setting faces three challenges: unreliable cross-modal distillation, modality-specific statistical shifts, and reduced plasticity after preservation. We propose MOSAIC, a compact continual learning framework. First, we identify the Toxic Teacher phenomenon and introduce Modality-Specific Warm-Up to stabilize newly learned modality representations before distillation. Second, we propose a statistics-decoupled MSBN architecture that isolates sensor statistics while maintaining a shared semantic backbone. Third, we design a curriculum-guided repulsive objective for Plasticity Recovery, preserving legacy knowledge while recovering modality-specific capacity. Experiments on three multimodal Parkinson's gait datasets show that MOSAIC improves final performance and mitigates forgetting. Project code is available at: https://github.com/minlinzeng/MOSAIC_Modality-Specific-Adaptation-for-Incremental-Continual-Learning-in-PD-Gait-Assessment.git

</details>


### [300] [From Verdict to Process: Agentic Reinforcement Learning for Multi-Stage Fact Verification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13262)
*Rongxin Yang,Shenghong He,Siyuan Zhu,Chao Yu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ProFact uses an agentic RL framework to end-to-end optimize multi-stage fact verification (decomposition, evidence gathering, answer generation, verdict prediction) with process-aware rewards, yielding improved verification accuracy and efficiency over baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current multi-stage verification pipelines optimize stages in isolation or with fixed heuristics, limiting adaptive coordination and potentially suboptimal outcomes.

Method: A unified policy trains across the stages (claim decomposition, evidence seeking, answer generation, verdict prediction). Process-aware rewards provide stage-level learning signals throughout the verification process to address sparse final supervision.

Result: Empirical results show ProFact consistently outperforms strong baselines in verification performance and inference efficiency.

Conclusion: Process-aware trajectory optimization is effective for multi-stage fact verification and enables better coordinated, end-to-end decision making.

Abstract: Recent approaches combining Large Language Models (LLMs) with retrieval-augmented reasoning have shown promise for automated fact verification. To process complex claims, these verification pipelines typically execute multi-stage workflows that coordinate tightly coupled modules, including claim decomposition, evidence gathering, and verdict prediction. However, existing methods optimize individual stages in isolation or rely on fixed heuristics, which limits adaptive coordination among stages and can lead to suboptimal outcomes. In this work, we propose ProFact, an agentic reinforcement learning framework for end-to-end optimization of multi-stage fact verification trajectories. ProFact trains a unified policy to coordinate claim decomposition, evidence seeking, answer generation, and verdict prediction. To address the sparse and delayed supervision provided by final veracity labels, ProFact introduces process-aware rewards that provide stage-level learning signals throughout the verification process. Empirical evaluation shows that ProFact consistently outperforms strong baselines in both verification performance and inference efficiency. These results highlight the effectiveness of process-aware trajectory optimization for multi-stage fact verification.

</details>


### [301] [ERTS: Adversarial Robustness Testing of Ethical AI via Semantic Perturbation in a Bounded Consequence Space](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13282)
*Pratyush Chaudhari*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ERTS is a closed-pipeline framework for adversarial testing of AI ethical reasoning, using a 22-dim Ethical Consequence Space (ECS), 17 semantic perturbations, 6 validity constraint classes (including semantic coherence), and a 4-component Ethical Instability Index (EII) to produce domain-adaptive robustness verdicts. It evaluates 4 baseline models and 2 production LLMs across 50 ethical scenarios in 8 domains, generating 1,500 adversarial test cases; only 33% pass clearance, with local Llama-3.2 showing notable vulnerabilities (ERS=0.737). The work claims novelty in merging a bounded ECS, semantic coherence constraints, and domain-adaptive assessment in one pipeline.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Formal methods for evaluating robustness of AI ethical reasoning in high-stakes contexts are underdeveloped. There is a need for bounded, theory-grounded consequence spaces, comprehensive semantic perturbations, and domain-adaptive evaluation.

Method: Define a 22-dimensional Ethical Consequence Space (ECS) grounded in established ethical theory; apply 17 semantic perturbation functions under 6 validity constraint classes including a novel semantic coherence constraint; compute a 4-component Ethical Instability Index (EII); provide domain-adaptive pre-deployment robustness verdicts; evaluate 4 structured baselines and 2 production LLMs (Gemini 2.0 Flash, Llama 3.2) across 50 scenarios in 8 domains; generate 1,500 adversarial test cases.

Result: Only 33% of models achieve assessment clearance; Llama-3.2 is particularly vulnerable to fairness corruption and information degradation (ERS = 0.737); across 1,500 tests, robustness gaps are evident.

Conclusion: ERTS offers a novel, integrated adversarial testing pipeline for ethical robustness by combining a bounded ECS, semantic coherence constraints, and domain-adaptive assessment; results indicate substantial robustness gaps in current systems and emphasize the need for formalized testing frameworks.

Abstract: As AI systems are deployed in high-stakes ethical contexts such as healthcare triage, autonomous vehicle control, and employment screening, formal methods for evaluating their robustness against adversarial manipulation of ethical reasoning remain underdeveloped. This paper introduces the Ethical Robustness Testing System (ERTS), a closed-pipeline framework that: (1) encodes ethical dilemmas into a 22-dimensional Ethical Consequence Space (ECS) grounded in established ethical theory; (2) applies 17 semantic perturbation functions subject to 6 validity constraint classes including a novel semantic coherence constraint; (3) measures decision deviation via a 4-component Ethical Instability Index (EII); and (4) produces domain-adaptive pre-deployment robustness assessment verdicts. We evaluate 4 structured baseline models and 2 production LLMs (Gemini 2.0 Flash and Llama 3.2) across 50 ethical scenarios spanning 8 deployment domains, generating 1,500 adversarial test cases. Results demonstrate that only 33% of models achieve assessment clearance, with the local Llama-3.2 model proving particularly vulnerable to fairness corruption and information degradation attacks (ERS = 0.737). To the best of our knowledge, no existing framework combines a bounded ethical consequence space, semantic coherence constraints, and domain-adaptive assessment in a single adversarial testing pipeline.

</details>


### [302] [Physics-Guided Spatiotemporal Learning for Coastal Wave Peak Period Estimation from Video](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13302)
*Abubakar Hamisu Kamagata,Dharm Singh Jat,Attlee Munyaradzi Gamundani,Abhishek Srivastava,Paramasivam Saravanakumar*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A physics-guided deep spatiotemporal learning framework uses passive coastal video to estimate nearshore wave peak periods with ROI detection, sim-to-real transfer, and physics-informed regularization; transformer architectures excel at instantaneous accuracy, while lightweight RCNNs offer better temporal stability; physics-guided regularization improves trend consistency and reduces implausible predictions; explainability auditing aligns attention with hydrodynamic behavior; overall approach is promising for cost-effective, long-term coastal monitoring.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Provide a scalable, physically grounded, and cost-effective alternative to traditional in-situ buoy/radar systems for nearshore wave parameter estimation using passive video and deep learning.

Method: (1) Automated temporal-variance-based region-of-interest detection to focus on surf-zone dynamics; (2) multi-stage Sim-to-Real transfer learning to bridge synthetic data and real video; (3) physics-informed regularization to enforce physically plausible trends and consistency; (4) evaluation of spatiotemporal architectures (transformer-based and recurrent-convolutional); (5) training regimes including synthetic pretraining, silver-label adaptation, expert fine-tuning; (6) ablation studies on regularization and explainability auditing to validate physics-consistency and attention alignment with hydrodynamic behavior.

Result: Transformer-based architectures delivered higher accuracy for instantaneous wave-peak-period predictions; lightweight recurrent-convolutional networks achieved greater temporal stability and operational oceanographic skill; ablations demonstrated the value of physics-guided regularization in trend-following and reduction of physically implausible outputs; explainability audits showed attention focusing in hydraulically active surf-zone regions in agreement with physically derived wave propagation; overall, the framework shows promise for cost-efficient, operational video-based coastal wave monitoring.

Conclusion: The study demonstrates that incorporating physics-informed constraints with deep learning on passive video can yield physically plausible, accurate nearshore wave parameters, offering a scalable and feasible alternative for long-term coastal monitoring and climate resilience.

Abstract: Wave parameters in the nearshore are crucial for coastal engineering, shoreline protection, marine hazard assessment, and coastal management for climate resilience. Traditional monitoring systems like buoys and radar platforms offer accurate monitoring but can have high installation and maintenance expenses and limited spatial coverage. Passive ocean monitoring using video has been achieved by leveraging deep learning, however, many methods are not physically interpretable, feasible, and validated for oceanography. In thiswork, a Physics-Guided Deep Spatiotemporal Learning Framework for direct estimation of nearshore wave peak periods from passive coastal video stream is proposed. The framework combines automated temporal-variance based region-of-interest detection, multi-stage Sim-to-Real transfer learning, and physics-informed regularization to enhance the predictive accuracy and physical consistency. A variety of spatiotemporal architectures were assessed, such as transformer-based and recurrent-convolutional ones, alongside synthetic pretraining,silver-label adaptation, and expert fine-tuning. The results show that transformer-based architectures outperformed in terms of the accuracy of the instantaneous prediction, while lightweight recurrent-convolutional architectures achieved higher temporal stability and operational oceanographic skill. Ablation studies also demonstrated the benefits of physics-guided regularization in terms of trend-following consistency, and physically implausible predictions. Explainability auditing also helped to focus attention in hydrodynamically active surf-zone regions and showed good agreement with the physically derived wave propagation behavior. In general, the proposed framework shows the promise of physics-guided video-based deep learning systems for long-term coastal wave monitoring that are cost-efficient and operationally feasible.

</details>


### [303] [ReSum: Synergizing LLM Reasoning and Summarization with Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13316)
