*Xucong Wang,Ziyu Ma,Yong Wang,Shidong Yang,Hailang Huang,Renda Li,Pengkun Wang,Xiangxiang Chu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ReSum introduces a summarization-aware adaptive RLVR framework where LLMs self-summarize to compress and organize reasoning trajectories. It uses contrastive rollout branches by masking or injecting a summarization phrase and a summarization-aware advantage, achieving ~4% performance gains and ~18.6% shorter rollouts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-horizon reasoning in LLMs with RLVR often leads to unnecessarily long rollouts, harming coherence and consuming context. Existing long-context organization relies on external mechanisms, not on the model's own trajectory management. A self-contained solution is needed to regulate reasoning length and improve stability.

Method: ReSum enables self-summarization in the LLM. When a model spontaneously triggers summarization, the framework masks the summarization phrase to create a contrastive branch; for non-summarization positions, it randomly injects the phrase to create a matched branch. A summarization-aware advantage enables finer-grained comparison between contrastive rollout trajectories. Rollouts are adaptively evaluated to determine whether self-summarization benefits ongoing reasoning, enabling the model to manage its trajectory.

Result: Extensive experiments show an average performance improvement of about 4% and a reduction in rollout length by 18.6%.

Conclusion: ReSum demonstrates that self-summarization can stabilize generation and enable the model to manage long-horizon reasoning trajectories more efficiently, yielding better performance with shorter rollouts. The approach offers a self-contained mechanism for improved coherence and context efficiency in RLVR.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) is a central technique for improving long-horizon reasoning in Large Language Models (LLMs). However, existing RLVR methods often encourage unnecessarily long reasoning rollouts, which can degrade reasoning coherence and exhaust the available context budget. Existing approaches to long-context organization often depend on external mechanisms to organize rollouts, rather than enabling the model to manage its own reasoning trajectory. To address this limitation, we propose ReSum, a novel RLVR framework that enables LLMs to compress and organize their reasoning trajectories through self-summarization. Our pilot studies show that self-summarization stabilizes generation by lowering token-level entropy, and that introducing a ``summarization'' phrase can substantially mitigate errors propagated from an incorrect rollout prefix. Motivated by these findings, ReSum adopts a summarization-aware adaptive rollout mechanism that contrastively evaluates whether self-summarization benefits the ongoing reasoning process. Specifically, when the model spontaneously triggers self-summarization, ReSum masks the summarization phrase to create a contrastive branch; for non-summarization positions, it instead randomly injects the phrase to create a matched branch. We further design a summarization-aware advantage to enable finer-grained comparison between contrastive rollout trajectories. Extensive experiments show that ReSum improves performance at an average of 4\% while reducing rollout length by 18.6\%.

</details>


### [304] [Can I Buy Your KV Cache?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13361)
*Luoyuan Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Precompute and distribute document KV caches to avoid recomputing prefill for AI agents; host caches to enable reuse with negligible accuracy cost, yielding large compute savings and potential monetization, but facing egress, compression, and payment-layer challenges.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Eliminate repetitive, compute-intensive prefill steps in large language model agents by sharing precomputed KV caches across agents; reduce latency and infrastructure costs while maintaining exact results.

Method: Publishers precompute a document's KV cache; agents load precomputed KV instead of running prefill; compare compute costs, latency, and accuracy; analyze KV placement, egress, and hosting strategies; discuss compression and payment layer issues.

Result: On Qwen3-4B, reuse is 9-50x cheaper in compute than prefill; savings scale with input length due to prefills' O(L^2) attention. Reuse is token-exact with no accuracy loss. Hosting and egress costs dominate if KV is shipped; provider-side hosting can remove egress. A single hot 3774-token document to 80M agents yields ~1.5M prefill cost vs 0.03M reuse compute (about 49.7x). A 0.1x cache-read tariff is economically favorable but must clear the measured envelope; provider margins set the remaining gap (~50x).

Conclusion: The idea enables an agent-native prefill CDN with lossless KV compression and cross-party payments as open problems. Practical deployment hinges on KV compression, secure hosting, real-time updates, and a sustainable economic model that covers egress and marginal costs.

Abstract: Right now, across the world, AI agents are repeating the same absurd act: to read one document, they each recompute it from scratch. Every agent re-runs prefill, the most compute-intensive step a large model takes, over identical text, only to rebuild a key-value (KV) cache identical to the one the agent before it just built. The same answer, computed a million times. We make a proposal that is almost offensively simple: compute it once. Let a publisher precompute a document's KV cache, and let every other agent buy the right to load it and skip prefill. It works, and it is token-exact: loading a precomputed KV and continuing matches prefilling from scratch (24/24 greedy tokens, and at the logits level), with no accuracy cost. On Qwen3-4B, reuse is 9-50x cheaper in compute than prefill, and the gap widens with length (prefill's attention scales with L^2), so a single reuse already pays it back. Then the part that matters: where the KV lives. Shipping it fails, because KV is nearly incompressible, so per-load egress costs more than the prefill it saves. Hosting it provider-side, exactly as production prompt-caching works, removes egress entirely. The size of the prize is set by our measured compute saving: serving one hot 3774-token document to 80M agents costs ~$1.5M to re-prefill but only ~$0.03M of reuse compute (49.7x less). The 0.1x cache-read tariff APIs charge passes a 10x discount to users while sitting inside this measured envelope, so the 10x is a floor that the measured ~50x compute saving clears, and the gap to the physical ~50x is provider margin: millions of dollars per popular document. We frame the resulting agent-native prefill CDN and leave lossless KV compression and a cross-party payment layer as the open problems.

</details>


### [305] [IterCAD: An Iterative Multimodal Agent for Visually-Grounded CAD Generation and Editing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13368)
*Tao Hu,Jiaxin Ai,Licheng Wen,Xueheng Li,Shu Zou,Siqi Li,Nianchen Deng,Xinyu Cai,Hongbin Zhou,Pinlong Cai,Daocheng Fu,Yu Yang,Hairong Zhang,Botian Shi,Xuemeng Yang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: IterCAD introduces a closed-loop, multimodal agent for CAD generation/editing that interacts with an executable CAD sandbox, covering Drawing-to-Code, Text-to-Code, and Interactive Editing, with data synthesis, progressive SFT and geometry-aware RL, plus a new evaluation suite (CD-TR/AUC-TR).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge the gap between current open-loop, one-shot CAD generation and iterative real-world CAD practices; ensure code executability and geometric fidelity; provide a unified evaluation framework for CAD agents.

Method: A multimodal agent engages in multi-turn interactions with an executable CAD sandbox across three tasks (Drawing-to-Code, Text-to-Code, Interactive Editing). Develop a data synthesis pipeline with industrial features to produce standard-compliant multi-view drawings and complex code-editing tasks plus high-fidelity interaction trajectories. Train via progressive supervised fine-tuning (SFT) followed by geometry-aware reinforcement learning with viable-prefix masking. Introduce IterCAD-Bench and Chamfer Distance Tolerance-Recall (CD-TR) curve with AUC-TR as a survivor-bias-free metric.

Result: IterCAD achieves highly competitive performance across benchmarks, significantly outperforming existing approaches in code executability and geometric precision, and excels at closed-loop iterative refinement.

Conclusion: The framework enables robust closed-loop CAD generation/editing through a unified multimodal agent, supported by a dedicated evaluation suite and new metrics, demonstrating strong practical viability for iterative design workflows.

Abstract: Computer-Aided Design is pivotal in modern manufacturing, yet existing automated methods predominantly rely on open-loop, one-shot generation, creating a mismatch with iterative real-world practices. In this paper, we present IterCAD, a unified multimodal agent framework for closed-loop, interactive CAD generation and editing. We formulate the task as a multi-turn interaction between a multimodal agent and an executable CAD sandbox, covering three tasks: Drawing-to-Code, Text-to-Code, and Interactive Editing. To support this, we develop a data synthesis pipeline incorporating advanced industrial manufacturing features to generate standard-compliant multi-view engineering drawings, complex code-editing tasks, and high-fidelity interaction trajectories. We optimize the agent via progressive SFT followed by geometry-aware reinforcement learning with viable-prefix masking to enhance code executability and geometric fidelity. Finally, we introduce the IterCAD-Bench evaluation suite and propose the Chamfer Distance Tolerance-Recall (CD-TR) curve alongside its AUC-TR metric, establishing a survivor-bias-free standard that unifies code validity and geometric precision. Extensive experiments demonstrate that IterCAD achieves highly competitive performance across multiple benchmarks, significantly outperforming existing approaches in both code executability and geometric precision, while exhibiting superior capabilities in closed-loop iterative refinement.

</details>


### [306] [A Quantitative Experimental Repeated Measures Study of Training Dynamics in a Small Llama Style Language Model Under a Compute-Aware Token Budget](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13370)
*Joe Dwyer*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Small Llama-style model trained under a fixed compute budget; interval-level analysis reveals non-monotonic training dynamics not captured by endpoint metrics; compute-aware evaluation recommended.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess training dynamics under compute-constrained token budgets to determine if endpoint metrics adequately reflect generalization or if trajectory-based telemetry reveals instability and diminishing returns.

Method: Six independent runs on a 4.26M-parameter model trained with CPU-based full precision on TinyStories, with a ~20M token budget. 21 training intervals, yielding 126 seed-by-interval observations. Repeated measures ANOVA used to test interval effects on validation loss, validation perplexity, rolling volatility, backslides, spikes, and between-seed variability.

Result: Significant interval effects for validation loss, validation perplexity, and rolling volatility. Descriptive trajectories show rapid early improvement followed by non-monotonic degradation. Validation loss declined from 8.3552 at initialization to 2.7996 around 4M tokens, then rose to 3.9010 at the final checkpoint. Perplexity followed a similar pattern. Recurrent validation-loss backslides observed; no interval-level evidence of a stable phase under predefined criteria. 126 observations across 21 intervals.

Conclusion: Endpoint metrics can obscure unstable dynamics under compute constraints. Trajectory-level telemetry reveals instability, regression, and diminishing returns; compute-aware evaluation should incorporate interval-level analysis to avoid overestimating generalization from final metrics.

Abstract: This study examines training dynamics in a small Llama-style language model trained under a fixed, compute-constrained token budget. Rather than evaluating efficiency solely through endpoint performance, the study uses a quantitative experimental repeated measures design to analyze how validation loss, validation perplexity, rolling volatility, backslide behavior, spike behavior, and between-seed variability change across token-based training intervals. Six independent training runs were conducted on a 4.26-million-parameter model using the TinyStories corpus, CPU-based full-precision training, and a target budget of approximately 20 million cumulative training tokens. Metrics were collected across 21 intervals, producing 126 seed-by-interval observations. Repeated measures ANOVA showed statistically significant interval effects for validation loss, validation perplexity, and rolling volatility. Descriptive trajectories revealed rapid early improvement followed by non-monotonic degradation during later training intervals. Mean validation loss decreased from 8.3552 at initialization to 2.7996 near 4 million tokens, but increased to 3.9010 by the final checkpoint. Validation perplexity followed the same pattern, falling sharply early in training before rising later. Derived telemetry further showed recurrent validation-loss backslides and no interval-summary evidence of a stable phase under the predefined criteria. These findings suggest that compute-aware language model evaluation should examine training trajectories rather than endpoint metrics alone. In constrained compute settings, additional token exposure may increase computational cost without producing proportional generalization gains, and interval-level telemetry can reveal instability, regression, and diminishing returns that final metrics may obscure.

</details>


### [307] [MiniMax Sparse Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13392)
*Xunhao Lai,Weiqi Xu,Yufeng Yang,Qiaorui Chen,Yang Xu,Lunbin Zeng,Xiaolong Li,Haohai Sun,Haichao Zhu,Vito Zhang,Pengyu Zhao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MiniMax Sparse Attention (MSA) is a blockwise sparse attention mechanism built on Grouped Query Attention (GQA). It uses an Index Branch to score and Top-k select key-value blocks per GQA group, with a Main Branch performing exact block-sparse attention on the chosen blocks. It is co-designed with a GPU kernel to deliver large practical speedups. On a 109B model, MSA matches GQA in performance while cutting per-token compute by 28.4x at 1M context; yields 14.2x prefill and 7.6x decoding wall-clock speedups on H800. Code and a multimodal model are released publicly.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable ultra-long-context processing (hundreds of thousands to millions of tokens) in LLMs by reducing the quadratic complexity of softmax attention while maintaining performance and deployability.

Method: Two-branch architecture: an Index Branch scores and selects a Top-k subset of key-value blocks for each GQA group; a Main Branch performs exact block-sparse attention over the selected blocks. Co-design with a GPU execution path using exp-free Top-k selection and KV-outer sparse attention to improve tensor-core utilization.

Result: Performance parity with GQA on a 109B model with native multimodal training, but with 28.4x reduction in per-token attention compute at 1M context. Hardware-specific speedups include 14.2x prefill and 7.6x decoding wall-clock improvements on H800; code and model released publicly.

Conclusion: MSA provides a simple, scalable sparse attention scheme enabling efficient ultra-long-context modeling suitable for deployment, backed by an accompanying GPU kernel and public code/model release.

Abstract: Ultra-long-context capability is becoming indispensable for frontier LLMs: agentic workflows, repository-scale code reasoning, and persistent memory all require the model to jointly attend over hundreds of thousands to millions of tokens, yet the quadratic cost of softmax attention makes this untenable at deployment scale. We introduce MiniMax Sparse Attention (MSA), a blockwise sparse attention built upon Grouped Query Attention (GQA). A lightweight Index Branch scores key-value blocks and independently selects a Top-k subset for each GQA group, enabling group-specific sparse retrieval while maintaining efficient block-level execution; the Main Branch then performs exact block-sparse attention over only the selected blocks. Designed around a principle of simplicity and scalability, MSA is deliberately streamlined, making it straightforward to deploy efficiently across a broad range of GPUs. To translate sparsity into practical speedups, we co-design MSA with a GPU execution path that uses exp-free Top-k selection and KV-outer sparse attention to improve tensor-core utilization under block-granular access. On a 109B-parameter model with native multimodal training, MSA performs on par with GQA while reducing per-token attention compute by 28.4x at 1M context. Paired with our co-designed kernel, MSA achieves 14.2x prefill and 7.6x decoding wall-clock speedups on H800. Our inference kernel is available at: https://github.com/MiniMax-AI/MSA. A production-grade natively multimodal model powered by MSA has been publicly released at: https://huggingface.co/MiniMaxAI/MiniMax-M3.

</details>


### [308] [Neuro-Symbolic Agents for Regulated Process Automation: Challenges and Research Agenda](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13405)
*Alexander Rombach,Chantale Lauer,Nijat Mehdiyev*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes compliance-by-construction for LLM-based regulated process automation, embedding symbolic regulations, process models, and constraints into the agent architecture; sees this as complementary to guardrail monitoring for safety and semantic correctness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Regulated, judgment-intensive domains require strong, verifiable adherence to rules. Guardrail-based monitoring can catch issues post hoc but cannot guarantee control-flow compliance or semantic correctness; integrating symbolic structures into the core architecture can enforce compliance by design.

Method: Introduce a conceptual framework (compliance-by-construction) that treats regulations, typed process models, and constraints as architectural components shaping decision-making; outline a structured set of neuro-symbolic research challenges at foundational and capability levels and argue for joint work to realize the approach.

Result: Conceptual articulation with a proposed paradigm and a roadmap of neuro-symbolic challenges; no empirical results or experiments are reported.

Conclusion: Advocates for the neuro-symbolic community to treat regulated process automation as a high-impact research domain and to pursue compliance-by-construction as a foundational complement to guardrails for regulated AI systems.

Abstract: LLM-based agents are entering regulated industries where they automate judgment intensive quality management processes. We argue that symbolic structures already embedded in these domains, including regulations, typed process models, and compliance constraints, should be treated not merely as external monitoring mechanisms but as core architectural components that shape the agent's decision-making and behavior. We propose compliance-by-construction as a complementary paradigm to guardrail-based monitoring: a structural foundation that prevents control-flow violations, while guardrails remain essential for catching semantic errors. We identify a structured set of neuro-symbolic research challenges on foundational and capability level and show that addressing them jointly enables compliance-by-construction. We call on the neuro-symbolic community to engage with regulated process automation as a high impact research domain.

</details>


### [309] [Optimizing Appliance Scheduling for Solar Energy Management Using Metaheuristic Algorithms](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13407)
*Hiba Ahmed,Alexander E. I. Brownlee,Jason Adair,Simon T. Powers*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes sequential multi-day appliance scheduling using ILS and SA to align residential load with solar generation, incorporating spillover across days and constraints like inverter limit and battery SOC; demonstrates feasibility and invites multi-objective trade-offs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Align solar generation with household demand, addressing the mismatch between daytime solar and user-driven appliance usage; extend scheduling across days to maintain task continuity.

Method: Metaheuristic optimization using Iterated Local Search and Simulated Annealing to determine appliance start times, considering durations, power, inverter limits, battery SOC, and solar forecasts; introduces spillover across days for continuity.

Result: Experiments show the framework effectively manages system constraints and maintains user convenience under exclusive solar generation; spillover enables sequential operation over multiple days.

Conclusion: Sequential multi-day scheduling improves operational continuity and constraint handling; opens avenues for multi-objective trade-offs among equipment size, ROI, and user satisfaction.

Abstract: Renewable energy is essential for meeting future energy demands; however, solar energy generation, which occurs only during daylight hours often does not align with household consumption patterns. Appliances such as cookers, washing machines, and dryers are typically operated according to user preferred schedules rather than solar energy availability, creating a scheduling optimization problem. The objective is to determine optimal appliance start times to maximize renewable energy utilization while minimizing user inconvenience and adhering to system constraints. This paper presents a metaheuristic approach using Iterated Local Search (ILS) and Simulated Annealing (SA) to optimize appliance start times, while considering appliance operating durations, power consumption, inverter limit, battery state of charge constraints, and solar generation forecasts. Unlike most existing work, the scheduling is extended beyond a single day to accommodate unfinished tasks from previous days (spillover), ensuring operational continuity and enabling sequential operation across multiple days. Experimental results show that the sequential multi-day scheduling framework effectively manages system constraints while ensuring user convenience under exclusive solar generation. These findings also open opportunities for future research on multi-objective trade-offs between investment in equipment of various sizes, return on that investment, and user satisfaction.

</details>


### [310] [Evaluation Sovereignty in Metadata-Driven Classification: A Multi-Track Framework for Weakly Supervised Information Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13436)
*Raymond Vasquez*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Evaluation metrics in ML can be biased by label-authority; introduce evaluation sovereignty and a multi-track framework to audit models under weak supervision, revealing large degradation when moving from silver to gold labels and a divergence between ranking metrics and true predictive validity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In large-scale metadata systems labels are incomplete/inconsistent/weakly supervised. Traditional evaluation assumes label-independence from supervision, which may overstate performance. There is a need to quantify how evaluation outcomes depend on label authority.

Method: Propose a multi-track evaluation framework that systematically varied training and evaluation label sources (silver vs gold). Empirically test on hierarchical multi-label classification of large-scale scientific metadata.

Result: Models performing well under operational (silver) evaluation degrade substantially under independent (gold) evaluation (e.g., Micro-F1 from ~0.54 to ~0.03 for fine-grained tasks). Ranking-based metrics remain above baseline, indicating a disconnect between latent signal and true validity.

Conclusion: Evaluation validity is a system-level property shaped by label governance. The paper provides a methodology to audit intelligent systems under weak supervision and calls for rethinking evaluation as sovereignty over labels.

Abstract: Evaluation in machine learning is typically treated as a neutral measurement process. However, in operational information systems, evaluation outcomes are often conditioned by the processes used to generate labels. This paper does not seek to improve classification performance. Instead, it examines the validity of performance measurement under differing label-authority regimes. This issue is particularly relevant in large-scale metadata-driven systems, where labels are often incomplete, inconsistent, or weakly supervised.
  We introduce evaluation sovereignty, defined as the degree to which performance metrics are independent of label authority and supervision regime, and propose a multi-track evaluation framework that systematically varies training and evaluation label sources. Using hierarchical multi-label classification on large-scale scientific metadata, we demonstrate that models exhibiting strong performance under operational ("silver") evaluation degrade substantially under independent ("gold") evaluation, particularly for fine-grained classification. For example, Micro-F1 decreases from approximately 0.54 to 0.03. Notably, ranking-based metrics remain above baseline, revealing a divergence between latent model signal and classification validity.
  These findings suggest that commonly reported performance metrics may reflect alignment with labeling processes rather than true predictive capability. We therefore reconceptualize evaluation validity as a system-level property shaped by label governance and provide a practical methodology for auditing intelligent systems operating under weak supervision.

</details>


### [311] [Why Sampling Is Not Choosing: Intentionality, Agency, and Moral Responsibility in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13441)
*Joseph Keshet*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The abstract argues that large language models (LLMs) do not possess genuine agency or moral responsibility; true moral agency requires commitment-bearing agency with intrinsic intentionality and self-attributed action, which LLMs lack. Despite coherent outputs, LLMs are probabilistic input-output mappings; their apparent intentionality is external to them, and their outputs are not owned as commitments or guided by reasons. Variability from sampling does not equal free will or authorship. The paper also argues that objections from the intentional stance, functionalism, compatibilism, and perceived moral reasoning do not establish genuine agency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To challenge prevalent claims that LLMs or AI systems can be moral agents or bear responsibility, by clarifying the conditions for moral agency and distinguishing surface-level behavior from genuine intentional action.

Method: Conceptual-philosophical analysis: define criteria for agency and responsibility; analyze LLMs as probabilistic, data-driven systems; evaluate alleged intentionality and authorial commitment; assess common objections (intentional stance, functionalism, compatibilism, observed moral reasoning).

Result: LLMs fail to meet the criteria for agency and moral responsibility. They lack intrinsic intentionality and commitment-bearing action; outputs are not owned as commitments nor guided by reasons; stochastic variation is not genuine choice or authorship; thus they do not constitute genuine moral agents.

Conclusion: Claims of agency/moral agency in LLMs are misguided. Genuine moral responsibility requires intrinsic, self-attributed agency; current LLMs, despite sophisticated outputs, do not fulfill these prerequisites, and common philosophical objections do not salvage their status as agents.

Abstract: Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have prompted claims that such systems exhibit agency or qualify as moral agents. This paper argues that these attributions are misguided. We maintain that moral responsibility requires commitment-bearing agency grounded in intrinsic intentionality and self-attributed action, and that such agency constitutes the form of free will relevant to responsibility. Although LLMs generate coherent and normatively evaluable outputs, their operation is fully characterized by probabilistic input-output mappings learned from data. Their apparent intentionality is derived rather than intrinsic, and their outputs are neither owned as commitments nor guided by reasons. Variability introduced by stochastic sampling does not amount to choice or authorship. We address objections from the intentional stance, functionalism, compatibilism, and the presence of moral reasoning in model outputs, arguing that none suffice to establish genuine agency.

</details>


### [312] [CloudCons: A Comprehensive End-to-End Benchmark for Cloud Resource Consolidation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13513)
*Xiaobin Zhang,Lefei Shen,Mouxiang Chen,Zhuo Li,Hongkai Li,Han Fu,Jianling Sun,Xiaoxue Ren,Chenghao Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: End-to-end benchmark CloudCons assesses forecasting models for cloud resource consolidation; foundation models have better zero-shot forecast accuracy but don't automatically improve decision utility; predictive quantile calibration is key to balancing resource efficiency and service reliability; provides datasets from Huawei Cloud, Azure, and Google Borg and actionable deployment guidelines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conservative over-provisioning yields low resource utilization. Forecast-then-optimize is used to anticipate demand, but forecast quality alone does not guarantee better downstream decisions. A holistic evaluation of forecasting models within the actual consolidation task is needed.

Method: Construct CloudCons with high-quality datasets from Huawei Cloud, Microsoft Azure, and Google Borg capturing diurnal rhythms, bursts, and high-frequency noise. Evaluate statistical, deep learning, and foundation models on forecasting for consolidation and measure downstream decision utility. Analyze how predictive quantiles affect the trade-off between resource efficiency and reliability.

Result: Foundation models achieve superior zero-shot forecasting accuracy but do not necessarily translate to improved decision utility. The predictive quantile selection acts as a critical lever; calibrated quantile settings can balance efficiency with reliability. The paper provides practical guidelines for deployment.

Conclusion: An end-to-end benchmark bridging forecasting accuracy and decision-level performance for cloud resource consolidation. It offers datasets, evaluation of multiple model families, and actionable guidelines, highlighting the need to consider downstream utility beyond mere forecast accuracy.

Abstract: Driven by conservative over-provisioning to guarantee service reliability, resource utilization in cloud data centers remains at low levels. To mitigate this, the forecast-then-optimize paradigm has emerged to optimize consolidation by anticipating future demands. While emerging time series foundation models promise to enhance this paradigm through zero-shot generalization, existing benchmarks focus solely on prediction error metrics. The actual decision utility of these advanced models remains unverified, rendering their practical value for downstream tasks uncertain. To bridge this gap, we propose CloudCons, a comprehensive end-to-end benchmark designed to evaluate forecasting models within the specific context of cloud resource consolidation. We build high-quality datasets that cover diverse workloads from Huawei Cloud, Microsoft Azure, and Google Borg, capturing distinct service characteristics ranging from synchronized diurnal rhythms to stochastic, pulse-like bursts and high-frequency noise. We conduct an extensive evaluation of statistical, deep learning, and foundation models. Our experiments reveal a pivotal finding: while foundation models demonstrate superior zero-shot forecasting accuracy, this advantage does not inherently translate into better decision utility. Of practical significance, we systematically analyze how the selection of predictive quantiles acts as a critical lever. We provide actionable guidelines for calibrating these selections to balance the trade-off between resource efficiency and service reliability, offering vital insights for real-world deployment decisions.

</details>


### [313] [Uncertainty-Aware Hybrid Retrieval for Long-Document RAG](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13550)
*Hoin Jung,Xiaoqian Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Training-free, uncertainty-aware multi-granularity RAG (UMG-RAG) with a parent-promotion variant (UMGP-RAG) that fuse dense and sparse retrieval across chunk granularities to improve QA generation while keeping a lightweight, plug-and-play pipeline.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Retrieval granularity affects evidence quality: large chunks preserve context but add noise; small chunks are precise but harder to retrieve due to insufficient cues. A training-free framework that leverages multiple granularities and estimates reliability is desirable to improve evidence fidelity without retraining retrievers or altering generators.

Method: For each query, treat chunk granularity as a reliability signal. Use existing dense and sparse retrievers as complementary experts across multiple granularities. Convert each expert's score list into an evidence distribution, estimate reliability via distribution entropy, and fuse candidates according to query-specific semantic, lexical, and granularity confidence. UMGP-RAG adds a parent-promotion step: use fine-grained hits to locate relevant evidence but return broader non-redundant parent chunks to maintain local coherence. This pipeline is training-free and plug-and-play with existing retrievers.

Result: Experiments on QA benchmarks show that uncertainty-aware fusion and the parent-promotion strategy improve generation quality while maintaining a lightweight retrieval pipeline.

Conclusion: Uncertainty-based fusion across multi-granularity evidence and the parent-promotion variant provide an effective, training-free enhancement for RAG systems, balancing granularity, relevance, and coherence.

Abstract: Retrieval augmented generation (RAG) depends critically on the quality and granularity of retrieved evidence. Large retrieval units preserve context but often introduce irrelevant content, which can dilute answer bearing evidence and worsen long context utilization. Fine-grained units are more compact, but they may be difficult to retrieve reliably because short chunks can lack semantic, lexical, or bridging cues needed to match the query. We propose Uncertainty-aware Multi-Granularity RAG (UMG-RAG), a training-free hybrid retrieval framework that treats chunk granularity as query-specific reliability estimation. Instead of training a new retriever or modifying the generator, UMG-RAG uses existing dense and sparse retrievers as complementary experts across multiple chunk granularities. For each query, it converts each expert-granularity score list into an evidence distribution, estimates reliability from distribution entropy, and fuses candidates according to query-specific semantic, lexical, and granularity confidence. We further introduce UMGP-RAG, a parent promotion variant that uses fine-grained hits to locate relevant evidence while returning broader non-redundant parent chunks for local coherence. Experiments on question answering benchmarks show that uncertainty-aware fusion and parent promotion improve generation quality while maintaining a lightweight, plug-and-play retrieval pipeline.

</details>


### [314] [Is It You or Your Environment? A Bayesian Inference Framework for Genomically-Anchored Personalized Physiological Interpretation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13556)
*Aruna Dey,Suraj Biswas*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Genome-informed Bayesian priors anchor personalized health AI to overcome cold-start; priors decay with data to favor empirical baselines; applied across six physiological domains with graded genomic anchors and deployment constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the persistent cold-start problem in personalized physiological interpretation by introducing a fixed, exogenous genetic anchor that is immune to reverse causation and available before observing behavioral data.

Method: Define an exogenous prior G-hat_genomic = mu + sum_i beta_i g_i using GWAS-derived effect sizes and risk-allele counts. Incoming measurement P yields delta = P - G-hat. A time-dependent weight w(t) blends the genomic prior with the data-driven baseline: G-hat_t = w(t)*G-hat_genomic + [1-w(t)]*P-bar_t. Extend across six domains, grade priors by evidence (robust anchors vs contested genes), and discuss the boundary among association, Mendelian randomization, and individual causation. Impose deployment constraints: evidence-graded priors, dynamic decay, ancestry-matched effect sizes, and attribution rather than deterministic output.

Result: A conceptual framework demonstrating how a fixed genomic anchor can resolve misattribution of environmental versus constitutional effects, with an illustrative HRV example and a taxonomy of priors (robust vs contested) across six domains.

Conclusion: Proposes deployment guidelines emphasizing graded priors, dynamic decay, ancestry considerations, and attribution-focused outputs, while clarifying inference boundaries between association, MR, and individual causation.

Abstract: Personalized health AI systems face a fundamental cold-start problem: machine learning models for physiological interpretation require weeks of individual behavioral data before they can distinguish constitutional variation from environmentally driven deviation. We propose a solution grounded in causal inference and Bayesian prior design. An individual's genomic profile serves as an exogenous genetic anchor -- a domain-informed, personalized prior that is fixed at conception, immune to reverse causation, and available before a single behavioral observation is collected. The anchor initializes a Bayesian belief state over an individual's physiological set point G-hat = mu + sum(beta_i * g_i), where beta_i are GWAS-derived effect sizes and g_i are risk-allele counts. Each incoming physiological measurement P produces a non-constitutional deviation delta = P - G-hat that separates the signal attributable to environment and state from the constitutionally fixed baseline. As behavioral data accrue, the prior decays according to G-hat_t = w(t)*G-hat_genomic + [1-w(t)]*P-bar_t, transitioning from genome-dominated to empirical-baseline-dominated inference. The same observed HRV of 55 ms generates a suppression hypothesis for a person whose prior predicts 80 ms, and an enhancement hypothesis for a person whose prior predicts 30 ms -- a reversal impossible without a personalized anchor. We develop this architecture across six physiological domains, grading genomic priors by evidence strength, distinguishing robustly replicated anchors (FTO, FADS1/2, FKBP5) from contested candidate genes (SLC6A4, MAOA, DRD2). We address the inference boundary between association, Mendelian randomization, and individual token causation, and define four constraints for deployment: evidence-graded priors, dynamic decay, ancestry-matched effect sizes, and attribution rather than deterministic output.

</details>


### [315] [A Three-Layer Framework for AI in Scientific Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13566)
*Guojun Liao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes a three-layer framework for AI in scientific discovery, elevating Layer 2 (model formation via qualitative reasoning) as the core, and illustrates it with three case studies where inadequate frameworks were replaced by new conceptual objects from neighboring fields.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current AI discussions emphasize search and execution but neglect the central act of discovery: forming and evolving models. The paper argues Layer 2 is the most important yet least developed, and that true discovery requires structural insight beyond trial-and-error.

Method: Introduce a three-layer model (search, model formation, execution). Argue that Layer 2—qualitative reasoning about when a framework is structurally inadequate and what representation spaces could fill the gap—is the main innovation. Support with three case studies (Chern's intrinsic proof of Gauss-Bonnet, Nesterov AGM convergence via Lyapunov functions, OpenAI's autonomous disproof of Erdos unit distance in 2026) that share a pattern of inadequacy, missing conceptual object, and neighboring-field resolution.

Result: A formal claim that Layer 2 is the pivotal but underdeveloped component of AI-driven discovery. Demonstrations show how breakthroughs arise from identifying gaps in current frameworks and importing concepts from adjacent domains, rather than from search alone or execution refinement.

Conclusion: Layer 2's development is essential to advance AI-assisted discovery. The authors call for research that cultivates structural reasoning and cross-domain conceptual transfer to enable genuine model formation, enabling AI to contribute more profoundly beyond optimization and data retrieval.

Abstract: Current discussions of AI in scientific discovery are often dominated by two visible capabilities: search over existing knowledge and execution through optimization, simulation, and automation. Both are important, but neither fully captures the central act of discovery: the formation and evolution of models. This paper proposes a three-layer view of AI in discovery. Layer 1 is search and retrieval by large language models. Layer 2, as the main innovation of this paper, is model formation through qualitative reasoning: the capacity to recognize when a current framework is structurally inadequate and to understand the problem within a broader representational space, not through trial and error, but through structural insight into what is missing and where it can be found. Layer 3 is execution, optimization, and refinement. The main claim is that Layer 2 is both the most important and the least developed. Search without model formation remains confined to inherited frameworks, while execution without conceptual revision only amplifies an existing formulation. We illustrate Layer 2 reasoning through three case studies: S. S. Chern's intrinsic proof of the Gauss-Bonnet theorem, the resolution of the Nesterov Accelerated Gradient convergence problem via Lyapunov functions, and the autonomous disproof of the Erdos unit distance conjecture by OpenAI in 2026. Each case exhibits the same structural signature: a framework that had become inadequate, a missing conceptual object, and a resolution found in an unexpected neighboring field.

</details>


### [316] [Multiagent Protocols with Aggregated Confidence Signals](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13591)
*Ali Elahi,Barbara Di Eugenio*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces three protocols to produce a final answer with a single aggregated confidence for multiagent systems by transforming and fusing individual confidence signals, yielding a more discriminative system-level confidence (AUARC) while preserving F1; evaluated across six debating pairs, five benchmarks, and four task types, with calibration helping but AUARC being less calibration-dependent.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the lack of a single, comparable confidence measure for multiagent system outputs, enabling reliable decision-making and better calibration across heterogeneous agents.

Method: Transform raw confidence signals to make them comparable, then aggregate via soft voting or a Bayesian fusion approach. Propose three protocols that produce a final answer with an aggregated confidence. Analyze two estimators (sequence probability and self-report) with parametric and non-parametric calibrators. Evaluate six homogeneous/heterogeneous debating pairs per benchmark, across five benchmarks and four task types.

Result: Aggregated confidence (AUARC) is substantially more discriminative than the best single agent or standard debate baselines. Correctness (F1) remains stable and recovers losses MAD incurs on more ambiguous tasks. Calibration improves F1 for both estimators; AUARC is less reliant on calibration. Experiments span multiple debating pairs, benchmarks, and task types.

Conclusion: The proposed three protocols enable a robust, discriminative system-level confidence for multiagent outputs across diverse task types and model capabilities, with calibration providing nuanced benefits and AUARC demonstrating resilience to calibration requirements.

Abstract: Confidence is used for reliability, oversight, and a range of downstream decision tasks in Natural Language Processing (NLP), yet no existing method produces or evaluates a confidence for the output of a multiagent system. Prior work uses confidence within multiagent debate (MAD) to weight messages, trigger debate, or calibrate individual agents, but it never aggregates these into a single confidence for the system itself. We introduce three protocols that produce a final answer along with a single aggregated confidence by first transforming raw confidence signals to make them comparable across models, then combining them via soft voting or a probability fusion we call Bayesian fusion. This aggregated confidence is substantially more discriminative (AUARC) than that of the best single agent or the standard debate baselines, while correctness (F1-score) stays stable and recovers the losses MAD incurs on more ambiguous tasks. Analyzing two estimators, sequence probability and self-report, alongside parametric and non-parametric calibrators, we find that calibration improves F1 for both estimators while AUARC is less reliant on it. We evaluate six homogeneous and heterogeneous debating pairs per benchmark, across five benchmarks and four task types, spanning a range of model capabilities and sizes.

</details>


### [317] [Reward Modeling for Multi-Agent Orchestration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13598)
*King Yeung Tsang,Zihao Zhao,Vishal Venkataramani,Haizhou Shi,Zixuan Ke,Semih Yavuz,Shafiq Joty,Hao Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: OrchRM provides self-supervised reward modeling for orchestrators in LLM-powered multi-agent systems, using intermediate artifacts to create win-lose pairs for a Bradley-Terry reward model, enabling efficient training and scalable test-time orchestration across domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address limited supervision and high computational cost in training orchestrators for MAS built on LLMs, enabling efficient, scalable orchestration without heavy sub-agent rollouts.

Method: Extracts intermediate artifacts from multi-agent executions to form win-lose pairs. Trains a Bradley-Terry reward model at the orchestration level, avoiding costly sub-agent rollouts. Works self-supervised and improves token efficiency and test-time performance across domains.

Result: Training efficiency improved up to 10x in token usage; MAS test-time scaling accuracy improved up to 8%. Gains transfer across mathematical reasoning, web QA, and multi-hop reasoning domains.

Conclusion: Orchestrator-level reward modeling is a scalable, robust direction for efficient MAS orchestration, enabling self-supervised training and better test-time performance; code will be released.

Abstract: Multi-Agent Systems (MAS) built on Large Language Models (LLMs) require effective orchestration to coordinate specialized agents, yet training such orchestrators is hindered by limited supervision and high computational cost. We propose Orchestration Reward Modeling (OrchRM), a self-supervised framework for evaluating orchestration quality without human annotations. OrchRM leverages intermediate artifacts from multi-agent executions to construct win-lose pairs for Bradley-Terry reward model training. Unlike existing MAS test-time scaling and orchestrator training frameworks that rely on costly sub-agent rollouts, OrchRM operates directly at the orchestration level, enabling efficient and high-performing reward-guided orchestrator training and MAS test-time scaling. OrchRM improves training efficiency by up to 10x in token usage while improving MAS test-time scaling performance by up to 8% in accuracy. These gains consistently transfer across multiple domains, including mathematical reasoning, web-based question answering, and multi-hop reasoning, demonstrating orchestration-level reward modeling as a scalable direction for robust multi-agent orchestration. Code will be available at https://github.com/Wang-ML-Lab/OrchRM.

</details>


### [318] [EpiBench: Verifiable Evaluation of AI Agents on Epigenomics Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13602)
*Harihara Muralidharan,Reema Baskar,Soo Hee Lee,Tim Proctor,Kenny Workman*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: EpiBench reveals limited pass rates for short-horizon epigenomics analysis tasks by current agents; top performers ~45% success, highlighting a gap in assay-specific scientific judgment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To create a verifiable, gradable benchmark that tests whether agents can make well-defined analysis decisions from realistic workflow states in epigenomics.

Method: A benchmark with 106 evaluations across CUT\&Tag/CUT\&RUN, ATAC-seq, ChIP-seq, DNA methylation; 5,088 valid trajectories from 16 model-harness pairs; measurements of deterministically gradable outcomes; across multiple models and tasks.

Result: No system passed a majority of attempts; highest pass rate ~45%; variability across assay types; many failed runs contained correct elements; agents could identify relevant files and compute intermediates but failed deeper assay-specific reasoning.

Conclusion: Demonstrates the gap between surface-level task orchestration and domain-specific scientific judgment; improvement needed via better reasoning, tool integration, and possibly task decomposition to enhance deterministically gradable outcomes.

Abstract: We introduce EpiBench, a verifiable benchmark for short-horizon epigenomics analysis. EpiBench evaluates whether agents can make well-defined analysis decisions from realistic workflow states and return deterministically gradable answers. The benchmark includes 106 evaluations across CUT\&Tag/CUT\&RUN, ATAC-seq, ChIP-seq, and DNA methylation workflows. Across 5,088 valid trajectories from 16 model-harness pairs, no system passed a majority of attempts: GPT-5.5 / Pi led at 45.0\% (143/318 attempts; 95\% confidence interval (CI), 36.3--53.7), followed by GPT-5.5 / OpenAI Codex at 39.9\% (127/318 attempts; 95\% CI, 31.6--48.3). Claude Opus 4.8 Max / Pi and GPT-5.4 / Pi each passed 39.0\% (124/318 attempts; 95\% CI, 30.2--47.8 and 31.0--47.0, respectively). Performance varies across assay types, and many failed runs still contain parts of the correct answer. Agents often found the right files and computed useful intermediate results, but failed when the task required deeper, assay-specific scientific judgment.

</details>


### [319] [Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning from Delayed Marketplace Feedback for Objective-Weight Adaptation in Three-Sided Dispatch](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13604)
*Haochen Wu,Yi Hou,Shiguang Xie*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Deploys an offline-to-online RL framework for adapting dispatch objective weights in a three-sided marketplace, using a store-level discrete multiplier to shift the optimizer's trade-off, trained on logged data with Double Q-learning and a conservative regularizer; shows safe, improved batching with preserved delivery quality in production.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve the balance between delivery quality and operational efficiency (batching/courier utilization) in a live, delayed-feedback marketplace by learning to adjust dispatch objectives online without replacing existing combinatorial optimizers.

Method: Train a centralized value function on offline marketplace data using Double Q-learning targets with a conservative regularizer to mitigate value overestimation. A store-level policy then selects a discrete multiplier that adjusts the dispatch optimizer's trade-off between delivery quality and batching efficiency. Execution is decentralized, preserving production constraints and safeguards while enabling offline policy learning with noisy, delayed, and coupled feedback.

Result: In production, the offline-trained policy increases batching and reduces courier-side time costs without degrading customer-facing delivery quality; demonstrates that live world feedback can be leveraged to safely adapt decision policies online.

Conclusion: World feedback from a live economic and logistics system can be safely exploited to adapt dispatch policies online. The combination of offline RL on centralized data with per-store action selection preserves feasibility while improving operational metrics.

Abstract: Dispatch in three-sided marketplaces provides a natural setting for reinforcement learning from world feedback: decisions are evaluated by delayed operational outcomes such as delivery speed, courier utilization, and merchant congestion. We present a deployed reinforcement learning system at DoorDash that adapts dispatch objective weights in a large-scale food-delivery marketplace using delayed signals. Rather than replacing the combinatorial assignment optimizer, a store-level policy learned from logged marketplace data selects a discrete multiplier that shifts the dispatch optimizer's tradeoff between delivery quality and batching efficiency. This interface enables offline policy learning under noisy, delayed, and coupled feedback while preserving production feasibility constraints and operational safeguards. We train a shared value function using centralized offline data and decentralized store-level execution, with Double Q-learning targets and a conservative regularizer to reduce out-of-distribution value overestimation. In a production switchback experiment, the offline-trained policy increases batching and reduces courier-side time costs without degrading customer-facing delivery quality. Results illustrate how world feedback from a live economic and logistics system can be used to safely adapt decision policies online.

</details>


### [320] [Reasoning as Pattern Matching: Shared Mechanisms in Human and LLM Everyday Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13607)
*Zach Studdiford,Gary Lupyan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The abstract argues that both humans and 25 LLMs show similar everyday causal reasoning errors, attributing them to pattern-matching rather than abstract world models, and identifies specific attention heads that implement this pattern-matching to predict errors caused by irrelevant prompt details.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To challenge the view that LLM failures reflect genuine reasoning deficits and to compare human and model reasoning processes, with a focus on mechanistic explanations via attention heads.

Method: Empirical study with human participants and 25 LLMs on common-sense tasks; identification and analysis of attention heads driving LLM responses to reveal pattern-matching; use of these heads to predict errors in response to irrelevant prompt details.

Result: Humans and models exhibit similar error patterns; certain attention heads appear to implement pattern-matching; these heads can predict seemingly inexplicable human errors caused by irrelevant prompt details.

Conclusion: Everyday causal reasoning in both humans and LLMs aligns more with pattern-matching than with abstract world models, suggesting that current LLM failures may reflect reliance on superficial cues rather than robust abstract reasoning.

Abstract: When large language models (LLMs) fail to generalize or make haphazard errors in reasoning, it is often taken as evidence that LLMs are not truly reasoning, but rather performing a kind of pattern matching. The implication is that people's behavior does not exhibit the same types of failures because human reasoning uses principled and abstract world models. We evaluate human participants and 25 LLMs on their ability to engage in common-sense reasoning about a variety of everyday situations and observe similar patterns of errors in both people and models. We then identify the set of attention heads driving LLM responses and find that these heads implement a form of pattern-matching. These attention heads allow us to predict seemingly inexplicable reasoning errors in people caused by ostensibly irrelevant prompt details. Taken together, our results suggest that everyday causal reasoning in people and LLMs is more consistent with a form of pattern-matching than with abstract world models.

</details>


### [321] [AgentBeats: Agentifying Agent Assessment for Openness, Standardization, and Reproducibility](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13608)
*Xiaoyuan Liu,Jianhong Tu,Yuqi Chen,Siyuan Xie,Sihan Ren,Tianneng Shi,Gal Gantar,Evan Sandoval,Donghyun Lee,Daniel Miao,Peter J. Gilbert,Nick Hynes,Mauro Staver,Warren He,David Marn,Andrew Low,Xi Zhang,Elron Bandel,Michal Shmueli-Scheuer,Siva Reddy,Alexandre Drouin,Alexandre Lacoste,Ramayya Krishnan,Elham Tabassi,Yu Su,Victor Barres,Chenguang Wang,Wenbo Guo,Dawn Song*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes Agentified Agent Assessment (AAA): an open, agent-agnostic evaluation framework for agent systems, implemented by AgentBeats, demonstrated via large-scale judge/subject-agent competition and a coding-case study to show scalability, fidelity, and reproducibility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evaluation of agent systems is fragmented, heavily LLM-centric, and tightly coupled to fixed benchmarks, causing test-production mismatch and unfair cross-design comparisons; there is a need for a unified, open assessment interface separating evaluation logic from agent implementations.

Method: Introduce AAA with standardized interaction protocols: A2A for task management and MCP for tool access. Evaluation is performed by judge agents, interoperating with subject agents through these protocols. AgentBeats is a concrete realization of AAA, identifying five operation modes to balance openness, privacy, and reproducibility. Validate via two studies: (1) a five-month open competition with 298 judge agents and 467 subject agents across heterogeneous benchmarks, (2) a coding-agent case study confirming fidelity to public records and uncovering previously missing head-to-head results.

Result: AAA demonstrates applicability across diverse benchmarks; AgentBeats supports real-world constraints and preserves evaluation fidelity while enabling new insights (e.g., head-to-head results). The large-scale field study and the coding case study together show coverage, practicality, and fidelity at scale.

Conclusion: AAA plus AgentBeats offer a clear path to open, standardized, and reproducible agent assessment across heterogeneous scenarios, enabling fair comparison and interoperability.

Abstract: Agent systems are advancing quickly across domains, but their evaluation remains fragmented. Most benchmarks rely on fixed, LLM-centric harnesses that require heavy integration, create test-production mismatch, and limit fair comparison across diverse agent designs. The root problem is the lack of an open, agent-agnostic assessment interface. We advocate Agentified Agent Assessment (AAA), where evaluation is performed by judge agents and all participants interact through standardized protocols: A2A for task management and MCP for tool access. Conventional benchmarking defines two separate interfaces, one for the benchmark and one for the agent, while AAA only needs one; this yields a generic, unified framework that separates assessment logic from agent implementation and enables reproducible, interoperable, and multi-agent evaluation. We further introduce AgentBeats as a concrete realization of AAA: we identify five practical operation modes that make standardized assessment compatible with real-world constraints on openness, privacy, and reproducibility.
  To evaluate our design at scale, we conduct two studies: a five-month open competition that drew 298 judge agents across 12 categories together with 467 subject agents from independent participants, showing that AAA applies across a heterogeneous range of benchmarks; and a case study on coding agents that confirms agentified evaluation preserves fidelity with the public record while surfacing previously missing head-to-head results, yielding research insights about agent design. Combining a community-scale field study and a controlled coding case study, we verify that AAA delivers coverage, practicality, and fidelity across heterogeneous scenarios at scale. Together, AAA and AgentBeats offer a clear path toward open, standardized, and reproducible agent assessment.

</details>


### [322] [Beyond Runtime Enforcement: Shield Synthesis as Defensibility Analysis for Adversarial Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13621)
*Achraf Hsain,Sultan Almuhammadi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Reframes shielded reinforcement learning as a design-time analytical tool for assessing defensibility of a topology-specification pair in network defense, not as a runtime safety mechanism. Introduces an asymmetric two-player safety game to generate a defensibility verdict, winning region, and shield; derives a defensibility fingerprint from attractor structure and shielded MARL behavior; shows architectural changes can greatly affect operational outcomes without altering formal safety margins, making shield synthesis a framework for architectural questions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Challenge the view that runtime shield synthesis is the primary value of specification-based safety; propose using automata-theoretic machinery as a diagnostic tool to obtain insights about system defensibility and topology.

Method: Constrained two-player safety game with asymmetric specifications: defender marks unsafe region; attacker restricts adversary actions during attractor computation. Solve the game to obtain a defensibility verdict, associated winning region, and a shield. Extract topology-level metrics from the attractor structure and combine with post-convergence behavior from shield-constrained adversarial multi-agent reinforcement learning to form a defensibility fingerprint.

Result: A formal defensibility verdict indicating whether a topology-specification pair is defensible, plus the winning region and a shield. A defensibility fingerprint combining structural attractor metrics with operational behavior from shielded MARL. A what-if analysis showing that formal safety margins can remain stable while operational outcomes shift significantly with architectural changes.

Conclusion: Shield synthesis is best viewed as a framework for answering architectural questions about if, where, and how a system can be defended; the defensibility verdict is the output, not the deployed safe policy.

Abstract: Shielded reinforcement learning is typically presented as a runtime safety mechanism that compiles temporal-logic specifications into automata restricting an agent's actions. We argue this is the wrong product. The same automata-theoretic machinery -- specification compilation, product game construction, attractor computation, and winning-region extraction -- is better read as a design-time analytical instrument whose outputs are structural insights about a system rather than runtime constraints on a deployed agent.
  We instantiate this through a constrained two-player safety game for network defense. The two specifications are enforced asymmetrically: the defender specification defines the unsafe region of the game, whereas the attacker specification restricts the adversary's legal actions during attractor computation. Solving the game yields a defensibility verdict -- a formal certificate that a topology-specification pair is or is not defensible -- with the associated winning region and shield.
  Beyond the binary verdict, we derive topology-level metrics from the attractor structure and combine them with post-convergence behavior from shield-constrained adversarial multi-agent reinforcement learning. Together these form a defensibility fingerprint capturing both a network's formal safety properties and its operational behavior under adaptive play.
  A what-if analysis shows that formal defensibility and operational effectiveness capture distinct aspects of security: small architectural changes can produce large shifts in operational outcomes while leaving formal safety margins nearly unchanged. Shield synthesis is thus most valuable not as a deployment mechanism for safe agents, but as a framework for answering architectural questions about whether, where, and how a system can be defended. The defensibility verdict is the output, not the safe policy.

</details>


### [323] [Before You Think: System 0, AI-Mediated Cognition and Cognitive Colonization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13658)
*Marianna Bergamaschi Ganapini,Massimo Chiriatti,Enrico Panai,Giuseppe Riva*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: System 0 uniquely accounts for AI's deep, often invisible influence on the self and reasoning—cognitive colonization—not fully captured by Tri-System Theory or Thinkframes; highlights urgent philosophical and practical implications.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To compare frameworks for AI's cognitive and epistemic effects and to articulate a novel mechanism of influence (cognitive colonization) that current theories overlook.

Method: Theoretical comparative analysis of three frameworks (Tri-System Theory, Thinkframes, System 0), plus the introduction and explication of cognitive colonization as a new concept; synthesis of implications for philosophy and practice.

Result: System 0 occupies a distinctive theoretical position not replicable by the other two frameworks; cognitive colonization explains how AI embeds external interests within the self, creating covert forms of influence that are widespread and hard to perceive.

Conclusion: Understanding and addressing invisible forms of AI influence is urgent for philosophy, design, and policy, with implications for safeguarding autonomy and epistemic integrity.

Abstract: This paper examines three recent frameworks for understanding the cognitive and epistemic consequences of artificial intelligence: Tri-System Theory, Thinkframes, and System 0. It argues that while the first two capture important dimensions of AI's influence on individual reasoning and collective epistemic practices, System 0 occupies a theoretically distinctive position that neither can fully replicate. The paper introduces the concept of cognitive colonization, according to which AI systems can embed external interests within the architecture of the self in ways that are difficult for users to perceive. Because such systems are already widely deployed, understanding these invisible forms of influence is an urgent philosophical and practical task.

</details>


### [324] [EurekAgent: Agent Environment Engineering is All You Need For Autonomous Scientific Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13662)
*Amy Xin,Jiening Siow,Junjie Wang,Zijun Yao,Fanjin Zhang,Jian Song,Lei Hou,Juanzi Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Environment design is the bottleneck in autonomous scientific discovery; EurekAgent advances the field by engineering the agent's environment across four dimensions to amplify productive behaviors while mitigating harms, achieving state-of-the-art results and low API costs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM-based agents have potential to automate discovery, but their performance is constrained by the environments in which they operate. Designing collaborative, resource-bounded, and controllable environments can unlock more reliable and scalable autonomous research.

Method: EurekAgent implements environment engineering along four dimensions: (1) permissions engineering for bounded execution and isolated evaluation, (2) artifact engineering for filesystem/Git-based collaboration, (3) budget engineering for budget-aware exploration, and (4) human-in-the-loop engineering for easy supervision and intervention.

Result: Achieves new state-of-the-art results on multiple mathematics, kernel engineering, and machine learning tasks, including discovery of 26-circle packing with total API cost under $11. Code and results are open-sourced.

Conclusion: Environment engineering is a core research direction for reliable autonomous research agents; EurekAgent demonstrates that engineered environments can unlock superior performance and reproducibility in autonomous discovery.

Abstract: LLM-based agents have shown increasing potential in automating scientific discovery. Given an optimizable metric and an execution environment, they can propose, validate, and iterate scientific solutions, and have produced results that outperform human-designed approaches. As model capabilities continue to improve, we argue that the bottleneck for autonomous scientific discovery is shifting from prescribing agent workflows to designing agent environments: the resources, constraints, and interfaces that shape agent behavior. We frame this as environment engineering: building environments that amplify productive behaviors, such as open-ended exploration, systematic artifact management, and inter-agent collaboration, while suppressing harmful behaviors, such as reward hacking and high-friction human oversight. We present EurekAgent, an environment-engineered agent system for metric-driven autonomous scientific discovery. EurekAgent engineers the environment along four dimensions: permissions engineering for bounded agent execution and isolated evaluation; artifact engineering for filesystem and Git-based collaboration; budget engineering for budget-aware exploration; and human-in-the-loop engineering for easy human supervision and intervention. EurekAgent sets new state-of-the-art results on multiple mathematics, kernel engineering, and machine learning tasks, including new state-of-the-art 26-circle packing results discovered with less than $11 in total API cost. We open-source our code and results, and call for environment engineering as a core research direction for developing reliable autonomous research agents.

</details>


### [325] [Agents-K1: Towards Agent-native Knowledge Orchestration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13669)
*Zongsheng Cao,Bihao Zhan,Jinxin Shi,Jiong Wang,Fangchen Yu,Zhijie Zhong,Zijie Guo,Tianshuo Peng,Zhuo Liu,Yi Xie,Xiang Zhuang,Yue Fan,Runmin Ma,Shiyang Feng,Xiangchao Yan,Anran Liu,Peng Ye,Wenlong Zhang,Shufei Zhang,Chunfeng Song,Fenghua Ling,Jie Zhou,Liang He,Bo Zhang,Lei Bai*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A framework (Agents-K1) converts full scientific papers into knowledge graphs to enable robust scientific information extraction and multi-hop reasoning, addressing limitations of abstract-only representations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM research agents overlook scientific knowledge orchestration; papers are reduced to abstracts and surface citations, losing entities, claims, evidence, mechanisms, and method lineages necessary for scientific reasoning.

Method: Agents-K1 comprises (1) a multimodal parser with a five-module schema capturing entities, multimodal evidence, citations, and typed inter-entity relations across full papers; (2) a 4B information-extraction backbone trained with GRPO under a rule-based reward; (3) a graphanything CLI that unifies web search, multimodal graph retrieval, and cross-document traversal; and (4) a tri-source agent interface for these components.

Result: Applied to 2.46 million papers across six subjects to produce Scholar-KG; released a one-million-paper subset; full Scholar-KG accessible via SCP link; experiments show superior performance in scientific information extraction, knowledge graph construction, and multi-hop scientific reasoning.

Conclusion: The pipeline is generalizable to other domains and schema-conformant data synthesis; Agents-K1 enables end-to-end knowledge orchestration from raw documents to agent-native scientific knowledge graphs.

Abstract: Current LLM-based research agents have advanced through agent orchestration, yet largely overlook scientific knowledge orchestration. Existing works often reduce papers to abstracts, surface mentions, and flat \texttt{cites} edges, omitting key entities, claims, evidence, mechanisms, and method lineages essential for scientific reasoning. To this end, we introduce \textbf{Agents-K1}, an end-to-end knowledge orchestration pipeline that converts raw documents into agent-native scientific knowledge graphs. Agents-K1 integrates three components under a unifying theoretical foundation: a multimodal parser whose five-module schema captures entities, multimodal evidence, citations, and typed inter-entity relations across the full paper rather than abstracts alone; a 4B information-extraction backbone trained with GRPO under a rule-based reward; and a graphanything CLI, a tri-source agent interface that unifies web search, multimodal graph retrieval, and cross-document traversal. On top of this, we process 2.46 million scientific papers across six subjects to produce \textbf{Scholar-KG}, of which we release a one-million-paper subset, and the full Scholar-KG is accessible via the SCP link below. The same pipeline can be extended to general-domain corpora and to schema-conformant data synthesis. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Agents-K1 achieves superior performance in scientific information extraction, knowledge graph construction, and multi-hop scientific reasoning.

</details>


### [326] [Automated reproducibility assessments in the social and behavioral sciences using large language models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13670)
*Tobias Holtdirk,Pietro Marcolongo,Anna Steinberg Schulten,Felix Henninger,Stefan Rose,Sarah Ball,Bolei Ma,Frauke Kreuter,Markus Weinmann,Stefan Feuerriegel*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLMs can automate reproducibility assessments; in a sample of 76 studies, they matched original effect sizes within +/-0.05 in 41% (7 studies had no viable estimate) and matched qualitative conclusions in 96%; humans matched effect sizes in 34% and qualitative conclusions in 74%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reproducibility checks in social/behavioral sciences are resource-intensive and difficult to scale; a scalable automation approach is desirable.

Method: Applied an LLM-based pipeline to 76 published studies with predefined claims. Compared LLM outputs to the original findings and to human reanalyses. Seven studies yielded no viable effect size; for the rest, Cohen's d was compared within a tolerance of +/-0.05 to the original effect. Qualitative conclusions about whether reanalysis supported the original claim were also compared.

Result: LLM recovered original effect sizes in 41% of applicable studies; qualitative conclusions matched the original in 96% of cases. Human reanalysts recovered original effect sizes in 34% and qualitative conclusions in 74% of cases.

Conclusion: LLMs show potential as scalable automated tools for reproducibility assessment in the social and behavioral sciences, effectively matching qualitative conclusions more often than humans; however, accuracy for exact effect size is limited and some studies yield no viable estimates, indicating room for methodological improvements and calibration.

Abstract: Reproducibility in the social and behavioral sciences is typically evaluated by independent researchers who reanalyze the original data to assess whether the published findings can be recovered. However, such approaches are resource-intensive and difficult to scale. Here, we show that large language models (LLMs) can automate reproducibility assessments. Using N=76 published studies with predefined claims from the behavioral and social sciences, we compare LLM-generated analysis with the original findings and human reanalysis. For 7 studies, the LLM could not produce a viable effect size estimate. For the remaining studies, our LLM pipeline recovered the original effect sizes in 41% of studies using a +/-0.05 tolerance in Cohen's d. Further, our LLM pipeline reached the same qualitative conclusion as the original study in 96% of cases, where conclusions indicate whether the reanalysis supports the original claim. For comparison, human reanalysts recovered the original effect sizes in 34% of studies and reached the same qualitative conclusion in 74% of cases. Together, these results show that LLMs can serve as a scalable tool for automated reproducibility assessment and provide a foundation for systematic auditing of empirical results in the social and behavioral sciences.

</details>
